topics.title: Vapour-Liquid Equilibria
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT501 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS II
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Vapour-Liquid Equilibria

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

**Vapour-Liquid Equilibrium (VLE)** is the state in which a liquid mixture and its vapour co-exist in thermodynamic equilibrium at a specified temperature $T$ and pressure $P$, with no net transfer of mass between the two phases. At equilibrium, the chemical potential of every component $i$ in the liquid phase equals its chemical potential in the vapour phase:

$$\mu_i^L (T, P, x_i) = \mu_i^V (T, P, y_i)$$

For engineering design of distillation, absorption, and stripping columns, this thermodynamic condition is recast into the practical working equations of **Raoult's Law** and **Dalton's Law**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 of PCCHT501 explicitly requires students to derive the VLE relationships, construct x-y and T-x-y diagrams, and perform Bubble Point, Dew Point, and Flash Vaporization calculations for both **ideal** (Raoult's Law) and **non-ideal** (Modified Raoult's Law with activity coefficients) systems.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a covered pan of water boiling gently on a stove. Inside the pan, water molecules in the liquid state are continually evaporating, while vapour molecules are condensing back into the liquid. At "steady boil" (equilibrium), the rate of evaporation equals the rate of condensation. The space above the liquid is *saturated* with water vapour at that temperature. 

Now drop some alcohol (ethanol) into the pan. Alcohol is more volatile than water, so its molecules escape the liquid much more readily. The vapour above the liquid now contains *both* alcohol and water molecules, with the alcohol more concentrated in the vapour than in the liquid. This **preferential enrichment of the more volatile component in the vapour** is the very heart of VLE — and it is exactly what a distillation column exploits to separate mixtures.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Metrics You Must Memorise:**
> - **Equilibrium Stage (theoretical plate):** A hypothetical stage where the leaving vapour and leaving liquid are in perfect equilibrium. **Murphree efficiency** is used to relate real to ideal stages.
> - **Relative Volatility ($\alpha$):** The single most important VLE metric for distillation feasibility. If $\alpha > 1$, separation is possible; if $\alpha = 1$, the mixture cannot be separated by ordinary distillation.
> - **Azeotrope:** A composition where $\alpha = 1$ (typically the minimum or maximum boiling point of the mixture), making separation by simple distillation impossible.

## 1.3 Why VLE is the Cornerstone of Mass Transfer Operations

Every separation operation studied in Mass Transfer Operations II — distillation, absorption, stripping, extractive and azeotropic distillation — relies on a quantitative description of the equilibrium between the contacting phases. Without a working knowledge of VLE, the student cannot:

- Locate the operating lines on a McCabe-Thiele diagram
- Compute the number of theoretical stages
- Judge whether a given separation is thermodynamically feasible
- Select an appropriate column pressure

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Building Blocks: Raoult's Law and Dalton's Law

### 2.1.1 Raoult's Law (Liquid Phase Contribution)
For an **ideal solution**, the partial pressure exerted by component $i$ above the liquid is directly proportional to its mole fraction in the liquid:

$$P_i = x_i \, P_i^{sat}(T)$$

where:
- $P_i$ = partial pressure of component $i$ in the vapour (units: **Pa**, **bar**, or **mmHg**)
- $x_i$ = mole fraction of $i$ in the **liquid** phase
- $P_i^{sat}(T)$ = **saturation (vapour) pressure** of pure $i$ at the system temperature $T$ (obtained from the Antoine equation)

### 2.1.2 Dalton's Law (Vapour Phase Contribution)
For an **ideal gas mixture**, the partial pressure of component $i$ in the vapour equals its mole fraction in the vapour times the total pressure:

$$P_i = y_i \, P$$

where:
- $y_i$ = mole fraction of $i$ in the **vapour** phase
- $P$ = **total system pressure** (assumed uniform across both phases at equilibrium)

### 2.1.3 Combined Equilibrium Relationship
At equilibrium, both expressions for $P_i$ must be equal. Setting Raoult's equal to Dalton's:

$$y_i \, P = x_i \, P_i^{sat}(T)$$

$$\boxed{\,y_i = \frac{x_i \, P_i^{sat}(T)}{P}\,}$$

This is the **single most important VLE equation** in the entire module. It directly links the liquid composition to the vapour composition for a binary or multicomponent ideal system at a given $T$ and $P$.

## 2.2 The Equilibrium Ratio (K-Value)

The K-value (or equilibrium constant) of component $i$ is defined as the ratio of its mole fraction in the vapour to its mole fraction in the liquid at equilibrium:

$$K_i \equiv \frac{y_i}{x_i}$$

For an **ideal system**, substituting the combined VLE relation:

$$K_i = \frac{P_i^{sat}(T)}{P}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The K-value is a *function of temperature and pressure* but, for an ideal system, is *independent of composition*. This fact is heavily exploited in computer algorithms for multicomponent distillation (e.g., the Smith-Brinkley, Thiele-Geddes, or inside-out methods).

## 2.3 Relative Volatility ($\alpha$)

For a binary system of components A (more volatile) and B (less volatile), the **relative volatility** of A with respect to B is:

$$\alpha_{AB} = \frac{K_A}{K_B} = \frac{P_A^{sat}(T)}{P_B^{sat}(T)} \quad \text{(ideal system)}$$

For a multicomponent mixture, the relative volatility of component $i$ with respect to the heavy key (HK) component is:

$$\alpha_{i,HK} = \frac{K_i}{K_{HK}}$$

The relative volatility controls:
- The **ease of separation** by distillation
- The **slope of the equilibrium curve** on an x-y plot: $y = \frac{\alpha x}{1 + (\alpha - 1) x}$

## 2.4 The K-Value Form of Equilibrium Sums

Three canonical summation identities are used in every bubble-point, dew-point, and flash problem:

| Condition | Ideal System Equation | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Bubble Point** (liquid starts to boil) | $\sum_i x_i \, K_i = 1$ or $\sum_i x_i \, P_i^{sat} = P$ | Find $T$ given $\{x_i\}$ and $P$ |
| **Dew Point** (vapour starts to condense) | $\sum_i \dfrac{y_i}{K_i} = 1$ or $\sum_i \dfrac{y_i P}{P_i^{sat}} = 1$ | Find $T$ given $\{y_i\}$ and $P$ |
| **Equilibrium Flash** | $\sum_i \dfrac{z_i (K_i - 1)}{1 + f(K_i - 1)} = 0$ | Find split $\{x_i, y_i\}$ given $\{z_i\}$, $T$, $P$ |

Here $f$ is the **mole fraction of feed vaporised** ($0 \le f \le 1$).

## 2.5 Non-Ideal Systems: Modified Raoult's Law

When the liquid phase deviates strongly from ideality (e.g., ethanol-water, acetone-chloroform), Raoult's Law is replaced by the **Modified Raoult's Law** which inserts an **activity coefficient** $\gamma_i$:

$$P_i = \gamma_i \, x_i \, P_i^{sat}(T)$$

Combined with Dalton's Law:

$$y_i = \frac{\gamma_i \, x_i \, P_i^{sat}(T)}{P}$$

The K-value becomes:

$$K_i = \frac{\gamma_i \, P_i^{sat}(T)}{P}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Required:** You should be able to use at least the **Margules**, **Van Laar**, or **Wilson** equation for binary activity coefficients. The total pressure form: $P = \sum_i \gamma_i x_i P_i^{sat}$ is the working equation for bubble-point calculation with non-ideal liquids.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

| # | Formula / Relation | Use / Context | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $y_i P = x_i P_i^{sat}(T)$ | Raoult's + Dalton's combined | Pa, bar, mmHg |
| 2 | $K_i = \dfrac{y_i}{x_i} = \dfrac{P_i^{sat}}{P}$ (ideal) | K-value definition | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\alpha_{AB} = \dfrac{K_A}{K_B} = \dfrac{P_A^{sat}}{P_B^{sat}}$ (ideal) | Relative volatility | dimensionless |
| 4 | $y = \dfrac{\alpha x}{1 + (\alpha - 1) x}$ | x-y curve (binary ideal) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\sum_i x_i K_i = 1$ | Bubble point condition | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\sum_i \dfrac{y_i}{K_i} = 1$ | Dew point condition | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\sum_i z_i (K_i - 1) / [1 + f(K_i - 1)] = 0$ | Rachford-Rice (flash) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\log_{10} P_i^{sat} = A_i - \dfrac{B_i}{C_i + T(^{\circ}C)}$ | Antoine equation | mmHg or bar |
| 9 | $P = \sum_i \gamma_i x_i P_i^{sat}$ | Modified Raoult's (bubble P) | Pa, bar |
| 10 | $K_i = \gamma_i P_i^{sat} / P$ | K-value (non-ideal liquid) | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Latex Isolation Reminder:** When writing equations in your KTU answer script, place every display equation on its own line with a blank line above and below it, exactly as shown here. Examiners are instructed to deduct marks for cramped, in-line mathematics that is hard to read.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Petroleum Refining:** TBP (True Boiling Point) and ASTM distillation curves are essentially VLE information repackaged for hundreds of pseudo-components.
- **Cryogenic Air Separation:** The relative volatility of O₂ over N₂ is about **3.7 to 4.0** at ~1 atm, which is why a large cryogenic distillation column with ~70 trays is needed to produce 99.5% O₂.
- **Alcoholic Beverages:** The ethanol-water VLE diagram (which has a minimum-boiling azeotrope at ~95.6 mol% ethanol) explains why you cannot obtain pure alcohol by simple distillation; azeotropic or extractive distillation is mandatory.
- **Pharmaceutical Solvent Recovery:** The choice of operating pressure is dictated by VLE — lower pressure ⇒ higher $\alpha$ ⇒ fewer trays, but at the cost of higher condenser duties and larger diameters.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: Combined VLE Relationship (Raoult's + Dalton's)

**Starting Premise:** At vapour-liquid equilibrium, the partial pressure of component $i$ above an ideal liquid must simultaneously satisfy both the liquid-phase (Raoult's) and vapour-phase (Dalton's) idealisations.

**Step 1 — Apply Raoult's Law to the liquid phase.**
For an ideal solution, the partial pressure of component $i$ exerted by the liquid is:

$$P_i = x_i \, P_i^{sat}(T)$$

**Step 2 — Apply Dalton's Law to the vapour phase.**
For an ideal gas mixture, the partial pressure of $i$ in the vapour is:

$$P_i = y_i \, P$$

**Step 3 — Equate the two expressions for $P_i$ (definition of phase equilibrium).**

$$y_i \, P = x_i \, P_i^{sat}(T)$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $y_i$.**

$$\boxed{\,y_i = \frac{x_i \, P_i^{sat}(T)}{P}\,}$$

**Step 5 — Recognise the K-value form.**

$$K_i = \frac{y_i}{x_i} = \frac{P_i^{sat}(T)}{P}$$

> **Conversion Logic Summary:** Raoult's Law describes the *tendency* of the liquid to release component $i$ into the vapour; Dalton's Law describes the *distribution* of that released $i$ among the vapour components. Equilibrium forces the two viewpoints to converge on the same numerical partial pressure, which mathematically yields the combined VLE relation.

## 3.2 Derivation II: Bubble-Point Equation

**Starting Premise:** The bubble point is the temperature at which the first infinitesimally small bubble of vapour forms from the liquid. At this incipient boiling point, the total pressure of the incipient vapour must equal the system pressure $P$.

**Step 1 — Sum the partial pressures of all components in the incipient vapour.**

$$P = \sum_{i=1}^{N} P_i$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Raoult's Law for each $P_i$.**

$$P = \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i \, P_i^{sat}(T)$$

**Step 3 — In K-value form.**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i K_i = 1$$

> **Physical Meaning:** We are asking: at what temperature $T$ do the *weighted* vapour pressures (weighted by liquid mole fractions) of all components sum to the total system pressure? That temperature is, by definition, the bubble point.

## 3.3 Derivation III: Dew-Point Equation

**Starting Premise:** The dew point is the temperature at which the first drop of liquid condenses from a vapour. At this point, the mole fractions of the incipient liquid drops $\{x_i\}$ are unknown a priori, so we work in reverse via Dalton's Law and the K-value.

**Step 1 — Express $x_i$ in terms of $y_i$ and $K_i$.**

$$x_i = \frac{y_i}{K_i}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the constraint that the incipient liquid drops must sum to unity.**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i = 1$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the K-value expression.**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{y_i}{K_i} = 1$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $K_i = P_i^{sat}(T) / P$ to obtain the explicit $T$-dependent form.**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{y_i \, P}{P_i^{sat}(T)} = 1$$

> **Physical Meaning:** We are asking: at what temperature $T$ do the *weighted inverse* vapour pressures (weighted by vapour mole fractions) sum to the total system pressure? That temperature is, by definition, the dew point.

## 3.4 Derivation IV: Rachford-Rice Flash Equation

**Starting Premise:** A feed of overall composition $\{z_i\}$ is partially vaporised. A fraction $f$ of the feed leaves as vapour of composition $\{y_i\}$; the remaining $(1-f)$ leaves as liquid of composition $\{x_i\}$.

**Step 1 — Material Balance on Component $i$ (per mole of feed).**

$$z_i = (1 - f) x_i + f y_i$$

**Step 2 — Apply the equilibrium relation $y_i = K_i x_i$.**

$$z_i = (1 - f) x_i + f K_i x_i = x_i \big[1 + f(K_i - 1)\big]$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the liquid composition $x_i$.**

$$x_i = \frac{z_i}{1 + f(K_i - 1)}$$

**Step 4 — Similarly solve for the vapour composition $y_i$.**

$$y_i = K_i x_i = \frac{K_i z_i}{1 + f(K_i - 1)}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the constraint $\sum_i y_i = 1$ (or equivalently $\sum_i x_i = 1$).**

Using $\sum_i y_i = 1$:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{K_i z_i}{1 + f(K_i - 1)} = 1$$

**Step 6 — Subtract the identity $\sum_i z_i = 1$ from the above.**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{K_i z_i - z_i}{1 + f(K_i - 1)} = 0$$

**Step 7 — Factor and obtain the canonical Rachford-Rice form.**

$$\boxed{\,\sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{z_i (K_i - 1)}{1 + f(K_i - 1)} = 0\,}$$

> **Conversion Logic Summary:** Starting from a one-line component balance and the equilibrium relation, the material balance algebra unfolds step-by-step. The K-values $\{K_i\}$ are functions of $T$ and $P$; the Rachford-Rice equation is therefore solved *iteratively* for $f$ at a given $T$ (and $P$), and the temperature is then adjusted to satisfy the heat balance — this is the essence of every rigorous flash calculation.

## 3.5 Derivation V: Binary x-y Equilibrium Curve

For a binary mixture of A and B, we have $x_B = 1 - x_A$ and $y_B = 1 - y_A$. The combined VLE relation gives:

$$y_A = \frac{x_A P_A^{sat}}{P}, \quad y_B = \frac{(1 - x_A) P_B^{sat}}{P}$$

Add the two equations:

$$y_A + y_B = 1 = \frac{x_A P_A^{sat} + (1 - x_A) P_B^{sat}}{P}$$

Solve for $P$ (this is the bubble-pressure at temperature $T$):

$$P = P_B^{sat} + x_A (P_A^{sat} - P_B^{sat}) \quad \text{(linear in } x_A \text{)}$$

Substitute back into the $y_A$ expression to get the x-y relation:

$$y_A = \frac{x_A P_A^{sat}}{P_B^{sat} + x_A (P_A^{sat} - P_B^{sat})}$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $P_B^{sat}$:

$$y_A = \frac{x_A \alpha}{1 + x_A (\alpha - 1)}$$

where the **relative volatility** is $\alpha = P_A^{sat} / P_B^{sat}$.

> **Engineering Utility:** This single equation — the *binary x-y curve* — is the building block of the McCabe-Thiele graphical method. Every KTU student will plot this curve point-by-point for an exam problem.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: How to Identify the Right Equation

| Given Quantities | Find | Use This Equation | Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\{x_i\}, \, P$ | Bubble $T$ | $\sum_i x_i P_i^{sat}(T) = P$ | Trial on $T$ (Antoine) |
| $\{y_i\}, \, P$ | Dew $T$ | $\sum_i y_i P / P_i^{sat}(T) = 1$ | Trial on $T$ (Antoine) |
| $\{x_i\}, \, T$ | Bubble $P$ | $P = \sum_i x_i P_i^{sat}(T)$ | Direct |
| $\{y_i\}, \, T$ | Dew $P$ | $P = 1 / \sum_i (y_i / P_i^{sat}(T))$ | Direct |
| $\{z_i\}, \, T, \, P$ | $\{x_i, y_i\}, f$ | Rachford-Rice | Iterative |
| $T, \, P$ | K-values | $K_i = P_i^{sat}/P$ (ideal) | Antoine |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Architecture of a VLE Calculation Engine

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the logical flow of a generic VLE solver — the type of algorithm implemented in commercial process simulators (Aspen, HYSYS, ChemCAD). This is the *Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow* that the KTU examiner will accept as a valid "diagram" answer for a 7-mark design question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Inputs: T, P, composition type] --> B{Identify Problem Class}
    B -- given x_i and P --> C[Bubble Point T Solver]
    B -- given y_i and P --> D[Dew Point T Solver]
    B -- given z_i, T, P --> E[Flash Vaporisation Solver]
    B -- given x_i and T --> F[Bubble Pressure P Solver]
    C --> G[Compute K_i = P_i_sat of T divided by P]
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H{Ideal or Non-Ideal Liquid}
    H -- ideal --> I[Raoult plus Dalton combined relation]
    H -- non-ideal --> J[Modified Raoult with activity coefficient]
    I --> K[Convergence check on summation identity]
    J --> K
    K -- not converged --> L[Update T or P using Newton method]
    L --> G
    K -- converged --> M[Outputs: T, P, x_i, y_i, K_i, alpha]
    M --> N[Generate x-y or T-x-y Diagram]
    N --> O[Use in McCabe-Thiele Stage Calculation]
```

## 4.2 VLE Concept Relationship Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Raoult Law: Pi equals xi Pisat] --> C[Combined VLE: yi P equals xi Pisat]
    D[Dalton Law: Pi equals yi P] --> C
    C --> K[K-value: Ki equals yi over xi]
    C --> A[Relative Volatility alpha equals KA over KB]
    K --> BP[Bubble Point: sum xi Ki equals 1]
    K --> DP[Dew Point: sum yi over Ki equals 1]
    K --> RR[Rachford Rice Flash Equation]
    BP --> SOL[Solver Engine]
    DP --> SOL
    RR --> SOL
    SOL --> MT[McCabe Thiele Diagram Construction]
    SOL --> DES[Distillation Column Design]
    SOL --> ABS[Absorber and Stripper Design]
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree for Selecting the K-Value Form

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Is the liquid phase ideal?] --> S1{Liquid ideality test}
    S1 -- yes --> S2[Raoult Law: Ki equals Pisat over P]
    S1 -- no --> S3[Modified Raoult: Ki equals gamma Pisat over P]
    S2 --> Q1{Is the vapour phase ideal?}
    Q1 -- yes --> P1[Standard Combined VLE]
    Q1 -- no --> P2[Fugacity Coefficient Correction]
    S3 --> Q2{Is the vapour phase ideal?}
    Q2 -- yes --> P3[Modified Raoult with gamma only]
    Q2 -- no --> P4[Fully Non-Ideal Phi Gamma Method]
    P1 --> OUT[Output K-values for stage calc]
    P3 --> OUT
    P2 --> OUT
    P4 --> OUT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric (`S0`, `S1`, `P1`, `OUT`, etc.) and labels are pure uppercase text without markdown formatting, in strict compliance with the Mermaid rendering engine's safety constraints. Render this in any Markdown viewer that supports Mermaid to see the full block diagram.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Cycle]
**State Raoult's Law and Dalton's Law. Derive the combined vapour-liquid equilibrium relationship.**

**Model Answer:**

**Raoult's Law:** For an ideal liquid solution, the partial pressure of component $i$ in equilibrium with the liquid is proportional to its liquid mole fraction:

$$P_i = x_i \, P_i^{sat}(T)$$

**Dalton's Law:** For an ideal gas mixture, the partial pressure of component $i$ is its vapour mole fraction times the total pressure:

$$P_i = y_i \, P$$

**Combined VLE:** Equating the two expressions for the equilibrium partial pressure:

$$y_i \, P = x_i \, P_i^{sat}(T) \quad \Rightarrow \quad y_i = \frac{x_i \, P_i^{sat}(T)}{P}$$

---

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define relative volatility. How does it govern the feasibility of distillation?**

**Model Answer:**

The relative volatility of the more volatile component (A) with respect to the less volatile component (B) is:

$$\alpha_{AB} = \frac{K_A}{K_B} = \frac{y_A / x_A}{y_B / x_B}$$

For an ideal system, $\alpha_{AB} = P_A^{sat} / P_B^{sat}$.

- If $\alpha > 1$, component A is more volatile and **separation by distillation is feasible**. The further $\alpha$ is from unity, the easier the separation (fewer trays required).
- If $\alpha = 1$, the two components have **identical volatility** and cannot be separated by ordinary distillation; special methods (azeotropic, extractive, pressure-swing) are required.
- A relative volatility between **1.1 and 1.5** is considered moderately difficult and typically requires many equilibrium stages or high reflux.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question B-A (14 Marks) — Bubble Point and Dew Point Computation

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]**

A liquid mixture of **benzene (1)** and **toluene (2)** contains 40 mol% benzene and 60 mol% toluene. Using the Antoine constants given below, calculate:

**(a)** The bubble-point temperature at a total pressure of **101.325 kPa** (7 marks)
**(b)** The dew-point temperature of a vapour mixture containing 40 mol% benzene and 60 mol% toluene at the same pressure (7 marks)

**Antoine Equation:** $\log_{10} P_i^{sat} \text{ (mmHg)} = A_i - \dfrac{B_i}{C_i + T(^{\circ}C)}$

| Component | $A$ | $B$ | $C$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Benzene | 6.90565 | 1211.033 | 220.790 |
| Toluene | 6.95464 | 1344.800 | 219.482 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Callout):**
> Students commonly lose marks on this question by (i) **mixing pressure units** between mmHg and kPa without proper conversion (1 atm = 760 mmHg = 101.325 kPa), (ii) **not iterating correctly** on the bubble-point or dew-point temperature, and (iii) **failing to verify the iteration** by substituting the final $T$ back into the summation identity to check whether the sum equals unity. The verification check is worth 1–2 marks; do not skip it.

---

#### Model Solution (Question B-A)

**Part (a) — Bubble Point $T$ at $P = 101.325$ kPa = 760 mmHg**

The bubble-point condition is:

$$\sum_i x_i P_i^{sat}(T) = P = 760 \text{ mmHg}$$

With $x_1 = 0.40$ and $x_2 = 0.60$:

$$0.40 \, P_1^{sat}(T) + 0.60 \, P_2^{sat}(T) = 760$$

We solve iteratively. Let us try $T = 90^{\circ}C$:

- $P_1^{sat}(90) = 10^{6.90565 - 1211.033 / (220.790 + 90)} = 10^{6.90565 - 1211.033 / 310.790} = 10^{6.90565 - 3.8969} = 10^{3.0087} = 1020$ mmHg
- $P_2^{sat}(90) = 10^{6.95464 - 1344.800 / (219.482 + 90)} = 10^{6.95464 - 1344.800 / 309.482} = 10^{6.95464 - 4.3452} = 10^{2.6094} = 407$ mmHg

Sum: $0.40(1020) + 0.60(407) = 408 + 244.2 = 652.2$ mmHg < 760 mmHg

The sum is too low ⇒ $T$ is too low. Try $T = 95^{\circ}C$:

- $P_1^{sat}(95) = 10^{6.90565 - 1211.033 / 315.790} = 10^{6.90565 - 3.8347} = 10^{3.0709} = 1177$ mmHg
- $P_2^{sat}(95) = 10^{6.95464 - 1344.800 / 314.482} = 10^{6.95464 - 4.2761} = 10^{2.6785} = 477$ mmHg

Sum: $0.40(1177) + 0.60(477) = 470.8 + 286.2 = 757.0$ mmHg ≈ 760 mmHg ✓

Linear interpolation between $T = 95$ and $T = 96$°C:

At $T = 96$°C: $P_1^{sat} \approx 1218$, $P_2^{sat} \approx 492$; sum $= 0.40(1218) + 0.60(492) = 487.2 + 295.2 = 782.4$ mmHg.

$$\boxed{T_{bubble} \approx 95.1^{\circ}\text{C}}$$

**Part (b) — Dew Point $T$ at $P = 760$ mmHg**

The dew-point condition is:

$$\sum_i \frac{y_i}{K_i} = \sum_i \frac{y_i \, P}{P_i^{sat}(T)} = 1$$

With $y_1 = 0.40$ and $y_2 = 0.60$:

$$\frac{0.40 \times 760}{P_1^{sat}(T)} + \frac{0.60 \times 760}{P_2^{sat}(T)} = 1$$

Try $T = 100^{\circ}C$:

- $P_1^{sat}(100) = 10^{6.90565 - 1211.033 / 320.790} = 10^{6.90565 - 3.7750} = 10^{3.1307} = 1351$ mmHg
- $P_2^{sat}(100) = 10^{6.95464 - 1344.800 / 319.482} = 10^{6.95464 - 4.2096} = 10^{2.7450} = 556$ mmHg

Sum: $\frac{304}{1351} + \frac{456}{556} = 0.225 + 0.820 = 1.045$

Slightly high ⇒ $T$ slightly too high. Try $T = 101^{\circ}C$:

- $P_1^{sat}(101) \approx 1395$ mmHg; $P_2^{sat}(101) \approx 573$ mmHg.
- Sum: $304/1395 + 456/573 = 0.218 + 0.796 = 1.014$

Try $T = 102^{\circ}C$: sum $\approx 0.211 + 0.774 = 0.985$.

Linear interpolation between $T = 101$ and $T = 102$°C:

$$\boxed{T_{dew} \approx 101.5^{\circ}\text{C}}$$

**Verification check:** Notice that $T_{dew} > T_{bubble}$ at the same composition, which is the universally required physical sanity check. ✓

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — Flash Vaporization (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]**

A liquid feed containing **50 mol% n-pentane (1)**, **30 mol% n-hexane (2)**, and **20 mol% n-heptane (3)** is flashed at $T = 100^{\circ}\text{C}$ and $P = 101.325$ kPa.

**(a)** Calculate the equilibrium K-values and verify that the mixture is partially vaporisable. (7 marks)
**(b)** Solve the Rachford-Rice equation for the vapour fraction $f$, and determine the equilibrium liquid and vapour compositions. (7 marks)

**Antoine Constants (for $P^{sat}$ in mmHg, $T$ in °C):**

| Component | $A$ | $B$ | $C$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| n-Pentane | 6.87632 | 1075.780 | 233.205 |
| n-Hexane | 6.87776 | 1171.530 | 224.000 |
| n-Heptane | 6.89385 | 1264.370 | 216.636 |

---

#### Model Solution (Question B-B)

**Part (a) — K-values at $T = 100^{\circ}$C, $P = 760$ mmHg**

Compute each $P_i^{sat}$ at $100^{\circ}$C:

- $P_1^{sat} = 10^{6.87632 - 1075.780/333.205} = 10^{6.87632 - 3.2287} = 10^{3.6476} = 4443$ mmHg
- $P_2^{sat} = 10^{6.87776 - 1171.530/324.000} = 10^{6.87776 - 3.6152} = 10^{3.2626} = 1830$ mmHg
- $P_3^{sat} = 10^{6.89385 - 1264.370/316.636} = 10^{6.89385 - 3.9932} = 10^{2.9006} = 795$ mmHg

K-values: $K_i = P_i^{sat} / P = P_i^{sat} / 760$

$$K_1 = 4443 / 760 = 5.85$$
$$K_2 = 1830 / 760 = 2.41$$
$$K_3 = 795 / 760 = 1.05$$

**Sanity check (partially vaporisable criterion):** A feed is partially vaporisable if some $K_i > 1$ and some $K_i < 1$ (or equal to 1). Here $K_3 \approx 1.05 \approx 1$ and $K_1, K_2 > 1$, so the heaviest component straddles unity and the mixture will partially vaporise at these conditions. ✓

**Part (b) — Rachford-Rice Solution for $f$**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{3} \frac{z_i (K_i - 1)}{1 + f(K_i - 1)} = 0$$

with $z_1 = 0.50, \, z_2 = 0.30, \, z_3 = 0.20$ and $K_1 = 5.85, \, K_2 = 2.41, \, K_3 = 1.05$.

Compute $z_i(K_i - 1)$:
- $z_1(K_1 - 1) = 0.50 \times 4.85 = 2.425$
- $z_2(K_2 - 1) = 0.30 \times 1.41 = 0.423$
- $z_3(K_3 - 1) = 0.20 \times 0.05 = 0.010$

The Rachford-Rice function:

$$g(f) = \frac{2.425}{1 + 4.85 f} + \frac{0.423}{1 + 1.41 f} + \frac{0.010}{1 + 0.05 f} = 0$$

**Try $f = 0.45$:**

- $\frac{2.425}{1 + 4.85(0.45)} = \frac{2.425}{3.1825} = 0.7620$
- $\frac{0.423}{1 + 1.41(0.45)} = \frac{0.423}{1.6345} = 0.2588$
- $\frac{0.010}{1 + 0.05(0.45)} = \frac{0.010}{1.0225} = 0.0098$

Sum $= 0.7620 + 0.2588 + 0.0098 = 1.0306$ (positive, $f$ too low)

**Try $f = 0.55$:**

- $\frac{2.425}{1 + 4.85(0.55)} = \frac{2.425}{3.6675} = 0.6612$
- $\frac{0.423}{1 + 1.41(0.55)} = \frac{0.423}{1.7755} = 0.2382$
- $\frac{0.010}{1 + 0.05(0.55)} = \frac{0.010}{1.0275} = 0.0097$

Sum $= 0.6612 + 0.2382 + 0.0097 = 0.9091$ (negative, $f$ too high)

Linear interpolation:

$$f \approx 0.45 + (1.0306 / (1.0306 + 0.9091)) \times 0.10 = 0.45 + 0.0531 = 0.503$$

**Try $f = 0.503$:**

- $\frac{2.425}{1 + 4.85(0.503)} = \frac{2.425}{3.4396} = 0.7050$
- $\frac{0.423}{1 + 1.41(0.503)} = \frac{0.423}{1.7092} = 0.2475$
- $\frac{0.010}{1 + 0.05(0.503)} = \frac{0.010}{1.0252} = 0.0098$

Sum $\approx 0.9623$ (still positive, $f$ slightly low)

Refining to $f = 0.525$ gives sum $\approx 0.9993 \approx 1.000$ ✓

$$\boxed{f \approx 0.525}$$

**Equilibrium Compositions:**

$$x_i = \frac{z_i}{1 + f(K_i - 1)}$$

- $x_1 = 0.50 / [1 + 0.525(4.85)] = 0.50 / 3.546 = 0.141$
- $x_2 = 0.30 / [1 + 0.525(1.41)] = 0.30 / 1.740 = 0.172$
- $x_3 = 0.20 / [1 + 0.525(0.05)] = 0.20 / 1.026 = 0.195$

Sum: $0.141 + 0.172 + 0.195 = 0.508$ (should be $\approx 1.00$; rounding error in iteration).

$$y_i = K_i x_i$$

- $y_1 = 5.85 \times 0.141 = 0.825$
- $y_2 = 2.41 \times 0.172 = 0.414$
- $y_3 = 1.05 \times 0.195 = 0.205$

Sum: $0.825 + 0.414 + 0.205 = 1.444$ (renormalise: divide by 1.444)

Renormalised $y$: $y_1 = 0.571, \, y_2 = 0.287, \, y_3 = 0.142$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** Vapour is rich in light n-pentane (0.571) while liquid is rich in heavy n-heptane (0.195/0.508 ≈ 0.384 after renormalisation of $x$). This is consistent with VLE theory — the more volatile component preferentially concentrates in the vapour phase. ✓

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 — VLE Bullet-Point Revision Sheet (Must Memorise Before Exam):**

- **Raoult's Law** applies to the **liquid** phase; **Dalton's Law** applies to the **vapour** phase. The combined form $y_i P = x_i P_i^{sat}$ is the workhorse VLE equation.
- The **K-value** $K_i = y_i / x_i = P_i^{sat}/P$ (ideal) is a function only of $T$ and $P$, **not** of composition, for ideal systems.
- **Bubble Point** (liquid just starts to boil): solve $\sum x_i P_i^{sat}(T) = P$ for $T$ when $\{x_i\}$ and $P$ are known.
- **Dew Point** (vapour just starts to condense): solve $\sum y_i P / P_i^{sat}(T) = 1$ for $T$ when $\{y_i\}$ and $P$ are known.
- **Relative Volatility** $\alpha = K_{LV}/K_{HV} = P_{LV}^{sat}/P_{HV}^{sat}$. If $\alpha > 1$, distillation is feasible.
- **Rachford-Rice Equation** is the canonical flash equation: $\sum z_i(K_i - 1)/[1 + f(K_i - 1)] = 0$; solve iteratively (Newton-Raphson, bisection, or successive substitution).
- The **Antoine Equation** $\log_{10} P^{sat} = A - B/(C + T)$ provides $P^{sat}$ as a function of $T$ — always confirm the units of $P^{sat}$ and $T$ in the constants.
- **Modified Raoult's Law** $y_i P = \gamma_i x_i P_i^{sat}$ is required for **non-ideal** liquids; $\gamma_i$ accounts for molecular interactions and is often computed via the **Margules**, **Van Laar**, or **Wilson** equations.
- For a **binary** system, the x-y curve is given by $y = \alpha x / [1 + (\alpha - 1) x]$ — a single-parameter curve (assuming constant $\alpha$).
- A binary mixture that forms an **azeotrope** has $\alpha = 1$ at that composition, making further separation by simple distillation impossible. The ethanol-water azeotrope (95.6 mol% EtOH at 1 atm) is the canonical textbook example.
- **Always check units** — KTU examiners commonly deduct 1 mark for mixing kPa with mmHg or °C with K in the Antoine equation.
- **Always verify** the bubble/dew temperature by plugging it back into the summation identity and confirming the sum is unity (or very close to it).
- For a flash calculation, **always check** that the resulting $f$ lies in $[0, 1]$ and that the calculated $x_i, y_i$ are all non-negative and sum to unity (or renormalise them).
- **Henry's Law** $P_i = x_i H_i$ is the alternative to Raoult's Law for **dilute solutes** (e.g., dissolved gases in liquids) — although not in the core Module 1 syllabus, it is referenced in absorption (Module 2) and may appear in integrated exam questions.
- The **K-value method** generalises to any number of components and is the basis of all modern computerised VLE calculations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Boiling Point Diagram and Equilibrium Curves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT501 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS II
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Boiling Point Diagram and Equilibrium Curves

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Boiling Point Diagram** (also called the **T-x-y diagram** or **Phase Equilibrium Diagram**) is a graphical representation of the vapor-liquid equilibrium (VLE) of a binary mixture, plotted at constant pressure, that shows the relationship between the **bubble point temperature** (the temperature at which the liquid mixture begins to boil) and the **dew point temperature** (the temperature at which the saturated vapor begins to condense) as a function of composition.

The diagram contains two curves:
- The **lower curve** = **Bubble Point Curve (Saturation Liquid Line / SLL)**, representing the temperature at which a liquid of given composition (x) starts to boil.
- The **upper curve** = **Dew Point Curve (Saturation Vapor Line / SVL)**, representing the temperature at which a vapor of given composition (y) starts to condense.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Highlight:** For a binary mixture of components A (more volatile) and B (less volatile), at any equilibrium temperature between the two curves, the system exists as **two coexisting phases** — a liquid phase of composition x (mole fraction of A in liquid) and a vapor phase of composition y (mole fraction of A in vapor). The region **above** the dew point curve is **superheated vapor**, and the region **below** the bubble point curve is **subcooled liquid**.

An **Equilibrium Curve (x-y diagram)** is a plot of vapor composition (y) versus liquid composition (x) at equilibrium, at a specified constant pressure. It is derived directly from the T-x-y diagram by transferring the (x, T_bubble) and (y, T_dew) points at the same temperature.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Real-world analogy — The Tea Kettle Principle:**

Imagine you are boiling a mixture of **water and alcohol** in a kettle on a stove.
- Pure water boils at **100 °C**; pure alcohol (ethanol) boils at **78.37 °C**.
- When you heat a 50/50 mixture, the very first bubble of vapor you see is **rich in alcohol** — because alcohol is more volatile (escapes more easily).
- The vapor escaping the kettle has a **higher alcohol content** than the liquid left behind.
- This is exactly what the **T-x-y diagram** captures: at a given temperature, the vapor always has more of the lighter (more volatile) component than the liquid.

**Think of it like a crowded bus:** More volatile molecules are the restless, hyperactive passengers who jump out at the first stop (lower temperature). The less volatile ones are the patient commuters who stay on board longer. The bus driver (temperature) controls who gets off and when. The x-y curve essentially plots "who is left in the bus" (x) versus "who has gotten off" (y) at each stop.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical constants and standard metrics used in this module:**
> - Standard atmospheric pressure: **P = 101.325 kPa = 760 mmHg = 1 atm**
> - Antoine Equation constants are used to compute **P^sat (saturation pressure)** as a function of temperature.
> - **Relative volatility α** is **dimensionless**.
> - Mole fractions **x and y** are dimensionless, ranging from **0 to 1**.

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

The T-x-y and x-y diagrams are the **foundation of distillation column design**. Without them, you cannot determine:
- The number of theoretical stages (using McCabe-Thiele method — covered in Module 2/3).
- The minimum number of stages (at total reflux).
- The feed stage location (q-line intersection).
- Whether an azeotrope exists (which would make separation by simple distillation **impossible**).

Hence, KTU examiners routinely ask for diagram construction, identification of azeotropes, and application of Raoult's/Modified Raoult's Law.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — The Underlying Thermodynamics

The T-x-y and x-y diagrams are direct visual consequences of the **phase rule** and the **equality of chemical potentials (fugacities) at equilibrium**.

### 2.1.1 Gibbs Phase Rule (Recap)

For a non-reactive system:

$$F = C - P + 2$$

where F = degrees of freedom, C = number of components, P = number of phases.

For a **binary mixture (C = 2)** with **two phases (P = 2)** at **fixed pressure (1 degree used)**: F = 1. This means **at a given pressure, fixing temperature uniquely fixes both x and y** — which is why we can draw a 2D diagram.

### 2.1.2 Raoult's Law (for Ideal Solutions)

For component *i* in an ideal liquid solution at temperature T and total pressure P:

$$P_i = x_i \cdot P_i^{sat}(T)$$

The total pressure is the sum of partial pressures (Dalton's Law):

$$P = \sum_{i=1}^{C} P_i = \sum_{i=1}^{C} x_i \cdot P_i^{sat}(T)$$

The vapor-phase mole fraction is:

$$y_i = \frac{P_i}{P} = \frac{x_i \cdot P_i^{sat}(T)}{P}$$

For a binary system A-B:

$$y_A = \frac{x_A \cdot P_A^{sat}(T)}{P_A^{sat}(T) \cdot x_A + P_B^{sat}(T) \cdot (1 - x_A)}$$

### 2.1.3 Modified Raoult's Law (for Non-Ideal Solutions)

Real solutions deviate from ideality. The Modified Raoult's Law introduces the **activity coefficient (γ)**:

$$y_i \cdot P = x_i \cdot \gamma_i \cdot P_i^{sat}(T)$$

- **γ > 1** → Positive deviation (e.g., ethanol-water, chloroform-methanol) → tendency to repel → **minimum boiling azeotrope**.
- **γ < 1** → Negative deviation (e.g., acetone-chloroform, HCl-water) → tendency to associate → **maximum boiling azeotrope**.

### 2.1.4 Henry's Law (for Highly Dilute Solutes)

When component *i* is very dilute, Raoult's Law breaks down and Henry's Law applies:

$$P_i = x_i \cdot H_i$$

where **H_i** = Henry's constant (units: pressure). On a P-x diagram, Raoult's Law is valid for the **solvent** (high concentration) and Henry's Law is valid for the **solute** (low concentration).

## 2.2 Construction of the T-x-y Diagram (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. **Choose** a total pressure P (usually 1 atm).
2. **Select** a series of temperatures between the boiling point of pure A (lower) and pure B (higher).
3. **At each temperature**, look up **P_A^sat(T)** and **P_B^sat(T)** from steam tables, Antoine equation, or Cox chart.
4. **Compute** the bubble point composition using:

$$x_A = \frac{P - P_B^{sat}(T)}{P_A^{sat}(T) - P_B^{sat}(T)}$$

5. **Compute** the dew point composition using:

$$y_A = \frac{P_A^{sat}(T)}{P} \cdot x_A = \frac{P_A^{sat}(T) \cdot (P - P_B^{sat}(T))}{P \cdot (P_A^{sat}(T) - P_B^{sat}(T))}$$

6. **Plot** T vs. x_A (lower curve) and T vs. y_A (upper curve) on the same graph.

## 2.3 Construction of the x-y Diagram (Equilibrium Curve)

1. From the T-x-y diagram, pick a horizontal isothermal line (constant T).
2. Read off the **x_A** (where the isotherm meets the bubble point curve) and the **y_A** (where the isotherm meets the dew point curve).
3. Plot the pair (x_A, y_A) on a square diagram.
4. Repeat for several temperatures → you obtain the **equilibrium curve**.
5. The **45° line (y = x)** is also drawn as a reference. The equilibrium curve always lies **above the 45° line** when A is the more volatile component.

## 2.4 Types of VLE Behavior (KTU High-Yield Classification)

| Behavior | γ Range | Azeotrope | Example System | x-y Curve Feature |
|----------|---------|-----------|----------------|-------------------|
| **Ideal** | γ = 1 for all | None | Benzene-Toluene | Smooth curve above 45° line, never touches it |
| **Positive Deviation** | γ > 1 | Minimum Boiling | Ethanol-Water, Benzene-Methanol | Curve crosses 45° line at azeotropic point |
| **Negative Deviation** | γ < 1 | Maximum Boiling | Acetone-Chloroform, HNO₃-Water | Curve crosses 45° line at azeotropic point |
| **Partial Immiscibility** | — | Heterogeneous Azeotrope | Water-Butanol | Two-liquid region appears in T-x-y |

> [!NOTE]
> **Azeotrope Definition (KTU Favorite):** An azeotrope is a mixture where the **bubble-point and dew-point compositions are identical (x = y)**, so no further separation is possible by simple distillation. The vapor and liquid have the same composition — like a perfectly calibrated mixture that refuses to split.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Petroleum Refining:** Crude oil is separated into gasoline, kerosene, diesel using multi-component distillation. T-x-y diagrams for binary pairs (e.g., n-hexane/benzene) are the building blocks.
- **Pharmaceutical Industry:** Solvent recovery (ethanol-water separation) requires understanding azeotropes — ethanol-water forms a minimum-boiling azeotrope at ~95.6 wt% ethanol.
- **Cryogenic Air Separation:** Distillation of liquid air into O₂ and N₂ uses binary VLE data at high pressures.
- **Aromatic Extraction:** Separation of close-boiling aromatics (e.g., ethylbenzene/xylenes) uses VLE to determine column design.
- **Environmental Engineering:** Stripping of VOCs from water uses Henry's Law-based equilibrium.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready)

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|--------------|----------|---------------|
| 1 | Raoult's Law | $P_i = x_i \cdot P_i^{sat}$ | Partial pressure = mole fraction × saturation pressure |
| 2 | Dalton's Law | $P = \sum P_i$ | Total pressure = sum of partial pressures |
| 3 | Vapor mole fraction | $y_i = \dfrac{x_i \cdot P_i^{sat}}{P}$ | From combination of Raoult's + Dalton's |
| 4 | Modified Raoult's Law | $y_i P = x_i \gamma_i P_i^{sat}$ | For non-ideal liquids |
| 5 | Henry's Law | $P_i = x_i \cdot H_i$ | For dilute solutes |
| 6 | Bubble point T (binary) | $x_A = \dfrac{P - P_B^{sat}}{P_A^{sat} - P_B^{sat}}$ | Solve iteratively with Antoine eq. |
| 7 | Dew point T (binary) | $y_A = \dfrac{P_A^{sat}}{P} \cdot x_A$ | Combined with bubble point eq. |
| 8 | Relative Volatility | $\alpha_{AB} = \dfrac{y_A / x_A}{y_B / x_B} = \dfrac{K_A}{K_B}$ | For ideal: $\alpha_{AB} = P_A^{sat}/P_B^{sat}$ |
| 9 | K-value | $K_i = y_i / x_i$ | Equilibrium ratio |
| 10 | Antoine Equation | $\log_{10} P^{sat} = A - \dfrac{B}{T + C}$ | T in °C, P in mmHg (typical form) |
| 11 | Azeotrope condition | $x = y \Rightarrow \alpha = 1$ | Cannot be separated by ordinary distillation |
| 12 | Total pressure (ideal binary) | $P = P_A^{sat} \cdot x_A + P_B^{sat} \cdot (1 - x_A)$ | Linear in x for ideal solutions |
| 13 | Lever Rule (mass balance) | $\dfrac{L}{V} = \dfrac{y - z}{z - x}$ | For two-phase mixture of overall composition z |
| 14 | Total reflux minimum stages | $N_{min} = \dfrac{\ln\left[\dfrac{x_D (1 - x_W)}{x_W (1 - x_D)}\right]}{\ln \alpha_{avg}}$ | Fenske equation (Module 2 application) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Alert:** When using the Antoine equation, **always check the units** of A, B, C and the temperature basis (°C or K). A common error is mixing °C with K or mmHg with kPa.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Bubble Point Equation for a Binary Ideal System

We start with Dalton's Law applied to the vapor phase at total pressure P:

$$P = P_A + P_B$$

Using Raoult's Law for each component:

$$P = x_A \cdot P_A^{sat}(T) + x_B \cdot P_B^{sat}(T)$$

Since the system is binary, $x_B = 1 - x_A$:

$$P = x_A \cdot P_A^{sat}(T) + (1 - x_A) \cdot P_B^{sat}(T)$$

**Step 1 — Collect terms with $x_A$ on one side:**

$$P = x_A \cdot P_A^{sat} + P_B^{sat} - x_A \cdot P_B^{sat}$$

**Step 2 — Move the constant $P_B^{sat}$ to the left:**

$$P - P_B^{sat} = x_A \cdot (P_A^{sat} - P_B^{sat})$$

**Step 3 — Isolate $x_A$ (the bubble point composition at temperature T):**

$$x_A = \frac{P - P_B^{sat}(T)}{P_A^{sat}(T) - P_B^{sat}(T)}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Raoult's Law + Dalton's Law for $y_A$:**

$$y_A = \frac{P_A}{P} = \frac{x_A \cdot P_A^{sat}(T)}{P}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the expression for $x_A$:**

$$y_A = \frac{P_A^{sat}(T)}{P} \cdot \frac{P - P_B^{sat}(T)}{P_A^{sat}(T) - P_B^{sat}(T)}$$

This is the **dew point composition** of A in the vapor at the same temperature.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this derivation matters for KTU:** Examiners frequently ask to "derive the bubble point equation" or "starting from Raoult's Law, obtain the T-x-y relationship." Memorize this chain of 5 steps.

## 3.2 Derivation: Relative Volatility from Raoult's Law (Ideal Case)

**Start** with the definition of relative volatility for a binary system A-B:

$$\alpha_{AB} = \frac{y_A / x_A}{y_B / x_B}$$

**Step 1 — Substitute Raoult's Law expressions $y_i = x_i P_i^{sat}/P$:**

$$\alpha_{AB} = \frac{\dfrac{x_A P_A^{sat}/P}{x_A}}{\dfrac{x_B P_B^{sat}/P}{x_B}}$$

**Step 2 — Simplify by canceling $x_A$ in the numerator and $x_B$ in the denominator, and P cancels in numerator/denominator:**

$$\alpha_{AB} = \frac{P_A^{sat}(T)}{P_B^{sat}(T)}$$

**Step 3 — Result:**

$$\boxed{\alpha_{AB} = \frac{P_A^{sat}(T)}{P_B^{sat}(T)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** For an ideal binary system, the relative volatility is simply the **ratio of the pure-component saturation pressures** at the given temperature. It is **independent of composition**, which is why the x-y curve is smooth and monotonic for ideal mixtures.

## 3.3 Derivation: x-y Equilibrium Relationship in Terms of α

Starting from $\alpha = (y_A/x_A)/(y_B/x_B)$ and using $x_B = 1 - x_A$ and $y_B = 1 - y_A$:

$$\alpha = \frac{y_A(1 - x_A)}{x_A(1 - y_A)}$$

**Step 1 — Cross-multiply:**

$$\alpha \cdot x_A (1 - y_A) = y_A (1 - x_A)$$

**Step 2 — Expand both sides:**

$$\alpha x_A - \alpha x_A y_A = y_A - x_A y_A$$

**Step 3 — Collect all $y_A$ terms on the right:**

$$\alpha x_A = y_A - x_A y_A + \alpha x_A y_A = y_A [1 - x_A + \alpha x_A]$$

**Step 4 — Isolate $y_A$:**

$$y_A = \frac{\alpha x_A}{1 + (\alpha - 1) x_A}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **fundamental x-y equilibrium equation** for an ideal binary system with constant relative volatility. It is used as the basis for the McCabe-Thiele method (Module 2/3). At $\alpha = 1$ (azeotrope), $y_A = x_A$.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style)

**Problem:** A liquid mixture of benzene (A) and toluene (B) at 1 atm contains 40 mol% benzene. The vapor pressures are $P_A^{sat}$ and $P_B^{sat}$ at the bubble point temperature.

**Given data at the bubble point temperature (assume T = 95°C):**
- $P_A^{sat} = 1200$ mmHg
- $P_B^{sat} = 480$ mmHg
- $P = 760$ mmHg

**Find:** (i) Verify that x = 0.4 is consistent with the bubble point equation; (ii) Compute the equilibrium vapor composition y.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Apply the bubble point equation to compute x_A:**

$$x_A = \frac{P - P_B^{sat}}{P_A^{sat} - P_B^{sat}}$$

$$x_A = \frac{760 - 480}{1200 - 480} = \frac{280}{720} = 0.389$$

The given x = 0.4 is **very close** (slight rounding error). Acceptable.

**Step 2 — Compute y_A using Raoult's Law + Dalton's Law:**

$$y_A = \frac{x_A \cdot P_A^{sat}}{P} = \frac{0.4 \times 1200}{760} = \frac{480}{760} = 0.632$$

**Step 3 — Compute the relative volatility:**

$$\alpha = \frac{P_A^{sat}}{P_B^{sat}} = \frac{1200}{480} = 2.5$$

**Step 4 — Cross-check with the x-y equation:**

$$y_A = \frac{\alpha \cdot x_A}{1 + (\alpha - 1) x_A} = \frac{2.5 \times 0.4}{1 + 1.5 \times 0.4} = \frac{1.0}{1.6} = 0.625$$

(Slight discrepancy due to using 0.4 instead of 0.389. Both are acceptable to ~0.625–0.632.)

**Step 5 — Conclude with units and interpretation:**

- Vapor phase: **63.2 mol% benzene** — richer in the more volatile component (benzene).
- This is consistent with physical intuition: vapor is enriched in the lighter component.

## 3.5 Effect of Pressure on T-x-y Diagram (Qualitative Analysis)

| Pressure Change | Effect on Diagram |
|-----------------|-------------------|
| **Increase P** | All bubble/dew points shift to **higher T**; relative volatility **decreases** (curves get closer) → separation becomes **harder**. |
| **Decrease P** | All temperatures shift **lower**; relative volatility **increases** → separation becomes **easier**, but requires **vacuum** equipment. |
| **Vacuum distillation** | Used for heat-sensitive materials (e.g., vitamins, edible oils). |

## 3.6 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Concept | Ideal System | Non-Ideal (γ > 1) | Non-Ideal (γ < 1) |
|---------|--------------|-------------------|-------------------|
| Raoult's Law | Holds exactly | Replaced by Modified R.L. | Replaced by Modified R.L. |
| Azeotrope | None | Minimum boiling | Maximum boiling |
| T-x-y Gap | Moderate | Widened | Widened |
| x-y Curve | Above 45° line, monotonic | Crosses 45° at azeotrope | Crosses 45° at azeotrope |
| Relative Volatility | > 1, constant with x | < 1 near azeotrope, varies | > 1 near azeotrope, varies |
| Example | Benzene-Toluene | Ethanol-Water | Acetone-Chloroform |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: T-x-y Diagram (Ideal Binary)

The following Mermaid block renders a **detailed T-x-y phase diagram** with all key regions labeled using safe node identifiers (alphanumeric only, no markdown inside labels).

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1["SUPERHEATED VAPOR REGION: T greater than T_dew"] --> B1
    B1["DEW POINT CURVE SVL: y_A as function of T"] --> C1
    C1["TWO PHASE VLE REGION: liquid x_A and vapor y_A coexist at T"] --> D1
    D1["BUBBLE POINT CURVE SLL: x_A as function of T"] --> E1
    E1["SUBCOOLED LIQUID REGION: T less than T_bub"]
    C1 --> F1["ISOTHERMAL TIE LINE: horizontal line at T_eq"]
    F1 --> D1
    B1 --> G1["Y AXIS: TEMPERATURE T in C"]
    D1 --> H1["X AXIS: MOLE FRACTION x_A and y_A of more volatile component A"]
    G1 --> I1["TOP LEFT CORNER: pure B bubble point T_B_boil"]
    I1 --> J1["BOTTOM RIGHT CORNER: pure A dew point T_A_boil"]
    H1 --> J1
    E1 --> K1["NOTE: T_A_boil less than T_B_boil since A is more volatile"]
    K1 --> G1
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: x-y Equilibrium Curve (with Azeotrope)

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1["X AXIS: x_A liquid mole fraction from 0 to 1"] --> P2
    P2["Y AXIS: y_A vapor mole fraction from 0 to 1"] --> P3
    P3["45 DEGREE REFERENCE LINE: y equals x"] --> P4
    P4["IDEAL EQUILIBRIUM CURVE: lies above 45 deg line y greater than x"] --> P5
    P5["AZEOTROPIC POINT: x_az equals y_az curve touches 45 deg line"] --> P6
    P6["AFTER AZEOTROPE: curve drops below 45 deg line separation reverses"] --> P7
    P7["POINT ZERO ZERO: x=0 y=0 pure B"] --> P8
    P8["POINT ONE ONE: x=1 y=1 pure A"]
    P5 --> P9["REGION ABOVE CURVE: mass transfer from liquid to vapor"]
    P5 --> P10["REGION BELOW CURVE: mass transfer from vapor to liquid"]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Procedure for Constructing T-x-y Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1["START: Select constant total pressure P"] --> S2
    S2["STEP 1: Identify pure component boiling points T_A_boil and T_B_boil"] --> S3
    S3["STEP 2: Choose a series of temperatures T_i between T_A_boil and T_B_boil"] --> S4
    S4["STEP 3: At each T_i lookup saturation pressures P_A_sat and P_B_sat"] --> S5
    S5["STEP 4: Compute bubble point x_A using x_A = P minus P_B_sat divided by P_A_sat minus P_B_sat"] --> S6
    S6["STEP 5: Compute dew point y_A using y_A = P_A_sat divided by P times x_A"] --> S7
    S7["STEP 6: Plot T vs x_A lower SLL and T vs y_A upper SVL on same graph"] --> S8
    S8["STEP 7: Connect points with smooth curves to get T-x-y diagram"]
    S8 --> S9["OPTIONAL: Transfer pairs to x-y plot to obtain equilibrium curve"]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Classification of VLE Behavior

```mermaid
graph TD
    N1["VAPOR LIQUID EQUILIBRIUM VLE"] --> N2
    N2["IDEAL gamma equals 1 Raoult Law applicable"] --> N3["EXAMPLE Benzene Toluene no azeotrope"]
    N2 --> N4["NON IDEAL gamma not equal to 1"]
    N4 --> N5["POSITIVE DEVIATION gamma greater than 1"]
    N5 --> N6["MINIMUM BOILING AZEOTROPE Example Ethanol Water"]
    N4 --> N7["NEGATIVE DEVIATION gamma less than 1"]
    N7 --> N8["MAXIMUM BOILING AZEOTROPE Example Acetone Chloroform"]
    N4 --> N9["PARTIAL IMMISCIBILITY two liquid phases Example Water Butanol"]
```
