<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the Bubble Point Curve and Dew Point Curve. How are they related to the T-x-y diagram?
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Bubble Point Curve (Saturation Liquid Line, SLL)** is the locus of temperatures at which a liquid mixture of given composition x_A first begins to boil (i.e., the first bubble of vapor appears) at a given total pressure. It is plotted as T vs. x_A.

The **Dew Point Curve (Saturation Vapor Line, SVL)** is the locus of temperatures at which a saturated vapor of given composition y_A first begins to condense (i.e., the first drop of liquid appears) at the same total pressure. It is plotted as T vs. y_A.

In a **T-x-y diagram** at constant pressure, the bubble point curve forms the **lower boundary** and the dew point curve forms the **upper boundary** of the two-phase (VLE) region. At any temperature between them, the system exists as two coexisting phases (liquid of composition x_A, vapor of composition y_A) connected by an isothermal tie line.

> **Marking Scheme:** 1 mark for SLL definition, 1 mark for SVL definition, 1 mark for relationship to T-x-y diagram.

### Question 2: State Raoult's Law and Dalton's Law. Show how they combine to give the vapor-phase composition in VLE.
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Raoult's Law** states that the partial pressure of a component *i* in an ideal liquid mixture is equal to its mole fraction in the liquid multiplied by its pure-component saturation pressure at that temperature:

$$P_i = x_i \cdot P_i^{sat}(T)$$

**Dalton's Law** states that the total pressure of a gas/vapor mixture equals the sum of the partial pressures of the individual components:

$$P = \sum_{i=1}^{C} P_i$$

**Combining both laws** for the vapor phase:

$$y_i = \frac{P_i}{P} = \frac{x_i \cdot P_i^{sat}(T)}{P}$$

For a binary mixture of A and B:

$$y_A = \frac{x_A P_A^{sat}}{x_A P_A^{sat} + (1 - x_A) P_B^{sat}}$$

> **Marking Scheme:** 1 mark each for the two laws, 1 mark for the combined expression.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a)** For a binary mixture of methanol (A) and water (B), the vapor pressures at 65 °C are: $P_A^{sat} = 100$ kPa and $P_B^{sat} = 25$ kPa. The total pressure is **101.3 kPa**. Compute:
1. The relative volatility α_AB.
2. The bubble point temperature if the liquid composition is $x_A = 0.3$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the construction of the T-x-y diagram and the x-y equilibrium curve for an ideal binary mixture. Sketch both diagrams and clearly label the bubble point curve, dew point curve, the 45° reference line, and the two-phase region. State the lever rule and its significance. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question A(a)

**Given:** $P_A^{sat} = 100$ kPa, $P_B^{sat} = 25$ kPa, P = 101.3 kPa, $x_A = 0.3$

**(1) Relative volatility:**

$$\alpha_{AB} = \frac{P_A^{sat}}{P_B^{sat}} = \frac{100}{25} = 4.0$$

(1 mark for formula, 1 mark for answer)

**(2) Bubble point at $x_A = 0.3$:**

We need to find T such that Raoult's Law gives P = 101.3 kPa:

$$P = x_A P_A^{sat}(T) + (1 - x_A) P_B^{sat}(T) = 0.3 P_A^{sat}(T) + 0.7 P_B^{sat}(T)$$

**Trial-and-error procedure:**

**Trial 1 (T = 65 °C):**
$$P = 0.3 \times 100 + 0.7 \times 25 = 30 + 17.5 = 47.5 \text{ kPa}$$
Too low → T must be **lower** (so that P^sat values increase).

**Trial 2 (T = 75 °C):** Assume $P_A^{sat} \approx 150$ kPa, $P_B^{sat} \approx 38$ kPa (from Antoine or steam tables):
$$P = 0.3 \times 150 + 0.7 \times 38 = 45 + 26.6 = 71.6 \text{ kPa}$$
Still low.

**Trial 3 (T = 85 °C):** Assume $P_A^{sat} \approx 225$ kPa, $P_B^{sat} \approx 58$ kPa:
$$P = 0.3 \times 225 + 0.7 \times 58 = 67.5 + 40.6 = 108.1 \text{ kPa}$$
Slightly **higher** than 101.3 → T is between 80 and 85 °C.

**Trial 4 (T = 82 °C):** Assume $P_A^{sat} \approx 200$ kPa, $P_B^{sat} \approx 51$ kPa:
$$P = 0.3 \times 200 + 0.7 \times 51 = 60 + 35.7 = 95.7 \text{ kPa}$$
Slightly low.

**Trial 5 (T = 83.5 °C):** Interpolating:
$$P \approx 102.0 \text{ kPa} \approx 101.3 \text{ kPa} \checkmark$$

**Therefore, the bubble point temperature ≈ 83.5 °C.** (5 marks for iterative procedure, final answer, and units)

---

#### Solution to Question A(b)

**Construction of T-x-y diagram (ideal binary):**

1. Choose constant total pressure P (typically 1 atm).
2. Pure A boils at T_A (lower), pure B boils at T_B (higher). Mark these two points on the T-axis.
3. At a series of temperatures T_i between T_A and T_B, obtain $P_A^{sat}(T_i)$ and $P_B^{sat}(T_i)$.
4. Compute $x_A$ (bubble) and $y_A$ (dew) using the formulas derived in Section 3.1.
5. Plot T vs. x_A (lower curve, SLL) and T vs. y_A (upper curve, SVL).

**Construction of x-y equilibrium curve:**

1. From the T-x-y diagram, draw horizontal isothermal tie lines between SLL and SVL.
2. Read off the (x_A, y_A) pair at each temperature.
3. Plot y_A vs. x_A on a square diagram.
4. The resulting curve lies above the y = x line (since A is more volatile).

**Lever Rule:** For a two-phase mixture with overall composition z, with liquid phase composition x and vapor phase composition y, the relative amounts of liquid (L) and vapor (V) are:

$$\frac{L}{V} = \frac{y - z}{z - x}$$

**Significance:** Allows material balance across the tie line, used to determine phase fractions in flash calculations and in constructing McCabe-Thiele diagrams.

> **Marking Scheme:** 4 marks for T-x-y construction, 2 marks for x-y construction, 1 mark for lever rule.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the three types of VLE behavior — **ideal, positive deviation, and negative deviation** — with one example for each. Use neat diagrams of x-y curves to show how azeotropes appear in non-ideal systems. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A binary mixture of n-hexane (A) and n-octane (B) is to be distilled at 1 atm. At a particular temperature, the saturation pressures are $P_A^{sat} = 800$ mmHg and $P_B^{sat} = 200$ mmHg. If the liquid composition is $x_A = 0.6$:
1. Compute the equilibrium vapor composition y_A.
2. Compute the relative volatility α_AB.
3. Verify whether separation by distillation is feasible. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question B(a)

**Ideal VLE:**
- Raoult's Law holds: $P_i = x_i P_i^{sat}$.
- Activity coefficient γ = 1.
- **No azeotrope** — the x-y curve lies entirely above the 45° line and never touches it.
- Example: **Benzene-Toluene**.

**Positive Deviation:**
- Activity coefficient γ > 1 (molecules of A and B repel each other in solution).
- Raoult's Law is replaced by **Modified Raoult's Law**: $y_i P = x_i \gamma_i P_i^{sat}$.
- Forms a **minimum-boiling azeotrope** — the x-y curve crosses the 45° line at the azeotropic composition.
- Example: **Ethanol-Water** (azeotrope at ~95.6 wt% ethanol, T_az = 78.2 °C).

**Negative Deviation:**
- Activity coefficient γ < 1 (molecules of A and B attract each other in solution).
- Forms a **maximum-boiling azeotrope** — the x-y curve also crosses the 45° line.
- Example: **Acetone-Chloroform** (azeotrope at ~64.7 °C).

**Sketch of x-y curves:**

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q1["IDEAL CURVE: smooth above 45 deg line, no crossing"] --> Q2
    Q2["POSITIVE DEVIATION: curve crosses 45 deg at minimum boiling azeotrope x_az"] --> Q3
    Q3["NEGATIVE DEVIATION: curve crosses 45 deg at maximum boiling azeotrope x_az"]
    Q1 --> Q4["X AXIS x_A from 0 to 1"]
    Q2 --> Q4
    Q3 --> Q4
    Q1 --> Q5["Y AXIS y_A from 0 to 1"]
    Q2 --> Q5
    Q3 --> Q5
```

> **Marking Scheme:** 2 marks for ideal, 2.5 marks for positive deviation with example, 2.5 marks for negative deviation with example.

---

#### Solution to Question B(b)

**Given:** $P_A^{sat} = 800$ mmHg, $P_B^{sat} = 200$ mmHg, P = 760 mmHg, $x_A = 0.6$

**(1) Equilibrium vapor composition y_A:**

$$y_A = \frac{x_A P_A^{sat}}{x_A P_A^{sat} + (1 - x_A) P_B^{sat}}$$

**Step 1 — Numerator:**
$$x_A P_A^{sat} = 0.6 \times 800 = 480 \text{ mmHg}$$

**Step 2 — Denominator (total pressure check):**
$$P = 0.6 \times 800 + 0.4 \times 200 = 480 + 80 = 560 \text{ mmHg}$$

**Step 3 — Check total pressure consistency:**
Since P = 560 mmHg ≠ 760 mmHg, the given saturation pressures are at a temperature **different from the bubble point** of the mixture. The mixture would boil at a different T. For this problem, we treat it as a **flash calculation** and assume the given $P_A^{sat}$ and $P_B^{sat}$ apply.

Using the given total pressure P = 760 mmHg:
$$y_A = \frac{480}{760} = 0.632$$

So $y_A \approx 0.632$ (i.e., **63.2 mol%** n-hexane in vapor). (2 marks)

**(2) Relative volatility:**

$$\alpha_{AB} = \frac{P_A^{sat}}{P_B^{sat}} = \frac{800}{200} = 4.0$$

(2 marks)

**(3) Feasibility of distillation:**

For separation by distillation to be feasible:
- α must be **significantly greater than 1** (typically α > 1.1).
- The x-y curve must lie **above the 45° line** in the composition range of interest.

Here, α = 4.0, which is **well above 1**, and y_A = 0.632 > x_A = 0.6, confirming the vapor is richer in A. Therefore, **distillation is highly feasible** for this system. (3 marks)

**Conclusion:** Yes, n-hexane can be easily separated from n-octane by distillation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (as identified by KTU Board Examiners):**
> 1. **Forgetting units:** When using the Antoine equation, students often write T in °C and substitute into a formula expecting K (or vice versa). Always check.
> 2. **Confusing bubble and dew point:** The bubble point is when liquid **boils** (find x at given T); the dew point is when vapor **condenses** (find y at given T). Mixing these up costs 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Assuming ideal for non-ideal systems:** Ethanol-water and acetone-chloroform are **non-ideal** — applying Raoult's Law gives large errors. Use Modified Raoult's Law with γ.
> 4. **Not identifying azeotropes:** If the x-y curve crosses the 45° line, students often miss that the system has an azeotrope and that simple distillation cannot break it.
> 5. **Incorrect relative volatility formula:** The correct formula is $\alpha_{AB} = (y_A/x_A)/(y_B/x_B)$. Some students write the inverse, leading to errors in Fenske equation (Module 2).
> 6. **Not labeling T-x-y diagrams:** KTU examiners specifically deduct marks for unlabeled axes, curves, and regions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Boiling Point Diagram (T-x-y)** is drawn at **constant pressure** with T on the y-axis and composition on the x-axis.
- ✅ The **lower curve is the SLL (bubble point)**, the **upper curve is the SVL (dew point)**.
- ✅ **x-y equilibrium curve** is obtained by transferring isothermal pairs from the T-x-y diagram.
- ✅ **Raoult's Law**: $P_i = x_i P_i^{sat}$ — valid for **ideal** solutions.
- ✅ **Modified Raoult's Law**: $y_i P = x_i \gamma_i P_i^{sat}$ — valid for **non-ideal** solutions.
- ✅ **Henry's Law**: $P_i = x_i H_i$ — valid for **dilute solutes**.
- ✅ **Vapor composition**: $y_i = x_i P_i^{sat}/P$ (ideal, binary).
- ✅ **Bubble point**: $x_A = (P - P_B^{sat})/(P_A^{sat} - P_B^{sat})$.
- ✅ **Dew point**: $y_A = (P_A^{sat}/P) \cdot x_A$.
- ✅ **Relative volatility**: $\alpha_{AB} = P_A^{sat}/P_B^{sat}$ (ideal). High α = easy separation.
- ✅ **Azeotrope**: condition $x = y$ → $\alpha = 1$ → **separation impossible by simple distillation**.
- ✅ **Positive deviation** (γ > 1) → **minimum boiling azeotrope** (e.g., ethanol-water).
- ✅ **Negative deviation** (γ < 1) → **maximum boiling azeotrope** (e.g., acetone-chloroform).
- ✅ **Ideal systems** (e.g., benzene-toluene) have **no azeotrope** and a smooth x-y curve.
- ✅ **Lever Rule**: $L/V = (y - z)/(z - x)$ — determines phase fractions.
- ✅ **Increasing pressure** shifts T-x-y to higher T and **decreases α** (separation harder).
- ✅ **Antoine Equation**: $\log_{10} P^{sat} = A - B/(T + C)$ — used to get $P^{sat}$ as a function of T.
- ✅ For KTU numericals, **always show the iterative procedure** (trial-and-error on T) for bubble/dew point problems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relative Volatility
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCHT501 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS II > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Relative Volatility ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Relative Volatility ($\alpha$)** is defined as the ratio of the vapor-liquid equilibrium constant ($K$-value) of the more volatile component to that of the less volatile component in a binary mixture. It quantitatively expresses the **ease of separation** of two components by distillation. For a binary mixture of components A (more volatile) and B (less volatile): $$\alpha_{AB} = \frac{K_A}{K_B} = \frac{y_A / x_A}{y_B / x_B}$$ Where: - $y_A, y_B$ = mole fractions of A and B in the vapor phase - $x_A, x_B$ = mole fractions of A and B in the liquid phase - $K_A, K_B$ = vapor-liquid equilibrium ratios ($K_i = y_i / x_i$) For **ideal solutions** (obeying Raoult's Law), relative volatility reduces to the ratio of pure component vapor pressures: $$\alpha_{AB} = \frac{P_A^{sat}}{P_B^{sat}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board-Exam Definition (Verbatim Style):** > "Relative volatility is the ratio of the equilibrium vapor composition to the equilibrium liquid composition ratio of component A to that of component B. It is a direct measure of the separability of components by distillation." ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Race Track" Intuition Imagine two runners on a racetrack, where the **finish line represents the vapor phase** and the **starting blocks represent the liquid phase**: - **Runner A** (the more volatile component, e.g., acetone) is a **sprinter** — he easily jumps from the liquid (starting block) to the vapor (finish line). His $K_A$ is large. - **Runner B** (the less volatile component, e.g., water) is **slower** — he barely makes it to the vapor. His $K_B$ is small. - **Relative Volatility ($\alpha$)** is simply the **speed ratio** between Runner A and Runner B. If $\alpha$ is very large (e.g., 10), Runner A is **10 times faster** → easy separation, few distillation trays needed. If $\alpha$ is barely above 1 (e.g., 1.05), both runners are nearly equally fast → very difficult separation, requiring many trays and high reflux. If $\alpha = 1$, both runners are **identical in speed** → impossible separation (azeotrope). ### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Key Numerical Boundaries | $\alpha$ Value | Interpretation | Distillation Difficulty | |:---:|:---|:---| | $\alpha = 1$ | **Azeotrope** — no separation by simple distillation | Impossible | | $1 < \alpha < 1.05$ | Very close-boiling mixture | Extremely difficult (extractive/special distillation) | | $1.05 < \alpha < 1.5$ | Difficult separation | Many theoretical stages required | | $1.5 < \alpha < 5$ | Moderate separation | Practical with conventional columns | | $\alpha > 5$ | Easy separation | Few trays needed | > [!NOTE] > **Physical Constants & Units:** Relative volatility is **dimensionless** (a ratio of like quantities). It has **no units**. Typical distillation design values range from **1.01 to 100+** depending on the system. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The "Why" Behind the Equation The relative volatility emerges naturally from the **vapor-liquid equilibrium (VLE)** framework. Let us systematically build the concept: **Step 1 — Component Material Balance (VLE Basis):** At equilibrium, the partial pressure of component $i$ in the vapor equals the partial pressure exerted by the liquid (modified Raoult's Law): $$p_i = y_i P = \gamma_i x_i P_i^{sat}$$ For ideal solutions, $\gamma_i = 1$, giving $K_i = P_i^{sat} / P$. **Step 2 — Constructing the Ratio:** Dividing $K_A$ by $K_B$ eliminates the **total pressure $P$** (a very useful simplification!): $$\alpha_{AB} = \frac{K_A}{K_B} = \frac{P_A^{sat} / P}{P_B^{sat} / P} = \frac{P_A^{sat}}{P_B^{sat}}$$ **Step 3 — Linking to Composition (The Working Equation):** Since $y_i = K_i x_i$ at equilibrium: $$\alpha_{AB} = \frac{y_A x_B}{x_A y_B}$$ **Step 4 — Expressing y in terms of x (The Distiller's Master Relation):** For a binary system ($x_B = 1 - x_A$ and $y_B = 1 - y_A$): $$y_A = \frac{\alpha \, x_A}{1 + (\alpha - 1) x_A}$$ This single equation is the **backbone of the McCabe-Thiele method** — you will use it repeatedly in Module 2. **Step 5 — Distillation Feasibility Criterion:** A mixture can be separated by simple distillation **if and only if** $\alpha \neq 1$. When $\alpha = 1$, the equilibrium curve coincides with the 45° line on the xy-diagram, meaning no enrichment occurs. ### 2.2 Temperature Dependence of Relative Volatility Vapor pressure follows the **Clausius-Clapeyron equation**: $$\ln P_i^{sat} = A_i - \frac{B_i}{T}$$ Therefore, $\ln \alpha = \ln P_A^{sat} - \ln P_B^{sat} = (A_A - A_B) - \frac{(B_A - B_B)}{T}$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Rule:** As temperature (and hence pressure) **increases**, the relative volatility **decreases**. This is why **vacuum distillation** is used for heat-sensitive materials — lower pressure → lower temperature → **higher $\alpha$** → easier separation. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description / Use | |:---:|:---|:---| | 1 | $\alpha_{AB} = \dfrac{K_A}{K_B} = \dfrac{y_A x_B}{x_A y_B}$ | General definition (all systems) | | 2 | $\alpha_{AB} = \dfrac{P_A^{sat}}{P_B^{sat}}$ | Ideal solutions (Raoult's Law) | | 3 | $\alpha_{AB} = \dfrac{\gamma_A P_A^{sat}}{\gamma_B P_B^{sat}}$ | Non-ideal solutions (modified Raoult's) | | 4 | $y_A = \dfrac{\alpha \, x_A}{1 + (\alpha - 1) x_A}$ | Equilibrium curve (binary, constant $\alpha$) | | 5 | $x_A = \dfrac{y_A}{\alpha - (\alpha - 1) y_A}$ | Inverse of #4 | | 6 | $\ln \alpha = A - \dfrac{B}{T}$ | Temperature dependence (linearized) | | 7 | $\alpha_{AB} \cdot \alpha_{BC} = \alpha_{AC}$ | For multi-component systems | | 8 | $\alpha = 1 \Rightarrow$ **Azeotrope** | Impossibility criterion | > [!NOTE] > In Formula 1 above, the absolute value notation has been replaced with explicit $x_B$ and $y_B$ to avoid any markdown conflicts. In Formula 3, the ratio format $\dfrac{\gamma_A P_A^{sat}}{\gamma_B P_B^{sat}}$ is the activity-coefficient form. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Relative volatility is the **single most important design parameter** in distillation. It directly determines: 1. **Minimum number of theoretical stages** (via the Fenske equation — covered later in this module) 2. **Minimum reflux ratio** (via the Underwood equation) 3. **Total number of actual stages** (via Gilliland correlation) 4. **Column operating pressure** selection 5. **Choice of separation technique** (e.g., extractive distillation when $\alpha < 1.05$) Industrial examples include separation of **benzene-toluene** ($\alpha \approx 2.5$), **ethanol-water** (forms azeotrope at $\alpha = 1$ at x ≈ 0.894), and **propane-butane** ($\alpha \approx 2$ at typical column conditions). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Equilibrium Curve Equation $y = f(x, \alpha)$ **Given:** Binary system, components A and B, with $x_A + x_B = 1$ and $y_A + y_B = 1$. **Step 1:** Start from the fundamental definition: $$\alpha = \frac{y_A / x_A}{y_B / x_B}$$ **Step 2:** Cross-multiply: $$\alpha \, x_A \, y_B = y_A \, x_B$$ **Step 3:** Substitute $y_B = 1 - y_A$ and $x_B = 1 - x_A$: $$\alpha \, x_A \, (1 - y_A) = y_A \, (1 - x_A)$$ **Step 4:** Expand both sides: $$\alpha \, x_A - \alpha \, x_A \, y_A = y_A - x_A \, y_A$$ **Step 5:** Collect all $y_A$ terms on the right-hand side: $$\alpha \, x_A = y_A - x_A \, y_A + \alpha \, x_A \, y_A$$ **Step 6:** Factor $y_A$ on the right: $$\alpha \, x_A = y_A \left[ 1 - x_A + \alpha \, x_A \right] = y_A \left[ 1 + (\alpha - 1) x_A \right]$$ **Step 7:** Solve for $y_A$: $$\boxed{y_A = \frac{\alpha \, x_A}{1 + (\alpha - 1) x_A}}$$ **Step 8 — Boundary Check (KTU Favourite Verification):** - At $x_A = 0$ (pure B): $y_A = 0 / 1 = 0$ ✓ - At $x_A = 1$ (pure A): $y_A = \alpha / \alpha = 1$ ✓ - At $x_A = 0.5$ (equimolar): $y_A = 0.5\alpha / (1 + 0.5\alpha - 0.5) = 0.5\alpha / (0.5 + 0.5\alpha) = \alpha / (1 + \alpha)$ ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Relationship Between $\alpha$ and $K$-Values from Raoult's Law **Given:** Ideal solution obeying Raoult's Law: $p_i = x_i P_i^{sat}$, and Dalton's Law: $p_i = y_i P$. **Step 1:** Equate partial pressures: $$y_i P = x_i P_i^{sat}$$ **Step 2:** Define $K_i$: $$K_i = \frac{y_i}{x_i} = \frac{P_i^{sat}}{P}$$ **Step 3:** Form the ratio: $$\alpha_{AB} = \frac{K_A}{K_B} = \frac{P_A^{sat} / P}{P_B^{sat} / P} = \frac{P_A^{sat}}{P_B^{sat}}$$ > **Comment:** The **total pressure $P$ cancels out** — a powerful result indicating that for ideal binary systems, $\alpha$ is **independent of total pressure** (though it still depends on temperature through the vapor pressures). ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of $\alpha$ on the Equilibrium Curve The following table summarizes how the $y$-$x$ equilibrium curve shape depends on $\alpha$. This is **high-yield** for KTU board exams and viva. | $\alpha$ | Curve Shape | $y$ at $x = 0.5$ | Practical Implication | |:---:|:---|:---:|:---| | $1.0$ | **45° straight line** (coincides with diagonal) | $0.500$ | Azeotrope — no separation | | $1.2$ | Slight bow above diagonal | $0.545$ | Very difficult separation | | $2.0$ | Moderate bow | $0.667$ | Practical, ~10–15 stages | | $5.0$ | Sharp bow near top-right corner | $0.833$ | Easy, ~5 stages | | $10.0$ | Steep rise, almost rectangular | $0.909$ | Very easy, ~3 stages | | $\infty$ | Step function (pure A in vapor for any $x > 0$) | $1.000$ | Ideal — single equilibrium stage | ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Problem:** A benzene-toluene mixture at 1 atm has a liquid composition of $x_{benzene} = 0.40$. Given vapor pressures at 95°C are: $P_{benzene}^{sat} = 1200$ mmHg, $P_{toluene}^{sat} = 480$ mmHg. Calculate (a) relative volatility, (b) equilibrium vapor composition. **Solution:** **(a) Relative Volatility:** $$\alpha = \frac{P_{benzene}^{sat}}{P_{toluene}^{sat}} = \frac{1200}{480} = 2.50$$ **(b) Equilibrium Vapor Composition:** $$y_{benzene} = \frac{\alpha \, x_A}{1 + (\alpha - 1) x_A} = \frac{2.50 \times 0.40}{1 + (2.50 - 1) \times 0.40}$$ $$y_{benzene} = \frac{1.000}{1 + 0.600} = \frac{1.000}{1.600} = 0.625$$ **Verification:** Since benzene is the more volatile component, $y > x$ ✓ (0.625 > 0.40). ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Conceptual Flow — From Molecular Behavior to Design Equation The following block-level architecture illustrates how relative volatility emerges from molecular-scale physics and feeds into column-scale design. ```mermaid flowchart TD A0[Molecular Level
Intermolecular Forces] --> A1[Pure Component
Vapor Pressure P_sat] A1 --> A2[Temperature
via Clausius-Clapeyron] A2 --> A3[Relative Volatility
alpha = PA_sat / PB_sat] A3 --> A4[Equilibrium Curve
y = alpha.x / 1 + alpha-1.x] A4 --> A5[McCabe-Thiele
Stepping Method] A4 --> A6[Fenske Equation
Min Stages] A4 --> A7[Underwood Equation
Min Reflux] A5 --> A8[Column Design
Number of Trays] A6 --> A8 A7 --> A8 A8 --> A9[Industrial Distillation
Column Operation] ``` ### 4.2 Distillation Feasibility Decision Tree This diagram captures the KTU decision logic: given $\alpha$, what is the separation strategy? ```mermaid flowchart TD B1[Start: Compute alpha from VLE data] --> B2{Is alpha = 1 ?} B2 -- Yes --> B3[Azeotrope Formed
No simple distillation possible] B3 --> B31[Consider:
Pressure Swing Distillation] B3 --> B32[Consider:
Extractive Distillation] B3 --> B33[Consider:
Azeotropic Distillation] B2 -- No --> B4{Is alpha greater than 1.05 ?} B4 -- No --> B5[Very Close Boiling
alpha less than 1.05] B5 --> B51[Extractive Distillation
with entrainer] B4 -- Yes --> B6{Is alpha between 1.05 and 5 ?} B6 -- Yes --> B7[Conventional Distillation
Use McCabe-Thiele] B6 -- No --> B8[Easy Separation
Few trays required] B7 --> B71[Apply Fenske + Underwood
+ Gilliland] B8 --> B81[Quick design
2 to 5 theoretical stages] ``` ### 4.3 Effect of $\alpha$ on the xy Equilibrium Curve (Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart LR C0[xy Diagram Origin
0,0] --> C1[Diagonal Line
y = x] C1 --> C2[Curve alpha = 1.0
Coincides with diagonal] C1 --> C3[Curve alpha = 1.5
Slight bow above] C1 --> C4[Curve alpha = 3.0
Moderate bow] C1 --> C5[Curve alpha = 10
Sharp step near top] C3 --> C31[Larger area between curve
and diagonal equals easier separation] C4 --> C41[Standard McCabe-Thiele
plot region] C5 --> C51[Sharp separation
rectangular hyperbolic shape] ``` ### 4.4 Application Hierarchy of Relative Volatility in Distillation Design ```mermaid flowchart TD D1[Relative Volatility alpha] --> D2[Equilibrium Data
xy Curve Generation] D1 --> D3[Minimum Stages
Fenske Equation] D1 --> D4[Minimum Reflux
Underwood Equation] D1 --> D5[Feed Stage Location
Kirkhoff Equation] D1 --> D6[Stage Efficiency
Murphree Correlation] D2 --> D7[McCabe-Thiele
Graphical Method] D3 --> D8[Actual Stages
via Gilliland] D4 --> D8 D7 --> D8 D8 --> D9[Final Column Design
Diameter, Height, Trays] D6 --> D9 ``` > [!NOTE] > The block diagrams above use alphanumeric node IDs prefixed with letters (A0, B1, C0, D1) to comply with Mermaid syntax safeguards. Reserved keywords such as `end`, `subgraph`, and `graph` are deliberately avoided as node identifiers. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]:** > Define relative volatility. What is its significance in distillation? **Model Answer (3 marks):** Relative volatility $\alpha_{AB}$ is defined as the ratio of the $K$-values (or vapor-liquid equilibrium ratios) of the more volatile component A to the less volatile component B: $$\alpha_{AB} = \frac{K_A}{K_B} = \frac{y_A \, x_B}{x_A \, y_B}$$ For ideal solutions, it equals the ratio of pure component vapor pressures: $\alpha_{AB} = P_A^{sat} / P_B^{sat}$. **Significance:** Relative volatility is a direct quantitative measure of the **ease of separation** by distillation. A value of $\alpha = 1$ indicates an azeotrope (impossible separation by simple distillation), $\alpha$ slightly greater than 1 indicates a difficult separation, and large $\alpha$ indicates easy separation requiring few stages. **(3 marks)** --- > **Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]:** > State any two methods to enhance the relative volatility of a difficult-to-separate mixture. **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Reduce operating pressure** — Lowering the column pressure decreases the operating temperature, which (by the Clausius-Clapeyron relation) **increases** the relative volatility. This is the principle behind vacuum distillation of heat-sensitive mixtures. 2. **Add an entrainer (extractive distillation)** — A third, less volatile component is added that selectively interacts with one of the original components, altering the activity coefficients and effectively increasing the relative volatility of the key components. **(3 marks)** ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) > **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]:** **(a)** Derive the relation $y_A = \dfrac{\alpha \, x_A}{1 + (\alpha - 1) x_A}$ starting from the definition of relative volatility for a binary mixture. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The vapor pressures of benzene and toluene at 100°C are 1350 mmHg and 560 mmHg respectively. For a liquid mixture containing 45 mol% benzene at 1 atm total pressure, calculate the relative volatility and the equilibrium vapor composition. State whether the separation is feasible by simple distillation. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution to Question A:** **Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):** Starting from the definition: $$\alpha = \frac{y_A / x_A}{y_B / x_B} = \frac{y_A \, x_B}{x_A \, y_B}$$ For a binary system, $x_B = 1 - x_A$ and $y_B = 1 - y_A$: $$\alpha = \frac{y_A (1 - x_A)}{x_A (1 - y_A)}$$ Cross-multiplying: $$\alpha \, x_A (1 - y_A) = y_A (1 - x_A)$$ $$\alpha \, x_A - \alpha \, x_A \, y_A = y_A - x_A \, y_A$$ Rearranging (collecting $y_A$ terms on the right): $$\alpha \, x_A = y_A - x_A \, y_A + \alpha \, x_A \, y_A = y_A \left[ 1 - x_A + \alpha \, x_A \right]$$ $$y_A = \frac{\alpha \, x_A}{1 + (\alpha - 1) x_A} \quad \blacksquare \quad \textbf{(7 marks)}$$ **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Step 1: Relative Volatility** $$\alpha = \frac{P_{benzene}^{sat}}{P_{toluene}^{sat}} = \frac{1350}{560} = 2.41$$ **Step 2: Equilibrium Vapor Composition** $$y_A = \frac{2.41 \times 0.45}{1 + (2.41 - 1) \times 0.45}$$ $$y_A = \frac{1.0845}{1 + 0.6345} = \frac{1.0845}{1.6345} = 0.6635$$ **Step 3: Feasibility** Since $\alpha = 2.41 \neq 1$ and $\alpha > 1.05$, the mixture **can be separated by simple distillation**. The moderate value ($\alpha \approx 2.4$) indicates a practical but not trivial separation requiring a reasonable number of theoretical stages. **(7 marks)** --- > **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice — KTU University Exam, Dec 2022, Modified]:** **(a)** Explain with a neat sketch how relative volatility affects the shape of the $y$-$x$ equilibrium curve in a binary distillation system. Comment on the limiting cases of $\alpha \rightarrow 1$ and $\alpha \rightarrow \infty$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For a non-ideal binary mixture, the activity coefficients are given by $\ln \gamma_A = 0.4 \, x_B^2$ and $\ln \gamma_B = 0.4 \, x_A^2$. The vapor pressures at the operating temperature are $P_A^{sat} = 800$ mmHg and $P_B^{sat} = 600$ mmHg. Calculate the relative volatility at $x_A = 0.3$. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution to Question B:** **Part (a) — Sketch & Explanation (7 marks):** [Student should draw a $y$-$x$ diagram with the 45° diagonal and several equilibrium curves for different $\alpha$ values] - The equilibrium curve is given by $y_A = \alpha x_A / [1 + (\alpha-1)x_A]$. - For $\alpha = 1$: The curve **coincides with the 45° line** (no separation possible — azeotrope). - As $\alpha$ increases above 1, the curve bows progressively upward, creating a larger area between itself and the diagonal. - For very large $\alpha$ (e.g., 10+): The curve becomes a **rectangular hyperbola** that approaches $y = 1$ sharply for small $x$. - **Limiting case $\alpha \rightarrow \infty$:** The curve becomes a step function — at any $x_A > 0$, the vapor is essentially pure A. A single equilibrium stage achieves complete separation. - **Limiting case $\alpha \rightarrow 1$:** The curve merges with the diagonal, meaning $y_A = x_A$ for all compositions — no enrichment occurs, separation is impossible. **(7 marks)** **Part (b) — Non-Ideal Relative Volatility (7 marks):** **Step 1: Calculate Activity Coefficients at $x_A = 0.3$** Given $x_A = 0.3$, therefore $x_B = 0.7$: $$\ln \gamma_A = 0.4 \times (0.7)^2 = 0.4 \times 0.49 = 0.196 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \gamma_A = e^{0.196} = 1.2165$$ $$\ln \gamma_B = 0.4 \times (0.3)^2 = 0.4 \times 0.09 = 0.036 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \gamma_B = e^{0.036} = 1.0367$$ **Step 2: Apply Modified Raoult's Law for Relative Volatility** For non-ideal systems: $$\alpha_{AB} = \frac{\gamma_A \, P_A^{sat}}{\gamma_B \, P_B^{sat}}$$ $$\alpha_{AB} = \frac{1.2165 \times 800}{1.0367 \times 600} = \frac{973.2}{622.0} = 1.565$$ **Step 3: Comment** Since $\alpha = 1.565 > 1.05$, the separation is feasible by conventional distillation, though moderate in difficulty. Note that the activity coefficients have **enhanced** the relative volatility compared to the ideal case ($\alpha_{ideal} = 800/600 = 1.333$). **(7 marks)** ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Relative Volatility Problems:** > > 1. **Forgetting to identify the more volatile component (MVC).** The numerator of $\alpha$ **must** always correspond to the MVC. Swapping A and B gives a value less than 1, which is mathematically valid but **conventionally rejected** in KTU answers. Always report $\alpha \geq 1$. > > 2. **Using total pressure in ideal solutions.** A frequent mistake is writing $\alpha = (P_A^{sat}/P)(P/P_B^{sat})$ — this is **circular**. For ideal solutions, $P$ **cancels** and $\alpha = P_A^{sat}/P_B^{sat}$ is independent of total pressure. > > 3. **Skipping the boundary check** ($x = 0 \Rightarrow y = 0$ and $x = 1 \Rightarrow y = 1$). Examiners award 1–2 marks for verifying limiting cases in derivations. > > 4. **Confusing relative volatility with selectivity** in membrane separations. They are analogous but **not identical** in derivation. > > 5. **Forgetting units.** While $\alpha$ itself is dimensionless, intermediate values like $P_A^{sat}$ and $P_B^{sat}$ **must be in the same units** (both mmHg or both kPa). Mixing units gives wrong answers and zero marks. > > 6. **Reporting $y_A < x_A$ in numerical problems.** This is a **red flag** indicating a sign or formula error. For the MVC, $y > x$ at equilibrium **always**. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Definition:** $\alpha_{AB} = K_A / K_B = (y_A x_B)/(x_A y_B)$ — always report for the MVC in the numerator. - **Ideal solutions:** $\alpha = P_A^{sat}/P_B^{sat}$ — independent of total pressure, depends only on temperature. - **Non-ideal solutions:** $\alpha = (\gamma_A P_A^{sat})/(\gamma_B P_B^{sat})$ — activity coefficients play a crucial role. - **Equilibrium relation:** $y_A = \alpha x_A / [1 + (\alpha - 1) x_A]$ — the McCabe-Thiele backbone. - **Temperature effect:** $\alpha$ **decreases** with increasing temperature; hence **vacuum distillation increases $\alpha$**. - **Azeotrope criterion:** $\alpha = 1$ → no separation by simple distillation. Special methods required. - **Multiplication rule:** $\alpha_{AB} \times \alpha_{BC} = \alpha_{AC}$ for ternary systems. - **Physical meaning:** Larger $\alpha$ → larger area between equilibrium curve and 45° line → fewer stages needed. - **KTU design value range:** Industrial distillation typically operates with $1.1 < \alpha < 20$. - **Dimensionless quantity:** No units, but vapor pressures used in calculation **must be in identical units**.

topics.title: Application of Raoult's Law. Distillation Methods - Flash Distillation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT501 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS II
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Application of Raoult's Law. Distillation Methods - Flash Distillation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Raoult's Law — The Foundation of Vapor–Liquid Equilibrium

> [!NOTE]
> **Raoult's Law (Formal Definition):** *"The partial pressure of a component in an ideal liquid solution is directly proportional to its mole fraction in the liquid phase, with the proportionality constant being the vapor pressure of the pure component at the same temperature."*

Mathematically, for a binary ideal mixture of components **A** (more volatile) and **B**:

$$P_A = x_A \, P_A^{sat} \quad ; \quad P_B = x_B \, P_B^{sat}$$

And the total system pressure (Dalton's Law of partial pressures):

$$P_{total} = P_A + P_B = x_A P_A^{sat} + x_B P_B^{sat}$$

where:
- $x_A, x_B$ = mole fractions in the **liquid** phase
- $P_A^{sat}, P_B^{sat}$ = **pure-component vapor pressures** (functions of temperature)
- $P_A, P_B$ = partial pressures in the vapor phase
- $P_{total}$ = total system pressure

The composition of the vapor in equilibrium with the liquid is given by:

$$y_A = \frac{P_A}{P_{total}} = \frac{x_A \, P_A^{sat}}{x_A P_A^{sat} + x_B P_B^{sat}}$$

### 1.2 The Three Key Engineering Applications of Raoult's Law

| # | Application | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **1** | **Bubble Point Calculation** | Find the temperature at which the first bubble of vapor forms when a liquid mixture is heated at a given pressure. |
| **2** | **Dew Point Calculation** | Find the temperature at which the first drop of liquid condenses when a vapor mixture is cooled at a given pressure. |
| **3** | **Flash (Equilibrium) Calculation** | Find the equilibrium compositions $x$ and $y$ when a feed is partially vaporized. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modified Raoult's Law (for Non-Ideal Solutions):** When solutions deviate from ideality, an **activity coefficient** $\gamma_i$ is introduced:
> $$P_i = \gamma_i \, x_i \, P_i^{sat}$$
> This is the basis of **Margules, Van Laar, and Wilson** equations for non-ideal VLE modeling (discussed in Module 2).

## 1.3 Distillation — Definition and Industrial Importance

> [!NOTE]
> **Distillation** is a **mass-transfer separation process** in which a liquid mixture is separated into components of differing volatilities by partial vaporization (and subsequent condensation). It exploits the difference in **boiling points** of the constituents.

### 1.4 Real-World Analogy — "Boiling the Chai Pan"

> [!TIP]
> **Imagine boiling a mixture of water and sugar syrup in a kettle (the "Chai Pan Analogy"):**
> 1. The water (more volatile / lower boiling point) escapes first as steam, leaving behind more concentrated sugar.
> 2. If you condense that steam on a cold lid, you recover **pure water** — this is **simple/equilibrium distillation in one stage**.
> 3. To make the separation *sharp* (i.e., a more concentrated distillate), you would need to repeat this many times — which is exactly what a **fractionating column** does in refineries.
> 4. The **flash drum** is like a sudden "throwing" of hot liquid into a low-pressure chamber — part flashes off as vapor, part drops as liquid. This is a **single-stage** separation.

## 1.5 Classification of Distillation Methods

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 1) classifies distillation as follows:

| # | Method | Feed State | Stages | Reflux |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | **Flash (Equilibrium) Distillation** | Partially vaporized | **Single-stage** | None |
| **2** | **Differential / Batch Distillation** | Liquid charged to a kettle | **Single-vessel, multi-stage in time** | None |
| **3** | **Rectification / Fractional Distillation** | Continuous feed | **Multi-stage (tray/packed column)** | Required |
| **4** | **Steam Distillation** | Liquid + direct steam contact | Single-stage (special) | — |
| **5** | **Azeotropic & Extractive Distillation** | Multi-component with entrainer/solvent | Multi-stage | — |

This module focuses on **(1) Flash Distillation** as the simplest equilibrium stage.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Raoult's Law — Thermodynamic Basis

Raoult's Law is a **limiting law** derived from the equality of fugacities of component $i$ in the liquid and vapor phases at equilibrium. For an **ideal solution**, the molecules of A and B are similar enough that:
- A–A, B–B, and A–B intermolecular forces are **comparable**.
- The **activity coefficient** $\gamma_i = 1$ for all components.
- No heat of mixing effect; volume of mixing is approximately zero.

The K-value (vapor–liquid equilibrium ratio) for an ideal system is:

$$K_i = \frac{y_i}{x_i} = \frac{P_i^{sat}}{P_{total}}$$

This $K$-value is the fundamental building block for **all** multi-stage distillation calculations (Module 3 onwards).

## 2.2 The Three Raoult's Law Problems

> [!NOTE]
> **Bubble-Point Problem (Given $x_i, P$ → Find $T$):**
> The temperature at which the **first infinitesimally small bubble** of vapor appears. Here $x_i$ is known, $T$ is unknown. The governing equation is:
> $$P = \sum_{i=1}^{c} x_i \, P_i^{sat}(T)$$
> This is solved **iteratively by trial** because $P_i^{sat}$ is a strong (exponential) function of $T$ (e.g., Antoine equation). Once $T$ is found, the vapor composition is calculated as $y_i = x_i P_i^{sat} / P$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Dew-Point Problem (Given $y_i, P$ → Find $T$):**
> The temperature at which the **first drop of liquid** condenses on cooling a vapor. $y_i$ is known. The governing equation is:
> $$P = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{c} \dfrac{y_i}{P_i^{sat}(T)}}$$
> After finding $T$, the liquid composition is $x_i = y_i P / P_i^{sat}(T)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Flash (Equilibrium) Problem (Given $T, P, z_i$ → Find $x_i, y_i$):**
> For a partially vaporized feed, the equilibrium compositions are calculated using the **flash equation**:
> $$\sum_{i=1}^{c} \frac{z_i \, (K_i - 1)}{1 + f \,(K_i - 1)} = 0$$
> where $f$ is the fraction of feed vaporized. For a **binary**, this reduces to a quadratic in $x_1$ (or $y_1$).

## 2.3 Flash Distillation — Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flash Distillation Definition:** A single-stage, continuous separation process in which a liquid feed is **partially vaporized** in a **flash drum** (or throttling valve + separator), and the resulting **vapor and liquid streams** are in **thermodynamic equilibrium** with each other before being separated.

### Working Principle (How it Works)
1. Feed (at high pressure) is **throttled** through a valve to a lower pressure.
2. The sudden pressure drop causes **partial vaporization** (latent heat comes from the sensible heat of the liquid).
3. The two-phase mixture enters a **flash drum** where vapor and liquid separate under gravity.
4. The **vapor** (rich in the more volatile component — MVK) exits the top; the **liquid** (rich in the less volatile component) exits the bottom.
5. Both streams leave at the **same temperature and pressure** (the flash drum's pressure) and are in **thermodynamic VLE**.

### Assumptions
- **Adiabatic** operation (no heat transfer with surroundings).
- **Constant relative volatility** $\alpha$ in some textbook problems.
- **Ideal behavior** (Raoult's Law / Dalton's Law applicable).
- **Negligible pressure drop** inside the drum.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Master these equations for any KTU 2024 mass transfer / distillation problem.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Key Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Raoult's Law | $P_i = x_i \, P_i^{sat}$ | $P_i$ in kPa or atm |
| 2 | Dalton's Law | $P_{total} = \sum y_i P_i = \sum x_i P_i^{sat}$ | $P_{total}$ in kPa |
| 3 | K-value (Ideal) | $K_i = P_i^{sat}/P_{total}$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Bubble Point | $P = \sum x_i P_i^{sat}(T)$ | solve iteratively for $T$ |
| 5 | Dew Point | $P = 1/\sum (y_i/P_i^{sat})$ | solve iteratively for $T$ |
| 6 | Relative Volatility | $\alpha_{AB} = (y_A/x_A) / (y_B/x_B) = K_A/K_B$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | VLE Relation (Binary, $\alpha$ const) | $y = \dfrac{\alpha x}{1 + (\alpha-1) x}$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | Total Mass Balance | $F = V + L$ | mol/h or kg/h |
| 9 | Component Mass Balance | $F z_F = V y + L x$ | mol/h |
| 10 | Fraction Vaporized | $f = V/F$ | dimensionless |
| 11 | Thermal Condition $q$ | $q = L/F = 1 - f$ | dimensionless |
| 12 | Flash Operating Line | $y = \dfrac{z_F - (1-f) x}{f}$ | dimensionless |
| 13 | q-line Equation | $y = \dfrac{z_F - q x}{1-q}$ | dimensionless |
| 14 | Flash Rachford–Rice | $\sum \dfrac{z_i(K_i - 1)}{1 + f(K_i - 1)} = 0$ | solve for $f$ or $x_i, y_i$ |
| 15 | Modified Raoult's Law | $P_i = \gamma_i x_i P_i^{sat}$ | $\gamma_i$ = activity coefficient |

> [!WARNING]
> **The pipe symbol `|` in tables breaks markdown. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead — e.g., write $x \mid_{z_F}$ as $x \vert_{z_F}$.**

## 2.5 Significance of the Thermal Condition $q$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The parameter $q$ is the **mole fraction of feed that remains as liquid** after flashing. It links feed enthalpy to the flash calculation.

| Feed Condition | Value of $q$ | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Subcooled liquid | $q > 1$ | Feed needs sensible heating to reach bubble point |
| Saturated liquid | $q = 1$ | Feed is at its bubble point; no vaporization on entry |
| Two-phase mixture | $0 < q < 1$ | Feed enters partially vaporized |
| Saturated vapor | $q = 0$ | Feed is at dew point; completely vaporizes on pressure drop |
| Superheated vapor | $q < 0$ | Feed must be condensed first before any liquid forms |

**KTU 2024 Hot Topic:** $q$ is rigorously defined by enthalpy balance:
$$q = \frac{h_V - h_F}{h_V - h_L} = 1 - f$$
where $h_F$ is feed enthalpy, $h_L$ is saturated liquid enthalpy, $h_V$ is saturated vapor enthalpy (all at drum pressure).

## 2.6 Engineering Utility

- **Petroleum refining:** Pre-fractionator overheads, crude unit atmospheric and vacuum flash drums.
- **Cryogenic air separation:** Primary flash separation of air components.
- **LNG plants:** Heavy hydrocarbon removal by cold flash.
- **Desalination:** Multi-stage flash (MSF) distillation for seawater.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Bubble Point from Raoult's Law

**Starting Point:** A liquid mixture of mole fractions $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_c$ is heated at constant total pressure $P$. We seek the temperature $T_b$ at which the **first bubble** of vapor is in equilibrium with the liquid.

**Step 1:** At the bubble point, the liquid composition equals the bulk liquid composition (infinitesimal vapor), and the sum of partial pressures equals total pressure:

$$P = P_1 + P_2 + \cdots + P_c$$

**Step 2:** Apply **Raoult's Law** to each component ($P_i = x_i P_i^{sat}$):

$$P = x_1 P_1^{sat}(T_b) + x_2 P_2^{sat}(T_b) + \cdots + x_c P_c^{sat}(T_b)$$

**Step 3:** Recognize that $P_i^{sat}(T)$ is given by the **Antoine equation** (or steam-table-like data):

$$\log_{10} P_i^{sat} = A_i - \frac{B_i}{T + C_i}$$

**Step 4:** This equation is **transcendental** in $T_b$ (cannot be solved analytically) — solved by **trial and error / Newton–Raphson** with vapor pressure data.

**Step 5:** Once $T_b$ is determined, the vapor composition is:

$$y_i = \frac{x_i \, P_i^{sat}(T_b)}{P}$$

## 3.2 Derivation — Dew Point from Raoult's Law

**Starting Point:** A vapor mixture of mole fractions $y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_c$ at pressure $P$ is cooled. We seek the temperature $T_d$ at which the **first drop of liquid** forms.

**Step 1:** At the dew point, $\sum x_i = 1$ (constraint on liquid). Using the ideal K-value relation $x_i = y_i / K_i = y_i P / P_i^{sat}$:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{c} x_i = \sum_{i=1}^{c} \frac{y_i \, P}{P_i^{sat}(T_d)} = 1$$

**Step 2:** Rearranging for the dew-point pressure condition:

$$P = \frac{1}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{c} \frac{y_i}{P_i^{sat}(T_d)}}$$

**Step 3:** Solve iteratively for $T_d$ using vapor-pressure data. After $T_d$ is found, the incipient liquid composition is:

$$x_i = \frac{y_i \, P}{P_i^{sat}(T_d)}$$

## 3.3 Derivation — Flash Distillation Operating Equation (Component Balance)

**Schematic representation of a flash drum:**

> Feed $F$ with composition $z_F$ enters → partial vaporization → Vapor $V$ with composition $y$ exits top; Liquid $L$ with composition $x$ exits bottom.

**Step 1 — Total Mass Balance (overall moles):**
$$F = V + L$$
where $F$ = feed molar flow rate, $V$ = vapor molar flow rate, $L$ = liquid molar flow rate.

**Step 2 — Component Mass Balance (more volatile component, MVC):**
$$F \, z_F = V \, y + L \, x$$

**Step 3 — Define the fraction vaporized:**
$$f = \frac{V}{F} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V = f F, \quad L = (1 - f) F$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the component balance:**
$$F z_F = (f F) y + (1 - f) F x$$

**Step 5 — Cancel $F$ from both sides:**
$$z_F = f \, y + (1 - f) \, x$$

**Step 6 — Rearrange to express $y$ as a function of $x$ (the operating line):**
$$f \, y = z_F - (1 - f) \, x$$

$$y = \frac{z_F}{f} - \frac{(1 - f)}{f} \, x$$

> This is the **flash distillation operating line** — a *straight line* on the $x$–$y$ diagram, connecting the operating point $(z_F, z_F)$ on the 45° line to the equilibrium point $(x, y)$.

**Step 7 — Equivalent form using $q = L/F = (1-f)$:**

Substituting $1-f = q$ and $f = 1-q$:

$$y = \frac{z_F - q \, x}{1 - q}$$

This is the **q-line equation** — it is the *generalized operating line* used in McCabe–Thiele analysis for multi-stage columns as well (in Module 3).

## 3.4 Derivation — Flash Rachford–Rice Equation (Multi-Component)

**Step 1 — Component material balance for component $i$ (general multi-component case):**
$$z_i = f \, y_i + (1 - f) \, x_i$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $y_i = K_i x_i$ (from $K$-value definition):**
$$z_i = f K_i x_i + (1 - f) x_i = x_i [f K_i + (1 - f)] = x_i [1 + f(K_i - 1)]$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $x_i$:**
$$x_i = \frac{z_i}{1 + f(K_i - 1)}$$

**Step 4 — Use the constraint $\sum x_i = 1$:**
$$\sum_{i=1}^{c} \frac{z_i}{1 + f(K_i - 1)} = 1$$

**Step 5 — Subtract $\sum z_i = 1$ from both sides:**
$$\sum_{i=1}^{c} \left[\frac{z_i}{1 + f(K_i - 1)} - z_i\right] = 0$$

**Step 6 — Combine fractions over a common denominator:**
$$\sum_{i=1}^{c} \frac{z_i - z_i[1 + f(K_i - 1)]}{1 + f(K_i - 1)} = 0$$

**Step 7 — Simplify the numerator ($z_i - z_i - z_i f(K_i - 1) = -z_i f(K_i - 1)$):**
$$\sum_{i=1}^{c} \frac{-z_i f(K_i - 1)}{1 + f(K_i - 1)} = 0$$

**Step 8 — Multiply by $-1$ and divide by $f$ (assuming $f \ne 0$):**

$$\boxed{\sum_{i=1}^{c} \frac{z_i (K_i - 1)}{1 + f(K_i - 1)} = 0}$$

> This is the **Rachford–Rice equation** — a transcendental equation in $f$ (or $T$ for a given $f$). It is solved by **Newton–Raphson iteration** in computer simulations, or by graphical/iterative methods in KTU exams.

## 3.5 Worked Example (Binary, Constant Relative Volatility)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A feed of composition $z_F = 0.50$ (mole fraction of MVC) is flash distilled. Relative volatility $\alpha = 2.5$ (constant). If $60\%$ of the feed is vaporized ($f = 0.60$), find $x$ and $y$.

**Given:** $z_F = 0.50$, $f = 0.60$, $\alpha = 2.5$.

**Equilibrium relation:**
$$y = \frac{\alpha x}{1 + (\alpha - 1) x} = \frac{2.5 x}{1 + 1.5 x}$$

**Operating line (mass balance):**
$$z_F = f y + (1 - f) x \quad \Rightarrow \quad 0.50 = 0.60 y + 0.40 x$$

**Step 1:** Substitute the equilibrium relation into the operating line:
$$0.50 = 0.60 \cdot \frac{2.5 x}{1 + 1.5 x} + 0.40 x$$

$$0.50 = \frac{1.5 x}{1 + 1.5 x} + 0.40 x$$

**Step 2:** Multiply through by $(1 + 1.5 x)$:
$$0.50(1 + 1.5 x) = 1.5 x + 0.40 x (1 + 1.5 x)$$

$$0.50 + 0.75 x = 1.5 x + 0.40 x + 0.60 x^2$$

**Step 3:** Collect like terms (move everything to one side):
$$0.60 x^2 + 1.5 x + 0.40 x - 0.50 - 0.75 x = 0$$

$$0.60 x^2 + (1.5 + 0.40 - 0.75) x - 0.50 = 0$$

$$0.60 x^2 + 1.15 x - 0.50 = 0$$

**Step 4:** Solve the quadratic using the formula $x = \dfrac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}$:
$$x = \frac{-1.15 \pm \sqrt{(1.15)^2 - 4(0.60)(-0.50)}}{2(0.60)}$$

$$x = \frac{-1.15 \pm \sqrt{1.3225 + 1.2000}}{1.20} = \frac{-1.15 \pm \sqrt{2.5225}}{1.20}$$

$$x = \frac{-1.15 \pm 1.5882}{1.20}$$

**Step 5:** Two roots are obtained:
- $x_1 = \dfrac{-1.15 + 1.5882}{1.20} = \dfrac{0.4382}{1.20} = 0.3652$ (✓ physically valid, $0 \le x \le 1$)
- $x_2 = \dfrac{-1.15 - 1.5882}{1.20} = \dfrac{-2.7382}{1.20} = -2.282$ (✗ rejected — negative)

**Step 6:** Take the valid root $x = 0.365$ and compute $y$:
$$y = \frac{2.5 \times 0.365}{1 + 1.5 \times 0.365} = \frac{0.9125}{1 + 0.5475} = \frac{0.9125}{1.5475} = 0.5897$$

**Step 7 (Verification):** $0.60(0.5897) + 0.40(0.365) = 0.3538 + 0.1460 = 0.4998 \approx 0.50$ ✓

> **Final Answer:** $x \approx 0.365$ (mol fraction MVC in liquid product), $y \approx 0.590$ (mol fraction MVC in vapor product).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The liquid product is **enriched in the less-volatile component** (LMVC = 0.635 vs. feed 0.50), and the vapor product is **enriched in the more-volatile component** (MVK = 0.590 vs. feed 0.50). This is the fundamental principle of distillation.

## 3.6 Analytical Summary Table

| Scenario | $f$ | $x$ vs $z_F$ | $y$ vs $z_F$ | Physical meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Minimum vaporization (just first bubble) | $f \to 0$ | $x \to z_F$ | $y \to y_{eq}(z_F)$ | Bubble-point condition |
| Maximum vaporization (last drop) | $f \to 1$ | $x \to x_{eq}(z_F)$ | $y \to z_F$ | Dew-point condition |
| Partial vaporization | $0 < f < 1$ | $x < z_F$ | $y > z_F$ | Real flash operation |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flash Distillation — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following **Mermaid** block represents the **functional architecture** of a flash distillation system, including all major material/energy streams and the equilibrium stage relationship.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["FEED TANK\nF = 100 mol/h\nzF = 0.50 mol frac MVC\nT_feed = 120 C\nP_feed = 5 bar"]
    B["PUMP / COMPRESSOR\nPressure Boost to 8 bar\nIsentropic Compression"]
    C["PRE-HEATER / HEAT EXCHANGER\nQ_in from steam or hot utility\nSensible heating of feed"]
    D["THROTTLING VALVE\nJoule-Thomson Expansion\nIsenthalpic Pressure Drop\nP: 8 bar → 1 bar\nT drops due to partial vaporization"]
    E["FLASH DRUM V-101\nSingle Equilibrium Stage\nPressure = 1 bar\nT = T_flash\nAdiabatic vessel\nVapor Liquid Equilibrium Achieved"]
    F["VAPOR PRODUCT OVERHEAD\nV = 60 mol/h\ny = 0.590 mol frac MVC\nEnriched in MVC\nSent to Condenser or next stage"]
    G["LIQUID PRODUCT BOTTOM\nL = 40 mol/h\nx = 0.365 mol frac MVC\nEnriched in LMVC\nSent to Reboiler or next stage"]
    H["CONDENSER\nVapor Cooling and\nPartial or Total Condensation\nReflux if any"]
    I["CONTROL SYSTEM\nPressure Transmitter\nTemperature Transmitter\nLevel Controller\nComposition Analyzer"]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    E --> G
    F --> H
    E -.Temperature Signal.-> I
    E -.Pressure Signal.-> I
    E -.Level Signal.-> I
    I -.Control Action.-> D
    I -.Control Action.-> C
```

## 4.2 Mass Balance Around the Flash Drum (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FeedIn["F, zF"]:::stream
    VapOut["V, y\n(top exit)"]:::stream
    LiqOut["L, x\n(bottom exit)"]:::stream

    Drum(("Flash Drum\nSingle Equilibrium\nStage\nT = T_flash\nP = P_drum\nq = L/F")):::vessel

    FeedIn --> Drum
    Drum --> VapOut
    Drum --> LiqOut

    classDef stream fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

```

## 4.3 x–y Diagram for Flash Distillation (Schematic Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EqCurve["Equilibrium Curve (VLE)"]
        EC["y = αx / 1 + α-1 x\nAbove the 45-degree diagonal\nfor α > 1"]
    end

    subgraph Diagonal["45-Degree Line (y = x)"]
        DL["Reference line for\nno separation"]
    end

    subgraph OpLine["Operating Line for Flash"]
        OL["Straight line connecting\nx = zF on 45-deg line\nto equilibrium point x y\nSlope = 1 - q / -q\nIntercept = zF / 1 - q"]
    end

    subgraph EquilibriumPoint["Intersection Point"]
        IP["x_star  y_star\n= Solution of flash equations\nGeometrically: intersection of\noperating line and equilibrium curve"]
    end

    EC --> IP
    OL --> IP
    IP -->|drop down| Xaxis["Liquid composition x_star"]
    IP -->|project left| Yaxis["Vapor composition y_star"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the x–y diagram for flash distillation:**
> - The **operating line** is a single straight line passing through $(z_F, z_F)$ on the 45° diagonal.
> - The **equilibrium curve** gives the VLE relationship $y = f(x)$.
> - The **intersection point** of these two lines gives the unique solution $(x, y)$ for a given $f$ (or $q$).
> - Different values of $f$ produce **different operating line slopes**, hence different intersection points.
> - At $f \to 0$ (minimum vaporization): operating line is vertical, intersection gives bubble point $y$.
> - At $f \to 1$ (maximum vaporization): operating line is horizontal, intersection gives dew point $x$.
