topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT501 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS II
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Principle of Fractionation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fractionation** is a mass transfer separation process in which a liquid mixture of two or more miscible components is separated into individual fractions or products of different compositions (and different boiling points) by the application and removal of heat, exploiting the differences in **relative volatility** ($\alpha$) of the constituents at equilibrium.

In the strict KTU 2024 syllabus context, *fractionation* refers to the cascading, multi-stage vapor-liquid contacting operation carried out in a **fractionating column** (distillation column), where each stage acts as an equilibrium contactor between rising vapor and descending liquid. The driving force is the difference in **vapor pressure** and **boiling point** of the components.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology (KTU Board Standard):**
> - **Feed (F):** The input liquid mixture to the column (units: **kmol/h** or **kg/h**).
> - **Distillate (D):** The overhead vapor product, condensed and collected as a liquid rich in the more volatile component (Light Key, LK).
> - **Bottoms (W or B):** The bottom liquid product, rich in the less volatile component (Heavy Key, HK).
> - **Reflux (L):** Condensed distillate returned to the column to enrich the rising vapor.
> - **Boil-up (V'):** Vapor generated at the reboiler.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are boiling a mixture of **water (boiling point 100 °C)** and **ethanol (boiling point 78.37 °C)** on a stove. You will notice that ethanol vapors rise more readily. If you condense these vapors on a cold spoon held above, the liquid droplets will be **richer in ethanol** than the original pot.

Now imagine stacking **50 such mini-boiling-condensing events** vertically on top of one another in a tower. At every level, vapor gets richer in ethanol, and the liquid flowing down gets richer in water. By the time vapor exits the top, it is almost **pure ethanol**; by the time liquid reaches the bottom, it is almost **pure water**.

**This stacked, multi-stage vapor-liquid contacting is exactly the principle of fractionation.**

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Snapshot:** The fractionating column in a petroleum refinery (e.g., the **Crude Distillation Unit, CDU**, with typical reflux ratios between **1.5 and 5.0**) operates on this exact principle, separating crude oil into naphtha, kerosene, diesel, and residue.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Industrial Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reflux Ratio | $R = L/D$ | 1.5 – 5.0 | dimensionless |
| Feed Thermal Condition | $q$ | 0 to 1 | dimensionless |
| Relative Volatility | $\alpha$ | 1.2 – 20 | dimensionless |
| Theoretical Stage | $N$ | 10 – 100 | dimensionless |
| Column Pressure Drop | $\Delta P$ | 0.5 – 10 | **kPa** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Step Logical Chain of Fractionation

Fractionation can be decomposed into the following sequential engineering logic:

1. **Vapor-Liquid Equilibrium (VLE) Establishment:** When a liquid mixture is partially vaporized, the vapor phase becomes enriched in the more volatile component (MVC). The quantitative relationship is given by the **equilibrium curve** (xy-diagram), where $y = f(x)$ represents the composition of vapor in equilibrium with liquid.

2. **Multi-Stage Contacting:** The mixture is brought into contact with vapor and liquid phases repeatedly across consecutive **theoretical stages** (also called **equilibrium stages**). A theoretical stage is a hypothetical contactor where the exiting streams are in perfect thermodynamic equilibrium.

3. **Counter-Current Flow:** Vapor flows **upward** (driven by reboiler heat input and pressure gradient), while liquid flows **downward** (driven by gravity and reflux return). This counter-current configuration maximizes the driving force ($\Delta y = y^* - y$).

4. **Reflux and Boil-up:** A portion of the condensed overhead (reflux, $L$) is returned, and a portion of the bottoms is reboiled (boil-up, $V'$). These internal recycles provide the necessary liquid and vapor traffic to enable separation.

5. **Component Recovery:** The Light Key (LK) is recovered primarily in the **distillate**, while the Heavy Key (HK) is recovered primarily in the **bottoms**. The fractional recovery $\eta_{LK}$ and $\eta_{HK}$ are key design specifications.

## 2.2 Why Fractionation Works — The "How" Behind the Phenomenon

- **Why vapor rises enriched:** Molecules of the MVC have higher **vapor pressure** ($P_i^{sat}$) at any given temperature, so they preferentially escape the liquid phase (Raoult's Law mechanism for ideal systems).
- **Why reflux is essential:** Without reflux, there would be no descending liquid stream to scrub the rising vapor. The reflux effectively "washes" the heavier components back down the column.
- **Why boil-up is essential:** Without boil-up, there would be no ascending vapor stream to strip the lighter components from the descending liquid.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **core equations** that KTU examiners test for this topic. Master these for guaranteed marks.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Raoult's Law (Ideal) | $P_i = x_i \cdot P_i^{sat}$ | $P_i$ in **kPa** or **atm** |
| 2 | Dalton's Law | $P = \sum y_i \cdot P$ | Total pressure basis |
| 3 | VLE Relation (Ideal) | $y_i = \dfrac{x_i \cdot P_i^{sat}}{P}$ | $y_i$ and $x_i$ are mole fractions |
| 4 | Relative Volatility | $\alpha_{ij} = \dfrac{K_i}{K_j} = \dfrac{P_i^{sat}}{P_j^{sat}}$ | $\alpha > 1$ for separation feasibility |
| 5 | Equilibrium Relation | $y = \dfrac{\alpha x}{1 + (\alpha - 1)x}$ | Binary system, $x$ = liquid mole fraction of MVC |
| 6 | Rayleigh Equation | $\ln \left( \dfrac{L_0}{L} \right) = \displaystyle \int_{x_0}^{x} \dfrac{dx}{y - x}$ | Differential (batch) distillation |
| 7 | Material Balance (Overall) | $F = D + W$ | **kmol/h** basis |
| 8 | Component Balance (A) | $F \cdot z_F = D \cdot x_D + W \cdot x_W$ | Mole fraction basis |
| 9 | Reflux Ratio | $R = L/D$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | Rectifying Section Slope | $\dfrac{L}{V} = \dfrac{R}{R+1}$ | Operating line slope |
| 11 | Stripping Section Slope | $\dfrac{L'}{V'} = \dfrac{R+q/(1-q)}{R+q/(1-q)-1}$ | Depends on $q$ |
| 12 | Minimum Reflux (Underwood) | $\left( \dfrac{L}{V} \right)_{min} = \dfrac{x_D - y_q}{x_D - x_q}$ | Pinch point at $q$-line |
| 13 | q-line Equation | $y = \dfrac{q}{q-1} \cdot x - \dfrac{z_F}{q-1}$ | Feed condition line |
| 14 | Fenske Equation (Min Stages) | $N_{min} = \dfrac{\ln \left[ \dfrac{x_D}{1-x_D} \cdot \dfrac{1-x_W}{x_W} \right]}{\ln \alpha_{LK,HK}}$ | Total reflux condition |
| 15 | Murphree Stage Efficiency | $E_{M} = \dfrac{y_n - y_{n+1}}{y_n^* - y_{n+1}}$ | $0 < E_M \leq 1$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility of Fractionation:**
> Fractionation is the **workhorse separation process** of the chemical and petrochemical industry. Approximately **90% of all industrial separations** involve some form of distillation. Applications include crude oil refining, air separation (producing $\text{O}_2$ and $\text{N}_2$ with $\alpha_{O_2/N_2} \approx 3.7$), ethanol-water separation, and pharmaceutical solvent recovery.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Equilibrium Relation $y = \dfrac{\alpha x}{1 + (\alpha - 1)x}$

This is the most heavily tested derivation in KTU Module 1. We start from Raoult's and Dalton's laws for a binary mixture of components A (MVC) and B (HMC).

**Step 1 — Apply Raoult's Law to each component:**

For component A:
$$P_A = x_A \cdot P_A^{sat}$$

For component B:
$$P_B = x_B \cdot P_B^{sat} = (1 - x_A) \cdot P_B^{sat}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Dalton's Law for the total pressure and partial pressure of A:**

The total pressure is:
$$P_{total} = P_A + P_B = x_A \cdot P_A^{sat} + (1 - x_A) \cdot P_B^{sat}$$

The mole fraction of A in the vapor phase is:
$$y_A = \dfrac{P_A}{P_{total}} = \dfrac{x_A \cdot P_A^{sat}}{x_A \cdot P_A^{sat} + (1 - x_A) \cdot P_B^{sat}}$$

**Step 3 — Introduce the definition of relative volatility $\alpha$:**

$$\alpha = \dfrac{P_A^{sat}}{P_B^{sat}}$$

Substituting $P_B^{sat} = P_A^{sat} / \alpha$ into the denominator:

$$y_A = \dfrac{x_A \cdot P_A^{sat}}{x_A \cdot P_A^{sat} + (1 - x_A) \cdot \dfrac{P_A^{sat}}{\alpha}}$$

**Step 4 — Factor out $P_A^{sat}$ from numerator and denominator:**

$$y_A = \dfrac{x_A}{x_A + \dfrac{(1 - x_A)}{\alpha}}$$

**Step 5 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $\alpha$:**

$$y_A = \dfrac{\alpha \cdot x_A}{\alpha \cdot x_A + (1 - x_A)}$$

**Step 6 — Expand the denominator and group terms:**

$$y_A = \dfrac{\alpha \cdot x_A}{\alpha \cdot x_A + 1 - x_A} = \dfrac{\alpha \cdot x_A}{1 + x_A(\alpha - 1)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Result:** The KTU board-accepted form of the binary VLE relation is:
> $$y = \dfrac{\alpha \cdot x}{1 + (\alpha - 1) \cdot x}$$
> This equation traces out the **equilibrium curve** on the xy-diagram and is the foundation for the McCabe-Thiele graphical method.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Rayleigh's Equation (Differential / Batch Distillation)

Consider a batch still containing an initial liquid of moles $L_0$ and mole fraction $x_0$ of MVC. A small amount of vapor $dV$ of composition $y$ escapes, leaving behind liquid of moles $L$ and composition $x$.

**Step 1 — Total moles balance:**

$$L = L_0 - dV$$

**Step 2 — Component A balance (differential):**

The change in moles of A in the liquid equals the moles of A removed in the vapor:

$$d(L \cdot x) = -y \cdot dV$$

Expanding the left-hand side using the product rule:
$$L \cdot dx + x \cdot dL = -y \cdot dV$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $dL = -dV$:**

$$L \cdot dx + x \cdot (-dV) = -y \cdot dV$$

$$L \cdot dx = -y \cdot dV + x \cdot dV = (x - y) \cdot dV$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $dV$:**

$$dV = \dfrac{L \cdot dx}{x - y}$$

**Step 5 — Recognize that $dL = -dV$, therefore:**

$$dL = -\dfrac{L \cdot dx}{x - y} = \dfrac{L \cdot dx}{y - x}$$

**Step 6 — Separate variables and integrate from initial to final state:**

$$\dfrac{dL}{L} = \dfrac{dx}{y - x}$$

$$\int_{L_0}^{L} \dfrac{dL}{L} = \int_{x_0}^{x} \dfrac{dx}{y - x}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result (Rayleigh Equation):**
> $$\ln \left( \dfrac{L_0}{L} \right) = \displaystyle \int_{x_0}^{x} \dfrac{dx}{y - x}$$
> This equation is used by KTU examiners in 14-mark Part B problems to determine the fraction of liquid remaining or the equilibrium composition in **differential/batch distillation**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the q-line Equation

The feed condition parameter $q$ is defined as the **fraction of feed that is liquid**:

$$q = \dfrac{\text{moles of liquid in feed}}{\text{total moles of feed}}$$

**Step 1 — Material balance around the feed plate (per mole of feed):**

Liquid flow below feed: $L' = L + q \cdot F$

Vapor flow above feed: $V = V' + (1 - q) \cdot F$

**Step 2 — Component balance around the feed plate:**

$$F \cdot z_F + V' \cdot y_{n+1} + L \cdot x_{n-1} = V \cdot y_n + L' \cdot x_n$$

After algebraic manipulation (substituting the flow relations and simplifying), the equation of the feed line (q-line) emerges:

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Result (q-line equation):**
> $$y = \dfrac{q}{q - 1} \cdot x - \dfrac{z_F}{q - 1}$$
> This line intersects the 45° diagonal at $x = y = z_F$ and has slope $\dfrac{q}{q-1}$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Principle of Fractionation — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    feed1[Feed Mixture F, zF] --> preheaterA[Preheater]
    preheaterA --> feedplateA[Feed Plate q-line]
    feedplateA --> rectifyingA[Rectifying Section Stages 1 to N1]
    rectifyingA --> condenserA[Total Condenser]
    condenserA --> refluxdrumA[Reflux Drum]
    refluxdrumA --> pumpA[Reflux Pump]
    pumpA --> refluxreturnA[Reflux Return L]
    refluxreturnA --> rectifyingA
    refluxdrumA --> distillateA[Distillate Product D, xD]
    feedplateA --> strippingA[Stripping Section Stages N1+1 to N]
    strippingA --> reboilerA[Reboiler V prime]
    reboilerA --> boilupA[Boil-up Vapor V prime]
    boilupA --> strippingA
    reboilerA --> bottomsA[Bottoms Product W, xW]
```

**Figure 1: Functional architecture of a continuous fractionating column showing the rectifying section, stripping section, total condenser, reflux drum, and reboiler.**

---

## 4.2 Vapor-Liquid Equilibrium Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    liquidA[Liquid Phase xA, xB] -->|partial vaporization| interfaceA[Vapor Liquid Interface]
    interfaceA --> vaporphaseA[Vapor Phase yA, yB]
    vaporphaseA -->|enrichment in MVC| enrichedvaporA[Enriched Vapor in MVC]
    liquidA -->|depletion in MVC| depletedliquidA[Depleted Liquid in MVC]
    enrichedvaporA -->|mass transfer of A| interfaceA
    depletedliquidA -->|mass transfer of B| interfaceA
```

**Figure 2: Mechanism of vapor-liquid equilibrium showing preferential escape of the more volatile component (MVC) into the vapor phase, driven by relative volatility $\alpha$.**

---

## 4.3 Feed Thermal Conditions and q-line Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    feedconditionA[Feed Condition] --> coldfeedA[Cold Liquid Feed, q greater than 1]
    feedconditionA --> saturatedliquidA[Saturated Liquid Feed, q equals 1]
    feedconditionA --> twoPhaseA[Two Phase Feed, 0 less than q less than 1]
    feedconditionA --> saturatedvaporA[Saturated Vapor Feed, q equals 0]
    feedconditionA --> superheatedA[Superheated Vapor Feed, q less than 0]
    coldfeedA --> qlinecaseA[Slope greater than 1, vertical intercept]
    saturatedliquidA --> qlinecaseB[Vertical line at x equals zF]
    twoPhaseA --> qlinecaseC[Slope between 0 and infinity]
    saturatedvaporA --> qlinecaseD[Horizontal line at y equals zF]
    superheatedA --> qlinecaseE[Negative slope, intersection at zF]
```

**Figure 3: Mapping of the five classical feed thermal conditions to their corresponding q-line slopes and geometric behavior on the McCabe-Thiele xy-diagram.**
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> **Define the term "fractionation" and distinguish it from a simple distillation process.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Fractionation is a mass transfer separation process in which a liquid mixture is separated into two or more products of different compositions by the application and removal of heat, exploiting differences in the relative volatility of the components, and using a cascade of multiple vapor-liquid equilibrium stages in a vertical column. (2 marks)
Unlike simple distillation, which involves only a single vaporization-condensation step and produces a vapor richer in MVC but still mixed, fractionation uses **multiple counter-current contact stages** with internal reflux and boil-up, achieving a much sharper separation with distillate and bottoms approaching the desired purity specifications. (1 mark)

---

> **Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **What is "relative volatility"? State its significance in the design of a fractionating column.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Relative volatility $\alpha_{ij}$ of component $i$ with respect to component $j$ is defined as the ratio of their K-values, which for an ideal system equals the ratio of their saturation vapor pressures. (1 mark)
$$\alpha_{ij} = \dfrac{K_i}{K_j} = \dfrac{P_i^{sat}}{P_j^{sat}}$$
**Significance:** (1) If $\alpha = 1$, separation by ordinary distillation is impossible. (2) Higher $\alpha$ means easier separation, fewer stages, and lower reflux requirements. (3) The minimum number of stages at total reflux is computed using the Fenske equation which directly uses $\alpha$. (2 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Question A. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Module 1 Internal Choice]**
>
> **(a)** Derive the binary vapor-liquid equilibrium relation $y = \dfrac{\alpha x}{1 + (\alpha - 1)x}$ starting from Raoult's and Dalton's laws. **State clearly all assumptions.** **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A mixture of benzene (MVC) and toluene (HMC) is to be fractionated to produce a distillate containing **94 mol% benzene** and a bottoms containing **5 mol% benzene**. The feed is a saturated liquid containing **45 mol% benzene**. The relative volatility $\alpha_{benzene/toluene}$ may be taken as **2.47**. Using the McCabe-Thiele method, determine:
> (i) the minimum number of theoretical stages $N_{min}$ using the Fenske equation, and
> (ii) the minimum reflux ratio $R_{min}$ using the Underwood equation.
> The feed line (q-line) slope is given by $\dfrac{q}{q-1} = \infty$ (saturated liquid feed). **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) Solution (7 Marks):**

**Assumptions:** (1 mark)
1. The system is **ideal**, obeying Raoult's and Dalton's laws.
2. The column operates at **constant total pressure** $P$.
3. The liquid and vapor phases are in **thermodynamic equilibrium** at each stage.
4. The mixture is **binary** with components A (MVC) and B (HMC).
5. The relative volatility $\alpha$ is **constant** across the operating temperature range.

**Derivation:** (6 marks — distribute as 1 mark per logical step as shown in Section 3.1 above)
- Step 1: Raoult's law → $P_A = x_A P_A^{sat}$ and $P_B = (1-x_A) P_B^{sat}$
- Step 2: Dalton's law → $y_A = \dfrac{x_A P_A^{sat}}{x_A P_A^{sat} + (1-x_A) P_B^{sat}}$
- Step 3: Introduce $\alpha = P_A^{sat}/P_B^{sat}$
- Step 4: Factor out $P_A^{sat}$
- Step 5: Multiply by $\alpha$
- Step 6: Final form $y = \dfrac{\alpha x}{1 + (\alpha - 1)x}$

**Part (b) Solution (7 Marks):**

**(i) Fenske Equation (3 marks):**
$$N_{min} = \dfrac{\ln \left[ \left( \dfrac{x_D}{1-x_D} \right) \cdot \left( \dfrac{1-x_W}{x_W} \right) \right]}{\ln \alpha}$$
$$N_{min} = \dfrac{\ln \left[ \left( \dfrac{0.94}{0.06} \right) \cdot \left( \dfrac{0.95}{0.05} \right) \right]}{\ln 2.47}$$
$$N_{min} = \dfrac{\ln \left[ 15.667 \cdot 19.000 \right]}{\ln 2.47} = \dfrac{\ln(297.67)}{0.9046}$$
$$N_{min} = \dfrac{5.6965}{0.9046} \approx 6.30$$

Therefore, the **minimum number of theoretical stages $N_{min} \approx 7$ (rounded up to next integer)**.

**(ii) Minimum Reflux Ratio (4 marks):**
For a **saturated liquid feed** ($q = 1$), the q-line is vertical, so the intersection point with the equilibrium curve is at $x = z_F = 0.45$. The corresponding $y_q$ is found from the equilibrium equation:
$$y_q = \dfrac{2.47 \cdot 0.45}{1 + (2.47 - 1) \cdot 0.45} = \dfrac{1.1115}{1.6615} = 0.6690$$
The minimum reflux ratio is:
$$R_{min} = \dfrac{x_D - y_q}{y_q - x_q} = \dfrac{0.94 - 0.6690}{0.6690 - 0.45} = \dfrac{0.2710}{0.2190} \approx 1.237$$

Therefore, the **minimum reflux ratio $R_{min} \approx 1.24$**.

---

> **Question B. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1 Internal Choice]**
>
> **(a)** A binary mixture containing **60 mol% n-pentane (MVC)** and **40 mol% n-hexane (HMC)** is subjected to differential (Rayleigh) batch distillation at atmospheric pressure. The relative volatility $\alpha = 2.85$ may be assumed constant. If distillation is continued until the liquid composition reaches **$x = 0.40$**, calculate the fraction of the original charge remaining in the still. **State the assumptions clearly.** **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** With the aid of a neat sketch, explain the principle of operation of a **total condenser** and a **partial condenser** in a fractionating column. Compare the two in terms of (i) reflux condition, (ii) product purity, and (iii) typical industrial use. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) Solution (7 Marks):**

**Assumptions:** (1 mark)
1. The system obeys Raoult's and Dalton's laws (ideal behavior).
2. Constant molar overflow applies.
3. $\alpha$ is **constant** over the composition range.
4. The still is well-mixed and operates at quasi-steady state.
5. The vapor leaving is in equilibrium with the liquid at every instant.

**Step 1 — Set up the Rayleigh equation** (2 marks):
$$\ln \left( \dfrac{L_0}{L} \right) = \int_{x_0}^{x} \dfrac{dx}{y - x}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the equilibrium relation $y = \dfrac{\alpha x}{1 + (\alpha - 1)x}$** (1 mark):

The denominator becomes:
$$y - x = \dfrac{\alpha x - x[1 + (\alpha - 1)x]}{1 + (\alpha - 1)x} = \dfrac{x[(\alpha - 1) - (\alpha - 1)x]}{1 + (\alpha - 1)x} = \dfrac{x(\alpha - 1)(1 - x)}{1 + (\alpha - 1)x}$$

Therefore:
$$\dfrac{1}{y - x} = \dfrac{1 + (\alpha - 1)x}{x(\alpha - 1)(1 - x)}$$

**Step 3 — Apply partial fraction decomposition** (2 marks):
$$\dfrac{1 + (\alpha - 1)x}{x(\alpha - 1)(1 - x)} = \dfrac{1}{\alpha - 1} \cdot \dfrac{1 + (\alpha - 1)x}{x(1 - x)} = \dfrac{1}{\alpha - 1} \left[ \dfrac{1}{x} + \dfrac{\alpha}{1 - x} \right]$$

**Step 4 — Integrate from $x_0 = 0.60$ to $x = 0.40$** (1 mark):
$$\ln \left( \dfrac{L_0}{L} \right) = \dfrac{1}{\alpha - 1} \left[ \ln \left( \dfrac{x}{x_0} \right) + \alpha \ln \left( \dfrac{1 - x_0}{1 - x} \right) \right]$$
$$\ln \left( \dfrac{L_0}{L} \right) = \dfrac{1}{1.85} \left[ \ln \left( \dfrac{0.40}{0.60} \right) + 2.85 \cdot \ln \left( \dfrac{0.40}{0.60} \right) \right]$$
$$= \dfrac{1}{1.85} \left[ -0.4055 + 2.85 \cdot (-0.4055) \right] = \dfrac{-0.4055 \cdot 3.85}{1.85} = \dfrac{-1.5611}{1.85} = -0.8438$$

Therefore:
$$\dfrac{L}{L_0} = e^{-0.8438} = 0.4301$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Result:** The fraction of the original charge remaining in the still is **$L/L_0 \approx 0.430$ or 43.0%**.

**Part (b) Solution (7 Marks):**

| Feature | Total Condenser | Partial Condenser |
|---|---|---|
| (i) **Vapor Handling** | All overhead vapor is condensed to liquid; reflux and distillate are both liquids of identical composition $x_D$. | Only a portion of the vapor is condensed; the remaining vapor is the distillate, so $y_D$ and $x_{reflux}$ are in equilibrium. |
| (ii) **Product Purity** | Higher distillate purity because reflux composition equals $x_D$ exactly. | Slightly lower purity because the vapor distillate is in equilibrium with a richer reflux liquid, giving an extra equilibrium stage free of charge. |
| (iii) **Typical Use** | Most common in chemical and petroleum industries; used when distillate is stored as a liquid. | Used when product is a gas (e.g., LPG, $\text{NH}_3$, $\text{Cl}_2$) or when the extra stage is needed for high-purity low-boiling separations. |

(7 marks distributed: 2 for sketch, 2 for total condenser explanation, 2 for partial condenser, 1 for comparison table)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the equilibrium assumption:** When deriving the VLE relation, students often write Raoult's law but forget to anchor it to Dalton's law. You will lose **2 marks minimum**.
> 2. **Using $|x|$ notation in tables:** Use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ in LaTeX — vertical pipes break the markdown table syntax.
> 3. **Mixing up $L$ and $V$:** In the rectifying section, $V = L + D$. In the stripping section, $L' = V' + W$. Conflating these costs 1 mark per error.
> 4. **Fenske at total reflux:** A common KTU trap — Fenske is only valid at total reflux ($R = \infty$). Always state this assumption explicitly.
> 5. **Rounding $N_{min}$:** Always round **up** to the next integer (you cannot have a fractional stage).
> 6. **q-line intercept sign:** The y-intercept is $-z_F/(q-1)$, which is **negative for $q<1$**. A common student error is missing the negative sign.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **The KTU-MUST-KNOW Checklist for "Principle of Fractionation":**
>
> - **Definition:** Fractionation = multi-stage counter-current vapor-liquid separation exploiting $\alpha > 1$.
> - **Driving force:** Concentration gradient $y - y^*$ (or $y^* - y$); equilibrium relationship is the upper bound.
> - **The Five Pillars of Design Equations:** (1) Fenske → $N_{min}$, (2) Underwood → $R_{min}$, (3) Gilliland → actual $N$ at operating $R$, (4) Erbar-Maddox → feed-stage location, (5) Murphree → real stage efficiency.
> - **Operating Lines:** Rectifying section: $y_{n+1} = \dfrac{R}{R+1} x_n + \dfrac{x_D}{R+1}$; Stripping section: $y_{n+1} = \dfrac{L'}{V'} x_n - \dfrac{W x_W}{V'}$.
> - **q-line:** Slope $q/(q-1)$, passes through $(z_F, z_F)$ on the 45° line; vertical for saturated liquid, horizontal for saturated vapor.
> - **Rayleigh equation:** Applies to **batch/differential** distillation only — not to continuous columns.
> - **McCabe-Thiele assumption:** Constant molar overflow, constant $\alpha$, equilibrium at every stage.
> - **Total reflux = minimum stages; Total stages (infinite) = minimum reflux** — the actual operating point lies between these two extremes.
> - **Practical reflux ratio:** $R_{actual} = (1.2 \text{ to } 2.0) \cdot R_{min}$ is a common industrial design heuristic.
> - **Condenser choice:** Total condenser is the default; partial condenser gives an extra theoretical stage and is preferred for gaseous products.
> - **Key constants to memorize:** $\alpha$ for benzene-toluene $\approx 2.47$; $\alpha$ for ethanol-water $\approx 2.0$–$3.0$ (composition-dependent); $R_{min}$ for binary saturated-liquid feeds computed from the $q$-line intersection on the equilibrium curve.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fractionation in Plate Columns
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT501 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS II
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fractionation in Plate Columns

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
**Fractionation in plate columns** is a continuous, multi-stage mass transfer separation process in which a liquid mixture is separated into its components (or groups of components) based on differences in **volatility** by contacting the rising vapor stream with the descending liquid stream on a series of horizontal **trays (plates)** stacked vertically inside a cylindrical column. Each plate acts as an equilibrium stage where intimate vapor–liquid contact enables partial mass transfer, and the cascade of plates progressively enriches the vapor in the more volatile component (MVC) while the liquid is stripped of the MVC.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the plate column is the **workhorse unit** of binary distillation, and "fractionation efficiency" quantifies how closely a real plate approaches the behavior of an **ideal/theoretical plate** (where the exiting vapor and liquid are in thermodynamic equilibrium).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"The Staircase vs. The Ramp" Analogy**
> Imagine you are at the bottom of a 10-floor building and need to reach the top. A **theoretical plate column** is a perfect **staircase** with crisp, even steps — at every step, you perfectly land at the next floor (equilibrium). A **real plate column** is a **worn staircase** where each step is slightly shorter than the ideal step — you still go up, but the rising vapor is not quite in equilibrium with the falling liquid. The **Murphree efficiency** is simply the ratio: *actual rise / ideal rise on each tread*.

Alternatively, think of a **waterfall series**: a tall waterfall (one huge equilibrium stage) is hard to build; instead, engineers build 30 small waterfalls (plates), where each small fall enriches vapor a little. The vapor enters the top, gets progressively richer in the light component, and leaves as **distillate**, while the liquid leaving the bottom is lean in the light component — the **bottoms/residue**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants
- **Tray spacing**: typically **0.45 m to 0.9 m** (18 to 36 inches).
- **Column diameter**: **0.3 m to 10 m** depending on capacity.
- **Number of plates**: 5 (light hydrocarbon) to **>100** (vacuum distillation of heavy oils).
- **Operating pressure**: vacuum, atmospheric, or pressurized columns.
- **Reflux ratio R = L/D** — a key design variable.
- **Relative volatility α** — drives separation feasibility.
- **g = 9.81 m/s²** — used in flooding correlations.

### 1.4 Syllabus Highlights (KTU 2024 – Module 1)
> [!NOTE]
> **Must-know deliverables for the KTU Board Exam:**
> 1. Construction of the **McCabe–Thiele diagram** with operating lines, q-line, and stepping-off procedure.
> 2. Definition and use of **Murphree plate efficiency** (E_MV, E_ML) and **overall column efficiency** (E_O).
> 3. Relationship between **point efficiency** and **Murphree efficiency** (Lewis-Math correlation).
> 4. Identification of **minimum reflux** (R_min) and **total reflux** (R = ∞) conditions.
> 5. Numerical problems on **number of theoretical plates** and **feed plate location**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Plate Columns? (The "Why" Behind the Design)
- A single equilibrium stage gives limited enrichment. To reach high purity (say 95 mol% distillate), **many stages in series** are required.
- A **plate (tray) column** mechanically provides these stages in a compact vertical geometry.
- Each plate has: an **active area** (bubbles contact liquid), **downcomers** (liquid transport), and **weir** (liquid level control).

### 2.2 The Ideal vs. Real Plate Distinction
**Theoretical (Ideal) Plate**: Vapor $y_n$ leaving and liquid $x_n$ leaving a plate are in **thermodynamic equilibrium**, i.e., $y_n = y^*(x_n)$ where $y^*$ is read off the equilibrium curve.

**Real Plate**: Vapor $y_n$ is **richer** than ideal only by a fraction; the gap is quantified by efficiency.

### 2.3 Operating Lines – The Engine of Plate Design
For a binary distillation column operating at constant molar overflow (CMO), the operating lines are linear.

**Rectifying Section (above feed):**

$$y_{n+1} = \frac{R}{R+1}x_n + \frac{x_D}{R+1}$$

- Slope = $\frac{R}{R+1}$, intercept on y-axis = $\frac{x_D}{R+1}$.
- Passes through $(x_D, x_D)$.

**Stripping Section (below feed):**

$$y_{m+1} = \frac{L'}{V'}x_m - \frac{W x_W}{V'}$$

- Slope = $\frac{L'}{V'}$, negative intercept.
- Passes through $(x_W, x_W)$.

### 2.4 The q-Line (Feed Condition Line)
The q-line tells us how the feed state changes molar flows across the feed plate.

$$y = \frac{q}{q-1}x - \frac{z_F}{q-1}$$

| Feed Condition | q value | q-line slope |
|---|---|---|
| Saturated liquid | q = 1 | Vertical (∞) |
| Saturated vapor | q = 0 | Horizontal (0) |
| Subcooled liquid | q > 1 | Positive, finite |
| Superheated vapor | q < 0 | 0 < slope < 1 |
| Two-phase feed | 0 < q < 1 | Positive, finite |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation | Symbols | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Murphree Vapor Efficiency | $E_{MV} = \dfrac{y_n - y_{n+1}}{y_n^* - y_{n+1}}$ | $y_n, y_{n+1}$: vapor mol frac leaving & entering plate n; $y_n^*$: vapor in eq. with $x_n$ | Range 0.4 – 0.9 typical |
| 2 | Murphree Liquid Efficiency | $E_{ML} = \dfrac{x_{n-1} - x_n}{x_{n-1} - x_n^*}$ | $x_{n-1}, x_n$: liquid leaving & entering; $x_n^*$: liquid in eq. with $y_n$ | Used in absorption |
| 3 | Overall Column Efficiency | $E_O = \dfrac{N_{theoretical}}{N_{actual}}$ | — | Empirical, used in preliminary design |
| 4 | q-line | $y = \dfrac{q}{q-1}x - \dfrac{z_F}{q-1}$ | $z_F$: feed composition | Slope depends on thermal state |
| 5 | Rectifying operating line | $y = \dfrac{R}{R+1}x + \dfrac{x_D}{R+1}$ | R = L/D, $x_D$ distillate | CMO assumption |
| 6 | Stripping operating line | $y = \dfrac{L'}{V'}x - \dfrac{W x_W}{V'}$ | $L', V'$: stripping liq/vap mol flow | CMO assumption |
| 7 | Fenske (Total Reflux) | $N_{min} = \dfrac{\ln \left[ \left(\dfrac{x_D}{1-x_D}\right) \left(\dfrac{1-x_W}{x_W}\right)\right]}{\ln \alpha_{avg}}$ | $\alpha_{avg}$: geometric mean relative volatility | For minimum stages |
| 8 | Underwood (Min Reflux) | $\sum \dfrac{q_i z_{Fi}}{\alpha_i - \theta} = 1$ | $\theta$: Underwood root between $\alpha_{LK}$ & $\alpha_{HK}$ | Then $R_{min} = \sum \dfrac{\alpha_i x_{Di}}{\alpha_i - \theta} - 1$ |
| 9 | Gilliland Correlation | $Y = 0.75(1-X)^{0.5668}$ | $X = \dfrac{R - R_{min}}{R+1}$, $Y = \dfrac{N - N_{min}}{N+1}$ | Graphical fit by Eduljee |
| 10 | Lewis-Math Relation | $\dfrac{E_{MV} - E_{OG}}{E_{MV}} = \dfrac{1}{mG/L}\left[\ln(1+E_{MV}(mG/L-1))\right]$ | m: slope of eq. curve; G/L: vapor to liquid molar flow ratio | Connects $E_{OG}$ (point) and $E_{MV}$ (plate) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Discipline:** All compositions are **mole fractions** when CMO is invoked. The equilibrium curve $y^*(x)$ must be on a **mole basis** for plate-by-plate stepping.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Crude oil atmospheric and vacuum distillation** in refineries — 30 to 50 plates per column.
- **Air separation** (cryogenic O₂/N₂) — > 70 plates in double-column scheme.
- **Ethanol-water separation** in bio-refineries — plate columns preferred over packed when fouling is an issue.
- **Wine and spirits distillation** — compact plate columns with bubble caps or sieve trays.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Rectifying Operating Line
Consider the mass balance around the top envelope of the column (rectifying section, plate n+1 to condenser).

**Step 1: Total molar balance around the top of the column**

$$V_{n+1} = L_n + D$$

This says: vapor rising into plate n equals the liquid descending plus the distillate withdrawn.

**Step 2: Component (light key) balance around the same envelope**

$$V_{n+1} y_{n+1} = L_n x_n + D x_D$$

The light key entering with vapor = light key leaving with liquid + light key leaving with distillate.

**Step 3: Apply the CMO assumption** — $V_{n+1} = V_n = V$ and $L_n = L_{n-1} = L$ (constant molar flows because molar latent heats of vaporization are equal for both components in binary, and sensible heat differences are negligible).

**Step 4: Substitute and define R = L/D**

From Step 1, $V = L + D = D(R+1)$.

Rearranging Step 2 for $y_{n+1}$:

$$y_{n+1} = \frac{L}{V} x_n + \frac{D}{V} x_D$$

**Step 5: Substitute L/V and D/V in terms of R**

$$\frac{L}{V} = \frac{L}{L+D} = \frac{R}{R+1}, \quad \frac{D}{V} = \frac{1}{R+1}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\, y_{n+1} = \frac{R}{R+1} x_n + \frac{x_D}{R+1} \,}$$

This is the **rectifying operating line** — a straight line of slope $\frac{R}{R+1}$ and y-intercept $\frac{x_D}{R+1}$. It passes through the point $(x_D, x_D)$ on the 45° diagonal.

### 3.2 Derivation of the q-Line
Consider the feed plate where the feed is introduced. The flow rates change from L, V (rectifying) to L', V' (stripping).

**Step 1: Mass balance across the feed plate**

$$L' = L + qF, \quad V' = V + (q-1)F$$

These follow from the definition of q (moles of saturated liquid formed per mole of feed during feed conditioning). The cases:
- q = 1 (saturated liquid): $L' = L + F$, $V' = V$ (no vapor added by feed).
- q = 0 (saturated vapor): $L' = L$, $V' = V - F$.

**Step 2: Derive the locus of feed-plate intersection**
The two operating lines and the 45° line meet at the feed stage. Eliminating L' and V' by combining the two operating line equations:

$$\frac{V'-L'}{F} = q-1 \implies V' - L' = (q-1)F$$

Also, $V - L = D + (q-1)F - 0$ from rectifying balance, so $V' - L' = D - (D - (q-1)F) = (q-1)F$ — consistent.

**Step 3: Combine using point $(x_F, y_F)$ where both lines meet on q-line**

Starting from $V'y = L'x - W x_W$ and $Vy = Lx + D x_D$, and using $V = L + D$, we get:

$$y(x_F - x) = q(x - x_F)$$

Solving for y:

$$\boxed{\, y = \frac{q}{q-1}x - \frac{z_F}{q-1} \,}$$

This is the **q-line** passing through $(z_F, z_F)$ with slope $\frac{q}{q-1}$.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Murphree Vapor Plate Efficiency
Let:
- $y_{n+1}$ = vapor composition entering plate n from below.
- $y_n$ = vapor composition leaving plate n (going up).
- $x_n$ = liquid composition leaving plate n.
- $y_n^*$ = vapor composition that would be in equilibrium with $x_n$ (read from equilibrium curve).

**Definition**: The actual enrichment achieved in the vapor stream is the **fractional approach** toward equilibrium:

$$E_{MV} = \frac{\text{actual change in vapor composition}}{\text{maximum possible change (to equilibrium)}}$$

The maximum change is from $y_{n+1}$ up to $y_n^*$. The actual change is from $y_{n+1}$ up to $y_n$.

$$E_{MV} = \frac{y_n - y_{n+1}}{y_n^* - y_{n+1}}$$

**Limiting cases**:
- $E_{MV} = 1$: ideal plate (vapor leaving is at equilibrium with liquid leaving).
- $E_{MV} = 0$: no mass transfer (vapor passes through unchanged).

### 3.4 Derivation of the Fenske Equation (Minimum Stages at Total Reflux)
At total reflux, no products are withdrawn: D = 0, W = 0, R = ∞, L = V. The operating lines coincide with the **diagonal $y = x$**.

**Step 1: Component balance on plate n at total reflux**

$$V(y_{n} - y_{n+1}) = L(x_{n-1} - x_n) \quad \text{and} \quad V = L$$

So $y_n - y_{n+1} = x_{n-1} - x_n$.

**Step 2: Equilibrium relation** (constant relative volatility $\alpha$):

$$y = \frac{\alpha x}{1 + (\alpha-1)x}$$

Equivalently, $\frac{y}{1-y} = \alpha \frac{x}{1-x}$.

**Step 3: Cascade over all N_min stages from bottom ($x_W$) to top ($x_D$)**:

At each plate the relationship $\frac{y_n}{1-y_n} = \alpha_n \frac{x_n}{1-x_n}$ holds, and at total reflux $y_n = x_{n+1}$. Cascading:

$$\frac{x_D}{1-x_D} = \alpha_1 \alpha_2 \cdots \alpha_{N_{min}} \frac{x_W}{1-x_W}$$

**Step 4: Take geometric mean of $\alpha$ across stages**:

$$N_{min} = \frac{\ln \left[ \left(\dfrac{x_D}{1-x_D}\right)\left(\dfrac{1-x_W}{x_W}\right)\right]}{\ln \alpha_{avg}}$$

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example
**Problem**: A distillation column separates a benzene-toluene mixture. Given $x_F = 0.50$, $x_D = 0.95$, $x_W = 0.05$, $R = 1.5 R_{min}$, saturated liquid feed (q = 1), $\alpha = 2.47$. Find: (a) $R_{min}$, (b) $N_{min}$, (c) actual plates by Gilliland.

**Step (a): Underwood root for saturated liquid feed (q = 1)**

$$\sum \frac{q_i z_{Fi}}{\alpha_i - \theta} = 1 \implies \frac{1.0 \times 0.50}{2.47 - \theta} + \frac{1.0 \times 0.50}{1.00 - \theta} = 1$$

Solving iteratively: try $\theta = 1.234$ →

$$\frac{0.50}{1.236} + \frac{0.50}{-0.234} = 0.4045 - 2.137 = -1.733 \quad \text{(too negative)}$$

Try $\theta = 1.45$ →

$$\frac{0.50}{1.02} + \frac{0.50}{-0.45} = 0.490 - 1.111 = -0.621$$

Try $\theta = 1.30$ →

$$\frac{0.50}{1.17} + \frac{0.50}{-0.30} = 0.427 - 1.667 = -1.24$$

Iterate → $\theta \approx 1.20$ gives sum ≈ 1.0. So $\theta = 1.20$.

**Compute $R_{min}$:**

$$R_{min} = \sum \frac{\alpha_i x_{Di}}{\alpha_i - \theta} - 1 = \frac{2.47 \times 0.95}{2.47 - 1.20} + \frac{1.00 \times 0.05}{1.00 - 1.20} - 1$$

$$= \frac{2.347}{1.27} + \frac{0.05}{-0.20} - 1 = 1.848 - 0.25 - 1 = 0.598$$

So $R_{min} \approx 0.60$, hence $R = 1.5 \times 0.60 = 0.90$.

**Step (b): Fenske $N_{min}$**

$$N_{min} = \frac{\ln\left[\left(\dfrac{0.95}{0.05}\right)\left(\dfrac{0.95}{0.05}\right)\right]}{\ln 2.47} = \frac{\ln(361)}{\ln 2.47} = \frac{5.886}{0.904} = 6.51 \approx 7 \text{ stages}$$

**Step (c): Gilliland correlation**

$$X = \frac{R - R_{min}}{R+1} = \frac{0.90 - 0.60}{0.90 + 1} = \frac{0.30}{1.90} = 0.158$$

$$Y = 0.75(1-X)^{0.5668} = 0.75(0.842)^{0.5668} = 0.75 \times 0.911 = 0.683$$

$$Y = \frac{N - N_{min}}{N+1} \implies 0.683 = \frac{N - 7}{N+1}$$

$$0.683(N+1) = N - 7 \implies 0.683N + 0.683 = N - 7 \implies 7.683 = 0.317N$$

$$N = 24.2 \approx 25 \text{ stages}$$

**Final answer**: $R_{min} = 0.60$, $N_{min} = 7$, $N_{actual} = 25$ theoretical stages.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Plate Column Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FeedIn[Liquid Feed F at zF] --> FeedPlate
    SteamIn[Steam Reboiler at bottom] --> Reboiler
    Reboiler --> ColBottom[Column Bottom: xW]
    ColBottom --> StripSection[Stripping Section: Plates 1 to f-1]
    StripSection --> FeedPlate[Feed Plate: stage f]
    FeedPlate --> RectSection[Rectifying Section: Plates f+1 to N]
    RectSection --> Condenser[Total Condenser]
    Condenser --> RefluxDrum[Reflux Drum]
    RefluxDrum --> DistOut[Distillate D at xD]
    RefluxDrum --> RefluxBack[Reflux L = RD]
    RefluxBack --> RectSection
```

### 4.2 Single Plate Internal – Mass Transfer Action

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VapIn[Vapor y sub n+1 from below] --> Bubbling[Through Liquid on Tray]
    Bubbling --> VapOut[Vapor y sub n leaves above]
    LiqIn[Liquid x sub n-1 from above] --> CrossFlow[Crosses Active Area]
    CrossFlow --> Bubbling
    CrossFlow --> LiqOut[Liquid x sub n leaves to downcomer]
    DC[Downcomer] --> LiqOut
    DCn[Downcomer from above] --> LiqIn
```

### 4.3 McCabe-Thiele Stepping Procedure (Algorithm Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Given: xF, xD, xW, q, R, alpha]) --> DrawEq[Draw equilibrium curve y* of x]
    DrawEq --> DrawDiag[Draw 45 degree diagonal]
    DrawDiag --> MarkPoints[Mark xD on diagonal and xW on x-axis]
    MarkPoints --> DrawQ[Draw q-line through xF, zF]
    DrawQ --> DrawRect[Draw rectifying line from xD to q-line intersection]
    DrawRect --> DrawStrip[Draw stripping line from xW to same intersection]
    DrawStrip --> Step[Step off stages between operating lines and eq curve]
    Step --> CountN[Count theoretical stages N]
    CountN --> LocateFeed[Locate feed plate at transition from rect to strip]
    LocateFeed --> Done([Report N and feed plate number])
```

### 4.4 Efficiency Hierarchy Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    E_Overall[Overall Efficiency EO] --> E_Murphree[Murphree Plate Efficiency EMV or EML]
    E_Murphree --> E_Point[Point Efficiency EOG on tray]
    E_Point --> MassTransfer[Local Mass Transfer Coefficient kOG]
    MassTransfer --> Hydrodynamics[Holding Time, Bubble Size, Interfacial Area]
```

### 4.5 Mass Transfer Flow on a Sieve Tray (Schematic Cross-Section)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Liquid level on tray] --> Weir[Outlet Weir]
    A --> Bubbles[Rising Vapor Bubbles]
    Bubbles --> Froth[Froth Zone Active Area]
    Froth --> Entrainment[Some Liquid Droplets Entrained Up]
    Entrainment --> Demister[Demister Pad Above]
    Demister --> VaporUp[Clean Vapor y_n to Next Tray]
    Weir --> Downcomer[Liquid x_n in Downcomer]
    Downcomer --> NextTray[Next Lower Tray]
```
