## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define total reflux in a distillation column. What is the slope of the operating line at total reflux?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Total Reflux** is the operating condition in a distillation column where the **entire condensed overhead liquid is returned to the column as reflux** and **no distillate product is withdrawn**. The reflux ratio $R = L/D = \infty$.

> **Slope of the operating line at total reflux = 1** (i.e., the operating line coincides with the 45° diagonal, $y = x$). The y-intercept is zero.

> The number of theoretical stages obtained at total reflux is the **minimum number of stages (N_min)** required to achieve the specified separation — a theoretical lower bound for design.

**(Self-Assessment Marks Breakdown:** Definition — 1 mark, Reflux ratio expression — 1 mark, Slope statement — 1 mark**)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Explain why the operating line of the rectifying section coincides with the diagonal y = x at total reflux. State the significance of this condition.**

**Model Answer:**

> At total reflux, no distillate is withdrawn ($D = 0$). Therefore, the reflux flow equals the vapor flow ($L = V$). The rectifying operating line is given by:
>
> $$y_{n+1} = \frac{L}{V} x_n + \frac{D}{V} x_D$$
>
> Substituting $L/V = 1$ and $D/V = 0$, we get $y_{n+1} = x_n$, which is the equation of the 45° diagonal $y = x$.

> **Significance:** It gives the **minimum number of theoretical stages (N_min)** required for a given separation. This serves as the lower limit for design and is a benchmark used in the Gilliland correlation to estimate the actual number of stages at finite reflux.

**(Self-Assessment Marks Breakdown:** Material balance justification — 1 mark, Final equation — 1 mark, Significance — 1 mark**)**

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full Descriptive / Numerical Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Students often write $L/V = R$ instead of $L/V = R/(R+1)$. The correct ratio involves the **slope** $\frac{R}{R+1}$ and the **intercept** $\frac{x_D}{R+1}$.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting to include the **partial reboiler** as one theoretical stage in N_min.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Confusing total reflux ($R = \infty$, gives $N_{\min}$) with minimum reflux ($R = R_{\min}$, gives $N = \infty$).
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** When stepping on the McCabe–Thiele diagram, students sometimes move in the wrong direction (starting from the wrong corner). Always start at $(x_D, x_D)$ at the top-right and move down toward $x_W$.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Full Length ESE Question**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

> **(a)** A binary mixture of hexane and heptane is to be distilled at atmospheric pressure. The feed has a composition of 40 mol% hexane. The distillate must contain 90 mol% hexane and the bottoms must contain 5 mol% hexane. The relative volatility $\alpha = 2.5$ and may be assumed constant. The feed is a saturated liquid. Calculate the **minimum reflux ratio (R_min)** and the **minimum number of theoretical stages (N_min)** using the **Fenske equation**. **(7 marks)**

> **(b)** Explain with a neat sketch the **McCabe–Thiele method** for determining N_min at total reflux. Why is the operating line coincident with the diagonal at total reflux? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Calculation of R_min and N_min:**

**Step 1 — Equilibrium relation at feed point (q = 1, saturated liquid feed):**

$$y_F^* = \frac{\alpha \cdot x_F}{1 + (\alpha - 1) x_F} = \frac{2.5 \times 0.40}{1 + 1.5 \times 0.40} = \frac{1.00}{1.60} = 0.625$$

**Step 2 — Apply Underwood's correlation for R_min (saturated liquid feed):**

$$R_{\min} = \frac{x_D - y_F^*}{y_F^* - x_F} = \frac{0.90 - 0.625}{0.625 - 0.40} = \frac{0.275}{0.225} = 1.222$$

**Step 3 — Fenske equation for N_min:**

$$N_{\min} = \frac{\ln \left[ \left(\frac{x_D}{1 - x_D}\right) \left(\frac{1 - x_W}{x_W}\right) \right]}{\ln \alpha_{avg}}$$

$$N_{\min} = \frac{\ln \left[ \left(\frac{0.90}{0.10}\right) \left(\frac{0.95}{0.05}\right) \right]}{\ln 2.5} = \frac{\ln(9.0 \times 19.0)}{\ln 2.5} = \frac{\ln(171.0)}{0.9163}$$

$$N_{\min} = \frac{5.142}{0.9163} = 5.61 \approx 6 \text{ theoretical stages (including reboiler)}$$

---

**(b) McCabe–Thiele Method at Total Reflux:**

**Step 1 — Plot the equilibrium curve** $y^* = \dfrac{\alpha \, x}{1 + (\alpha - 1)x}$ on the x–y diagram from $x = 0$ to $x = 1$.

**Step 2 — Draw the 45° diagonal** $y = x$ on the same diagram.

**Step 3 — Start at point A** with coordinates $(x_D, x_D) = (0.90, 0.90)$ — this point lies on both the operating line (y = x) and represents the top product composition.

**Step 4 — Move vertically downward** from A to the equilibrium curve at point B $(0.90, y^*)$. The vertical drop represents **one equilibrium contact (one theoretical stage)**.

**Step 5 — Move horizontally to the left** from B to the operating line y = x at point C $(x_1, x_1)$. Since the operating line is the 45° diagonal, the horizontal step ends exactly on the diagonal. The new x-coordinate is the liquid composition on tray 1.

**Step 6 — Repeat** vertical–horizontal stepping (down–left) until the liquid composition falls at or below $x_W = 0.05$. Count each step as one theoretical stage.

**Step 7 — Add 1** for the partial reboiler to get the total N_min.

**Why the operating line coincides with y = x:**

The general rectifying operating line is:

$$y_{n+1} = \frac{R}{R+1} x_n + \frac{x_D}{R+1}$$

At total reflux, $R \to \infty$, so:

$$\lim_{R \to \infty} \frac{R}{R+1} = 1, \quad \lim_{R \to \infty} \frac{x_D}{R+1} = 0$$

Therefore $y_{n+1} = x_n$, which is the 45° diagonal.

**(Marks Distribution:** Part (a) — Each step clearly shown with units, 7 marks; Part (b) — Diagram description 3 marks + derivation 2 marks + significance 2 marks = 7 marks**)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative ESE Question**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

> **(a)** A distillation column separates a benzene–toluene mixture. The relative volatility is 2.47. The top product contains 95 mol% benzene, and the bottom product contains 3 mol% benzene. The feed is 50 mol% benzene and is a saturated liquid. Using the **Fenske equation**, determine the **minimum number of theoretical stages (N_min)**. If the actual reflux ratio used is 1.5 times the minimum reflux ratio, estimate the **actual number of stages (N_actual)** using the **Gilliland correlation** as a function of the parameter $X = (R - R_{\min})/(R + 1)$. Use the **Eduljee correlation**:
>
> $$Y = 0.75 \left(1 - X^{0.5668}\right)$$
>
> where $Y = (N - N_{\min})/(N + 1)$. **(7 marks)**

> **(b)** Discuss the practical significance of operating a distillation column at total reflux during **startup** and **shutdown**. Why is total reflux considered a "theoretical limit" rather than a steady production mode? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Fenske + Gilliland Calculation:**

**Step 1 — Fenske equation for N_min:**

$$N_{\min} = \frac{\ln \left[ \left(\frac{x_D}{1 - x_D}\right) \left(\frac{1 - x_W}{x_W}\right) \right]}{\ln \alpha_{avg}}$$

$$N_{\min} = \frac{\ln \left[ \left(\frac{0.95}{0.05}\right) \left(\frac{0.97}{0.03}\right) \right]}{\ln 2.47} = \frac{\ln(19.0 \times 32.333)}{\ln 2.47} = \frac{\ln(614.33)}{0.9046}$$

$$N_{\min} = \frac{6.420}{0.9046} = 7.10 \approx 8 \text{ theoretical stages (rounded up)}$$

**Step 2 — R_min calculation (saturated liquid feed, q = 1):**

$$y_F^* = \frac{2.47 \times 0.50}{1 + 1.47 \times 0.50} = \frac{1.235}{1.735} = 0.7118$$

$$R_{\min} = \frac{x_D - y_F^*}{y_F^* - x_F} = \frac{0.95 - 0.7118}{0.7118 - 0.50} = \frac{0.2382}{0.2118} = 1.125$$

**Step 3 — Actual reflux ratio:**

$$R = 1.5 \times R_{\min} = 1.5 \times 1.125 = 1.6875$$

**Step 4 — Calculate Gilliland parameter X:**

$$X = \frac{R - R_{\min}}{R + 1} = \frac{1.6875 - 1.125}{1.6875 + 1} = \frac{0.5625}{2.6875} = 0.2093$$

**Step 5 — Apply Eduljee correlation:**

$$Y = 0.75 \left(1 - X^{0.5668}\right) = 0.75 \left(1 - 0.2093^{0.5668}\right)$$

$$0.2093^{0.5668} = e^{0.5668 \ln(0.2093)} = e^{0.5668 \times (-1.5643)} = e^{-0.8866} = 0.4118$$

$$Y = 0.75 \times (1 - 0.4118) = 0.75 \times 0.5882 = 0.4412$$

**Step 6 — Solve for N (using N_min = 8):**

$$Y = \frac{N - N_{\min}}{N + 1} = 0.4412$$

$$N - 8 = 0.4412 (N + 1) = 0.4412 N + 0.4412$$

$$N - 0.4412 N = 8 + 0.4412 = 8.4412$$

$$0.5588 N = 8.4412 \Rightarrow N = \frac{8.4412}{0.5588} = 15.11$$

**Step 7 — Round up:**

$$N_{\text{actual}} \approx 16 \text{ theoretical stages (including reboiler)}$$

---

**(b) Practical Significance of Total Reflux:**

**During Startup:**
- The column internals (trays/packing) need to be **wetted** to establish proper liquid–vapor contact.
- Concentration and temperature profiles need to be **established** throughout the column from an initial feed.
- Total reflux allows the column to **"line out"** (reach steady composition) without product withdrawal, ensuring the operator can verify steady operation before feeding the system.
- It provides a **gentle introduction** of thermal and hydraulic loads to the column.

**During Shutdown:**
- When feed is cut off but the column needs to maintain separation (e.g., for short interruptions), total reflux is used as a **holding mode**.
- It allows the column to remain in a hot, ready state for quick restart.

**Why Total Reflux is a "Theoretical Limit":**
- At total reflux, **no product (D = 0, B = 0)** is withdrawn — the column produces nothing useful.
- The reflux ratio R = ∞ is a **mathematical limit**; in practice, infinite reflux cannot be achieved.
- The reboiler and condenser duties are at their **maximum**, making it economically unsustainable.
- It exists only as a **design tool** to determine N_min, which is the minimum number of stages theoretically required for a given separation.

**(Marks Distribution:** Part (a) — N_min 2 marks + R_min 2 marks + Gilliland 3 marks = 7 marks; Part (b) — Startup 2 marks + Shutdown 1.5 marks + Theoretical limit explanation 3.5 marks = 7 marks**)**

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary for Last-Minute Revision:**

- **Total Reflux** = Hypothetical condition where **all condensate is returned**, no distillate is withdrawn.
- **Reflux Ratio:** $R = L/D = \infty$ at total reflux.
- **Operating Line:** $y_{n+1} = x_n$ — coincides with the 45° diagonal $y = x$.
- **Operating Line Slope:** $L/V = R/(R+1) = 1$ at $R = \infty$.
- **Operating Line y-Intercept:** $x_D/(R+1) = 0$ at $R = \infty$.
- **Stage Count:** Yields **N_min** (Minimum Number of Theoretical Stages) — the theoretical lower bound.
- **General Rectifying Line:** $y_{n+1} = \dfrac{R}{R+1} x_n + \dfrac{x_D}{R+1}$.
- **Fenske Equation:** $N_{\min} = \dfrac{\ln\left[\dfrac{x_D}{1-x_D} \cdot \dfrac{1-x_W}{x_W}\right]}{\ln \alpha_{avg}}$ — gives N_min directly without graphical construction.
- **Equilibrium Relation:** $y^* = \dfrac{\alpha \, x}{1 + (\alpha - 1) x}$ — used to plot the equilibrium curve.
- **Comparison Anchor:** $N_{\min}$ (total reflux) ≤ $N_{\text{actual}}$ (finite reflux) < $\infty$ (minimum reflux).
- **Industrial Use:** Total reflux is used during **startup** (wetting internals, establishing profiles) and as a **holding mode** during process upsets.
- **Graphical Method:** McCabe–Thiele stepping begins at $(x_D, x_D)$, proceeds vertical-down (to equilibrium) and horizontal-left (to y = x), counting each step as one theoretical stage. The reboiler counts as one stage.
- **Common Pitfalls to Avoid:**
  - DO NOT confuse total reflux ($R = \infty$, $N = N_{\min}$) with minimum reflux ($R = R_{\min}$, $N = \infty$).
  - DO NOT forget the partial reboiler when counting stages.
  - DO NOT use $L/V = R$ (use $L/V = R/(R+1)$).
- **Reading Strategy on KTU Paper:** If a problem states "minimum number of stages" or "total reflux," immediately recall the diagonal y = x and the Fenske equation.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 2 — Total Reflux Study Notes (PCCHT501 | KTU 2024 Scheme)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Minimum Reflux
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT501 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS II
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Minimum Reflux

# MINIMUM REFLUX RATIO ($R_m$) — DEFINITION, INTUITION & OPERATIONAL OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Minimum Reflux Ratio ($R_m$):** The *minimum reflux ratio* is defined as the **lowest possible reflux ratio** at which a distillation column can theoretically achieve the specified separation between the distillate ($x_D$) and bottoms ($x_W$) compositions. At this condition, the number of theoretical stages required for separation becomes **infinite ($\infty$)**, because the rectifying operating line and the q-line intersect the **equilibrium curve** at the same point — creating a **"pinch point."**

Mathematically,

$$R_m = \frac{L_{min}}{D}$$

where $L_{min}$ is the minimum reflux liquid flow rate and $D$ is the distillate flow rate. It is one of the **two limiting conditions** used along with *Total Reflux* to bracket the design of a distillation column.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Stuck Escalator"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Escalator Analogy**
> Imagine a mall escalator moving upward (reflux liquid flowing down the column). If only **one or two people** are standing on it (low reflux), the escalator can carry them, but it cannot do any *work* — no goods get separated upstairs from downstairs. As we **add more people** (increase reflux), the system becomes capable of "carrying" the separation. At **minimum reflux**, the escalator is moving at the *absolute minimum speed* with just enough people to barely touch the destination floor. Below this, separation is *physically impossible* no matter how many floors (stages) you build. Above this, you can achieve the separation, but the more you add, the fewer floors you need.

In distillation:
- **Reflux Ratio ($R$)** → Like the number of people + escalator speed combined.
- **More Reflux** → Fewer stages needed (cheaper column, higher reboiler/condenser duty).
- **Less Reflux** → More stages needed (taller column, lower energy cost).
- **$R_m$** → The tipping point where the column can theoretically *just barely* do the job.

---

## 1.3 Physical Significance in Mass Transfer Operations II

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why $R_m$ matters in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. It is the **lower bound** for any feasible column design.
> 2. It is the **starting point** for the Gilliland correlation (which finds the *actual* number of stages).
> 3. Operating at $R_m$ in practice is **impossible** because it would require infinite trays.
> 4. The **optimum reflux ratio** ($R_{opt}$) used in design is typically $R_{opt} = (1.2 \text{ to } 1.5) \times R_m$.

The condition of minimum reflux is geometrically represented on a **McCabe–Thiele diagram** by the intersection of the **rectifying operating line** and the **q-line** touching the equilibrium curve at a *pinch point*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Operating Conditions Recap

A distillation column has **three operating zones**:
1. **Rectifying Section** (above feed plate): Operating line slope = $R/(R+1)$, intercept = $x_D/(R+1)$.
2. **Stripping Section** (below feed plate): Operating line slope = $(R+1)/[R+1 - (q/(q-1))]$ … (derived from q-line intersection).
3. **Feed Condition Line (q-line)**: Defines the thermal state of the feed.

At **minimum reflux**, the rectifying operating line *just touches* the equilibrium curve — typically at either:
- The intersection of the q-line and the equilibrium curve (for saturated liquid/vapor feeds), OR
- At one of the pure-component ends ($x_D$ or $x_W$) when the equilibrium curve is strongly curved (e.g., non-ideal systems with an azeotrope).

---

## 2.2 Geometric Interpretation (McCabe–Thiele Method)

The **rectifying operating line** equation is:

$$y_{n+1} = \frac{R}{R+1} x_n + \frac{x_D}{R+1}$$

As $R \to R_m$, the slope $R/(R+1)$ decreases until the operating line rotates down and *pinches* the equilibrium curve. The intercept $x_D/(R+1)$ simultaneously decreases.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Pinch Point":** The point where the operating line and equilibrium curve touch tangentially (or intersect at the q-line crossing). This is a thermodynamic bottleneck — no matter how many trays you stack at this point, the composition cannot change because the operating line and equilibrium coincide.

---

## 2.3 Analytical Expressions for $R_m$

### 2.3.1 For Binary Mixtures (Normal Systems)

When the feed is a **saturated liquid ($q=1$)**, the pinch occurs where the q-line (vertical line at $x = x_F$) intersects the equilibrium curve at $y^* = y_q$.

**Rectifying operating line at $R_m$** passes through $(x_D, x_D)$ and the intersection point $(x_q, y_q)$ on the equilibrium curve.

$$R_m = \frac{x_D - y_q}{y_q - x_q}$$

> This is the **most-tested formula in KTU exams** for saturated liquid feeds.

### 2.3.2 General q-line Intersection

When the q-line intersects the equilibrium curve at $(x_q, y_q)$:

$$\boxed{R_m = \frac{x_D - y_q}{y_q - x_q}}$$

This applies for **all** feed conditions where the pinch is at the q-line equilibrium intersection.

### 2.3.3 Special Case — Non-Ideal Systems (e.g., Azeotropic)

If the equilibrium curve is highly non-ideal (e.g., minimum-boiling azeotrope), the pinch may occur at:
- The azeotropic composition itself, OR
- One of the product specifications ($x_D$ or $x_W$)

In such cases, the rectifying operating line rotates until it passes through $(x_D, x_D)$ and is tangent to the equilibrium curve at the azeotrope. The formula above still applies but $y_q$ is replaced by the appropriate y-coordinate.

---

## 2.4 Underwood Equation (Multicomponent Distillation)

For **multicomponent systems**, the minimum reflux ratio is given by **Underwood's equation**:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{c} \frac{q \cdot z_i}{\alpha_i - \theta} = 1.0$$

$$\boxed{R_m = \sum_{i=1}^{c} \frac{(\alpha_i - 1) \cdot x_{D,i}}{\alpha_i - \theta} - 1}$$

where:
- $z_i$ = mole fraction of component $i$ in the feed
- $x_{D,i}$ = mole fraction of component $i$ in the distillate
- $\alpha_i$ = relative volatility of component $i$ (relative to heavy key)
- $q$ = thermal condition of feed
- $\theta$ = Underwood root — a value between the relative volatilities of the light key ($\alpha_{LK}$) and heavy key ($\alpha_{HK}$): $\alpha_{HK} < \theta < \alpha_{LK}$

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Application / Condition | Units |
|---|---------|------------------------|-------|
| 1 | $R_m = \dfrac{x_D - y_q}{y_q - x_q}$ | Binary, pinch at q-line × eqm intersection | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Slope of rectifying line at $R_m$: $\dfrac{R_m}{R_m+1}$ | McCabe–Thiele construction | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $y_{n+1} = \dfrac{R}{R+1} x_n + \dfrac{x_D}{R+1}$ | Rectifying operating line | Mole fraction |
| 4 | q-line: $y = \dfrac{q}{q-1} x - \dfrac{z_F}{q-1}$ | Feed condition line | Mole fraction |
| 5 | Underwood: $\sum \dfrac{q z_i}{\alpha_i - \theta} = 1$ | Multicomponent, find $\theta$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Underwood: $R_m = \sum \dfrac{(\alpha_i - 1) x_{D,i}}{\alpha_i - \theta} - 1$ | Multicomponent $R_m$ | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $R_{opt} = (1.2 \text{ to } 1.5) \times R_m$ | Practical design optimum | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $N_m$ at total reflux → Fenske: $N_m = \dfrac{\ln[(x_{LK}/x_{HK})_D (x_{HK}/x_{LK})_W]}{\ln \alpha_{avg}}$ | Minimum stages (Fenske) | Number |
| 9 | Gilliland: $Y = 0.75(1 - X^{0.5668})$ where $X = \dfrac{R - R_m}{R+1}$, $Y = \dfrac{N - N_m}{N+1}$ | Actual stages from $R_m$ & $N_m$ | Dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note for KTU Exams:** Always write $R_m$ as a *ratio* (dimensionless), not as a flow rate. Examiners specifically deduct marks if students confuse $R_m$ with $L_{min}$.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the **petrochemical, pharmaceutical, and cryogenic air separation industries**, the Underwood equation is used to size **crude oil distillation towers** (atmospheric and vacuum), **ethylene fractionators**, and **air separation units (ASUs)** for producing oxygen, nitrogen, and argon. Determining $R_m$ correctly is essential because:

1. **Capital Cost** of the column depends on the *actual* number of trays, which is determined from $R_m$ via the Gilliland correlation.
2. **Operating Cost** (reboiler steam, condenser cooling water) is proportional to $(R+1) \times D$.
3. **Process Safety:** Operating too close to $R_m$ causes the column to "pinch" and flood, especially during feed composition upsets.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Rectifying Operating Line at $R_m$

### Starting Point: Material Balance on Rectifying Section

For a section of the column between the top tray and tray $n+1$:

**Total Material Balance:**
$$V_{n+1} = L_n + D$$

**Component Material Balance (light component):**
$$V_{n+1} \, y_{n+1} = L_n \, x_n + D \, x_D$$

Dividing the second equation by the first and defining $R = L/D$, $V = L + D$:

$$\begin{aligned}
y_{n+1} &= \frac{L_n}{V_{n+1}} x_n + \frac{D}{V_{n+1}} x_D \\[6pt]
y_{n+1} &= \frac{L_n}{L_n + D} x_n + \frac{D}{L_n + D} x_D \\[6pt]
y_{n+1} &= \frac{R}{R+1} x_n + \frac{x_D}{R+1}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the standard rectifying operating line. At **minimum reflux**, this line passes through two special points:

1. **Point A:** $(x_D, x_D)$ — because $y_{n+1} = x_n = x_D$ at the top.
2. **Point B (Pinch):** $(x_q, y_q)$ — the intersection of the q-line and the equilibrium curve.

Since both points lie on the line:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Slope at } R_m &= \frac{x_D - y_q}{x_D - x_q}
\end{aligned}$$

But we also know the slope equals $R_m/(R_m + 1)$:

$$\frac{R_m}{R_m + 1} = \frac{x_D - y_q}{x_D - x_q}$$

Rearranging — multiplying both sides by $(R_m + 1)(x_D - x_q)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
R_m (x_D - x_q) &= (R_m + 1)(x_D - y_q) \\
R_m \, x_D - R_m \, x_q &= R_m \, x_D - R_m \, y_q + x_D - y_q \\
- R_m \, x_q &= - R_m \, y_q + x_D - y_q \\
R_m (y_q - x_q) &= x_D - y_q
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{R_m = \frac{x_D - y_q}{y_q - x_q}}$$

**This is the canonical KTU formula for binary minimum reflux ratio.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Underwood's Equation (Multicomponent)

### Assumptions
- Constant molar overflow (CMO)
- Constant relative volatilities
- The feed is a mixture of $c$ components with $z_i$ as feed compositions

### Step 1 — Define Underwood Variable $\theta$

Consider a hypothetical column operating at $R = R_m$ where the operating lines coincide with the equilibrium curve over an extended range. Under these conditions, the mass transfer driving force vanishes, and the component balance on the rectifying section reduces to:

$$V y_{n+1} - L x_n = D x_{D,i}$$

Using the equilibrium relation $y_i = \alpha_i x_i / \sum(\alpha_j x_j)$ at the pinch and combining with the q-line, Underwood showed that:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{c} \frac{q \, z_i}{\alpha_i - \theta} = 1$$

This is solved iteratively for $\theta$ where $\alpha_{HK} < \theta < \alpha_{LK}$.

### Step 2 — Calculate $R_m$ from $\theta$

$$R_m + 1 = \sum_{i=1}^{c} \frac{(\alpha_i - 1) \, x_{D,i}}{\alpha_i - \theta}$$

$$\boxed{R_m = \sum_{i=1}^{c} \frac{(\alpha_i - 1) \, x_{D,i}}{\alpha_i - \theta} - 1}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style

> **Problem:** A binary mixture of benzene-toluene is distilled at 1 atm. Feed: $x_F = 0.40$, $q = 1$ (saturated liquid). Distillate: $x_D = 0.95$, Bottoms: $x_W = 0.05$. The equilibrium curve gives $y^* = 0.62$ when $x = 0.40$. Calculate $R_m$.

### Solution

**Given:**
- $x_F = 0.40$, $x_D = 0.95$, $x_W = 0.05$
- $q = 1$ (saturated liquid)
- Equilibrium data at $x_F = 0.40$: $y_q = 0.62$

**Step 1 — Identify pinch point:**
Since $q = 1$, the q-line is a vertical line at $x = x_F = 0.40$. It intersects the equilibrium curve at $(0.40, 0.62)$, so $x_q = 0.40$ and $y_q = 0.62$.

**Step 2 — Apply minimum reflux formula:**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_m &= \frac{x_D - y_q}{y_q - x_q} \\
&= \frac{0.95 - 0.62}{0.62 - 0.40} \\
&= \frac{0.33}{0.22} \\
&= 1.50
\end{aligned}$$

**Answer:** $R_m = 1.50$ (dimensionless)

**Step 3 — Practical operating reflux:**
$$R_{opt} = 1.3 \times R_m = 1.3 \times 1.50 = 1.95$$

**Step 4 — Verification via operating line slope:**

Slope of rectifying line at $R_m$:

$$\frac{R_m}{R_m + 1} = \frac{1.50}{2.50} = 0.60$$

Check using two points $(x_D, x_D) = (0.95, 0.95)$ and $(x_q, y_q) = (0.40, 0.62)$:

$$\text{Slope} = \frac{0.95 - 0.62}{0.95 - 0.40} = \frac{0.33}{0.55} = 0.60 \;\; \checkmark$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When Does the Pinch Occur?

| Feed Condition ($q$) | q-Line Slope | Pinch Location on Equilibrium Curve |
|----------------------|--------------|-------------------------------------|
| Saturated liquid ($q = 1$) | Vertical (undefined slope) | At $x = x_F$ |
| Saturated vapor ($q = 0$) | Horizontal (slope = 0) | At $y = y_F$ |
| Subcooled liquid ($q > 1$) | Negative slope | Below $x = x_F$ |
| Superheated vapor ($q < 0$) | Positive slope, less than 1 | Above $y = y_F$ |
| Two-phase feed ($0 < q < 1$) | Negative slope (interpolated) | Between $(x_F, y_F)$ region |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** Always draw the q-line FIRST on the McCabe–Thiele diagram before plotting operating lines. The intersection with the equilibrium curve gives the pinch point.

---

## 3.5 Gilliland Correlation — From $R_m$ to Actual Stages

Once $R_m$ and $N_m$ (minimum stages at total reflux, from Fenske equation) are known, the **actual number of stages ($N$)** for a chosen operating reflux $R$ is found using the **Gilliland correlation**:

$$\begin{aligned}
X &= \frac{R - R_m}{R + 1} \\[6pt]
Y &= \frac{N - N_m}{N + 1} \\[6pt]
Y &= 0.75 \left(1 - X^{0.5668}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

This correlation is **empirical** and is used in nearly all KTU 14-mark design problems as the second step after determining $R_m$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 McCabe–Thiele Diagram at Minimum Reflux (Binary System)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Equilibrium Curve y vs x"] --> B{"Pinch Point at xq yq"}
    B --> C["Rectifying Operating Line at Rm: slope Rm divided by Rm plus 1"]
    B --> D["Q line: passes through xF zF with slope q divided by q minus 1"]
    B --> E["Stripping Operating Line: passes through xW xW and pinch point"]
    F["Distillate Point: xD xD on 45 degree diagonal"] --> C
    G["Bottoms Point: xW xW on 45 degree diagonal"] --> E
    H["45 degree Diagonal y equals x"] --> A
    C -.Touches.-> B
    E -.Touches.-> B
    D -.Intersects at pinch.-> B
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - The **45° diagonal** ($y=x$) is the locus where vapor and liquid compositions are equal (total reflux).
> - The **equilibrium curve** lies above the diagonal for $\alpha > 1$ systems.
> - At $R_m$, both operating lines pivot down and **touch** the equilibrium curve at the pinch point — leaving **zero driving force** for mass transfer at that tray. This is why infinite trays are needed.

---

## 4.2 Flow Architecture — Design Sequence Using $R_m$

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1["Step 1: Specify xF xD xW q"] --> S2["Step 2: Plot equilibrium curve"]
    S2 --> S3["Step 3: Draw q line through xF zF"]
    S3 --> S4{"Identify Pinch Point xq yq"}
    S4 --> S5["Step 4: Compute Rm using formula xD minus yq divided by yq minus xq"]
    S5 --> S6["Step 5: Compute Fenske Nm at total reflux"]
    S6 --> S7["Step 6: Choose Ropt as 1.2 to 1.5 times Rm"]
    S7 --> S8["Step 7: Apply Gilliland correlation to find N actual"]
    S8 --> S9["Step 8: Feed tray location by Kirkholmes or mass balance"]
    S9 --> S10["Step 9: Column diameter from Souders Brown"]
```

---

## 4.3 Limiting Conditions Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Total Reflux R equals infinity"] --> A1["Zero Distillate D equals 0"]
    A --> A2["Minimum Stages Nm from Fenske"]
    A --> A3["Operating Line equals 45 degree diagonal"]
    B["Minimum Reflux R equals Rm"] --> B1["Infinite Stages N equals infinity"]
    B --> B2["Operating Lines Pinch Equilibrium Curve"]
    B --> B3["Pinch Point at q line intersection"]
    C["Operating Reflux R greater than Rm"] --> C1["Finite Stages N between Nm and infinity"]
    C --> C2["Operating Lines Clear of Equilibrium Curve"]
    C --> C3["Practical Design Region"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Real columns operate in the region $1.2 R_m \le R \le 2 R_m$. The exact value is chosen by **economic optimization** balancing column height (capital) vs. reboiler duty (operating cost).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define minimum reflux ratio ($R_m$). Why is it called the lower bound of column operation?**

**Model Answer:**
The minimum reflux ratio is the lowest reflux ratio at which a specified separation between distillate ($x_D$) and bottoms ($x_W$) can be achieved in a distillation column. At this condition, the operating lines intersect the equilibrium curve at a "pinch point," requiring an infinite number of stages for the separation. It is called the lower bound because operating below $R_m$ makes the specified separation **thermodynamically impossible** regardless of how many trays are installed.

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is a pinch point in distillation? Under what condition does it occur at the q-line intersection?**

**Model Answer:**
A pinch point is a location on the McCabe–Thiele diagram where the operating line (rectifying or stripping) **touches or intersects** the equilibrium curve, creating zero driving force for mass transfer. At minimum reflux, this occurs at the intersection of the q-line and the equilibrium curve for most ideal binary systems when the feed is a saturated liquid, vapor, or two-phase mixture.

---

## PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

A binary distillation column operating at 1 atm separates a benzene–toluene mixture.

**Given:**
- Feed composition: $x_F = 0.50$ (mole fraction benzene)
- Distillate: $x_D = 0.95$
- Bottoms: $x_W = 0.08$
- Feed condition: Saturated liquid ($q = 1$)
- Equilibrium relation: $y^* = \dfrac{2.40 \, x}{1 + 1.40 \, x}$ (benzene-toluene at 1 atm)

**Part (a)** [7 Marks] — Determine the minimum reflux ratio $R_m$ and draw the operating line.

**Part (b)** [7 Marks] — If the actual operating reflux ratio is $R = 2.0$, and minimum stages at total reflux from Fenske's equation are $N_m = 7$, find the actual number of stages using the Gilliland correlation.

---

#### **Model Solution**

**Part (a): Minimum Reflux Ratio**

**Step 1 — Find pinch point on equilibrium curve at $x_q = x_F = 0.50$:**

$$y_q = \frac{2.40 \times 0.50}{1 + 1.40 \times 0.50} = \frac{1.20}{1.70} = 0.706$$

So the pinch point is $(x_q, y_q) = (0.50, 0.706)$.

**Step 2 — Apply minimum reflux formula:**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_m &= \frac{x_D - y_q}{y_q - x_q} \\
&= \frac{0.95 - 0.706}{0.706 - 0.50} \\
&= \frac{0.244}{0.206} \\
&= 1.184
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Operating line equation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
y_{n+1} &= \frac{R_m}{R_m + 1} x_n + \frac{x_D}{R_m + 1} \\
&= \frac{1.184}{2.184} x_n + \frac{0.95}{2.184} \\
&= 0.542 \, x_n + 0.435
\end{aligned}$$

The line passes through $(0.95, 0.95)$ and $(0.50, 0.706)$ — verified by substitution.

---

**Part (b): Actual Number of Stages using Gilliland**

**Step 1 — Compute parameter $X$:**

$$X = \frac{R - R_m}{R + 1} = \frac{2.0 - 1.184}{2.0 + 1} = \frac{0.816}{3.0} = 0.272$$

**Step 2 — Compute parameter $Y$ using Gilliland:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Y &= 0.75 \left(1 - X^{0.5668}\right) \\
&= 0.75 \left(1 - (0.272)^{0.5668}\right) \\
&= 0.75 \left(1 - 0.272^{0.5668}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

Computing $(0.272)^{0.5668}$:

$$0.272^{0.5668} = e^{0.5668 \times \ln(0.272)} = e^{0.5668 \times (-1.302)} = e^{-0.738} = 0.478$$

Therefore:

$$Y = 0.75 \times (1 - 0.478) = 0.75 \times 0.522 = 0.392$$

**Step 3 — Solve for actual stages $N$:**

$$Y = \frac{N - N_m}{N + 1} \implies 0.392 = \frac{N - 7}{N + 1}$$

$$0.392 (N + 1) = N - 7$$
$$0.392 N + 0.392 = N - 7$$
$$7 + 0.392 = N - 0.392 N$$
$$7.392 = 0.608 N$$
$$N = \frac{7.392}{0.608} = 12.16 \approx 13 \text{ stages}$$

**Answer:** Actual number of stages $\approx 13$ (rounded up).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

A multicomponent mixture has the following composition (light key = n-butane, heavy key = n-pentane):

| Component | $z_i$ | $\alpha_i$ | $x_{D,i}$ |
|-----------|-------|-----------|-----------|
| Propane ($C_3$) | 0.20 | 5.00 | 0.32 |
| n-Butane ($C_4$, LK) | 0.40 | 2.50 | 0.64 |
| n-Pentane ($C_5$, HK) | 0.30 | 1.00 | 0.04 |
| n-Hexane ($C_6$) | 0.10 | 0.40 | 0.00 |

Feed condition: $q = 1$ (saturated liquid).

**Part (a)** [7 Marks] — Find the Underwood root $\theta$.

**Part (b)** [7 Marks] — Compute $R_m$ and the optimum operating reflux.

---

#### **Model Solution**

**Part (a): Underwood Root**

We need to solve:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{4} \frac{q \, z_i}{\alpha_i - \theta} = 1.0$$

With $q = 1$:

$$\frac{0.20}{5.00 - \theta} + \frac{0.40}{2.50 - \theta} + \frac{0.30}{1.00 - \theta} + \frac{0.10}{0.40 - \theta} = 1.0$$

Constraint: $\alpha_{HK} < \theta < \alpha_{LK}$, i.e., $1.00 < \theta < 2.50$.

**Trial $\theta = 1.50$:**

$$\frac{0.20}{3.50} + \frac{0.40}{1.00} + \frac{0.30}{-0.50} + \frac{0.10}{-1.10}$$

$$= 0.0571 + 0.4000 - 0.6000 - 0.0909 = -0.2338$$

Negative — increase $\theta$.

**Trial $\theta = 2.00$:**

$$\frac{0.20}{3.00} + \frac{0.40}{0.50} + \frac{0.30}{-1.00} + \frac{0.10}{-1.60}$$

$$= 0.0667 + 0.8000 - 0.3000 - 0.0625 = 0.5042$$

Too high — $\theta$ is between 1.50 and 2.00.

**Trial $\theta = 1.80$:**

$$\frac{0.20}{3.20} + \frac{0.40}{0.70} + \frac{0.30}{-0.80} + \frac{0.10}{-1.40}$$

$$= 0.0625 + 0.5714 - 0.3750 - 0.0714 = 0.1875$$

Still high.

**Trial $\theta = 1.70$:**

$$\frac{0.20}{3.30} + \frac{0.40}{0.80} + \frac{0.30}{-0.70} + \frac{0.10}{-1.30}$$

$$= 0.0606 + 0.5000 - 0.4286 - 0.0769 = 0.0551$$

Close to 1.0 — still slightly low.

**Trial $\theta = 1.72$:**

$$\frac{0.20}{3.28} + \frac{0.40}{0.78} + \frac{0.30}{-0.72} + \frac{0.10}{-1.32}$$

$$= 0.0610 + 0.5128 - 0.4167 - 0.0758 = 0.0813$$

**Trial $\theta = 1.69$:**

$$\frac{0.20}{3.31} + \frac{0.40}{0.81} + \frac{0.30}{-0.69} + \frac{0.10}{-1.29}$$

$$= 0.0604 + 0.4938 - 0.4348 - 0.0775 = 0.0419$$

**Trial $\theta = 1.68$:**

$$\frac{0.20}{3.32} + \frac{0.40}{0.82} + \frac{0.30}{-0.68} + \frac{0.10}{-1.28}$$

$$= 0.0602 + 0.4878 - 0.4412 - 0.0781 = 0.0287$$

**Trial $\theta = 1.67$:**

$$= \frac{0.20}{3.33} + \frac{0.40}{0.83} + \frac{0.30}{-0.67} + \frac{0.10}{-1.27}$$

$$= 0.0601 + 0.4819 - 0.4478 - 0.0787 = 0.0155$$

**Final $\theta \approx 1.65$** (closer to 1):

$$\frac{0.20}{3.35} + \frac{0.40}{0.85} + \frac{0.30}{-0.65} + \frac{0.10}{-1.25}$$

$$= 0.0597 + 0.4706 - 0.4615 - 0.0800 = -0.0112$$

By linear interpolation between $\theta = 1.65$ and $\theta = 1.67$:

$$\theta = 1.65 + 0.02 \times \frac{0.0112}{0.0112 + 0.0155} = 1.65 + 0.02 \times 0.419 = 1.658$$

$$\boxed{\theta \approx 1.66}$$

---

**Part (b): Minimum Reflux Ratio**

$$R_m = \sum_{i=1}^{4} \frac{(\alpha_i - 1) \, x_{D,i}}{\alpha_i - \theta} - 1$$

With $\theta = 1.66$:

**Component 1 (Propane):**

$$\frac{(5.00 - 1)(0.32)}{5.00 - 1.66} = \frac{4.00 \times 0.32}{3.34} = \frac{1.28}{3.34} = 0.3832$$

**Component 2 (n-Butane):**

$$\frac{(2.50 - 1)(0.64)}{2.50 - 1.66} = \frac{1.50 \times 0.64}{0.84} = \frac{0.96}{0.84} = 1.1429$$

**Component 3 (n-Pentane):**

$$\frac{(1.00 - 1)(0.04)}{1.00 - 1.66} = \frac{0}{0.66} = 0$$

**Component 4 (n-Hexane):**

$$\frac{(0.40 - 1)(0)}{0.40 - 1.66} = 0 \;\; (\text{since } x_{D,4} = 0)$$

**Sum:**
$$R_m + 1 = 0.3832 + 1.1429 + 0 + 0 = 1.5261$$

$$R_m = 1.5261 - 1 = 0.526$$

$$\boxed{R_m \approx 0.53}$$

**Optimum operating reflux:**
$$R_{opt} = 1.3 \times R_m = 1.3 \times 0.53 = 0.69$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Confusion:** $R_m$ is dimensionless. Writing "$R_m = 1.5$ kmol/h" costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Wrong Pinch Location:** Using $(x_F, y_F)$ when equilibrium is at $x_q \neq x_F$ (e.g., $q \neq 1$) loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Underwood Sign Error:** The term $\dfrac{0.30}{1.00 - \theta}$ becomes negative for $\theta > 1.00$. Forgetting the negative sign in trial calculations is the #1 reason students get the wrong $\theta$.
> 4. **Gilliland Rounding:** Always round UP the number of stages (e.g., 12.16 → 13) since you cannot install a fractional tray.
> 5. **Forgetting the "-1":** Underwood's formula gives $R_m + 1$, not $R_m$. Students often forget to subtract 1.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Minimum Reflux — Key Takeaways:**

- ✅ $R_m$ is the **minimum reflux ratio** at which specified separation is *just barely* possible (with infinite trays).
- ✅ The corresponding **operating line** passes through $(x_D, x_D)$ and the **pinch point** $(x_q, y_q)$.
- ✅ For binary systems with q-line pinch: $\;R_m = \dfrac{x_D - y_q}{y_q - x_q}$.
- ✅ The **pinch point** is where the operating line and equilibrium curve meet — zero driving force for mass transfer.
- ✅ **McCabe–Thiele** uses geometric construction; **Underwood** uses analytical calculation for multicomponent.
- ✅ Underwood's equation is solved iteratively for $\theta$ in the range $\alpha_{HK} < \theta < \alpha_{LK}$.
- ✅ $R_m$ and $N_m$ (from **Fenske's equation** at total reflux) are the two **limiting design parameters**.
- ✅ The **Gilliland correlation** connects $R_m$, $N_m$, and the actual operating reflux $R$ to find the **actual number of stages**.
- ✅ **Practical design** uses $R_{opt} = (1.2 \text{ to } 1.5) \times R_m$.
- ✅ At $R_m$: rectifying operating line **intersects/touches** the equilibrium curve; at total reflux ($R = \infty$): operating line = 45° diagonal.
- ✅ Underwood's formula gives $R_m + 1$, so always **subtract 1** at the end.
- ✅ Common KTU exam value: for benzene-toluene with $x_F = 0.40$, $x_D = 0.95$, $q = 1$: $R_m \approx 1.50$.
- ✅ For **non-ideal/azeotropic systems**, the pinch may be at the azeotrope composition rather than the q-line intersection.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optimum Reflux
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT501 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS II
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Optimum Reflux

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **OPTIMUM REFLUX RATIO (R_opt):** The reflux ratio at which the **total annualized cost** of distillation operation (i.e., the sum of operating cost and fixed/capital cost) is **minimized**. Mathematically, it is the reflux ratio that satisfies the condition $\frac{d(\text{Total Cost})}{dR} = 0$.

In KTU board parlance, **optimum reflux ratio** is the design reflux ratio that an engineer chooses to minimize the *total cost* of operating a distillation column over its lifetime, balancing two competing expenses:

1. **Operating Cost (Variable / Utility Cost):** Steam to reboiler, cooling water to condenser, pump work. This **increases** with reflux ratio because higher $R$ means more liquid being vaporized and condensed per unit of distillate.
2. **Capital Cost (Fixed Cost):** Cost of column shell, trays/packing, reboiler, condenser, reflux pump. This **decreases** with reflux ratio because higher $R$ requires **fewer equilibrium stages** to achieve the desired separation.

The optimum lies between two theoretical extremes:

| Condition | Reflux Ratio | Number of Stages | Operating Cost | Capital Cost |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Total Reflux** | $R \to \infty$ | $N_{min}$ (minimum) | Infinite | Minimum (theoretical) |
| **Minimum Reflux** | $R_{min}$ | $N \to \infty$ | Minimum (theoretical) | Infinite |
| **OPTIMUM** | $R_{opt}$ | $N_{opt}$ | Moderate | Moderate |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Car Speed Paradox":**
> Imagine driving from Kochi to Bangalore.
> - If you drive at the **slowest possible speed** (analogous to $R = R_{min}$), you barely use fuel, but the trip takes an eternity — so the *time cost* (analogous to capital cost: more stages needed) is enormous.
> - If you drive at **maximum speed** ($R = R_{min}$... wait, reverse: at $R = \infty$), you arrive almost instantly (fewer stages), but you burn through a tank of petrol per hour — *fuel cost* (operating cost) is enormous.
> - The **optimum speed** (≈ 80–90 km/h on Indian highways) is the **optimum reflux ratio** — fast enough to minimize time/capital, slow enough to minimize fuel/utility cost.

In distillation language: **reflux is the "rework"** of partially purified liquid. The more you recycle, the better the separation per stage (fewer stages needed) — but you're paying to boil and condense that recycle liquid over and over.

### 1.3 Standard Industry Metrics (Bolded)

The following empirical benchmarks are universally tested in KTU ESE papers:

- **Typical industrial range:** $R_{opt} = (1.2 \text{ to } 1.5) \times R_{min}$
- **Most commonly used rule of thumb:** $R_{opt} \approx 1.3 \times R_{min}$
- **Shortcut for rough design:** $R_{opt} \approx 1.4 \, R_{min}$
- **Cost components:** Operating cost is typically $\approx 50\%$ of total annualized cost at $R_{opt}$ in petroleum/refinery columns.

### 1.4 The KTU 2024 Course Outcome Mapping

| CO Code | Course Outcome | Bloom's Level |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **CO2** | Apply mass transfer principles to design distillation columns | Apply / Analyze |
| **CO3** | Evaluate economic trade-offs in separation process design | Evaluate |

> [!NOTE]
> The topic "Optimum Reflux" specifically tests **CO3** (economic evaluation) in KTU 2024 ESE papers, with a typical mark weightage of **7 to 14 marks** when combined with Gilliland or Underwood sub-parts.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Competing Cost Curves (The Heart of the Concept)

The total annualized cost (TAC) of a distillation column is the sum of operating cost (OC) and capital/fixed cost (FC):

$$\text{TAC}(R) = \text{OC}(R) + \text{FC}(R)$$

**Step-by-step logic of each curve:**

1. **Operating Cost (OC) behavior with $R$:**
   - Higher $R \Rightarrow$ more liquid returned to column $\Rightarrow$ more vapor boil-up $V = D(R+1)$ in the reboiler.
   - Steam cost $\propto V \Rightarrow$ OC rises **almost linearly (or slightly super-linearly)** with $R$.
   - At $R = R_{min}$, vapor boil-up is minimum → OC is at its **theoretical minimum** (but practically, this is unattainable since it requires infinite stages).
   - At $R \to \infty$, OC also $\to \infty$.

2. **Fixed/Capital Cost (FC) behavior with $R$:**
   - Higher $R \Rightarrow$ easier separation per stage $\Rightarrow$ **fewer equilibrium stages** required.
   - Number of stages $N$ decreases hyperbolically as $R$ increases beyond $R_{min}$.
   - At $R = R_{min}$, $N \to \infty$ → FC $\to \infty$.
   - At $R \to \infty$, $N \to N_{min}$ → FC is at its **theoretical minimum** (but again, infinite OC).

3. **Intersection / Minimum of the Sum:**
   - The OC and FC curves, when added, give a **U-shaped TAC curve**.
   - The minimum of this U-curve is **$R_{opt}$**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Optimum" is Not "Minimum":** The term *minimum* refers to a theoretical lower bound (unattainable, requires infinite stages). The term *optimum* refers to the practical, cost-minimizing reflux ratio that an engineer actually designs for. KTU examiners **frequently** test this distinction — a 2-mark conceptual question.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\text{TAC}(R) = \text{OC}(R) + \text{FC}(R)$ | Total annualized cost definition | Currency/year |
| 2 | $V = D(R+1)$ | Vapor boil-up rate in reboiler | kmol/h |
| 3 | $L = R \cdot D$ | Liquid reflux rate | kmol/h |
| 4 | $R_{opt} \approx (1.2 \text{ to } 1.5) R_{min}$ | **Empirical optimum** (most tested) | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $\frac{d(\text{TAC})}{dR} = 0$ | Mathematical condition for optimum | — |
| 6 | $Y = \frac{N - N_{min}}{N + 1}$ | Gilliland abscissa | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $X = \frac{R - R_{min}}{R + 1}$ | Gilliland ordinate | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $Y = 0.75(1 - X^{0.5668})$ | **Gilliland correlation** (analytical form) | Approximation |
| 9 | $\sum_{i} \frac{z_i \, \alpha_i}{\alpha_i - \theta} = 1 - q$ | **Underwood equation** for $R_{min}$ | $\theta$ = Underwood root |
| 10 | $R_{min} + 1 = \sum_{i} \frac{\alpha_i \, x_{D,i}}{\alpha_i - \theta}$ | Underwood equation for $R_{min}$ | Multi-component |
| 11 | $R_{min} = \frac{x_{D,LK} - y^{*}_{LK}/K_{LK}}{y^{*}_{LK}/K_{LK} - x_{B,LK}}$ | **Underwood shortcut for binary** | At constant $\alpha$ |
| 12 | $\frac{dN}{dR} \cdot \text{Stage cost} + \frac{dV}{dR} \cdot \text{Steam cost} = 0$ | Rigorous optimum condition | — |
| 13 | $R_{opt} \cdot R_{min} \approx \text{constant}$ (Kirkhoff) | Kirkhoff empirical rule | Rare in exams |
| 14 | Operating cost $\propto V$ | Reboiler duty $\propto$ vapor rate | — |
| 15 | Fixed cost $\propto N$ (column height) | Capital cost per tray | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Formulas 4, 6, 7, 8, and 11 are the **TOP 5 high-yield formulas** for Module 2 ESE problems. Master them with unit clarity: $R$ is **dimensionless**, $N$ is a **count (integer or real)**, $V, L, D$ are **kmol/h**.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The concept of optimum reflux is the **economic backbone** of every refinery and petrochemical plant:

- **Refinery Crude Distillation Columns:** Engineers must choose between expensive tall columns (low $R$, many stages) versus wide columns with high reflux (high $R$, fewer stages). Optimum reflux governs the design.
- **Air Separation Units (ASU):** Cryogenic distillation of air uses $R_{opt}$ to balance electricity cost (compressors) versus column shell cost.
- **Aromatics Plants (Benzene/Toluene/Xylene):** Close-boiling mixtures require very high $R$ → the *optimum* is critical for cost.
- **Beer Distillation & Bioethanol:** Optimum reflux is used to balance fermentation capital cost vs. energy cost of distillation.

### 2.4 Key Assumptions in Standard KTU Problems

When KTU asks you to "find the optimum reflux ratio," they implicitly assume:

1. **Feed condition $q$** is fixed.
2. **Distillate and bottoms compositions** ($x_D, x_B$) are fixed (i.e., the separation specification is fixed).
3. **Column operates at atmospheric pressure** (unless otherwise stated).
4. **Constant molar overflow** is valid (i.e., sensible heat effects are negligible).
5. **Relative volatility $\alpha$** is constant across the column.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Optimum Condition $\frac{d(\text{TAC})}{dR} = 0$

**Starting point:** The total annualized cost function

$$\text{TAC}(R) = \text{OC}(R) + \text{FC}(R)$$

**Step 1 — Identify the operating cost function.**
The vapor boil-up rate in the reboiler is given by the total material balance:

$$V = D(R + 1)$$

where $D$ is the distillate flow rate (kmol/h). The reboiler duty is:

$$Q_R = V \cdot \lambda = D(R+1)\lambda$$

where $\lambda$ is the latent heat of vaporization (kJ/kmol). The annual operating cost is:

$$\text{OC} = C_{steam} \cdot Q_R \cdot 8760 = 8760 \, C_{steam} \, \lambda \, D (R+1)$$

**Step 2 — Identify the fixed cost function.**
The fixed cost depends primarily on the number of stages $N$, the column diameter (which depends on $V$), and the heat exchanger areas:

$$\text{FC} = C_{tray} \cdot N + C_{shell}(D, V) + C_{reboiler}(Q_R) + C_{condenser}(Q_C)$$

For a simplified analytical optimum, KTU problems often reduce this to:

$$\text{FC} \approx C_{tray} \cdot N(R)$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate the total cost with respect to $R$.**

$$\frac{d(\text{TAC})}{dR} = \frac{d(\text{OC})}{dR} + \frac{d(\text{FC})}{dR} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the cost expressions.**

$$8760 \, C_{steam} \, \lambda \, D + C_{tray} \cdot \frac{dN}{dR} = 0$$

**Step 5 — Recognize that $dN/dR < 0$** (number of stages decreases as reflux increases). Rearranging:

$$\frac{dN}{dR} = -\frac{8760 \, C_{steam} \, \lambda \, D}{C_{tray}}$$

This is the **rigorous graphical optimum** — you find the slope of the $N$ vs $R$ curve that equates the marginal saving in capital cost to the marginal increase in operating cost.

**Step 6 — In practice**, this requires:
- Plotting $N$ vs $R$ using Gilliland correlation for several values of $R$.
- Computing the cost at each $R$.
- Plotting TAC vs $R$ and reading off the minimum.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse the *marginal* cost condition with the *total* cost minimum. The rigorous optimum is found by **plotting**, not by setting OC = FC. The two are equal **only at the minimum**, but the slopes of the two curves are equal-and-opposite at that minimum.

### 3.2 Gilliland Correlation — Full Step-by-Step Usage

The **Gilliland correlation** is a graphical/analytical shortcut that relates the actual number of stages $N$ to the minimum number of stages $N_{min}$ and the minimum reflux $R_{min}$. It is **mandatory** for any KTU 14-mark question on optimum reflux design.

**Step 1 — Determine $N_{min}$** using the **Fenske equation**:

$$N_{min} = \frac{\ln\left[\left(\frac{x_{D,LK}}{x_{D,HK}}\right)\left(\frac{x_{B,HK}}{x_{B,LK}}\right)\right]}{\ln \alpha_{avg}}$$

**Step 2 — Determine $R_{min}$** using the **Underwood equation** (binary form):

$$R_{min} = \frac{x_{D,LK} - \frac{y_{F,LK}^{*}}{\alpha_{LK}}}{\frac{y_{F,LK}^{*}}{\alpha_{LK}} - x_{B,LK}}$$

where $y_{F,LK}^{*}$ is the vapor composition of the light key in equilibrium with the feed (from $q$-line intersection with the equilibrium curve).

**Step 3 — Choose a trial reflux ratio $R_{trial}$** in the range $1.2 \, R_{min}$ to $1.5 \, R_{min}$.

**Step 4 — Compute the Gilliland abscissa:**

$$X = \frac{R_{trial} - R_{min}}{R_{trial} + 1}$$

**Step 5 — Read the ordinate $Y$** from the Gilliland chart (or compute via the analytical fit):

$$Y = 0.75 \left(1 - X^{0.5668}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Back-calculate the actual number of stages:**

$$N = \frac{Y(N_{min} + 1) + N_{min}}{1 - Y}$$

**Step 7 — Apply the Murphree efficiency correction** (if given):

$$N_{actual} = \frac{N}{E_{Mv}}$$

**Step 8 — Compute the cost** at this $R_{trial}$:

$$\text{TAC} = C_{steam} \cdot 8760 \cdot D(R_{trial} + 1) \lambda + C_{tray} \cdot N_{actual}$$

**Step 9 — Repeat for 4–5 values of $R$**, plot TAC vs $R$, and read off the **optimum reflux ratio $R_{opt}$**.

### 3.3 Worked Analytical Example — Finding $R_{opt}$ (KTU-Style)

**Problem Statement (KTU University Exam - May 2024 style):**
A binary distillation column is to be designed to separate a benzene-toluene mixture. Given:
- $x_F = 0.50$, $x_D = 0.95$, $x_B = 0.05$ (mole fraction benzene)
- $\alpha = 2.47$ (constant)
- Feed is saturated liquid ($q = 1$)
- Tray cost = ₹50,000 per stage (annualized)
- Steam cost = ₹2,500 per kmol of distillate vapor
- $D = 100$ kmol/h
- Latent heat $\lambda = 30{,}000$ kJ/kmol
- 8000 operating hours/year

**Find $R_{opt}$.**

**Step 1 — Compute $N_{min}$ via Fenske:**

$$\begin{aligned}
N_{min} &= \frac{\ln\left[\left(\frac{0.95}{0.05}\right)\left(\frac{0.05}{0.95}\right)\right]}{\ln 2.47}
\end{aligned}$$

Wait — for a binary system, the light key is benzene (more volatile). Fenske for binary:

$$N_{min} = \frac{\ln\left[\frac{x_D (1 - x_B)}{x_B (1 - x_D)}\right]}{\ln \alpha_{LK/HK}}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
N_{min} &= \frac{\ln\left[\frac{0.95 \times 0.95}{0.05 \times 0.05}\right]}{\ln 2.47} \\
&= \frac{\ln(361)}{0.9046} \\
&= \frac{5.886}{0.9046} \\
&\approx 6.51 \text{ stages}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $R_{min}$ via Underwood (binary form, $q = 1$):**

For a saturated liquid feed, the $q$-line intersects the 45° line on the $y$-$x$ diagram. The equilibrium relation is $y = \frac{\alpha x}{1 + (\alpha - 1)x}$.

At the pinch point (feed intersection with equilibrium curve), for $q = 1$, the pinch lies on the 45° line: $y = x = x_F = 0.50$.

The Underwood formula (binary, $q = 1$):

$$R_{min} = \frac{1}{\alpha - 1}\left[\frac{x_D}{x_F} - \alpha \frac{1 - x_D}{1 - x_F}\right]$$

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{min} &= \frac{1}{2.47 - 1}\left[\frac{0.95}{0.50} - 2.47 \times \frac{0.05}{0.50}\right] \\
&= \frac{1}{1.47}\left[1.90 - 2.47 \times 0.10\right] \\
&= \frac{1}{1.47}\left[1.90 - 0.247\right] \\
&= \frac{1.653}{1.47} \\
&\approx 1.124
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Set up cost analysis at various $R$.**

For each trial $R$ in the range $1.2 R_{min} = 1.35$ to $1.5 R_{min} = 1.69$:

| $R$ | $X$ (Gilliland) | $Y$ (Gilliland) | $N$ | OC (₹/yr) | FC (₹/yr) | TAC (₹/yr) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1.30 | 0.087 | 0.402 | 11.6 | 8.45 Cr | 5.80 L | 8.51 Cr |
| 1.40 | 0.171 | 0.336 | 10.3 | 9.00 Cr | 5.15 L | 9.05 Cr |
| 1.50 | 0.244 | 0.275 | 9.4 | 9.55 Cr | 4.70 L | 9.60 Cr |
| 1.69 | 0.330 | 0.218 | 8.5 | 10.51 Cr | 4.25 L | 10.55 Cr |

**Note on the table above:** This is an illustrative representation. In an actual KTU 14-mark problem, students would perform the full cost calculation per Equation 8.4 and obtain a **U-curve** with a clear minimum.

**Step 4 — The optimum from the U-curve:**

Reading the TAC column, the **minimum is around $R \approx 1.2 \, R_{min}$** in this case. So:

$$R_{opt} \approx 1.2 \times 1.124 \approx 1.35$$

This is consistent with the empirical rule $R_{opt} = 1.2$ to $1.5 \times R_{min}$.

### 3.4 The Underwood Equation Derivation (Conceptual)

The Underwood equation is derived from the **pinch-point analysis** at minimum reflux:

At $R = R_{min}$, the operating line and the $q$-line intersect the equilibrium curve at a single point — the **pinch point**. This means an infinite number of stages are required to pass through this point. The Underwood equation arises from setting the sum of component feed flows equal to the sum of vapor and liquid flows at the pinch:

$$\sum_{i} \frac{z_i}{1 - \theta/\alpha_i} = 1 - q$$

Solving for the Underwood root $\theta$ (with $\alpha_{HK} < \theta < \alpha_{LK}$), and then:

$$R_{min} + 1 = \sum_{i} \frac{\alpha_i \, x_{D,i}}{\alpha_i - \theta}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The full multi-component Underwood derivation is **beyond Module 2 scope** but is a favorite 7-mark question in KTU ESE for high-scoring students.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Cost vs Reflux Ratio Trade-off

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Separation Spec xD, xB, xF, alpha, q] --> B[Compute Nmin using Fenske Equation]
    B --> C[Compute Rmin using Underwood Equation]
    C --> D{Select Trial R values in range 1.2 to 1.5 times Rmin}
    D --> E[Compute X parameter for Gilliland]
    E --> F[Read Y from Gilliland chart or analytical fit]
    F --> G[Back-calculate actual N from Y and Nmin]
    G --> H[Compute Operating Cost OC proportional to D times R plus 1]
    G --> I[Compute Fixed Cost FC proportional to N]
    H --> J[Compute Total Annualized Cost TAC equals OC plus FC]
    I --> J
    J --> K{All R values tested?}
    K -->|No| D
    K -->|Yes| L[Plot TAC versus R curve]
    L --> M[Identify Minimum of U-curve]
    M --> N[Ropt = Reflux ratio at minimum TAC]
    N --> O[End: Report Ropt and Nopt]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Optimum Reflux Design Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT PARAMETERS]
        IP1[Feed Composition xF]
        IP2[Distillate Spec xD]
        IP3[Bottoms Spec xB]
        IP4[Relative Volatility alpha]
        IP5[Feed Thermal Condition q]
        IP6[Cost Data: Steam, Trays, Utilities]
    end

    subgraph SHORTCUT[SHORTCUT CALCULATIONS]
        SC1[Fenske Module: Compute Nmin]
        SC2[Underwood Module: Compute Rmin]
        SC3[Gilliland Module: Compute N at trial R]
        SC4[Erbar-Maddox Cross-check]
    end

    subgraph ECONOMIC[ECONOMIC ANALYSIS]
        EA1[Operating Cost Calculator: OC versus R]
        EA2[Fixed Cost Calculator: FC versus N of R]
        EA3[TAC Aggregator: OC plus FC]
        EA4[U-Curve Plotter: TAC versus R]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[DESIGN OUTPUT]
        OP1[Optimum Reflux Ratio Ropt]
        OP2[Optimum Number of Stages Nopt]
        OP3[Column Diameter and Height]
        OP4[Reboiler and Condenser Duties]
    end

    INPUT --> SHORTCUT
    SHORTCUT --> ECONOMIC
    ECONOMIC --> OUTPUT
    SC3 --> EA1
    SC3 --> EA2
    EA1 --> EA3
    EA2 --> EA3
    EA3 --> EA4
    EA4 --> OP1
    EA4 --> OP2
```

### 4.3 The U-Shape Cost Curve (Textual Schematic)

```
   Cost (₹/yr)
      │
  TAC │           *  ← TAC curve (U-shape)
      │        *      *
      │      *          *
      │     *             *
      │    *                *
      │   *  FC curve         *  ← OC curve (rising)
      │  * (decreasing)         *
      │ *                        *
      │*___________________________*__________ R
      0   Rmin   Ropt         ∞
            ↑      ↑
            │      └─ Ropt (minimum TAC)
            └─ Rmin (infinite stages)
```

**Interpretation of the U-curve:**
- **Left limb (R near $R_{min}$):** Steep rise in FC (infinite stages) dominates → TAC is high.
- **Right limb (R near $\infty$):** Steep rise in OC (infinite steam) dominates → TAC is high.
- **Bottom of U:** $R_{opt}$ — the **economic sweet spot**.

### 4.4 The Optimization Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is detailed cost data available?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| P1[Use rigorous TAC plot method]
    Q1 -->|No| P2[Use empirical rule: Ropt = 1.2 to 1.5 times Rmin]
    P1 --> R1[Plot TAC vs R using Gilliland for N values]
    R1 --> R2[Read minimum of TAC curve]
    R2 --> S1[Report Ropt and Nopt]
    P2 --> S1
    S1 --> END[Design Complete]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
