## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Cold Reflux Mechanism at the Top Tray

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Subcooled Reflux L0 at T0] --> B[Top Tray Stage 1]
    C[Saturated Vapor V1 from below] --> B
    B --> D[Additional Vapor Condenses dV]
    D --> E[Releases Latent Heat to Warm L0]
    E --> F[Warm Reflux at Bubble Point Tb]
    F --> G[Internal Reflux L1 = L0 + dV]
    G --> H[Stage 2 Below]
    B --> I[Uncondensed Vapor V0 to Condenser]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
The vertical pipe on the left delivers the cold reflux; the right pipe carries vapor up. Inside the top tray, the two streams exchange both mass and energy. The "extra" condensation branch (`D → E → F`) is the key physical phenomenon that makes $L_{\text{int}} > L_0$.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Open Steam System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Saturated Steam S at Pressure P, yS = 0] --> B[Sparger at Column Base]
    B --> C[Steam Bubbles Up Through Liquid]
    C --> D[Stripping Action - Volatile A Transfers to Vapor Phase]
    D --> E[Rising Vapor Reaches Rectifying Section]
    E --> F[Condenser - Distillate D Recovered]
    F --> G[Reflux L0 Returned to Top]
    B --> H[Bottoms B Leaves as Saturated Liquid xB]
    H --> I[Non-volatile Component Recovered]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
The steam enters at the bottom and provides the energy and stripping action. The bottoms product is a saturated liquid (no reboiler), and the steam itself exits at the top as part of the overhead vapor (to be condensed and separated in a decanter if the system is partially miscible — typical for ethanol-water).

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Ponchon-Savarit Stepping Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Construct H-x,y Diagram] --> B[Step 2: Locate Distillate D, Bottoms B, Feed F]
    B --> C[Step 3: Locate Difference Points DR and DS]
    C --> D[Step 4: Draw Rectifying Operating Line D to DR]
    D --> E[Step 5: Draw Stripping Operating Line B to DS]
    E --> F[Step 6: Draw q-line Through F]
    F --> G[Step 7: Step Off Stages Using Tie Lines]
    G --> H[Step 8: Count Number of Theoretical Stages N]
    H --> I[Step 9: Locate Feed Stage - Transition from R to S Operating Line]
    I --> J[Step 10: Apply Murphree Efficiency to Get Actual Stages]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
The flow above is the complete rigorous design procedure. Each step is sequential and depends on the previous one. In the KTU exam, partial credit is awarded for each of the 10 steps, so students should describe the entire process in words, even if they cannot complete the drawing.

---

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Enthalpy-Composition Diagram Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Pure A x = 1] --> B[Saturated Liquid Curve hL x]
    B --> C[Two-Phase Region]
    C --> D[Saturated Vapor Curve HV y]
    D --> E[Pure B x = 0]
    B -. Tie Line at T1 .-> D
    C -. Feed Point F at zF, HF .-> C
    F[Steam Point S at 0, HSteam] -. Operating Line .-> B
    G[Distillate D at xD, HD] -. Operating Line .-> B
    H[Bottoms B at xB, hB] -. Operating Line .-> F
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Bottom curve: saturated liquid enthalpy $h^L$ vs $x$.
- Top curve: saturated vapor enthalpy $H^V$ vs $y$.
- Middle: two-phase region with horizontal tie lines connecting equilibrium $(x, y)$ pairs at constant $T$.
- Outside points (Steam, Distillate, Bottoms, Feed) are "single-phase" states used to draw operating lines.
- The Δ-points lie on the extrapolated operating lines.

> [!NOTE]
> **Important KTU drawing tip:** In exam sketches, you do *not* need to draw a perfectly to-scale H–x, y diagram. The examiner awards marks for: (1) correct shape of two curves, (2) correct location of the special points (F, D, B, S, Δ_R, Δ_S), (3) correct slope of operating lines, and (4) correct sequence of tie-line stepping. Even a rough sketch with these elements labeled will earn most of the marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]`
**Define cold reflux. Derive the relationship between the internal and external reflux flow rates for a distillation column receiving subcooled reflux.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 mark):** Cold (subcooled) reflux is the liquid condensate returned to the top of a distillation column at a temperature $T_0$ below the bubble point $T_b$ of that liquid at column pressure.

**Derivation (2 marks):** An energy balance on the top tray shows that a portion of the rising vapor condenses to supply the sensible heat required to warm the cold reflux to its bubble point:

$$L_{\text{int}} = L_0 \left[1 + \frac{C_{pL}(T_b - T_0)}{\lambda}\right]$$

The bracketed term is the *cold reflux correction factor* $K \geq 1$. For saturated reflux, $K = 1$ and $L_{\text{int}} = L_0$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often forget to define $C_{pL}$ (molar liquid heat capacity) and $\lambda$ (latent heat) before writing the formula. This loses ½ mark in KTU valuation. Always write the units: $C_{pL}$ in kJ/kmol·K, $\lambda$ in kJ/kmol, $T$ in K.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2]`
**What is open steam? When is it preferred over a conventional reboiler in distillation columns?**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 mark):** Open steam (or direct steam) is live saturated steam injected directly into the bottom of a distillation column, replacing the reboiler. The steam serves both as the heating medium (latent heat supply) and as the stripping agent.

**Preferred conditions (2 marks):** Open steam is preferred when:
1. The bottoms product is essentially water (e.g., ethanol-water, methanol-water, acetic acid-water systems) — adding a reboiler is wasteful.
2. The bottoms contains heat-sensitive materials that would degrade on a hot reboiler surface.
3. The bottoms is corrosive to reboiler metallurgy.
4. The desired bottoms is a non-volatile solute (e.g., a salt) and steam is used to strip a volatile solvent.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Tip:** On the H–x, y diagram, an open-steam operating line passes through the **steam point** $(x = 0, y = 0)$, whereas a reboiler-based operating line passes through the **bottoms point** $(x_B, y_B^*)$. Many students confuse the two and lose full marks.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the construction of the **enthalpy–composition (H–x, y) diagram** for a binary system. List the various features (curves, regions, tie lines) and explain the significance of the *tie line* and the *lever-arm rule*.

**(b) [7 marks]** A distillation column is to be designed for the separation of a benzene–toluene mixture using the Ponchon-Savarit method. The column has a total condenser with saturated liquid reflux at the bubble point, a partial reboiler, and a saturated liquid feed. The following data are given:

- $F = 100$ kmol/h, $z_F = 0.50$
- $x_D = 0.95$, $x_B = 0.05$
- Reflux ratio $R = L_0/D = 1.5$
- $D = 50$ kmol/h, $B = 50$ kmol/h
- Latent heat of benzene $\lambda_B = 30{,}800$ kJ/kmol
- Latent heat of toluene $\lambda_T = 33{,}000$ kJ/kmol
- $C_{pL} = 140$ kJ/kmol·K (assumed constant)
- Relative volatility $\alpha = 2.47$

Estimate:
1. The number of theoretical stages by McCabe–Thiele.
2. The location of the q-line.
3. The internal reflux if the reflux is subcooled to $T_0 = 50$ °C, with $T_b = 80$ °C.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks, Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the **Ponchon-Savarit** procedure for determining the number of theoretical stages in a binary distillation column. Clearly explain the role of:
- The difference point (Δ-point)
- The q-line
- The tie-line construction

**(b) [7 marks]** An ethanol–water column uses **open steam** at the bottom. The following data are given:

- $F = 200$ kmol/h, $z_F = 0.10$ (ethanol mole fraction)
- $x_D = 0.85$, $x_B = 0.001$ (essentially pure water)
- $D = 22.5$ kmol/h, $B = 177.5$ kmol/h
- Saturated liquid feed at its bubble point
- Equilibrium relationship near the top: $y^* \approx 0.85$ at $x = 0.50$
- Open steam is pure (no ethanol) at the column pressure

Calculate:
1. The minimum open-steam flow rate $S_{\min}$.
2. The actual open-steam flow rate if the operating $S = 1.5 \, S_{\min}$.
3. The mass of bottoms product per hour (molecular weight of water = 18).

---

### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — H–x, y Diagram Construction (7 marks):**

1. **Axes (1 mark):** $x$ or $y$ (mole fraction of more volatile component) on the abscissa; molar enthalpy $H$ (kJ/kmol) on the ordinate.
2. **Saturated liquid curve (1 mark):** $H^L$ vs $x$, calculated from $H^L = (1-x) H_B^L + x H_A^L + \Delta H_{\text{mix}}^L$ (typically $\Delta H_{\text{mix}}^L \approx 0$ for ideal systems).
3. **Saturated vapor curve (1 mark):** $H^V$ vs $y$, calculated from $H^V = (1-y) H_B^V + y H_A^V + \Delta H_{\text{mix}}^V$.
4. **Tie line (1.5 marks):** A horizontal line at constant temperature $T$ connecting a point on the lower curve $(x, H^L)$ to a point on the upper curve $(y, H^V)$. Represents vapor–liquid equilibrium at that $T$.
5. **Lever-arm rule (1.5 marks):** For a mixture of overall composition $z$ and enthalpy $H$ lying on a tie line, the ratio of liquid to vapor moles equals the inverse ratio of the corresponding tie-line segment lengths:
$$\frac{\text{moles of liquid}}{\text{moles of vapor}} = \frac{H^V - H}{H - H^L}$$
6. **Other features (1 mark):** Single-phase regions above the upper curve and below the lower curve; critical point; azeotrope (if present).

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Calculate L and V from reflux ratio.**
$R = L_0/D = 1.5 \Rightarrow L_0 = 1.5 \times 50 = 75$ kmol/h.
For a total condenser, $V = L_0 + D = 75 + 50 = 125$ kmol/h.

**Step 2 — Equilibrium data for benzene–toluene at 1 atm.**
Using $y = \dfrac{\alpha x}{1 + (\alpha-1)x} = \dfrac{2.47 x}{1 + 1.47 x}$:

| $x$ | 0.05 | 0.10 | 0.20 | 0.30 | 0.40 | 0.50 | 0.60 | 0.70 | 0.80 | 0.90 | 0.95 |
|-----|------|------|------|------|------|------|------|------|------|------|------|
| $y$ | 0.114 | 0.210 | 0.371 | 0.500 | 0.595 | 0.712 | 0.760 | 0.829 | 0.890 | 0.946 | 0.972 |

**Step 3 — McCabe–Thiele stepping (3 marks).**

Rectifying operating line:

$$y = \frac{R}{R+1} x + \frac{x_D}{R+1} = \frac{1.5}{2.5} x + \frac{0.95}{2.5} = 0.6 x + 0.38$$

q-line (saturated liquid feed, $q = 1$): vertical line at $x = z_F = 0.50$.

Stripping operating line — first find intersection with q-line at $x = 0.50$:
$y_{\text{intersect}} = 0.6(0.50) + 0.38 = 0.68$.

Slope of stripping line:

$$\frac{\bar{L}}{\bar{V}} = \frac{L_0 + q F}{V_0 + (q-1) F} = \frac{75 + 100}{125 + 0} = \frac{175}{125} = 1.40$$

But the line passes through $(x_B, x_B) = (0.05, 0.05)$, so:

$$y - 0.05 = 1.40(x - 0.05)$$
$$y = 1.40 x - 0.02$$

Verify at $x = 0.50$: $y = 1.40(0.50) - 0.02 = 0.70 - 0.02 = 0.68$. ✓

Stepping from $(x_D, x_D) = (0.95, 0.95)$ to $(x_B, x_B) = (0.05, 0.05)$ using the standard procedure yields approximately:

- 7 stages from $x_D$ down to the q-line crossing.
- 5 stages from the q-line crossing to $x_B$.
- **Total: 12 theoretical stages** (including the reboiler as a stage).

**Step 4 — Internal reflux (1 mark).**
$$\Delta T = 80 - 50 = 30 \text{ K}, \quad K = 1 + \frac{140 \times 30}{\lambda_{\text{avg}}}$$
Average $\lambda = (30{,}800 + 33{,}000)/2 = 31{,}900$ kJ/kmol.
$$K = 1 + \frac{4200}{31{,}900} = 1 + 0.1317 = 1.1317$$
$$L_{\text{int}} = 75 \times 1.1317 = 84.88 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 5 — Final answer summary (1 mark).**
- $N_{\text{theoretical}} = 12$ (McCabe–Thiele, with reboiler)
- Feed stage = 7th from the top
- $L_{\text{int}} = 84.9$ kmol/h (vs. external $L_0 = 75$ kmol/h)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not** confuse external and internal reflux — write both values and the correction factor clearly.
> 2. **Do not** omit the reboiler as a theoretical stage — it counts as one equilibrium stage.
> 3. **Do not** forget to convert the average latent heat correctly — use the arithmetic mean only if specified; if Raoult's-law latent heats are given, the mean is acceptable.
> 4. **Do not** include the partial condenser as a stage if the problem says *total* condenser.

---

### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Ponchon–Savarit Procedure (7 marks):**

1. **Step 1 (1 mark):** Construct the H–x, y diagram at the operating pressure with saturated liquid and vapor curves and tie lines.
2. **Step 2 (1 mark):** Locate the feed point $F(z_F, H_F)$ on the diagram. For a saturated liquid feed, $F$ is on the lower (liquid) curve; for a saturated vapor feed, on the upper curve. For subcooled liquid, $F$ is below the lower curve.
3. **Step 3 (1 mark):** Locate the rectifying-section difference point $\Delta_R$. For a total condenser with saturated liquid reflux, $\Delta_R$ lies on the saturated vapor curve at $x = x_D$, projected upward to balance heat duties. For a subcooled reflux, $\Delta_R$ is *above* the upper curve.
4. **Step 4 (1 mark):** Locate the stripping-section difference point $\Delta_S$. For a partial reboiler, $\Delta_S$ lies on the saturated liquid curve at $x = x_B$, projected downward to balance duties. For an **open steam** system, $\Delta_S$ coincides with the steam point $(0, 0, H_S^V)$.
5. **Step 5 (1 mark):** Draw the rectifying operating line as a straight line from $D(x_D, H_D^V)$ through $\Delta_R$ on the H–x, y diagram.
6. **Step 6 (1 mark):** Draw the stripping operating line as a straight line from $B(x_B, H_B^L)$ through $\Delta_S$ (or the steam point, for open steam).
7. **Step 7 (1 mark):** Step off theoretical stages starting at $D$ using tie lines alternating with operating-line jumps. The transition from rectifying to stripping operating line gives the feed stage location.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Calculate $S_{\min}$ (3 marks).**
At minimum steam, the operating line from the steam point $(0, 0)$ touches the equilibrium curve tangentially. For a dilute aqueous system at the bottom, the equilibrium curve near $x_B = 0.001$ is approximately linear: $y^* \approx m x$ with $m \approx 13$ (from $y^* = 0.013$ at $x = 0.001$, as given).

The pinch point occurs where the operating line from $(0, 0)$ is tangent to the equilibrium curve. For this highly diluted case, the pinch effectively occurs near the bottom, and:

$$S_{\min} = \frac{B \, x_B}{y_B^* - 0} = \frac{177.5 \times 0.001}{0.013} = \frac{0.1775}{0.013} = 13.65 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 2 — Actual steam rate (2 marks).**
$$S_{\text{actual}} = 1.5 \, S_{\min} = 1.5 \times 13.65 = 20.48 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 3 — Mass of bottoms per hour (2 marks).**
Bottoms is essentially water with a tiny amount of ethanol. Molecular weight ≈ 18 kg/kmol.
$$\text{Mass of bottoms} = 177.5 \times 18 = 3195 \text{ kg/h} \approx 3.2 \text{ tonnes/h}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout for Question B:**
> 1. **Steam-point identification:** In open-steam problems, the operating line in the stripping section passes through the **steam point** $(0, 0)$, *not* the bottoms point on the saturated liquid curve.
> 2. **Minimum-steam formula:** Use $S_{\min} = B x_B / y^*$ (not $B / y^*$). Mixing these up is a common 1-mark error.
> 3. **Steam-mole balance:** Verify that total moles are conserved: $F + S = D + B$? With $F = 200$, $S = 20.48$, $D = 22.5$, $B = 177.5$ → $200 + 20.48 = 220.48 \neq 22.5 + 177.5 = 200$. The discrepancy comes from assuming the bottoms + steam are also the sources of mass; the correct consistency check uses $D = F z_F / x_D = 200 \times 0.10 / 0.85 = 23.5$ kmol/h. If the problem gives $D = 22.5$, accept it as given (small inconsistency may be due to rounding of $x_D$ or $z_F$).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (print before the exam):**

- ☐ **Cold reflux** = subcooled liquid entering the top of the column; internal reflux > external reflux.
- ☐ **Correction factor:** $K = 1 + \dfrac{C_{pL}\Delta T}{\lambda}$, with $\Delta T = T_b - T_0$.
- ☐ **Open steam** = saturated steam injected directly at the base; replaces the reboiler.
- ☐ **Steam point on H–x,y:** $(0, 0, H_S^V)$ — pure steam, no volatile component.
- ☐ **Use open steam when:** bottoms is water, heat-sensitive, or corrosive.
- ☐ **Ponchon–Savarit method** = rigorous graphical method using H–x, y diagram.
- ☐ **No constant-molal-overflow assumption** — handles variable latent heats and sensible heats.
- ☐ **H–x, y diagram has 2 curves:** lower (saturated liquid) and upper (saturated vapor); two-phase region between.
- ☐ **Tie lines** are horizontal (at constant T), connecting equilibrium $(x, y)$ pairs.
- ☐ **Lever-arm rule:** $\dfrac{n_L}{n_V} = \dfrac{H^V - H_{\text{mix}}}{H_{\text{mix}} - H^L}$
- ☐ **Feed-point location:** $H_F = q H_F^L + (1-q) H_F^V$; on the q-line between $H^L$ and $H^V$.
- ☐ **Difference point (Δ):** every operating line in a section passes through a single Δ-point.
- ☐ **Rectifying Δ_R:** on the saturated vapor curve at $x_D$ (for saturated reflux), above for subcooled reflux.
- ☐ **Stripping Δ_S:** on the saturated liquid curve at $x_B$ (reboiler), or at the steam point (open steam).
- ☐ **Stepping procedure:** start at $D$, draw tie line to vapor curve, jump to operating line, repeat.
- ☐ **Feed stage = transition** from rectifying to stripping operating line.
- ☐ **Number of theoretical stages** = number of tie-line steps between $D$ and $B$.
- ☐ **Actual stages** = theoretical stages / Murphree efficiency $E_{MV}$.
- ☐ **KTU keyword to recognize "Ponchon-Savarit":** "enthalpy–composition," "rigorous," "non-constant molal overflow," "tie line," "Δ-point."
- ☐ **KTU keyword to recognize "open steam":** "direct steam," "sparger," "no reboiler," "steam point."
- ☐ **KTU keyword to recognize "cold reflux":** "subcooled," "$T_0 < T_b$," "internal reflux," "extra condensation."

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip (Topper's Strategy):** When the KTU question says "design a distillation column graphically" without specifying the method, the *default expectation* in Module 2 is the **Ponchon–Savarit method** (because the syllabus lists it under Module 2 explicitly). Always mention the H–x, y diagram, the Δ-point, and the tie-line stepping in your answer. Just writing a McCabe–Thiele answer on a Ponchon–Savarit question will fetch you **at most 4 out of 14 marks**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of Net Flow and Difference Point
Ponchon-Savarit Method - Material and Enthalpy Balance
Number of Plates
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT501 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS II
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Number of Plates

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In binary distillation column design, the **Number of Theoretical Plates (NTP)** or **Number of Ideal Stages** refers to the count of hypothetical, perfectly efficient trays (or contact stages) at which vapor and liquid leave in thermodynamic equilibrium with each other. It represents the minimum number of stages theoretically required to achieve a specified separation between a more volatile component (MVC) and a less volatile component (LVC) under given operating conditions of reflux ratio ($R$), feed composition ($x_F$), distillate composition ($x_D$), and bottoms composition ($x_B$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** The "Number of Plates" topic under Module 2 specifically addresses determination of NTP by graphical (McCabe–Thiele) and analytical (Lewis–Sorel / Smoker) procedures, including feed plate location, effect of feed condition (q-line), total reflux (minimum plates), and minimum reflux (infinite plates) limits.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Multi-Story Sieve:**
> Imagine a tall apartment building where, on every floor, dirty water (liquid) and steam (vapor) meet in a sieve. The sieve traps the "dirt" (less volatile component) and lets the clean steam (more volatile component) rise. Each floor is a **theoretical plate** — it does a perfect job of separating the two. The question "How many plates do I need?" simply asks: *how many such perfect floors must the building have so that the steam leaving the top is 95% pure and the water leaving the bottom is 95% impure?*

In real columns, plates are not 100% efficient, so the **Actual Number of Plates (NAP)** is always greater than the NTP, corrected using the **Murphree vapor efficiency** ($E_{Mv}$) or **Overall Column Efficiency** ($E_o$).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

- **Standard Operating Pressure:** Atmospheric ($P = 101.325$ kPa) unless otherwise stated; vacuum used for heat-sensitive mixtures.
- **Relative Volatility ($\alpha$):** Typically in the range **1.1 ≤ α ≤ 20** for most industrial binaries; for ideal binaries, $\alpha \approx P_1^{sat}/P_2^{sat}$.
- **Reflux Ratio ($R$):** $1.2 R_{min} \le R \le 1.5 R_{min}$ as the **KTU-recommended economic optimum**.
- **Feed Quality (q):** Saturated liquid (q=1), saturated vapor (q=0), sub-cooled liquid (q>1), superheated vapor (q<0), two-phase (0<q<1).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Why and How

The determination of NTP is rooted in the simultaneous solution of two fundamental relationships applied stage-by-stage:

1. **Phase Equilibrium Relationship (PER):** $y = f(x)$ — relates the vapor and liquid compositions leaving an ideal stage.
2. **Operating Line Relationship (OLR):** $y = mx + c$ — derived from component material balances over a section of the column (rectifying, stripping, or feed).

The **stage-by-stage calculation** proceeds alternately:
- Start at a known terminal composition (e.g., $x_D$ at the top).
- Apply the OLR to find the vapor composition in equilibrium with the next stage's liquid ($y_{n+1}$).
- Apply the PER to find the liquid composition leaving the next stage ($x_n$).
- Repeat until the desired bottom composition is reached.

### 2.2 Key Structural Steps

**Step 1 — Fix the operating conditions:**
Specify $x_F$, $x_D$, $x_B$, $R$ (or $R/R_{min}$), $q$, and pressure. Obtain the equilibrium curve from VLE data or relative volatility.

**Step 2 — Construct the operating lines:**

> The **Rectifying Section** (above the feed) material balance yields:
> $$ V \cdot y_{n+1} = L \cdot x_n + D \cdot x_D $$
> Where $V = L + D = D(R+1)$ and $L = R \cdot D$.

> The **Stripping Section** (below the feed) material balance yields:
> $$ \bar{L} \cdot x_n = \bar{V} \cdot y_{n+1} + W \cdot x_B $$
> Where $\bar{L} = L + qF$ and $\bar{V} = V - (1-q)F$.

**Step 3 — Locate the q-line intersection:**
The feed condition line passes through $(x_F, x_F)$ on the 45° line with slope $q/(q-1)$.

**Step 4 — Step off stages graphically (McCabe–Thiele):**
Draw horizontal steps from operating line to equilibrium curve and vertical steps back. Count crossings on the equilibrium curve.

**Step 5 — Switch operating line at feed stage:**
Use the rectifying line above the intersection and the stripping line below.

**Step 6 — Apply efficiency correction (if asked for actual plates):**
$$ NAP = \frac{NTP}{E_o} \quad \text{or} \quad NAP = \frac{NTP}{\ln[1 + E_{Mv}(\lambda - 1)] / \ln \lambda} $$
where $\lambda = m \cdot (V/L)$ is the stripping factor.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Quick-Reference Table — All Key Equations for "Number of Plates"**

| # | Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Variables & Units | Section / Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Rectifying Operating Line | $y = \dfrac{R}{R+1}x + \dfrac{x_D}{R+1}$ | $R = L/D$ (dimensionless) | Above feed plate |
| 2 | Stripping Operating Line | $y = \dfrac{\bar{L}}{\bar{V}}x - \dfrac{W x_B}{\bar{V}}$ | $\bar{L}$, $\bar{V}$ in kmol/h | Below feed plate |
| 3 | q-line Equation | $y = \dfrac{q}{q-1}x - \dfrac{x_F}{q-1}$ | $q$ = feed thermal condition | Feed plate location |
| 4 | Equilibrium Relation (constant $\alpha$) | $y = \dfrac{\alpha x}{1+(\alpha-1)x}$ | $\alpha = K_1/K_2$ | Ideal binary VLE |
| 5 | Minimum Reflux Ratio | $R_{min} = \dfrac{x_D - y^*_F}{y^*_F - x_F}$ | $y^*_F$ from q-line ∩ equilibrium | Pinch point at feed |
| 6 | Minimum Reflux (saturated liquid, q=1) | $R_{min} = \dfrac{1}{(\alpha - 1)}\left[\dfrac{x_D}{x_F} - \alpha\dfrac{1-x_D}{1-x_F}\right]$ | Special analytical form | Pinch at feed intersection |
| 7 | Fenske Equation (Minimum Plates) | $N_{min} = \dfrac{\ln\left[\left(\dfrac{x_D}{1-x_D}\right)\left(\dfrac{1-x_B}{x_B}\right)\right]}{\ln \alpha_{avg}}$ | Total reflux condition | Top-to-bottom stepping |
| 8 | Gilliland Correlation (approximate) | $Y = 0.75(1-X^{0.5668})$, $X = \dfrac{R - R_{min}}{R+1}$ | $Y = \dfrac{N - N_{min}}{N+1}$ | Quick NTP estimation |
| 9 | Erbar-Maddox Correlation | Graphical: $N_{min}$ vs $(R/R_{min})$ | More accurate than Gilliland | Design verification |
| 10 | Murphree Plate Efficiency | $E_{Mv} = \dfrac{y_n - y_{n+1}}{y^*_n - y_{n+1}}$ | $y^*_n$ = vapor in eq. with $x_n$ | Actual plates |
| 11 | Overall Column Efficiency | $E_o = \dfrac{NTP}{NAP}$ | Dimensionless | NAP calculation |
| 12 | Feed Stage Location (Kirkhoff) | $\dfrac{N_R}{N_S} = \dfrac{\log\left[\dfrac{x_D}{x_B}\cdot\dfrac{(1-x_B)}{(1-x_D)}\right]_{R=0}}{\log\left[\dfrac{x_D}{x_B}\cdot\dfrac{(1-x_B)}{(1-x_D)}\right]_{R=\infty}}$ | $N_R$ rectifying, $N_S$ stripping | Optimal feed plate |
| 13 | Smoker's Equation | Analytical NTP for constant $\alpha$ and $m$ | Complex root-finding | Non-graphical solution |
| 14 | Kremser Equation (absorbers) | $\dfrac{x_N - y_{N+1}/m}{x_0 - y_{N+1}/m} = \dfrac{1}{A^N+1}\cdot\ldots$ | $A = L/(mV)$ | Lean gas absorption |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Relevance:** Number-of-plates calculations are the **backbone of every distillation column design** in petroleum refining (crude oil fractionators), cryogenic air separation (oxygen/nitrogen/argon), ethanol-water separation, pharmaceutical solvent recovery, and natural gas processing (deethanizers, depropanizers). The NTP directly governs the **column height** (HETP × NTP for packed columns, or tray spacing × NAP for trayed columns), and hence the **capital cost (CAPEX)** of the unit.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Rectifying Section Operating Line

Consider the envelope drawn around the top section of the column from the top down to a stage $n$ (above the feed). The total and component material balances are:

**Total balance:** $V_{n+1} = L_n + D$

**Component balance (for MVC):** $V_{n+1} y_{n+1} = L_n x_n + D x_D$

Assuming constant molar overflow (CMO) — the standard KTU assumption — $L_n = L$ and $V_{n+1} = V$ are constant. Substituting $L = R \cdot D$ and $V = D(R+1)$:

$$ D(R+1) y_{n+1} = R D x_n + D x_D $$

Dividing throughout by $D(R+1)$:

$$ y_{n+1} = \frac{R}{R+1} x_n + \frac{x_D}{R+1} $$

This is the **rectifying operating line** with:
- **Slope:** $m_R = \dfrac{R}{R+1}$
- **y-intercept:** $c_R = \dfrac{x_D}{R+1}$

It passes through the points $(x_D, x_D)$ and $\left(x_D, \dfrac{x_D}{R+1}\right)$ when $x=0$ on the line, and is the locus of all $(x_n, y_{n+1})$ pairs in the rectifying section.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stripping Section Operating Line

Envelope drawn from the bottom up to a stage $m$ (below the feed). Total and component balances:

**Total balance:** $\bar{L}_m = \bar{V}_{m+1} + W$

**Component balance:** $\bar{L}_m x_m = \bar{V}_{m+1} y_{m+1} + W x_B$

Under CMO: $\bar{L} = L + qF$ and $\bar{V} = V - (1-q)F$. Rearranging:

$$ y_{m+1} = \frac{\bar{L}}{\bar{V}} x_m - \frac{W x_B}{\bar{V}} $$

This is the **stripping operating line** with:
- **Slope:** $m_S = \dfrac{\bar{L}}{\bar{V}}$
- **y-intercept:** $c_S = -\dfrac{W x_B}{\bar{V}}$

The line passes through $(x_B, x_B)$ on the 45° diagonal.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — q-line (Feed Condition Line)

A material + energy balance around the feed plate gives the locus of intersection of the rectifying and stripping operating lines:

$$ y = \frac{q}{q-1} x - \frac{x_F}{q-1} $$

> **Derivation Sketch:** For 1 mol of feed, the fraction that is liquid is $q$. The mass balance across the feed point:
> $y = \dfrac{q}{q-1} x - \dfrac{z_F}{q-1}$
> All points $(x_F, x_F)$ on the 45° line satisfy this — confirming the q-line passes through the feed composition point.

**Special cases:**

| Feed Condition | q | q-line slope | q-line passes through |
|---|---|---|---|
| Saturated liquid | 1 | ∞ (vertical) | $(x_F, x_F)$ |
| Saturated vapor | 0 | 0 (horizontal) | $(x_F, x_F)$ |
| Sub-cooled liquid | > 1 | positive finite | $(x_F, x_F)$ |
| Superheated vapor | < 0 | positive finite | $(x_F, x_F)$ |
| Two-phase mixture (q=0.5) | 0.5 | $-1$ | $(x_F, x_F)$ |

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Fenske Equation for Minimum Number of Plates ($N_{min}$)

At **total reflux** ($R = \infty$, $D = 0$, $L = V$), no products are withdrawn, and the operating lines for both sections collapse onto the 45° diagonal: $y_{n+1} = x_n$.

For an ideal binary with constant relative volatility, the equilibrium relation is:

$$ \frac{y_n}{1-y_n} = \alpha \frac{x_n}{1-x_n} $$

Since $y_{n+1} = x_n$ at total reflux:

$$ \frac{x_n}{1-x_n} = \alpha \frac{x_{n-1}}{1-x_{n-1}} $$

**Stage 1 → 2:** $\dfrac{y_1}{1-y_1} = \alpha \dfrac{x_1}{1-x_1}$, and $x_1 = y_2$.

Applying this recursively from the top ($x_D$) down to the bottom ($x_B$) over $N_{min}$ stages:

$$ \frac{x_D}{1-x_D} = \alpha^{N_{min}} \frac{x_B}{1-x_B} $$

Solving for $N_{min}$:

$$ N_{min} = \frac{\ln\left[\left(\dfrac{x_D}{1-x_D}\right)\left(\dfrac{1-x_B}{x_B}\right)\right]}{\ln \alpha_{avg}} $$

> This is the **Fenske Equation** — exact for constant $\alpha$; uses geometric mean $\alpha_{avg} = \sqrt{\alpha_{top} \cdot \alpha_{bottom}}$ for non-constant systems.

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Minimum Reflux Ratio ($R_{min}$)

As $R \to R_{min}$, the operating lines pivot such that they intersect the equilibrium curve at a **pinch point**. This requires an infinite number of stages. For the common case where the pinch lies at the intersection of the q-line and the equilibrium curve:

The rectifying line at $R_{min}$ passes through $(x_D, x_D)$ and $(x_F, y_F^*)$, where $y_F^*$ is the vapor composition in equilibrium with $x_F$ as read from the q-line intersection. Its slope equals $R_{min}/(R_{min}+1)$:

$$ \frac{R_{min}}{R_{min}+1} = \frac{x_D - y_F^*}{x_D - x_F} $$

Solving:

$$ R_{min} = \frac{x_D - y_F^*}{y_F^* - x_F} $$

**Special case — saturated liquid feed (q = 1) with constant $\alpha$:**

Substituting $y_F^* = \dfrac{\alpha x_F}{1+(\alpha-1)x_F}$:

$$ R_{min} = \frac{1}{\alpha - 1}\left[\frac{x_D}{x_F} - \alpha \frac{1-x_D}{1-x_F}\right] $$

### 3.6 Derivation 6 — Gilliland Correlation (Empirical Bridge)

Once $N_{min}$ and $R_{min}$ are known, the actual NTP at operating reflux $R$ is found via the **Gilliland correlation** (1940), an empirical fit:

$$ X = \frac{R - R_{min}}{R + 1}, \quad Y = \frac{N - N_{min}}{N + 1} $$

The best-fit equation (Eduljee, 1975):

$$ Y = 0.75 \left(1 - X^{0.5668}\right) $$

This single equation recovers N for any $R \in (R_{min}, \infty)$ with mean error < 5%.

### 3.7 Derivation 7 — Murphree Vapor Efficiency & Actual Plates

Real plates are not ideal. Murphree defined efficiency as:

$$ E_{Mv} = \frac{y_n - y_{n+1}}{y_n^* - y_{n+1}} $$

where $y_n^*$ is the vapor composition in equilibrium with the liquid leaving plate $n$ (i.e., $x_n$).

**Actual plate count using overall efficiency:**

$$ NAP = \frac{NTP}{E_o} \quad \text{where} \quad E_o = \frac{\text{ideal stages}}{\text{actual stages}} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** For multi-component systems, use the **Lewis-Matheson** (stage-by-stage with component balances) or **Thiele-Geddes** methods — but for the 2024 scheme Module 2, focus remains on **binary** McCabe–Thiele.

### 3.8 Comprehensive Worked Example — Step-by-Step NTP Calculation

**Problem:** A distillation column separates a benzene-toluene mixture at 1 atm. Given:
- $x_F = 0.40$, $x_D = 0.95$, $x_B = 0.05$
- $R = 2.0$ (operating reflux ratio)
- Saturated liquid feed ($q = 1$)
- Relative volatility $\alpha = 2.47$ (constant)

**Find:** NTP and feed plate location.

**Step 1 — Compute $R_{min}$ (saturated liquid, q = 1):**

$$ y_F^* = \frac{\alpha x_F}{1 + (\alpha-1)x_F} = \frac{2.47 \times 0.40}{1 + 1.47 \times 0.40} = \frac{0.988}{1.588} = 0.6222 $$

$$ R_{min} = \frac{x_D - y_F^*}{y_F^* - x_F} = \frac{0.95 - 0.6222}{0.6222 - 0.40} = \frac{0.3278}{0.2222} = 1.475 $$

**Step 2 — Compute $N_{min}$ (Fenske):**

$$ N_{min} = \frac{\ln\left[\left(\dfrac{0.95}{0.05}\right)\left(\dfrac{0.95}{0.05}\right)\right]}{\ln 2.47} = \frac{\ln(19 \times 19)}{\ln 2.47} = \frac{\ln(361)}{0.9046} = \frac{5.886}{0.9046} = 6.51 $$

So $N_{min} \approx 6.5$ → in practice 7 stages (using integer ceiling).

**Step 3 — Apply Gilliland:**

$$ X = \frac{R - R_{min}}{R+1} = \frac{2.0 - 1.475}{3.0} = \frac{0.525}{3.0} = 0.175 $$

$$ Y = 0.75(1 - 0.175^{0.5668}) = 0.75(1 - 0.357) = 0.75 \times 0.643 = 0.482 $$

$$ N = \frac{N_{min} + Y}{1 - Y} = \frac{6.51 + 0.482}{0.518} = \frac{6.992}{0.518} = 13.50 $$

**Step 4 — Feed plate location (Kirkhoff approximate rule):**

Rectifying stages $N_R$ above feed:
- If $R \to \infty$: $N_R^{R=\infty} = \dfrac{\ln(0.95/0.05)}{\ln 2.47} = 3.255$
- Total $N = 13.50$; total at infinite reflux: $N_{min} = 6.51$

By the **Kirkhoff optimum rule** (analogous to Fenske split at $R_{min}$):

$$ \frac{N_R}{N_S} = \frac{\ln\left[\left(\dfrac{x_D}{1-x_D}\right)\left(\dfrac{1-x_F}{x_F}\right)\right]}{\ln\left[\left(\dfrac{x_F}{1-x_F}\right)\left(\dfrac{1-x_B}{x_B}\right)\right]} = \frac{\ln\left[\dfrac{0.95}{0.05} \times \dfrac{0.60}{0.40}\right]}{\ln\left[\dfrac{0.40}{0.60} \times \dfrac{0.95}{0.05}\right]} $$

$$ = \frac{\ln(28.5)}{\ln(12.667)} = \frac{3.350}{2.539} = 1.319 $$

So $N_R : N_S = 1.319 : 1$. With $N_R + N_S = 13.5$:

$$ N_R = \frac{1.319}{2.319} \times 13.5 = 7.68 \approx 8 \text{ stages} $$
$$ N_S = 13.5 - 8 = 5.5 \approx 6 \text{ stages} $$

**Step 5 — Verify by graphical McCabe–Thiele stepping:**

The McCabe–Thiele plot would show:
- Equilibrium curve: $y = \dfrac{2.47 x}{1 + 1.47 x}$
- 45° diagonal
- q-line: vertical at $x = 0.40$
- Rectifying line: slope $= R/(R+1) = 0.667$, y-intercept $= x_D/(R+1) = 0.317$
- Stripping line: through $(0.05, 0.05)$ and intersection point $(0.40, y_{int})$

Intersection point at $x=0.40$: $y_{int} = 0.667 \times 0.40 + 0.317 = 0.5838$.
Stripping line slope: $(0.5838 - 0.05)/(0.40 - 0.05) = 0.5338/0.35 = 1.525$.

Stepping off: starting from $(x_D, x_D) = (0.95, 0.95)$ and moving horizontally to the rectifying line, then vertically to the equilibrium curve, repeating — about **13.5 stages** with the **feed plate at the 8th step from the top**.

    reboilA --> vaporReturnA["Vapor V to Bottom Plate"]
    condOLR["Rectifying OLR y R by R plus 1 times x plus xD by R plus 1"] -.-> rectSecA

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**Q1.** What is meant by the **Minimum Number of Plates** ($N_{min}$) of a distillation column? State the Fenske equation and list the assumptions used in its derivation.

**Model Answer:**
$N_{min}$ is the number of theoretical plates required when the column operates at **total reflux** ($R \to \infty$), meaning no distillate or bottoms product is withdrawn — all condensate is returned as reflux and all liquid is reboiled to vapor. Under this condition the operating lines collapse onto the 45° diagonal, and separation depends only on the equilibrium stages.

**Fenske Equation:**
$$ N_{min} = \frac{\ln\left[\left(\dfrac{x_D}{1-x_D}\right)\left(\dfrac{1-x_B}{x_B}\right)\right]}{\ln \alpha_{avg}} $$

**Assumptions:**
1. Constant molar overflow.
2. Constant relative volatility $\alpha$ (or geometric mean used).
3. Total condenser and partial reboiler.
4. Binary system.
5. Equilibrium at every theoretical stage.

**Marks Distribution (KTU Pattern):** Definition (1M) + Equation (1M) + Assumptions (1M) = **3 Marks**.

---

**Q2.** Explain the significance of the **q-line** in the McCabe–Thiele method. How does its slope change with feed condition?

**Model Answer:**
The **q-line** is the locus of intersection of the rectifying and stripping operating lines. It pinpoints the **feed plate** location, where the calculation switches from the rectifying operating line (above feed) to the stripping operating line (below feed). It passes through the feed composition point $(x_F, x_F)$ and has slope $q/(q-1)$.

| Feed Condition | q | Slope of q-line |
|---|---|---|
| Sub-cooled liquid | > 1 | positive finite |
| Saturated liquid | 1 | infinite (vertical) |
| Two-phase | 0 < q < 1 | negative |
| Saturated vapor | 0 | 0 (horizontal) |
| Superheated vapor | < 0 | positive finite |

**Marks Distribution:** Significance (1M) + Equation (1M) + Feed-condition table (1M) = **3 Marks**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module 2 Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** A continuous distillation column is to be designed to separate **10,000 kg/h** of a mixture containing **40 mol% benzene (MVC)** and **60 mol% toluene (LVC)** at atmospheric pressure. The distillate should contain **95 mol% benzene** and the bottoms **5 mol% benzene**. The feed is a **saturated liquid** ($q = 1$), and the reflux ratio is **1.5 times the minimum reflux**. The relative volatility $\alpha$ may be taken as **2.47**. Calculate:
   1. The minimum number of theoretical plates using the Fenske equation. **(4 marks)**
   2. The minimum reflux ratio $R_{min}$. **(4 marks)**
   3. The actual number of theoretical plates using the Gilliland correlation. **(4 marks)**
   4. The optimum feed plate location. **(2 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the effect of **(i)** increasing the reflux ratio, **(ii)** changing feed quality from saturated liquid to saturated vapor, and **(iii)** introducing a feed preheater on the number of theoretical plates. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**Part (a) — Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1: Compute $N_{min}$ using Fenske equation:**

$$ N_{min} = \frac{\ln\left[\left(\dfrac{x_D}{1-x_D}\right)\left(\dfrac{1-x_B}{x_B}\right)\right]}{\ln \alpha} $$

$$ N_{min} = \frac{\ln\left[\left(\dfrac{0.95}{0.05}\right)\left(\dfrac{0.95}{0.05}\right)\right]}{\ln 2.47} = \frac{\ln(19 \times 19)}{\ln 2.47} = \frac{\ln 361}{0.9046} $$

$$ N_{min} = \frac{5.886}{0.9046} = 6.51 \approx 7 \text{ stages} $$

**Step 2: Compute $R_{min}$ for saturated liquid feed:**

The vapor composition in equilibrium with $x_F = 0.40$:

$$ y_F^* = \frac{\alpha x_F}{1+(\alpha-1)x_F} = \frac{2.47 \times 0.40}{1+1.47 \times 0.40} = \frac{0.988}{1.588} = 0.6222 $$

$$ R_{min} = \frac{x_D - y_F^*}{y_F^* - x_F} = \frac{0.95 - 0.6222}{0.6222 - 0.40} = \frac{0.3278}{0.2222} = 1.475 $$

**Step 3: Compute operating reflux ratio:**

$$ R = 1.5 R_{min} = 1.5 \times 1.475 = 2.213 $$

**Step 4: Apply Gilliland correlation:**

$$ X = \frac{R - R_{min}}{R+1} = \frac{2.213 - 1.475}{2.213 + 1} = \frac{0.738}{3.213} = 0.2297 $$

$$ Y = 0.75(1 - X^{0.5668}) = 0.75(1 - 0.2297^{0.5668}) $$

Computing $0.2297^{0.5668}$: $\ln(0.2297) = -1.471$, $-1.471 \times 0.5668 = -0.834$, $e^{-0.834} = 0.434$.

$$ Y = 0.75(1 - 0.434) = 0.75 \times 0.566 = 0.4245 $$

$$ N = \frac{N_{min} + Y}{1 - Y} = \frac{6.51 + 0.4245}{1 - 0.4245} = \frac{6.9345}{0.5755} = 12.05 \approx 12 \text{ stages} $$

**Step 5: Optimum feed plate location (Kirkhoff):**

The fraction of stages above the feed:

$$ \frac{N_R}{N} = \frac{\ln\left[\dfrac{x_D}{1-x_D} \cdot \dfrac{1-x_F}{x_F}\right]}{\ln\left[\dfrac{x_D}{1-x_D} \cdot \dfrac{1-x_B}{x_B}\right]} = \frac{\ln\left[\dfrac{0.95}{0.05} \cdot \dfrac{0.60}{0.40}\right]}{\ln(19 \times 19)} $$

$$ = \frac{\ln(28.5)}{5.886} = \frac{3.350}{5.886} = 0.5692 $$

$$ N_R = 0.5692 \times 12 = 6.83 \approx 7 \text{ stages above feed} $$
$$ N_S = 12 - 7 = 5 \text{ stages below feed} $$

**Final Answer (a):** $N_{min} = 6.51 \approx 7$ stages, $R_{min} = 1.475$, $NTP = 12$ stages, Feed plate = **7th stage from top**.

**Part (b) — Discussion:**

**(i) Effect of increasing reflux ratio:**
As $R$ increases from $R_{min}$, the slope of the rectifying line $R/(R+1)$ increases (moves closer to unity), the operating line moves away from the equilibrium curve, and the **stepping-off requires fewer stages**. At $R \to \infty$, $N \to N_{min}$. However, beyond $R \approx 1.5 R_{min}$, the gain in reduced stages is marginal while **reboiler and condenser duties increase linearly**, making operation uneconomical. The optimum economic $R \approx (1.2 \text{ to } 1.5) R_{min}$.

**(ii) Effect of changing feed from saturated liquid to saturated vapor:**
For $q=1$ (saturated liquid), the q-line is vertical at $x = x_F$. For $q=0$ (saturated vapor), the q-line is horizontal at $y = x_F$. This changes the **intersection point** of the rectifying and stripping operating lines, which in turn alters the optimum feed plate location. With a saturated vapor feed, more of the separation occurs in the stripping section, so the feed plate moves **downwards**. The total NTP may change slightly (typically 1–2 stages), but the dominant effect is on feed plate position.

**(iii) Effect of feed preheater:**
A preheater increases the feed enthalpy and reduces $q$ (moves the feed closer to two-phase or vapor). This:
- Shifts the q-line slope and moves the feed plate location.
- **Reduces reboiler steam duty** (less vaporization required in the reboiler).
- May **slightly reduce total NTP** if the feed is optimally conditioned.
- The preheater trades external heat input for internal reboiler duty — overall energy savings if heat integration is available.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Fenske misuse (–2 marks):** Using the Fenske equation at any $R \ne \infty$ is a common mistake. Fenske is ONLY for $N_{min}$ at total reflux.
> 2. **Wrong y$_F^*$ (–2 marks):** Computing $y_F^*$ from the q-line intersection but reading the wrong intersection on the equilibrium curve is a classic error. Always verify that $y_F^*$ lies on the q-line and on the equilibrium curve simultaneously.
> 3. **Gilliland direction (–1 mark):** Reversing $X$ and $Y$ — remember $X$ is the abscissa (R-based) and $Y$ is the ordinate (N-based).
> 4. **Forgetting efficiency correction (–2 marks):** If the question asks for "actual plates" or "real plates", you MUST divide by $E_o$ or apply Murphree — NTP alone is incomplete.
> 5. **q-line through wrong point (–1 mark):** q-line ALWAYS passes through $(x_F, x_F)$ — never through $(0,0)$ or $(1,1)$.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** A distillation column operating at total reflux has 8 theoretical plates (including the reboiler). The system is benzene-toluene with $\alpha = 2.47$. If the bottoms composition is $x_B = 0.08$, determine the distillate composition $x_D$ achievable. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **McCabe–Thiele method** for determining the number of theoretical plates. List the steps involved and the key assumptions. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**Part (a) — Using Fenske Equation (Rearranged):**

Given: $N_{min} = 8$, $x_B = 0.08$, $\alpha = 2.47$. Find $x_D$.

Applying Fenske:

$$ N_{min} = \frac{\ln\left[\left(\dfrac{x_D}{1-x_D}\right)\left(\dfrac{1-x_B}{x_B}\right)\right]}{\ln \alpha} $$

$$ 8 = \frac{\ln\left[\left(\dfrac{x_D}{1-x_D}\right)\left(\dfrac{0.92}{0.08}\right)\right]}{\ln 2.47} $$

$$ 8 \times \ln 2.47 = \ln\left[\left(\dfrac{x_D}{1-x_D}\right) \times 11.5\right] $$

$$ 8 \times 0.9046 = \ln\left[11.5 \cdot \dfrac{x_D}{1-x_D}\right] $$

$$ 7.237 = \ln\left[11.5 \cdot \dfrac{x_D}{1-x_D}\right] $$

$$ e^{7.237} = 1394.6 = 11.5 \cdot \dfrac{x_D}{1-x_D} $$

$$ \dfrac{x_D}{1-x_D} = \frac{1394.6}{11.5} = 121.27 $$

$$ x_D = \frac{121.27}{122.27} = 0.9918 $$

**Answer (a):** $x_D \approx 0.992$ (i.e., 99.2 mol% benzene in distillate).

**Part (b) — McCabe–Thiele Method Explanation:**

**Sketch (textual):**
- y-axis: vapor mole fraction $y$
- x-axis: liquid mole fraction $x$
- 45° diagonal line ($y = x$)
- Equilibrium curve (sigmoid, below diagonal for $0 < \alpha < \infty$ in this system)
- q-line through $(x_F, x_F)$
- Rectifying operating line: through $(x_D, x_D)$ with slope $R/(R+1)$
- Stripping operating line: through $(x_B, x_B)$, intersecting rectifying line at q-line

**Step-by-step procedure:**
1. Plot the equilibrium curve $y = f(x)$ from VLE data.
2. Draw the 45° diagonal.
3. Mark points $(x_D, x_D)$, $(x_F, x_F)$, and $(x_B, x_B)$ on the diagonal.
4. Draw the q-line through $(x_F, x_F)$ with slope $q/(q-1)$.
5. Compute $R$ (typically $1.2R_{min}$ to $1.5R_{min}$), find $R_{min}$ first.
6. Draw the rectifying line through $(x_D, x_D)$ with slope $R/(R+1)$.
7. Connect the intersection of the rectifying line with the q-line to $(x_B, x_B)$ — this is the stripping line.
8. Starting at $(x_D, x_D)$, draw a horizontal line to the rectifying line, then a vertical line to the equilibrium curve.
9. Continue this "stepping-off" process; **switch from rectifying to stripping line at the feed plate** (where the step crosses the feed composition $x_F$ or where the changeover minimizes total steps).
10. Continue stepping until $x \le x_B$ is reached.
11. Count the number of steps on the equilibrium curve → that is the **NTP**.
12. (Optional) If the reboiler is an equilibrium stage, subtract 1 from the count.

**Key Assumptions:**
- Constant molar overflow (CMO)
- Negligible heat of mixing
- Theoretical (100% efficient) plates
- Binary system
- Equilibrium at every stage
- Constant operating pressure

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Number of Plates**

- **NTP** = theoretical stages at operating $R$; **$N_{min}$** = stages at total reflux (Fenske); **infinite** stages at $R_{min}$.
- **Fenske Equation** uses $\alpha_{avg}$ (geometric mean at top and bottom for non-constant $\alpha$).
- **$R_{min}$ calculation** requires identifying the pinch point — usually at q-line ∩ equilibrium curve for $q=1$ saturated liquid feed.
- **Gilliland Correlation** is empirical; use $R = (1.2 \text{ to } 1.5) R_{min}$ for economic design.
- **Rectifying line slope** = $R/(R+1)$; **intercept** = $x_D/(R+1)$.
- **Stripping line slope** = $\bar{L}/\bar{V} = (L+qF)/(V-(1-q)F)$.
- **q-line slope** = $q/(q-1)$; always passes through $(x_F, x_F)$.
- **Feed plate** is where you switch from rectifying to stripping operating line during stepping.
- **Optimum feed plate** minimizes total NTP; if misplaced, total stages increase (sometimes significantly).
- **Murphree Efficiency** $E_{Mv}$ relates real and ideal stages: $E_{Mv} = (y_n - y_{n+1})/(y_n^* - y_{n+1})$.
- **Overall efficiency** $E_o = NTP/NAP$; typical values 0.5–0.7 for sieve trays, 0.4–0.6 for bubble-cap.
- **Smoker's Equation** provides analytical NTP for constant-$\alpha$ systems — use it when graphical is not possible.
- **Kirkhoff rule** for feed plate: $N_R/N_S = \ln[(x_D/(1-x_D))\cdot((1-x_F)/x_F)] / \ln[(x_F/(1-x_F))\cdot((1-x_B)/x_B)]$.
- **Constant molar overflow (CMO)** is the most common KTU assumption — verify before deriving operating lines.
- **Total condenser** + **partial reboiler** are standard KTU assumptions unless specified otherwise.
- **Saturated liquid feed** ($q=1$) is the most common exam case — q-line is vertical.
- **Special formula for $R_{min}$ at q=1:** $R_{min} = \dfrac{1}{\alpha-1}\left[\dfrac{x_D}{x_F} - \alpha\dfrac{1-x_D}{1-x_F}\right]$.
- **Erbar-Maddox correlation** is more accurate than Gilliland — use it for design verification.
- **Lewis-Matheson method** is for multi-component systems — not in Module 2 scope for KTU 2024.

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Marks-Losing Mistakes in KTU Board Exams on this Topic:**
> 1. Confusing $N_{min}$ (total reflux) with operating NTP.
> 2. Using $R = 1.5$ literally without first computing $R_{min}$ to verify it is feasible.
> 3. Forgetting to switch operating lines at the feed plate in the McCabe–Thiele plot.
> 4. Reporting NTP when the question asks for actual plates (missing efficiency).
> 5. Wrong $y_F^*$ in $R_{min}$ — must satisfy both q-line AND equilibrium curve.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Feed Plate Location
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT501 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS II
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Feed Plate Location

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of continuous binary distillation column design, the **Feed Plate Location** refers to the optimal tray (or stage) within the column where the fresh feed stream should be physically introduced to achieve the desired separation between the distillate (D) and bottoms (W) products with the **minimum number of theoretical stages** and maximum thermodynamic efficiency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Standard):**
> The **Feed Plate** is the stage in a distillation column at which the transition occurs between the **Rectifying (Enriching) Section** (above the feed) and the **Stripping (Exhausting) Section** (below the feed). At this precise location, the operating lines of both sections intersect, and this intersection point must mathematically satisfy the **q-line equation** for thermodynamically consistent design.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Real-World Intuition

Imagine a multi-storey building where two different elevators operate — one going UP from the lobby (Rectifying Section) and one coming DOWN from the top (Stripping Section). Passengers (the more volatile component — MVC) board at the lobby and need to go up, while passengers (the less volatile component — LVC) come down to exit at the lobby.

**The Feed Plate is the LOBBY itself** — the strategic floor where the feed (new passengers) enters the system. If you place the lobby on the wrong floor, passengers will have to travel extra, inefficient routes, wasting energy (reboiler duty) and increasing congestion (more theoretical stages).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:**
> The location of the feed plate directly influences:
> - The **reflux ratio (R)** requirement
> - The **reboiler heat duty (Q_R)**
> - The **number of stages** in each section
> - The **thermodynamic efficiency** of the column

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Reflux Ratio | R | Dimensionless |
| Feed Quality (Thermal Condition) | q | Dimensionless |
| Distillate Rate | D | kmol/h |
| Bottoms Rate | W | kmol/h |
| Feed Composition | z_F | Mole fraction |
| Distillate Composition | x_D | Mole fraction |
| Bottoms Composition | x_W | Mole fraction |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Conceptual Breakdown of Feed Plate Location

The determination of the feed plate is fundamentally rooted in the **McCabe-Thiele Method** (graphical stage-by-stage construction). The logic is broken down as follows:

- **Step 1: Establish the Material Balance Envelope**
  Above the feed plate lies the **Rectifying Section** where liquid (L) flows down and vapor (V) flows up. A total condenser is assumed, so V = L + D = (R+1)D.

- **Step 2: Establish the Lower Material Balance Envelope**
  Below the feed plate lies the **Stripping Section** where liquid (L') flows down and vapor (V') flows up. The streams L' and V' differ from L and V because the feed changes the internal flows.

- **Step 3: Identify the q-line (Feed Line)**
  The thermal condition of the feed is quantified by the parameter **q**, which represents the **moles of saturated liquid formed per mole of feed** when the feed is flashed adiabatically to the column pressure.

- **Step 4: Locate the Intersection Point**
  The operating line of the rectifying section and the operating line of the stripping section MUST intersect at a point that lies on the q-line. This intersection point dictates the feed plate location.

- **Step 5: Stage Construction**
  Stepping off stages on the McCabe-Thiele diagram starting from x_D (top) downward, the stage that crosses the q-line is designated as the **Feed Plate**.

### 2.2 The Feed Quality Parameter 'q' — The Heart of Feed Plate Location

The parameter **q** is critical because it defines how the feed integrates into the column's internal flows:

$$q = \frac{\text{Moles of saturated liquid formed per mole of feed}}{\text{Total moles of feed}}$$

| Feed Condition | q Value | Phase Description |
|---|---|---|
| Subcooled Liquid | q > 1 | Cold liquid feed |
| Saturated Liquid | q = 1 | Liquid at bubble point |
| Two-Phase (Liquid + Vapor) | 0 < q < 1 | Partially vaporized |
| Saturated Vapor | q = 0 | Vapor at dew point |
| Superheated Vapor | q < 0 | Hot vapor feed |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Feed Plate Location

| Formula Name | Equation | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **q-line Equation** | $y = \dfrac{q}{q-1}x - \dfrac{z_F}{q-1}$ | Passes through (z_F, z_F) on 45° line |
| **Rectifying Operating Line** | $y = \dfrac{R}{R+1}x + \dfrac{x_D}{R+1}$ | y-intercept at x = 0 |
| **Stripping Operating Line** | $y = \dfrac{L'}{V'}x - \dfrac{W x_W}{V'}$ | Passes through (x_W, x_W) |
| **Modified Stripping Line** | $y = \dfrac{RD+qF}{(R+1)D+(q-1)F}x - \dfrac{(1-q)x_W+qz_F}{(R+1)+(q-1)}$ | Uses external flows |
| **Liquid Flow in Stripping** | $L' = L + qF = RD + qF$ | Below feed plate |
| **Vapor Flow in Stripping** | $V' = V + (q-1)F = (R+1)D + (q-1)F$ | Below feed plate |
| **Intersection Point (x_q, y_q)** | $x_q = \dfrac{(R+1)z_F + (q-1)x_D - q x_W + (R+1)x_D}{(R+1)(q-1)+R-q+1}$ | Solved simultaneously |
| **Slope of q-line** | $\text{Slope} = \dfrac{q}{q-1}$ | Always passes through (z_F, z_F) |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL RULE:** Never use the vertical pipe symbol `|` inside KTU answer sheets for tables. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX-rendered answers.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Petroleum Refining**: Crude oil distillation towers have multiple feed/side-draw plates where different petroleum cuts are withdrawn.
- **Cryogenic Air Separation**: Optimal feed plate placement in O₂/N₂ columns minimizes compressor work.
- **Solvent Recovery**: Pharmaceutical industries use precise feed plate locations to recover expensive solvents at minimum energy cost.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the q-line Equation

Consider a feed stream of F kmol/h entering the column at the feed plate. Let:
- $F$ = molar feed rate
- $z_F$ = mole fraction of MVC in feed
- $q$ = fraction of feed that becomes saturated liquid

**Step 1: Flash Material Balance Around the Feed Plate**

For the liquid portion of the feed (qF kmol/h) joining the liquid stream L from above:
$$L' = L + qF$$

For the vapor portion of the feed [(1−q)F kmol/h] joining the vapor stream V from below:
$$V' = V - (1-q)F = V + (q-1)F$$

**Step 2: Component Mass Balance for the More Volatile Component**

Total MVC entering the feed plate:
- From liquid feed: $qF \cdot z_F$
- From vapor feed: $(1-q)F \cdot z_F$
- Total: $F \cdot z_F$

This must equal the MVC leaving via L' and V' streams:

$$qF \cdot z_F + (1-q)F \cdot z_F = L' \cdot x + V' \cdot y$$

Since $L' = L + qF$ and $V' = V + (q-1)F$, substituting:

$$F z_F = (L + qF)x + [V + (q-1)F]y$$

**Step 3: Eliminate Internal Streams Using Rectifying Section Balance**

From the rectifying section: $V = L + D = (R+1)D$

And the overall balance: $L = R \cdot D$

Substituting L = RD:

$$F z_F = (RD + qF)x + [(R+1)D + (q-1)F]y$$

**Step 4: Rearrange to Standard q-line Form**

Dividing through by F and isolating y:

$$\frac{q z_F}{1} = \frac{RD \cdot x}{F} + q x + (R+1)\frac{D}{F}y + (q-1)y$$

After algebraic rearrangement, the elegant q-line equation emerges:

$$y = \frac{q}{q-1}x - \frac{z_F}{q-1}$$

This equation describes a **straight line on the x-y diagram** with:
- **Slope** = $\dfrac{q}{q-1}$
- **y-intercept** = $-\dfrac{z_F}{q-1}$
- **Passes through the point** $(z_F, z_F)$ on the 45° diagonal line.

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Proof: Feed Plate is at the Intersection Point

We need to prove that the rectifying operating line, stripping operating line, AND q-line all intersect at a common point.

**Step 1: Setup the Two Operating Lines**

Rectifying Line: $y = \dfrac{R}{R+1}x + \dfrac{x_D}{R+1}$

Stripping Line: $y = \dfrac{L'}{V'}x - \dfrac{W x_W}{V'}$

**Step 2: Set Them Equal to Find Intersection**

$$\frac{R}{R+1}x + \frac{x_D}{R+1} = \frac{L'}{V'}x - \frac{W x_W}{V'}$$

Rearranging:

$$x\left[\frac{R}{R+1} - \frac{L'}{V'}\right] = -\frac{W x_W}{V'} - \frac{x_D}{R+1}$$

**Step 3: Substitute L' and V' Values**

$L' = RD + qF$ and $V' = (R+1)D + (q-1)F$

**Step 4: Verify Intersection Lies on q-line**

The intersection point $(x_q, y_q)$ obtained above, when substituted into the q-line equation $y = \dfrac{q}{q-1}x - \dfrac{z_F}{q-1}$, satisfies the equation identically.

This is the **mathematical cornerstone** of KTU feed plate problems!

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A binary mixture is to be distilled at atmospheric pressure. Given:
- $x_D = 0.95$, $x_W = 0.05$, $z_F = 0.50$
- Reflux ratio $R = 1.5 R_{min}$ (assume $R_{min} = 0.86$)
- Feed is saturated liquid ($q = 1$)
- $D = 50$ kmol/h, $F = 100$ kmol/h

**Find:** The feed plate location and intersection coordinates.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Calculate the actual reflux ratio:
$$R = 1.5 \times 0.86 = 1.29$$

**Step 2:** Write the q-line equation for saturated liquid ($q = 1$):

For $q = 1$:
$$y = \frac{1}{1-1}x - \frac{z_F}{1-1}$$

This becomes **vertical** (infinite slope), so the q-line is a vertical line at $x = z_F = 0.50$.

**Step 3:** Write the Rectifying Operating Line:
$$y = \frac{R}{R+1}x + \frac{x_D}{R+1} = \frac{1.29}{2.29}x + \frac{0.95}{2.29}$$
$$y = 0.5633x + 0.4148$$

**Step 4:** Calculate L' and V':
$$L' = RD + qF = 1.29 \times 50 + 1 \times 100 = 164.5 \text{ kmol/h}$$
$$V' = (R+1)D + (q-1)F = 2.29 \times 50 + 0 = 114.5 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 5:** Calculate W (Bottoms rate):
$$W = F - D = 100 - 50 = 50 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 6:** Write the Stripping Operating Line:
$$y = \frac{L'}{V'}x - \frac{W x_W}{V'} = \frac{164.5}{114.5}x - \frac{50 \times 0.05}{114.5}$$
$$y = 1.4367x - 0.0218$$

**Step 7:** Find the Intersection Point (Feed Plate Coordinates)

Setting rectifying = stripping:
$$0.5633x + 0.4148 = 1.4367x - 0.0218$$
$$0.4366 = 0.8734x$$
$$x_q = 0.5000$$

This confirms the intersection lies on the vertical q-line at $x = z_F = 0.50$!

Substituting back:
$$y_q = 0.5633(0.50) + 0.4148 = 0.6965$$

**Feed Plate Coordinates: $(0.50, 0.6965)$**

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** Since $q = 1$ (saturated liquid), the feed plate MUST have $x_q = z_F$. Our result confirms this fundamental thermodynamic principle.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Distillation Column with Feed Plate

```mermaid
graph TD
    FeedIn[Feed Stream F, zF]:::feedNode
    Condenser[Total Condenser]:::condNode
    Reboiler[Partial Reboiler]:::reboilNode
    Distillate[Distillate D, xD]:::prodNode
    Bottoms[Bottoms W, xW]:::prodNode
    RefluxDrum[Reflux Drum]:::refluxNode
    RectSection[Rectifying Section: V up, L down]:::rectNode
    StripSection[Stripping Section: V prime up, L prime down]:::stripNode
    FeedPlate[FEED PLATE: Intersection of Operating Lines]:::feedPlateNode
    
    FeedIn --> FeedPlate
    FeedPlate --> RectSection
    FeedPlate --> StripSection
    RectSection --> Condenser
    Condenser --> RefluxDrum
    RefluxDrum --> Distillate
    RefluxDrum --> RectSection
    StripSection --> Reboiler
    Reboiler --> Bottoms
    Reboiler --> StripSection
    
    classDef feedNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef condNode fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef reboilNode fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef prodNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef refluxNode fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef rectNode fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef stripNode fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef feedPlateNode fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF
```

### 4.2 Flowchart: Algorithm for Determining Feed Plate Location

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start: Given F, zF, xD, xW, R, q]):::startNode
    S1[Step 1: Calculate D and W from overall balance]:::stepNode
    S2[Step 2: Write Rectifying Operating Line equation]:::stepNode
    S3[Step 3: Calculate L prime and V prime]:::stepNode
    S4[Step 4: Write Stripping Operating Line equation]:::stepNode
    S5[Step 5: Calculate q-line slope and intercept]:::stepNode
    S6[Step 6: Solve for Intersection xq, yq]:::stepNode
    S7[Step 7: Verify intersection lies on q-line]:::stepNode
    S8[Step 8: Locate feed plate on McCabe-Thiele construction]:::stepNode
    End([Output: Feed Plate Coordinates and Stage Number]):::endNode
    
    Start --> S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 --> S8 --> End
    
    classDef startNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef stepNode fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef endNode fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
```

### 4.3 q-line Behavior on x-y Diagram for Different Feed Conditions

```mermaid
graph LR
    Axis[45 Degree Diagonal Line y equals x]:::axisNode
    Q1[Saturated Liquid q=1: VERTICAL line at x=zF]:::liqNode
    Q2[Two-Phase q between 0 and 1: slope between 0 and INFINITY]:::twoPhaseNode
    Q3[Saturated Vapor q=0: HORIZONTAL line at y=zF]:::vapNode
    Q4[Subcooled Liquid q greater than 1: slope between 0 and 1]:::subcoolNode
    Q5[Superheated Vapor q less than 0: negative slope]:::superNode
    
    Axis --> Q1
    Axis --> Q2
    Axis --> Q3
    Axis --> Q4
    Axis --> Q5
    
    classDef axisNode fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef liqNode fill:#4682B4,stroke:#FFF,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef twoPhaseNode fill:#20B2AA,stroke:#FFF,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef vapNode fill:#DC143C,stroke:#FFF,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef subcoolNode fill:#9370DB,stroke:#FFF,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef superNode fill:#FF8C00,stroke:#FFF,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

Define the term 'feed plate' in a distillation column. What is its significance in the McCabe-Thiele method?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **(1 Mark)** The feed plate is the stage in a distillation column where the feed enters and where the column transitions from the rectifying section to the stripping section.
- **(1 Mark)** It represents the point of intersection of the rectifying and stripping operating lines.
- **(1 Mark)** Its correct location ensures minimum number of stages, optimal energy consumption, and thermodynamic consistency of the design.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

What is the q-line? Write the q-line equation and explain the significance of the parameter 'q'.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **(1 Mark)** The q-line is a straight line on the x-y diagram representing the locus of possible intersection points of the rectifying and stripping operating lines, defined by the thermal condition of the feed.
- **(1 Mark)** Equation: $y = \dfrac{q}{q-1}x - \dfrac{z_F}{q-1}$, passing through $(z_F, z_F)$.
- **(1 Mark)** The parameter 'q' indicates the fraction of feed that becomes saturated liquid upon entering the column, and determines the slope of the q-line.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

**Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]*

A binary distillation column is to be designed for separating a mixture of benzene-toluene. Given the following data:
- Feed composition: $z_F = 0.40$ (mole fraction of benzene)
- Distillate composition: $x_D = 0.95$
- Bottoms composition: $x_W = 0.05$
- Feed rate: $F = 100$ kmol/h
- Feed condition: Saturated liquid ($q = 1$)
- Reflux ratio: $R = 2.0$
- Relative volatility $\alpha = 2.47$

**(a)** Determine the feed plate location coordinates using the operating line equations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** If the feed is changed to a saturated vapor condition ($q = 0$), calculate the new feed plate location and comment on the shift in intersection point. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

### Part (a) Solution — Saturated Liquid Feed ($q = 1$)

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Calculate D and W:
$$D = \frac{F(z_F - x_W)}{x_D - x_W} = \frac{100(0.40 - 0.05)}{0.95 - 0.05} = \frac{35}{0.90} = 38.89 \text{ kmol/h}$$
$$W = F - D = 100 - 38.89 = 61.11 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Rectifying Operating Line:
$$y = \frac{R}{R+1}x + \frac{x_D}{R+1} = \frac{2.0}{3.0}x + \frac{0.95}{3.0} = 0.6667x + 0.3167$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Calculate L' and V' for $q = 1$:
$$L' = RD + qF = 2.0 \times 38.89 + 1 \times 100 = 177.78 \text{ kmol/h}$$
$$V' = (R+1)D + (q-1)F = 3.0 \times 38.89 + 0 = 116.67 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Stripping Operating Line:
$$y = \frac{L'}{V'}x - \frac{W x_W}{V'} = \frac{177.78}{116.67}x - \frac{61.11 \times 0.05}{116.67}$$
$$y = 1.5238x - 0.0262$$

**Step 5 (1.5 Marks):** Solve for Intersection (Feed Plate):
$$0.6667x + 0.3167 = 1.5238x - 0.0262$$
$$0.3429 = 0.8571x$$
$$x_q = 0.4001 \approx 0.40$$

$$y_q = 0.6667(0.40) + 0.3167 = 0.5834$$

**Step 6 (0.5 Mark):** Verify on q-line: For $q = 1$, q-line is vertical at $x = z_F = 0.40$. ✓

**Answer: Feed Plate Coordinates = $(0.40, 0.5834)$**

---

### Part (b) Solution — Saturated Vapor Feed ($q = 0$)

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Recalculate L' and V' for $q = 0$:
$$L' = RD + qF = 2.0 \times 38.89 + 0 = 77.78 \text{ kmol/h}$$
$$V' = (R+1)D + (q-1)F = 3.0 \times 38.89 + (-1) \times 100 = 116.67 - 100 = 16.67 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** New Stripping Operating Line:
$$y = \frac{L'}{V'}x - \frac{W x_W}{V'} = \frac{77.78}{16.67}x - \frac{61.11 \times 0.05}{16.67}$$
$$y = 4.6667x - 0.1833$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** New q-line (horizontal at $y = z_F = 0.40$ for $q = 0$):
$$y = 0.40$$

**Step 4 (1.5 Marks):** Find new intersection with rectifying line:
$$0.6667x + 0.3167 = 4.6667x - 0.1833$$
$$0.5000 = 4.0000x$$
$$x_q = 0.125$$

**Step 5 (0.5 Mark):** Calculate $y_q$:
$$y_q = 0.6667(0.125) + 0.3167 = 0.4000$$

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** Verification on horizontal q-line: $y_q = 0.40 = z_F$ ✓

**Step 7 (1 Mark):** Comment on Shift:
The intersection point shifted from $(0.40, 0.5834)$ for saturated liquid to $(0.125, 0.4000)$ for saturated vapor. The x-coordinate decreased significantly because introducing vapor feed (which joins vapor stream) reduces the rectifying duty per stage, shifting the operating line intersection to a lower x-value. This means more stages are needed in the rectifying section, but fewer in the stripping section.

**Answer: New Feed Plate Coordinates = $(0.125, 0.4000)$**

---

**Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — *[KTU University Exam - Sample 2024]*

A continuous distillation column operating at 1 atm separates a methanol-water mixture. Given:
- $x_D = 0.98$, $x_W = 0.02$, $z_F = 0.30$
- Reflux ratio $R = 1.5$
- Feed is a subcooled liquid at $20°C$ with $q = 1.15$
- $F = 200$ kmol/h

**(a)** Derive the q-line equation for this feed and find the slope. **(5 Marks)**

**(b)** Determine the feed plate coordinates using the operating line intersection method and verify that the point lies on the q-line. **(9 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

### Part (a) Solution (5 Marks)

**Step 1 (2 Marks):** q-line equation with $q = 1.15$ and $z_F = 0.30$:
$$y = \frac{q}{q-1}x - \frac{z_F}{q-1} = \frac{1.15}{0.15}x - \frac{0.30}{0.15}$$
$$y = 7.6667x - 2.0000$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Slope of q-line:
$$\text{Slope} = \frac{q}{q-1} = \frac{1.15}{0.15} = 7.6667$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Verify passes through $(z_F, z_F)$:
$$y = 7.6667(0.30) - 2.0000 = 2.3000 - 2.0000 = 0.3000 ✓$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Since $q > 1$, the slope is positive and steep (between 0 and $\infty$), indicating a subcooled liquid feed condition.

---

### Part (b) Solution (9 Marks)

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Calculate D and W:
$$D = \frac{200(0.30 - 0.02)}{0.98 - 0.02} = \frac{56}{0.96} = 58.33 \text{ kmol/h}$$
$$W = 200 - 58.33 = 141.67 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Rectifying Operating Line:
$$y = \frac{1.5}{2.5}x + \frac{0.98}{2.5} = 0.6x + 0.392$$

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Calculate L' and V':
$$L' = RD + qF = 1.5 \times 58.33 + 1.15 \times 200 = 87.50 + 230 = 317.50 \text{ kmol/h}$$
$$V' = (R+1)D + (q-1)F = 2.5 \times 58.33 + 0.15 \times 200 = 145.83 + 30 = 175.83 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Stripping Operating Line:
$$y = \frac{317.50}{175.83}x - \frac{141.67 \times 0.02}{175.83} = 1.8057x - 0.01611$$

**Step 5 (2 Marks):** Solve for Intersection:
$$0.6x + 0.392 = 1.8057x - 0.01611$$
$$0.40811 = 1.2057x$$
$$x_q = 0.3385$$

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** Calculate $y_q$:
$$y_q = 0.6(0.3385) + 0.392 = 0.5951$$

**Step 7 (1 Mark):** Verify on q-line:
$$y = 7.6667(0.3385) - 2.0000 = 2.595 - 2.000 = 0.595 \approx 0.5951 ✓$$

**Answer: Feed Plate Coordinates = $(0.3385, 0.5951)$**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing Internal vs. External Flows**: Many students use L and V (above feed) when calculating stripping section. Always use L' and V' below the feed plate!
> 
> 2. **Forgetting to Convert F to kmol/h**: If F is given in kg/h, you MUST convert using molecular weight first. This is a 1-mark deduction trap.
> 
> 3. **Sign Error in q-line**: When $q < 1$ (vapor feeds), the denominator $(q-1)$ is NEGATIVE. Many students miss the negative sign, leading to wrong intersection coordinates. **Always carry the sign carefully.**
> 
> 4. **Not Verifying the Intersection on q-line**: KTU examiners award bonus marks when students verify their intersection point lies on the q-line. Always show this verification step.
> 
> 5. **Unit Inconsistency**: Always express compositions in mole fractions (NOT mass fractions) when using the McCabe-Thiele method. Mixing units costs 2 marks typically.
> 
> 6. **Forgetting Reboiler as an Equilibrium Stage**: When using McCabe-Thiele, the reboiler counts as one theoretical stage. Don't forget to subtract it when reporting the total stages.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ⭐ **Feed Plate Definition**: The tray where feed enters; intersection point of rectifying and stripping operating lines on x-y diagram.

- ⭐ **q-line Equation**: $y = \dfrac{q}{q-1}x - \dfrac{z_F}{q-1}$ — ALWAYS passes through $(z_F, z_F)$ on the 45° line.

- ⭐ **Feed Plate Location Rule**: Intersection point of operating lines MUST satisfy the q-line equation. This is the **mathematical consistency check** for KTU problems.

- ⭐ **q-value Memory Aid**:
  - $q = 1$: Saturated liquid → **Vertical** q-line
  - $q = 0$: Saturated vapor → **Horizontal** q-line
  - $0 < q < 1$: Two-phase → Slope between 0 and $\infty$
  - $q > 1$: Subcooled liquid → Positive slope
  - $q < 0$: Superheated vapor → Negative slope

- ⭐ **Internal Flow Changes at Feed Plate**:
  - $L' = L + qF = RD + qF$
  - $V' = V + (q-1)F = (R+1)D + (q-1)F$

- ⭐ **Operating Line Equations to Memorize**:
  - Rectifying: $y = \dfrac{R}{R+1}x + \dfrac{x_D}{R+1}$
  - Stripping: $y = \dfrac{L'}{V'}x - \dfrac{W x_W}{V'}$

- ⭐ **Optimal Feed Plate Rule**: Place feed at the stage where the operating line transition occurs. In McCabe-Thiele construction, this is the stage that crosses the q-line.

- ⭐ **Practical Significance**: Incorrect feed plate location increases total stages and energy consumption, making the column uneconomical.

- ⭐ **KTU Examination Tip**: Always state units (kmol/h), show all working steps, and verify the intersection on the q-line for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Minimum Reflux Conditions. Rectification in Packed Columns
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT501 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS II
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Minimum Reflux Conditions. Rectification in Packed Columns

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Minimum Reflux Ratio ($R_{min}$)

The **Minimum Reflux Ratio** is defined as the smallest possible ratio of liquid reflux to distillate that permits a specified separation to be achieved in a distillation column. Operationally, it corresponds to the condition in which an infinite number of theoretical stages would be required to effect the desired separation.

$$\boxed{R_{min} = \frac{L_{min}}{D}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** At $R = R_{min}$, the rectifying and stripping operating lines, along with the **q-line**, all intersect at a single point on the equilibrium curve. This intersection is the **pinch point**, and the column is "infinitely pinched" — meaning the driving force for mass transfer collapses to zero at that point.

## 1.2 Rectification in Packed Columns

**Rectification** is the enrichment of a vapor mixture in the more volatile component(s) by counter-current contact with a liquid reflux. In **packed columns**, this contact is achieved not on discrete trays (as in plate columns), but on the surface of irregular or structured packing elements where continuous vapor–liquid contact occurs.

| Feature | Plate Column | Packed Column |
|---|---|---|
| Contact mechanism | Stage-wise (discrete) | Differential (continuous) |
| Design basis | Theoretical stages + efficiency | **HTU / HETP** |
| Pressure drop | Higher | Lower |
| Foaming/corrosion tolerance | Better | Sensitive to maldistribution |
| Typical application | Large diameter, high capacity | Vacuum, corrosive, foaming systems |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: Climbing a Steep Hill

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine driving a car up a mountain pass. **Reflux** is like the throttle/fuel — the more you feed back, the more "push" the engine (column) has to climb toward the desired purity. **Minimum reflux** is the absolute minimum throttle that *just barely* allows you to crest the hill — at that point, the engine is straining, the road is flat at the top (pinch point), and the climb takes an infinitely long time (infinite stages). **Total reflux** is the opposite extreme — you are at standstill, recycling everything, achieving the easiest possible separation in the *fewest* stages but producing *zero* product.

In a packed column, this same trade-off is managed by adjusting liquid and vapor flow rates, with mass transfer occurring continuously along the height. The design therefore relies on **transfer units (HTU, NTU)** rather than discrete equilibrium stages.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Lines and the q-line

The classical McCabe–Thiele construction gives the rectifying and stripping operating lines as:

$$y_{n+1} = \frac{R}{R+1}x_n + \frac{x_D}{R+1} \quad \text{(Rectifying)}$$

$$y_{m+1} = \frac{L'}{V'}x_m - \frac{W x_W}{V'} \quad \text{(Stripping)}$$

The **q-line** describes the thermal condition of the feed:

$$y = \frac{q}{q-1}x - \frac{z_F}{q-1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> At $R_{min}$, the rectifying and stripping lines both pass through the **feed-stage intersection point** with the equilibrium curve. The q-line passes through this same point, creating the **pinch**.

## 2.2 Effect of Feed Thermal Condition on $R_{min}$

| Feed State | q-value | $R_{min}$ Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| Subcooled liquid | q > 1 | $R_{min}$ decreases (lowest) |
| Saturated liquid | q = 1 | $R_{min}$ standard |
| Saturated vapor | q = 0 | $R_{min}$ higher |
| Superheated vapor | q < 0 | $R_{min}$ highest (most energy) |

The general material balance around the feed plate gives:

$$R_{min} = \frac{x_D - y^*_F}{y^*_F - x_F}$$

where $y^*_F$ is the intersection of the q-line with the equilibrium curve.

## 2.3 The Underwood Equation (Multicomponent Minimum Reflux)

For multicomponent systems, the analytical determination of $R_{min}$ is given by **Underwood's** algebraic method. The Underwood parameter $\theta$ is the root of:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{c} \frac{q \cdot z_i}{\alpha_i - \theta} = 1$$

where $\theta$ satisfies $\alpha_{LK} > \theta > \alpha_{HK}$, $\alpha_i$ is the relative volatility of component $i$ with respect to the heavy key, $z_i$ is the feed composition, and $q$ is the feed thermal condition.

The minimum reflux is then:

$$R_{min} = \sum_{i=1}^{c} \frac{\alpha_i x_{D,i}}{\alpha_i - \theta} - 1$$

## 2.4 The Gilliland Correlation (Number of Stages vs. Reflux)

The empirical **Gilliland correlation** relates the actual number of stages $N$ to $N_{min}$ (at total reflux) and $R$ to $R_{min}$ via:

$$Y = 0.75 \left[1 - X^{0.5668}\right]$$

where

$$Y = \frac{R - R_{min}}{R + 1}, \qquad X = \frac{N - N_{min}}{N + 1}$$

## 2.5 Rectification in Packed Columns — HTU / HETP Approach

In packed columns, the **Height of a Transfer Unit (HTU)** model is used:

$$Z = HTU_{OG} \cdot N_{OG}$$

The number of overall gas-phase transfer units is:

$$N_{OG} = \int_{Y_2}^{Y_1} \frac{dY}{Y^* - Y}$$

Alternative design uses **HETP** (Height Equivalent to a Theoretical Plate):

$$Z = HETP \times N_{theoretical}$$

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_{min} = (x_D - y^*_F)/(y^*_F - x_F)$ | Binary minimum reflux (q-line intersection) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\sum_i \frac{q z_i}{\alpha_i - \theta} = 1$ | Underwood root for $\theta$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | $R_{min} = \sum_i \frac{\alpha_i x_{D,i}}{\alpha_i - \theta} - 1$ | Multicomponent $R_{min}$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | $Y = 0.75(1 - X^{0.5668})$ | Gilliland correlation | dimensionless |
| 5 | $X = (N - N_{min})/(N+1)$ | Gilliland abscissa | dimensionless |
| 6 | $Y = (R - R_{min})/(R+1)$ | Gilliland ordinate | dimensionless |
| 7 | $Z = HTU_{OG} \cdot N_{OG}$ | Packed column height | m |
| 8 | $N_{OG} = \int dY/(Y^*-Y)$ | Overall gas transfer units | dimensionless |
| 9 | $Z = HETP \cdot N_t$ | Packed column via theoretical plates | m |
| 10 | $N_{min} = \frac{\ln[(x_D/x_W)(x_{W,HK}/x_{D,LK})]}{\ln(\alpha_{LK}/\alpha_{HK})}$ | Fenske equation — $N_{min}$ | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\frac{d(LV)}{dx} = 0$ | Colburn criterion for non-constant molal overflow | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** Minimum reflux and packed column design underpin the design of **air separation (LOX/LIN plants)**, **ethylene fractionators**, **crude oil atmospheric/vacuum distillation**, and **fine-chemical vacuum columns** where packed internals dominate due to low pressure drop.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
