# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Minimum Reflux Expression (Binary Case)

**Starting point:** At $R_{min}$, the operating lines and q-line all intersect on the equilibrium curve. For a saturated liquid feed ($q = 1$), the q-line is vertical: $x = x_F$.

The rectifying operating line passes through $(x_D, x_D)$ and $(x_F, y^*_F)$:

$$y^*_F = \frac{R_{min}}{R_{min}+1} x_F + \frac{x_D}{R_{min}+1}$$

**Step 1 — Rearrange to isolate $R_{min}$:**

$$y^*_F (R_{min}+1) = R_{min} x_F + x_D$$

$$y^*_{F} R_{min} + y^*_{F} = R_{min} x_F + x_D$$

**Step 2 — Collect $R_{min}$ terms:**

$$R_{min} (y^*_{F} - x_F) = x_D - y^*_{F}$$

**Step 3 — Solve:**

$$\boxed{R_{min} = \frac{x_D - y^*_F}{y^*_F - x_F}}$$

This is the **standard KTU-board expression** for binary minimum reflux.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Underwood Equation (Multicomponent)

**Step 1 — Total Condenser Material Balance (component $i$):**

$$V y_i = L x_i + D x_{D,i}$$

Hence, the operating line ratio in the rectifying section:

$$\frac{L}{V} = 1 - \frac{D}{V} = \frac{R}{R+1}$$

**Step 2 — The stage-wise component balance for stage $n$** (rectifying section):

$$V y_{n+1,i} = L x_{n,i} + D x_{D,i}$$

Rearranging: $y_{n+1,i} = \frac{L}{V} x_{n,i} + \frac{D}{V} x_{D,i}$.

**Step 3 — The Underwood parameter** $\theta$ is defined by the relation that must hold for the components below the pinch (between the light key and the heavy key) when the operating line and the q-line meet on the equilibrium curve. The defining identity is:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{c} \frac{z_i (1-q)}{\alpha_i - \theta} + \sum_{i=1}^{c} \frac{q \cdot z_i}{\alpha_i - \theta} = 1 - 1 = 0 \text{ (constraint)}$$

Combining with the component balances and applying the limit at the pinch gives:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{c} \frac{q \cdot z_i}{\alpha_i - \theta} = 1$$

**Step 4 — Minimum Reflux from Underwood:**

Applying the rectifying line composition to the column-top product distribution $x_{D,i}$:

$$V y_{D,i} = L x_{D,i} + D x_{D,i}$$

Since $y_{D,i} = \alpha_i x_{D,i} / \sum_j \alpha_j x_{D,j}$ in relative form and the constraint $V = L + D$, one obtains:

$$R_{min} = \sum_{i=1}^{c} \frac{\alpha_i x_{D,i}}{\alpha_i - \theta} - 1$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Gilliland Functional Form

**Step 1 — The empirical regression** by Gilliland (1940) and refined by Molokanov (Eduljee, 1980) fits the data of many binary and multicomponent distillations to:

$$Y = 0.75 \left[ 1 - X^{0.5668} \right]$$

**Step 2 — Limiting cases:**

- $X \to 0$ (i.e., $N \to N_{min}$): $Y \to 0.75$ → $R = R_{min} + 0.75(R+1) = \infty$. Consistent: minimum reflux requires infinite stages.

- $X \to 1$ (i.e., $R \to \infty$): $Y \to 0.75(1 - 1) = 0$ → $R - R_{min} = 0$, which is consistent only when $R_{min} = R$ in the formal limit. The correct interpretation is $R \to \infty \Rightarrow Y \to 0$ but practical Gilliland range is $0.01 < Y < 0.99$.

**Step 3 — Practical use:**

1. Compute $N_{min}$ via the **Fenske equation**.
2. Compute $R_{min}$ via the **Underwood equation**.
3. Choose a working $R$ (typically $R = 1.2$ to $1.5 R_{min}$).
4. Compute $X$ and $Y$, then read off $N$ from the Gilliland chart/correlation.

## 3.4 Packed Column Height via HTU Method

**Starting from the differential mass balance on the gas phase** for component transferred across a differential height $dZ$:

$$d(V y) = K_{y} a (y^* - y) \, dZ$$

For constant molar flow, $V = $ const:

$$V \, dy = K_{y} a (y^* - y) \, dZ$$

**Definition of HTU:**

$$HTU_{OG} = \frac{V}{K_{y} a}$$

**Step 1 — Substitute:**

$$dZ = \frac{V}{K_y a} \cdot \frac{dy}{y^* - y} = HTU_{OG} \cdot \frac{dy}{y^* - y}$$

**Step 2 — Integrate from $y_2$ (bottom) to $y_1$ (top):**

$$Z = HTU_{OG} \int_{y_2}^{y_1} \frac{dy}{y^* - y} = HTU_{OG} \cdot N_{OG}$$

**Step 3 — For dilute systems** (linear equilibrium, $y^* = m x$ and constant $L$):

$$N_{OG} = \frac{(R - m V/L)}{(R + 1)} \cdot \ln\left[ \frac{(R+1)(x_D - x^*)}{(R+1)(x_F - x^*)} \right] + \ln\left[\frac{x_F - x^*}{x_W - x^*}\right]$$

(Colburn equation for absorption-type transfer.)

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: When to Use What?

| Design Need | Method | Reference Equation |
|---|---|---|
| Binary, graphical | McCabe–Thiele pinch | $R_{min} = (x_D - y^*_F)/(y^*_F - x_F)$ |
| Binary, stage count vs. R | McCabe–Thiele stepping | Step-by-step |
| Multicomponent, $R_{min}$ | Underwood | $\sum q z_i/(\alpha_i - \theta) = 1$ |
| Multicomponent, $N_{min}$ | Fenske | $\ln$-form with relative volatilities |
| Multicomponent, $N$ at chosen $R$ | Gilliland | $Y = 0.75(1 - X^{0.5668})$ |
| Packed column, rigorous | HTU / HETP | $Z = HTU_{OG} \cdot N_{OG}$ |
| Vacuum/trace components | Rate-based model | Maxwell–Stefan or two-film |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Pinch Point and Operating-Line Geometry (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Equilibrium Curve y* vs x"]:::curve
    nodeB["Rectifying Operating Line slope R / R+1"]:::rect
    nodeC["Stripping Operating Line slope Lprime / Vprime"]:::strip
    nodeD["q-line passes through Feed Point xF yF"]:::qline
    nodeE["Pinch Point: intersection on equilibrium curve"]:::pinch
    nodeF["Diagonal x = y reference line"]:::diag
    nodeG["Rmin Condition: infinite stages required"]:::rcond
    nodeH["Total Reflux Condition: minimum stages Nmin"]:::tcond
    nodeB --> nodeE
    nodeC --> nodeE
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeA --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeG
    nodeA --> nodeH
    nodeF --> nodeA

    classDef curve fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,color:#0B5394
    classDef rect fill:#FCE5CD,stroke:#E69138,color:#7F6000
    classDef strip fill:#D9EAD3,stroke:#6AA84F,color:#274E13
    classDef qline fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#BF9000,color:#7F6000
    classDef pinch fill:#F4CCCC,stroke:#CC0000,color:#990000
    classDef diag fill:#CFE2F3,stroke:#3D85C6,color:#073763
    classDef rcond fill:#D9D2E9,stroke:#674EA7,color:#351C75
    classDef tcond fill:#D9D2E9,stroke:#674EA7,color:#351C75
```

## 4.2 Packed Column Rectification Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["Feed F at thermal condition q enters column"]:::feed
    node2["Vapor V rises upward through packing"]:::vapor
    node3["Liquid L flows downward over packing surface"]:::liquid
    node4["Packing elements provide interfacial area a"]:::pack
    node5["Mass Transfer dVdy equals Ky a ystar minus y dZ"]:::mt
    node6["Distillate D leaves top with composition xD"]:::dist
    node7["Reflux L returned to top of column"]:::reflux
    node8["Bottoms W leaves bottom with composition xW"]:::bottoms
    node9["Reboiler generates vapor Vprime entering bottom"]:::reboil
    node10["Total height Z equals HTUog times Nog"]:::htu
    node11["Equivalent HETP approach Z equals HETP times Nt"]:::hetp
    node1 --> node2
    node1 --> node3
    node2 --> node4
    node3 --> node4
    node4 --> node5
    node5 --> node6
    node5 --> node8
    node6 --> node7
    node7 --> node3
    node8 --> node9
    node9 --> node2
    node5 --> node10
    node5 --> node11

    classDef feed fill:#FCE5CD,stroke:#E69138,color:#7F6000
    classDef vapor fill:#F4CCCC,stroke:#CC0000,color:#990000
    classDef liquid fill:#CFE2F3,stroke:#3D85C6,color:#073763
    classDef pack fill:#D9D2E9,stroke:#674EA7,color:#351C75
    classDef mt fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#BF9000,color:#7F6000
    classDef dist fill:#D9EAD3,stroke:#6AA84F,color:#274E13
    classDef reflux fill:#D9EAD3,stroke:#6AA84F,color:#274E13
    classDef bottoms fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,color:#0B5394
    classDef reboil fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,color:#0B5394
    classDef htu fill:#FCE5CD,stroke:#E69138,color:#7F6000
    classDef hetp fill:#FCE5CD,stroke:#E69138,color:#7F6000
```

## 4.3 Gilliland Iteration Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA["Step 1: Specify Feed zF and product specs xD xW"]:::s1
    stepB["Step 2: Compute alpha relative volatility for all components"]:::s2
    stepC["Step 3: Find Underwood root theta between alpha HK and alpha LK"]:::s3
    stepD["Step 4: Calculate Rmin from Underwood expression"]:::s4
    stepE["Step 5: Calculate Nmin from Fenske equation"]:::s5
    stepF["Step 6: Choose R typically 1.2 to 1.5 times Rmin"]:::s6
    stepG["Step 7: Compute Y and X for Gilliland"]:::s7
    stepH["Step 8: Read N from Gilliland correlation or chart"]:::s8
    stepI["Step 9: For packed column compute HTU and integrate for Nog"]:::s9
    stepA --> stepB --> stepC --> stepD --> stepF
    stepA --> stepE --> stepF
    stepF --> stepG --> stepH
    stepH --> stepI

    classDef s1 fill:#FCE5CD,stroke:#E69138,color:#7F6000
    classDef s2 fill:#FCE5CD,stroke:#E69138,color:#7F6000
    classDef s3 fill:#FCE5CD,stroke:#E69138,color:#7F6000
    classDef s4 fill:#FCE5CD,stroke:#E69138,color:#7F6000
    classDef s5 fill:#FCE5CD,stroke:#E69138,color:#7F6000
    classDef s6 fill:#D9EAD3,stroke:#6AA84F,color:#274E13
    classDef s7 fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#BF9000,color:#7F6000
    classDef s8 fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#BF9000,color:#7F6000
    classDef s9 fill:#D9D2E9,stroke:#674EA7,color:#351C75
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Schematic Interpretation:** The pinch-point diagram shows that as the reflux ratio is reduced toward $R_{min}$, the operating lines rotate about the intersection point with the q-line until they are tangent to the equilibrium curve. Beyond that, no separation is feasible at finite stages.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**  
Define minimum reflux ratio. Explain its physical significance in distillation column design with the help of a McCabe–Thiele diagram.

**Model Answer:**  
The minimum reflux ratio $R_{min}$ is the lowest value of $L/D$ at which a desired separation between distillate composition $x_D$ and bottoms composition $x_W$ is just feasible. Physically, at $R = R_{min}$, the rectifying and stripping operating lines and the q-line all meet on the equilibrium curve, producing a *pinch point* where the driving force $(y^* - y)$ is zero. Since the composition does not change across this region, the column would require an **infinite number of stages** to achieve the specification. The actual operating reflux is taken as $R = 1.2$ to $1.5 R_{min}$ as a compromise between energy cost and capital cost. *(3 marks: definition 1, pinch point 1, infinite stages 1)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted to 2024 Scheme]**  
Differentiate between HETP and HTU methods for the design of a rectification packed column.

**Model Answer:**  

| Aspect | HETP Method | HTU Method |
|---|---|---|
| Concept | Each theoretical plate is replaced by an equivalent height of packing | Differential mass transfer integrated along the height |
| Design parameter | HETP (m) — Height Equivalent to a Theoretical Plate | $HTU_{OG}$ (m) — Height of an Overall Transfer Unit |
| Coupled with | Number of theoretical stages $N_t$ from McCabe–Thiele | Number of transfer units $N_{OG}$ from driving-force integral |
| Equation | $Z = HETP \times N_t$ | $Z = HTU_{OG} \times N_{OG}$ |
| Best suited for | Packings with well-defined "stage" behavior (e.g., structured) | Continuous-contact packings (e.g., random Pall rings) |

*(3 marks: HETP definition 1, HTU definition 1, contrast 1)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024 ESE):** Answer **ONE** full question from each pair. Each long question carries sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each.

### Question A (14 Marks) — Multicomponent Minimum Reflux

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** A saturated liquid feed ($q = 1$) of composition $z_{LK} = 0.40$, $z_{HK} = 0.40$, $z_{LNK} = 0.20$ is to be separated. Relative volatilities are: $\alpha_{LK} = 2.5$, $\alpha_{HK} = 1.0$, $\alpha_{LNK} = 5.0$. Distillate and bottoms compositions are $x_{D,LK} = 0.95$, $x_{D,HK} = 0.05$, $x_{D,LNK} \approx 0$. Determine the minimum reflux ratio $R_{min}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the system above, $N_{min}$ by Fenske is 8.2 stages. The operating reflux is $R = 1.4 R_{min}$. Using the Gilliland correlation, determine the actual number of stages $N$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Step 1 — Find Underwood root $\theta$:**

The Underwood equation for $q = 1$:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{3} \frac{z_i}{\alpha_i - \theta} = 1$$

$$\frac{0.20}{5.0 - \theta} + \frac{0.40}{2.5 - \theta} + \frac{0.40}{1.0 - \theta} = 1$$

**Step 2 — Trial: $\theta = 0.7$:**

$$\frac{0.20}{4.3} + \frac{0.40}{1.8} + \frac{0.40}{0.3} = 0.0465 + 0.2222 + 1.3333 = 1.602 \neq 1$$

**Step 3 — Trial: $\theta = 0.4$:**

$$\frac{0.20}{4.6} + \frac{0.40}{2.1} + \frac{0.40}{0.6} = 0.0435 + 0.1905 + 0.6667 = 0.9007 \approx 0.9$$

**Step 4 — Trial: $\theta = 0.36$:**

$$\frac{0.20}{4.64} + \frac{0.40}{2.14} + \frac{0.40}{0.64} = 0.0431 + 0.1869 + 0.6250 = 0.855$$

**Step 5 — Refine by interpolation between $\theta = 0.4$ (0.9) and $\theta = 0.7$ (1.6):**

$$\theta \approx 0.4 + (0.9 - 1.0)/(0.9 - 1.6) \times 0.3 = 0.4 + 0.0435 = 0.444$$

**Step 6 — Verify at $\theta = 0.44$:**

$$\frac{0.20}{4.56} + \frac{0.40}{2.06} + \frac{0.40}{0.56} = 0.0439 + 0.1942 + 0.7143 = 0.9524 \approx 0.95$$

Accepting the root $\theta \approx 0.44$ (between $\alpha_{HK}=1.0$ and $\alpha_{LK}=2.5$ — condition satisfied). *(3 marks)*

**Step 7 — Calculate $R_{min}$:**

$$R_{min} = \sum_{i} \frac{\alpha_i x_{D,i}}{\alpha_i - \theta} - 1$$

With $x_{D,LNK} \approx 0$, $x_{D,LK} = 0.95$, $x_{D,HK} = 0.05$:

$$R_{min} = \frac{5.0 \times 0}{5.0 - 0.44} + \frac{2.5 \times 0.95}{2.5 - 0.44} + \frac{1.0 \times 0.05}{1.0 - 0.44} - 1$$

$$R_{min} = 0 + \frac{2.375}{2.06} + \frac{0.05}{0.56} - 1 = 1.1529 + 0.0893 - 1$$

$$\boxed{R_{min} \approx 0.242}$$

*(4 marks: 1 substitution, 1 numerical, 1 result, 1 unit/note)*

**(b) Step 1 — Compute $R$:**

$$R = 1.4 \times R_{min} = 1.4 \times 0.242 = 0.3388$$

**Step 2 — Compute Gilliland $Y$:**

$$Y = \frac{R - R_{min}}{R + 1} = \frac{0.3388 - 0.242}{0.3388 + 1} = \frac{0.0968}{1.3388} = 0.0723$$

**Step 3 — Invert Gilliland to find $X$:** Given $Y = 0.75(1 - X^{0.5668})$:

$$1 - X^{0.5668} = \frac{0.0723}{0.75} = 0.0964$$

$$X^{0.5668} = 0.9036$$

$$X = 0.9036^{1/0.5668} = 0.9036^{1.7642}$$

Using logarithms: $\ln X = 1.7642 \times \ln 0.9036 = 1.7642 \times (-0.10135) = -0.1788$

$$X = e^{-0.1788} = 0.8363$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $N$:**

$$X = \frac{N - N_{min}}{N + 1} = 0.8363$$

$$N - 8.2 = 0.8363 (N + 1)$$

$$N - 8.2 = 0.8363 N + 0.8363$$

$$N - 0.8363 N = 8.2 + 0.8363$$

$$0.1637 N = 9.0363$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 55.2 \text{ stages} \approx 56 \text{ stages (round up)}}$$

*(7 marks: 1 R-compute, 1 Y-compute, 1 invert, 1 X-compute, 1 N-compute, 1 result, 1 round-up note)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Rectification in Packed Columns (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** A rectification column is packed with 25 mm Pall rings. The system has $HTU_{OG} = 0.45$ m. The operating line has slope $L/V = 0.6$ and the equilibrium relation is $y^* = 0.7x$. The inlet gas is $y_1 = 0.10$ (bottom) and outlet gas is $y_2 = 0.02$ (top). Calculate the height of packing required. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a neat sketch the operating principle of a rectification section in a packed column. Discuss the role of maldistribution and how it affects HETP. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Step 1 — Identify the integration:**

$$N_{OG} = \int_{y_2}^{y_1} \frac{dy}{y^* - y} = \int_{0.02}^{0.10} \frac{dy}{0.7 x - y}$$

**Step 2 — Material balance to express $x$ in terms of $y$:**

Operating line: $y = (L/V) x + (D/V) y_{D}$. For a stripping-type or rectifying section with constant slopes, $L/V = 0.6 \Rightarrow x = (y - y_{intercept})/0.6$. The intercept corresponds to top of section. For this problem we use the simplification that $L/V = 0.6$ implies $V/L = 1.667$, and the Colburn-type analytical form for **absorption** with linear equilibrium $y^* = m x$:

$$N_{OG} = \frac{1}{(L/mV) - 1} \ln\left[\frac{(1 - mV/L)y_1 - y_2^*}{(1 - mV/L)y_2 - y_2^*}\right]$$

But since the problem does not provide the inlet liquid composition directly, we use the integral form with a *linear* relationship between $y$ and $x$ through the operating line $x = a y + b$ where $a = 1/(L/V) = 1.667$ and intercept from material balance $b$.

**Step 3 — Simplification (practical KTU approach):**

For a column where the operating and equilibrium lines are both linear, $N_{OG}$ can be expressed using the Kremser equation:

$$N_{OG} = \frac{y_1 - y_2}{(y^* - y)_{lm}}$$

where the log-mean driving force is computed from the driving forces at top and bottom of the section.

**Step 4 — Assume inlet liquid $x_2 = 0.005$ (top), $x_1 = 0.15$ (bottom). Then $y^*_2 = 0.7 \times 0.005 = 0.0035$, $y^*_1 = 0.7 \times 0.15 = 0.105$.**

Driving forces:
- Top: $y^*_2 - y_2 = 0.0035 - 0.02 = -0.0165$ (impossible; revise)
- Top: $y_2 - y^*_2 = 0.02 - 0.0035 = 0.0165$
- Bottom: $y_1 - y^*_1 = 0.10 - 0.105 = -0.005$ (impossible; revise)

**Step 5 — Recompute with $x_2 = 0.030$, $x_1 = 0.140$:**

- $y^*_2 = 0.7 \times 0.030 = 0.021$, driving force $= 0.02 - 0.021 = -0.001$ (still inconsistent)

**Step 6 — Use the rigorous approach via $m = 0.7$, $L/V = 0.6$, $L/mV = 0.857 > 1$ (pinch on vapor side), so the Kister form applies:**

$$N_{OG} = \frac{1}{1 - L/mV} \ln\left[\left(1 - \frac{L}{mV}\right)\frac{y_1 - m x_2}{y_2 - m x_2} + \frac{L}{mV}\right]$$

Assuming $x_2 = 0$ (pure vapor entering — gives a robust KTU answer):

$$N_{OG} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.857} \ln\left[(1 - 0.857)\frac{0.10 - 0}{0.02 - 0} + 0.857\right]$$

$$= \frac{1}{0.143} \ln\left[0.143 \times 5 + 0.857\right] = \frac{1}{0.143} \ln[1.572]$$

$$= 6.993 \times 0.4520 = 3.16 \text{ transfer units}$$

**Step 7 — Packing height:**

$$Z = HTU_{OG} \times N_{OG} = 0.45 \times 3.16$$

$$\boxed{Z \approx 1.42 \text{ m}}$$

*(7 marks: 1 method selection, 1 numerical substitution, 1 logarithms, 1 HTU, 1 multiplication, 1 result, 1 sanity check)*

**(b) Operating Principle & Maldistribution:** *(7 marks)*

The rectification section of a packed column operates with vapor rising and liquid descending counter-currently over the packing surface. The **driving force** for transfer of the more volatile component from the liquid to the vapor is $(y^* - y)$ where $y^*$ is the equilibrium vapor composition corresponding to the local liquid composition. As the vapor rises, it becomes progressively richer in the light key. The liquid descending becomes leaner.

**Maldistribution** refers to the non-uniform distribution of liquid across the packing cross-section. Causes include:

1. Poor initial liquid distributor design.
2. Packing geometry-induced channeling.
3. Wall effects in small-diameter columns.
4. Vapor maldistribution from the reboiler.

**Effect on HETP:** Maldistribution reduces the effective wetted area, decreases interfacial area $a$, and creates radial composition gradients. As a result, the actual HETP **increases** (worse performance). The scale-up rule of thumb is HETP roughly doubles when maldistribution becomes severe. The standard correction is to use a $Z$-factor (Cornell correlation) or to specify distributors with > 40 drip points per m² for random packings.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Underwood root bounds** — forgetting that $\theta$ must lie between $\alpha_{HK}$ and $\alpha_{LK}$; the wrong root gives physically impossible $R_{min}$ (negative or zero).
> 2. **Gilliland inversion** — students often forget the exponent 0.5668 and write $X = 1 - Y/0.75$, which is **wrong**. Always use $X = (1 - Y/0.75)^{1/0.5668}$.
> 3. **Pinch point at minimum reflux** — failure to identify that the operating lines meet *on* the equilibrium curve (not at the q-line intersection in $x$-$y$ space, but rather the q-line and equilibrium curve intersection).
> 4. **Sign of driving force in HTU** — for rectification (stripping from liquid), the integration is for $dy/(y^*-y)$; sign convention must be consistent. Always use $|y^* - y|$.
> 5. **HETP vs HTU units** — HETP is in meters per stage, while $N_{OG}$ is dimensionless. Mixing these up is a common 1–2 mark deduction.
> 6. **Forgetting q in Underwood** — when $q \neq 1$, students often default to $q = 1$; the correct form is $\sum q z_i / (\alpha_i - \theta) = 1$.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **$R_{min}$** is the reflux at which a separation is *just* feasible, requiring infinite stages.
- **Pinch point** is the geometric intersection of the operating lines, the q-line, and the equilibrium curve.
- **Binary $R_{min}$** = $(x_D - y^*_F)/(y^*_F - x_F)$ for saturated liquid feed.
- **Multicomponent $R_{min}$** is found via the **Underwood** equation; root $\theta$ must lie between the heavy and light key relative volatilities.
- **Fenske** gives $N_{min}$ at total reflux; **Gilliland** interpolates between $N_{min}$ and $R_{min}$ to give $N$ at chosen $R$.
- **Operating reflux** is typically $1.2 R_{min} \le R \le 1.5 R_{min}$ (economic optimum).
- **Packed columns** use $Z = HTU_{OG} \cdot N_{OG}$ or $Z = HETP \cdot N_t$.
- **HETP** depends strongly on maldistribution, liquid rate, and physical properties (surface tension, viscosity).
- **Structured packings** generally have lower HETP (0.2–0.5 m) than random packings (0.4–1.0 m).
- **Maldistribution** can double the effective HETP — distributor design is critical.
- The **HTU** is *not* a constant; it varies along the column with flow rates and composition.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Height of Packed Towers. Azeotropic and Extractive Distillation (Qualitative Treatment Only). Problem Solving in Distillation Using Excel/MATLAB/SCILAB/Python - Only for Self Study/Microproject/Assignment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT501 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS II
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Height of Packed Towers. Azeotropic and Extractive Distillation (Qualitative Treatment Only). Problem Solving in Distillation Using Excel/MATLAB/SCILAB/Python - Only for Self Study/Microproject/Assignment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Height of Packed Towers — Formal Definition

In continuous contact mass transfer equipment, the **Height of a Packed Tower (Z)** is the total vertical length of the packed section required to achieve a specified composition change in either the gas (vapour) or liquid phase. Unlike plate columns, packed columns do not have discrete equilibrium stages; mass transfer occurs continuously over the height. The height is therefore calculated using the **HTU–NTU concept** (Height of a Transfer Unit × Number of Transfer Units) or the **HETP concept** (Height Equivalent to a Theoretical Plate × Number of theoretical stages from McCabe–Thiele construction).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The two accepted design philosophies for packed column height are:
> 1. **HTU–NTU Method** (rate-based, continuous-contact model) — used for absorption, stripping, humidification.
> 2. **HETP Method** (equilibrium-stage analogy) — used when packed-column performance is reported as HETP values, typically for distillation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — A "Marble Slide"

Imagine a long, tilted marble slide divided into friction zones. Each zone is a "transfer unit" — a slice of length over which the marble loses a *fixed fraction* of its remaining potential energy. The number of zones (NTU) and the length of each zone (HTU) together give the total slide length. A "shorter" zone (lower HTU = better mass-transfer coefficient) means you need fewer zones for the same drop. This is mathematically equivalent to saying **Z = HTU × NTU**.

### 1.3 Azeotropic Distillation — Intuition

Some liquid mixtures (e.g., ethanol–water at 95.6 mol % ethanol) form a **constant-boiling azeotrope** that cannot be separated by simple distillation. Azeotropic distillation adds a third component called an **entrainer** (e.g., benzene, cyclohexane, diethyl ether) that forms a *new* low-boiling azeotrope with one of the original components, effectively "smuggling" it overhead and breaking the original azeotrope.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key idea:** The entrainer does not need to alter relative volatility in the bulk — it just needs to form a heterogeneous (two-liquid-phase) azeotrope that decants and is easily recovered.

### 1.4 Extractive Distillation — Intuition

For close-boiling mixtures (e.g., **C₄ hydrocarbons**: n-butane / isobutane; or **BTX aromatics**: benzene/toluene), ordinary distillation needs an uneconomically large number of trays. Extractive distillation adds a **high-boiling, non-volatile solvent** (e.g., **NMP, sulfolane, furfural, phenol**) near the top of the column. The solvent selectively *hydrogen-bonds* or *polarity-interacts* with one component, **boosting the relative volatility (α)** of the other. The solvent exits the column bottom and is recovered in a separate solvent-recovery column.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key idea:** Extractive distillation *reshapes* the vapour–liquid equilibrium (VLE) curve, whereas azeotropic distillation *creates a new* azeotrope.

### 1.5 Standard Metrics to Remember

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| HETP | Height Equivalent to a Theoretical Plate | m |
| HₒG | Overall gas-phase HTU | m |
| HₒL | Overall liquid-phase HTU | m |
| NₒG, NₒL | Number of overall gas/liquid transfer units | dimensionless |
| Kᵧa, Kₓa | Overall volumetric mass-transfer coefficients | kmol/(s·m³·Δy or Δx) |
| Ω | Cross-sectional area of tower | m² |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Two Methods Exist — Operational Logic

- **HTU–NTU** is rooted in the **two-film theory** of Whitman (1923). It requires local mass-transfer coefficients (kₓ, kᵧ) and the effective interfacial area (a). It is preferred for **gas absorption and stripping** where dilute conditions apply.
- **HETP** is empirical. Manufacturers (e.g., Sulzer, Raschig, Pall) supply HETP values for their packings as a function of loading. It is preferred for **distillation columns** where the operating line is nearly straight and the equilibrium curve is mildly curved.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic — HTU–NTU Derivation (Gas-Phase Basis)

**Step 1 — Differential material balance on the gas phase over a slice dz:**

$$ G \, dy = K_y \, a \, \Omega \, (y - y^*) \, dz $$

where *G* is the molar gas flow rate (kmol/s), *y* is the operating gas-phase mole fraction, and *y\** is the gas composition in equilibrium with the bulk liquid composition *x* at that height.

**Step 2 — Isolate dz:**

$$ dz = \frac{G}{K_y \, a \, \Omega} \cdot \frac{dy}{y - y^*} $$

**Step 3 — Integrate over the entire packed height Z:**

$$ Z = \frac{G}{K_y \, a \, \Omega} \int_{y_1}^{y_2} \frac{dy}{y - y^*} $$

**Step 4 — Define HTU and NTU:**

The integrand factor is grouped into two separate terms:

$$ \boxed{Z = H_{oG} \cdot N_{oG}} $$

where

$$ H_{oG} = \frac{G}{K_y \, a \, \Omega} \quad \text{and} \quad N_{oG} = \int_{y_1}^{y_2} \frac{dy}{y - y^*} $$

**Step 5 — Why split?** Because *HₒG* depends mainly on hydrodynamics (flow rates, packing geometry, viscosity) and *NₒG* depends mainly on the *thermodynamic driving force* (equilibrium + operating line). This makes design modular.

### 2.3 Equivalent Forms for Liquid Phase and Individual Films

| Basis | Height Equation | HTU Definition | NTU Definition |
|---|---|---|---|
| Overall gas | $Z = H_{oG} N_{oG}$ | $H_{oG} = G / (K_y a \Omega)$ | $N_{oG} = \int dy/(y - y^*)$ |
| Overall liquid | $Z = H_{oL} N_{oL}$ | $H_{oL} = L / (K_x a \Omega)$ | $N_{oL} = \int dx/(x^* - x)$ |
| Individual gas film | $Z = H_G N_G$ | $H_G = G / (k_y a \Omega)$ | $N_G = \int dy/(y - y_i)$ |
| Individual liquid film | $Z = H_L N_L$ | $H_L = L / (k_x a \Omega)$ | $N_L = \int dx/(x_i - x)$ |

### 2.4 Resistances-in-Series (Add-Up) — The "Electrical Analogy"

The two film resistances and the overall resistance obey the same rules as resistors in series:

$$ \frac{1}{K_y} = \frac{1}{k_y} + \frac{m}{k_x} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \frac{1}{H_{oG}} = \frac{1}{H_G} + \frac{m G/L}{H_L} $$

$$ \frac{1}{K_x} = \frac{1}{k_x} + \frac{1}{m \, k_y} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \frac{1}{H_{oL}} = \frac{1}{H_L} + \frac{L/(mG)}{H_G} $$

where *m* is the local slope of the equilibrium line $y^* = m x$ (constant for dilute systems).

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Favourite:** If asked "Which phase controls the resistance?", compare $1/H_G$ vs $(mG/L)/H_L$. The larger term is the controlling resistance — usually the gas film for absorption of a sparingly-soluble gas (ammonia in water), and the liquid film for absorption of a very soluble gas (HCl in water).

### 2.5 Kremser Equations — Analytical Shortcut for Dilute Systems

When the operating line and equilibrium line are straight (constant *L*, *G*, *m*):

$$ N_{oG} = \frac{\left(\frac{y_1 - m x_2}{y_2 - m x_2}\right) \left(1 - \frac{mG}{L}\right) + \left(\frac{mG}{L}\right) - 1}{\ln\left(\frac{mG}{L}\right) - \ln\left(\frac{y_1 - m x_2}{y_2 - m x_2}\right) \cdot \ldots} $$

The cleaner final form most often used:

$$ N_{oG} = \frac{y_1 - y_2^*}{(y - y^*)_{lm}} $$

with log-mean driving force:

$$ (y - y^*)_{lm} = \frac{(y_1 - y_1^*) - (y_2 - y_2^*)}{\ln\left[\frac{y_1 - y_1^*}{y_2 - y_2^*}\right]} $$

**Dilute-absorber Kremser closed form:**

$$ \frac{y_1 - m x_2}{y_2 - m x_2} = \frac{A^{N_{oG}+1} - A}{A^{N_{oG}+1} - 1} \quad \text{where} \quad A = \frac{L}{mG} = \text{Absorption Factor} $$

### 2.6 HETP ↔ HTU Conversion

When *m* and *G/L* are constant over the tower:

$$ HETP = H_{oG} \cdot \frac{\ln\left(\frac{mG}{L}\right)}{\left(\frac{mG}{L}\right) - 1} $$

> [!NOTE]
> For a constant-pressure column where the operating and equilibrium lines are very close to parallel, *HETP* ≈ *HₒG* ≈ *HₒL*.

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Z = H_{oG} N_{oG} = H_{oL} N_{oL} = HETP \cdot N_{th}$ | Universal height-of-tower relations |
| 2 | $H_{oG} = G / (K_y a \Omega)$ | HTU definition — gas basis |
| 3 | $H_{oL} = L / (K_x a \Omega)$ | HTU definition — liquid basis |
| 4 | $N_{oG} = \int dy / (y - y^*)$ | NTU definition — gas basis |
| 5 | $N_{oL} = \int dx / (x^* - x)$ | NTU definition — liquid basis |
| 6 | $1/H_{oG} = 1/H_G + (mG/L)/H_L$ | Resistances in series — gas basis |
| 7 | $1/H_{oL} = 1/H_L + (L/mG)/H_G$ | Resistances in series — liquid basis |
| 8 | $A = L / (mG)$ | Absorption factor (dimensionless) |
| 9 | $A^{N_{oG}+1}$ form | Kremser equation for dilute absorber |
| 10 | $HETP = H_{oG} \cdot \ln(mG/L) / [(mG/L) - 1]$ | Conversion between HETP and HTU |

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Ammonia absorption in water** (fertilizer industry): design uses *HₒG* with gas-film control.
- **CO₂ capture in amine solutions** (CCUS, post-combustion): *HₒL* is critical because CO₂ reacts in the liquid film.
- **Ethanol dehydration by azeotropic distillation** with cyclohexane: produces fuel-grade ethanol for E10/E85 blending.
- **BTX separation** (Benzene/Toluene/Xylene) by extractive distillation with sulfolane: petroleum refining pillar.
- **Natural-gas sweetening** (removal of H₂S/CO₂ using MDEA) and **air-separation by pressure-swing adsorption** all rely on packed-tower height calculations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of HTU–NTU from Differential Mass Balance

Consider a counter-current packed absorber with:
- Gas (G) flowing upward, molar flow rate *G* (kmol/s)
- Liquid (L) flowing downward, molar flow rate *L* (kmol/s)
- The solute is A, dilute in the carrier gas

**Step 1 — Pick a differential slice of height *dz* at arbitrary location:**

At this slice, the gas composition is *y* and the bulk liquid composition is *x*. The gas-side interfacial composition is *yᵢ*, the liquid-side interfacial composition is *xᵢ*, and the gas in equilibrium with *x* is *y\** (i.e., $y^* = m x$).

**Step 2 — Apply steady-state mass balance to the gas phase across *dz*:**

The solute entering from below in the gas is $G y$ at the bottom of the slice; the solute leaving above is $G (y + dy)$. The flux *out of the gas* into the liquid over the slice is:

$$ N_A \, a \, \Omega \, dz = K_y (y - y^*) \, a \, \Omega \, dz $$

Mass balance:

$$ G y = G (y + dy) + K_y (y - y^*) \, a \, \Omega \, dz $$

$$ \Rightarrow \quad G \, dy = -K_y (y - y^*) \, a \, \Omega \, dz $$

**Step 3 — Note the sign convention and rearrange to positive form:**

The gas is losing solute as it rises, so dy is negative as z increases. Taking the absolute magnitude:

$$ G \cdot (-dy) = K_y (y - y^*) \, a \, \Omega \, dz $$

$$ dz = \frac{G}{K_y \, a \, \Omega} \cdot \frac{dy}{y - y^*} $$

**Step 4 — Integrate from the bottom of the tower (where gas enters at $y_1$ and $z = 0$) to the top (where gas leaves at $y_2$ and $z = Z$):**

$$ \int_0^Z dz = \frac{G}{K_y \, a \, \Omega} \int_{y_1}^{y_2} \frac{dy}{y - y^*} $$

**Step 5 — Recognise the two key engineering groups:**

$$ \boxed{Z = \underbrace{\frac{G}{K_y \, a \, \Omega}}_{H_{oG}} \cdot \underbrace{\int_{y_1}^{y_2} \frac{dy}{y - y^*}}_{N_{oG}}} $$

> **Physical Meaning:** $H_{oG}$ is the *height of one transfer unit* — a slice over which the gas composition changes by an amount equal to the average driving force. $N_{oG}$ is the *number of such slices* required for the entire composition change.

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — Packed Ammonia Absorber

**Given (a typical KTU-style problem):**

A packed column is to absorb NH₃ from an air–NH₃ mixture using water. The gas enters at $y_1 = 0.040$ (mole fraction NH₃) and leaves at $y_2 = 0.0010$. The inlet water is solute-free, $x_2 = 0$. The total gas flow $G = 0.030$ kmol/s, the liquid flow $L = 0.45$ kmol/s, the tower cross-section $\Omega = 0.20$ m², and the equilibrium relation is $y^* = 1.20 x$ (Henry's-law form). The overall mass-transfer coefficient $K_y a = 0.080$ kmol/(s·m³). Calculate the tower height.

**Solution — Step A: Compute the operating line**

Material balance (solute) from top to an arbitrary section:

$$ G y_1 + L x_2 = G y + L x \quad \Rightarrow \quad y = y_1 - \frac{L}{G}(x - x_2) $$

Substituting values:

$$ y = 0.040 - \frac{0.45}{0.030}(x - 0) = 0.040 - 15.0\, x $$

Note that $(L/G) = 15$ — this is a relatively concentrated system; m = 1.20. Since $L/(mG) = 15/1.20 = 12.5 \gg 1$, we are in the **liquid-film-controlled** regime? No — actually $mG/L = (1.20)(0.030)/0.45 = 0.080 \ll 1$, so the **gas-film** dominates. We will use $H_{oG}$.

**Solution — Step B: Compute the equilibrium line endpoints**

At the bottom (z = 0): $x_1 = (y_1 - 0.040)/(-15.0) = 0$ is NOT correct. Let us solve: $y_1 = 0.040 - 15 x_1 \Rightarrow x_1 = (0.040 - 0.040)/15 = 0$. Wait — at the top, $x_2 = 0$ corresponds to $y_1 = 0.040$. At the bottom, the gas leaves with $y_2 = 0.0010$, so $x_1 = (0.040 - 0.0010)/15 = 0.0390/15 = 0.0026$.

Equilibrium values at the two ends:

$$ y_1^* = m x_1 = 1.20 \times 0.0026 = 0.00312 $$
$$ y_2^* = m x_2 = 1.20 \times 0 = 0 $$

Driving forces at the two ends:

$$ \Delta y_{\text{top}} = y_1 - y_1^* = 0.040 - 0.00312 = 0.03688 $$
$$ \Delta y_{\text{bottom}} = y_2 - y_2^* = 0.0010 - 0 = 0.0010 $$

**Solution — Step C: Compute $N_{oG}$ using the log-mean driving force (Kremser) form**

$$ N_{oG} = \frac{(y_1 - y_2) - (y_1^* - y_2^*)}{\Delta y_{lm}} $$

$$ y_1 - y_2 = 0.040 - 0.0010 = 0.0390 $$
$$ y_1^* - y_2^* = 0.00312 - 0 = 0.00312 $$
$$ \Delta y_{lm} = \frac{0.03688 - 0.0010}{\ln(0.03688 / 0.0010)} = \frac{0.03588}{\ln(36.88)} = \frac{0.03588}{3.608} = 0.00994 $$

$$ N_{oG} = \frac{0.0390 - 0.00312}{0.00994} = \frac{0.03588}{0.00994} = 3.61 $$

**Solution — Step D: Compute $H_{oG}$**

$$ H_{oG} = \frac{G}{K_y a \, \Omega} = \frac{0.030}{0.080 \times 0.20} = \frac{0.030}{0.016} = 1.875 \text{ m} $$

**Solution — Step E: Compute the tower height Z**

$$ \boxed{Z = H_{oG} \times N_{oG} = 1.875 \times 3.61 = 6.77 \text{ m}} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** A 6.77-m packed section is reasonable for an industrial NH₃ absorber. We will add ~0.6–1.0 m for inlet liquid distribution and mist eliminator, so total shell height ≈ 8 m. The Kremser equation applies because *mG/L* = 0.080 and *L/(mG)* = 12.5 — both treated as constant.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Azeotropic vs Extractive Distillation

| Criterion | Azeotropic Distillation | Extractive Distillation |
|---|---|---|
| State of added agent | **Entrainer** (volatile, forms new azeotrope) | **Solvent** (high-boiling, non-volatile under operating conditions) |
| Effect on VLE | Creates a *new* heterogeneous azeotrope | *Alters* relative volatility (α) of original pair |
| Solvent recovery | Decanter + small entrainer-recovery column | Dedicated solvent-recovery column (reboiler-side) |
| Number of columns | 2 (main + entrainer recovery) | 2 (main + solvent recovery) |
| Heat of vaporization impact | Significant (entrainer vaporizes overhead) | Solvent stays liquid; smaller vapour load |
| Typical entrainer / solvent | Benzene, cyclohexane, diethyl ether | NMP, sulfolane, furfural, phenol, DMSO |
| Industrial example | Ethanol–water with cyclohexane | C₄ split (n/i-butane) with NMP; BTX with sulfolane |
| Feed location | Below the azeotrope-forming plate | Solvent fed near the **top**; feed in the middle |
| Operating pressure | Often near-atmospheric | Can be moderate vacuum to protect solvent |
| Sensitivity to feed composition | High (must operate close to azeotrope) | More forgiving (solvent stream is large) |
| Energy consumption | Higher (extra vaporization) | Lower (solvent not vaporized) |
| Criterion for agent selection | Forms two-liquid-phase (heterogeneous) azeotrope at low T | High boiling point, high selectivity, no reaction |

### 3.4 Self-Study/Microproject — Spreadsheet & Numerical Tools (Conceptual)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following is intended for the **Self Study / Microproject / Assignment** portion. Per the KTU 2024 scheme, this is not for ESE valuation. Use it to deepen practical competence.

#### 3.4.1 Microsoft Excel Approach

A typical microproject involves solving the **McCabe–Thiele construction** or the **Ponchon–Savarit method** in a spreadsheet:

1. Build an `Inputs` block: feed composition $z_F$, relative volatility $\alpha$, reflux ratio $R$, distillate composition $x_D$, bottoms composition $x_B$.
2. Build an `Equilibrium` table: $x$ from 0.01 to 0.99, $y^* = \alpha x / [1 + (\alpha - 1) x]$.
3. Build the **rectifying operating line** $y = (R/(R+1)) x + x_D/(R+1)$.
4. Build the **stripping operating line** by intersection of q-line with rectifying line.
5. **Iterative stepping**: starting from $x_D$, step down to $x_B$, counting stages.
6. For packed towers, convert $N_{th}$ to height using $Z = N_{th} \times HETP$.

#### 3.4.2 MATLAB / SCILAB / Python (Conceptual Outline)

- Use `fsolve` to compute the intersection of operating and equilibrium lines.
- Use a `while` loop to step stages until the desired $x_B$ is reached.
- Use `ode45` (MATLAB) / `odeint` (Python) to integrate the differential form for **packed tower** simulation:

$$ \frac{dy}{dz} = -\frac{K_y a \Omega}{G} (y - y^*) $$

- For **azeotropic/extractive** VLE, modify the equilibrium relation to include a binary-interaction term (Wilson, NRTL, or UNIQUAC equation).
- For binary-azeotrope search: solve simultaneously for $x_{az}$ such that $y^* = x$ and $T_{\text{bubble}}(x_{az}) = T_{\text{dew}}(x_{az})$.

> [!TIP]
> **For self-study:** Plotting $y^*$ vs $x$ for the same binary at 1 atm and 5 atm is illuminating — pressure-sensitive azeotropes (e.g., THF/water) can be "broken" simply by changing pressure. This is sometimes a *third* alternative to extractive/azeotropic distillation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Flow — HTU–NTU Design Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Specify inlet and outlet gas / liquid compositions] --> B[Determine L and G from material balance]
    B --> C[Establish equilibrium relation y* = f x from VLE data]
    C --> D[Plot operating line and equilibrium curve]
    D --> E[Compute driving forces y - y* at both ends]
    E --> F{Use Kremser closed form OR graphical integration}
    F --> G[Compute N_oG from integral or closed-form]
    G --> H[Look up K_y a for chosen packing from vendor data]
    H --> I[Compute H_oG = G / K_y a Omega]
    I --> J[Multiply: Z = H_oG times N_oG]
    J --> K[Add 0.5 to 1.0 m for liquid distributor and end effects]
    K --> L[Specify packing height and tower shell height]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Azeotropic Distillation System (e.g., Ethanol Dehydration)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Feed: 95 mol percent ethanol-water near azeotrope] --> B[Azeotropic Column C1]
    B --> C[Overhead: Ethanol plus water plus cyclohexane heteroazeotrope at 64.85 C]
    B --> D[Bottom: Anhydrous ethanol product]
    C --> E[Condenser: Partial condensation]
    E --> F[Decanter: Two liquid phases form]
    F --> G[Aqueous phase returned to C1 as reflux]
    F --> H[Organic phase rich in cyclohexane returned to C1]
    D --> I[Product cooler and storage]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Extractive Distillation System (e.g., BTX Separation)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Feed: C4 mixture of n-butane and isobutane] --> B[Middle of Extractive Column C1]
    S[Solvent feed: NMP near top of C1] --> B
    B --> C[Overhead: Purified isobutane product]
    B --> D[Bottom: n-butane plus NMP solvent mixture]
    D --> E[SolvR[Solvent Recovery Column C2]]
    E --> F[Overhead: Purified n-butane product]
    E --> G[Bottom: Lean NMP solvent]
    G --> H[Cooler and pump]
    H --> S
```

### 4.4 Comparative Flow — Choice of Special Distillation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Binary mixture needing separation] --> B{Azeotrope present?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Does pressure swing break it?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Use Pressure-Swing Distillation: two columns at different P]
    C -- No --> E[Use Azeotropic Distillation with entrainer]
    B -- No --> F{Close boiling alpha less than 1.1?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Use Extractive Distillation with high-boiling solvent]
    F -- No --> H[Use ordinary distillation with adequate trays]
```

### 4.5 HTU-NTU Concept Visual (Functional)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Z = H_oG times N_oG] --> Q1[H_oG depends on: G, K_y a, Omega]
    P --> Q2[N_oG depends on: thermodynamics, operating and equilibrium lines]
    Q1 --> R1[Hydrodynamic block: packing, flow regime]
    Q2 --> R2[Thermodynamic block: VLE, driving force]
    R1 --> S[Combine: Total tower height Z]
    R2 --> S
```
