  3. **Emulsification/flooding regime** (high $I_v > 3$ cm/s) — fine emulsion forms, phases invert, throughput collapses, **must not operate here**. (1 mark)
- **Optimum operating range:** $I_v = 0.5$ to $2.5 \text{ cm/s}$ — corresponds to the dispersion regime. (1 mark)
- **Advantages over conventional sieve plate:** lower HETS (0.3–0.6 m), smaller axial mixing, no moving parts inside the column, handles systems with $\Delta \rho$ as low as **50 kg/m³**. (1 mark)

**(b) Numerical calculation (7 marks):**

Given: $A = 1.5 \text{ cm} = 0.015 \text{ m}$, $f = 1.2 \text{ Hz}$, $d_h = 4 \text{ mm} = 0.004 \text{ m}$, $\rho_c = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\sigma = 0.030 \text{ N/m}$, $\mu_d = 0.6 \text{ cP} = 0.6\times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}$, $\mu_c = 1.0 \text{ cP} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}$, $\Delta \rho = 120 \text{ kg/m}^3$.

**Step 1 — Compute pulse intensity:**

$$2Af = 2 \times 0.015 \times 1.2 = 0.036 \text{ m/s} = 3.6 \text{ cm/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute pulse Weber number (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
We_p &= \frac{\rho_c (2Af)^2 d_h}{\sigma} \\
&= \frac{1000 \times (0.036)^2 \times 0.004}{0.030} \\
&= \frac{1000 \times 1.296 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.004}{0.030} \\
&= \frac{5.184 \times 10^{-3}}{0.030} \\
&= 0.173 \\
\Rightarrow We_p &= 0.173 \quad &\text{(dimensionless)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Sauter mean drop diameter using Misek's correlation (4 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{32} &= 0.30 \, d_h \, We_p^{-0.6} \left(\frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho_c}\right)^{-0.10} \left(\frac{\mu_d}{\mu_c}\right)^{0.2} \\
&= 0.30 \times 0.004 \times (0.173)^{-0.6} \times \left(\frac{120}{1000}\right)^{-0.10} \times \left(\frac{0.6}{1.0}\right)^{0.2} \\
&= 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \times 3.054 \times 1.260 \times 0.944 \\
&= 4.36 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} \\
\Rightarrow d_{32} &\approx 4.36 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (frequently lost marks):**
> 1. Forgetting to convert $A$ from cm to m — loses **1 mark** immediately.
> 2. Using the wrong pulse-intensity definition ($Af$ instead of $2Af$) — makes $We_p$ off by a factor of 4.
> 3. Stopping the calculation at $d_h \cdot We_p^{-0.6}$ without including the **density-ratio and viscosity-ratio correction factors** — these last two factors are worth **1 mark each**.
> 4. Failing to comment on whether the resulting drop size (4.36 mm) is *physically reasonable* — comment that it is *larger than the hole diameter* (4 mm) and is *atypically high*, indicating the column is in the **mixer–settler regime** (low $I_v$ for this system). A typical industrial column would operate at higher $I_v$ to bring $d_{32}$ into the **0.5–1.5 mm** range.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Pulse Columns and Centrifugal Extractors**

- ☐ **Pulse intensity**: $I_v = 2Af$ — the single most important design variable in a pulsed column.
- ☐ **Three hydrodynamic regimes**: mixer-settler (low) → dispersion (optimum, $0.5$–$2.5 \text{ cm/s}$) → emulsification/flooding (high). Always design for the **dispersion regime**.
- ☐ **Misek drop-size correlation**: $d_{32} \propto I_v^{-1.2}$ — doubling $I_v$ shrinks drops by **2.3×**, multiplying interfacial area by the same factor.
- ☐ **Billerbeck flooding correlation**: flooding velocity $\propto I_v^{0.75}$ — design throughput must be **50–60% of flooding velocity** for safe operation.
- ☐ **HTU–NTU method** is preferred over the McCabe–Thiele for pulsed columns because of the continuous-contact (differential) nature of the device.
- ☐ **Centrifugal extractor** is the only practical choice when $\Delta \rho < 50 \text{ kg/m}^3$, when residence time < 30 s, or when solids are present.
- ☐ **Podbielniak (POD)**: 1 rotor = 3–5 theoretical stages; residence time 1–10 s; G-factor 500–5000. Applications: **penicillin**, lube-oil refining, vitamin recovery.
- ☐ **Alfa-Laval**: perforated rotating disc — used for edible oils, fish meal, pharmaceuticals.
- ☐ **Separation factor** $G = \omega^2 r / g$ — for $r = 0.3 \text{ m}$ and $N = 3000 \text{ rpm}$, $G \approx 1500$; for $N = 5000 \text{ rpm}$, $G \approx 4200$.
- ☐ **Selection rule** (recall exactly for ESE):
  - $\Delta \rho > 150$ → gravity column (sieve-plate, RDC).
  - $50 \leq \Delta \rho \leq 150$ → **pulsed column**.
  - $\Delta \rho < 50$ OR residence < 30 s OR solids → **centrifugal extractor**.
- ☐ **Advantages of pulsed column** over conventional sieve plate: 30–60% lower HETS, lower axial mixing, no internal moving parts, handles emulsifying systems better.
- ☐ **Disadvantages of centrifugal extractor**: high capital cost, high maintenance for high-speed seals, limited stage count per rotor (need multiple rotors in series for > 5 stages).
- ☐ **Common numerical traps** in KTU 14-mark problems: unit conversion (cm → m), use of $2Af$ vs $Af$, inclusion of all three correction factors in Misek, identifying the *physically reasonable* range of $N_{eq}$ (always 2–6 for one rotor).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Leaching - Rate of Leaching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT501 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS II
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Leaching - Rate of Leaching

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Leaching (Solid-Liquid Extraction):** Leaching is a diffusional mass transfer operation in which a soluble constituent (the **solute** or **leachable**) is preferentially extracted from a solid matrix (the **inert carrier** or **rafﬁnate**) by contact with a liquid solvent (the **extract** or **menstruum**). The insoluble residue that remains after the solute has been removed is termed the **spent solid** or **extracted solid**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rate of Leaching:** The rate of leaching is defined as the rate at which the soluble component is transferred from the solid phase into the bulk liquid phase, expressed per unit interfacial area, per unit time. Mathematically, it is the **molar flux** ($N_A$) of the solute crossing the solid–liquid interface, governed by combined internal and external mass transfer resistances.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine making a cup of **tea** in a tea bag. The hot water (solvent) is poured over the tea leaves (solid containing solute). The flavorful compounds (tannins, theaflavins) dissolve and diffuse out of the leaves into the water. Initially, you see a dark, concentrated stream jetting out of the bag (rapid external washing). Then, as the surface solutes are exhausted, the color seeps out more slowly — this is the **diffusion-controlled regime**, where the solute must travel from the *interior* of the leaf through tiny pores to reach the bulk water. The rate of leaching, therefore, is highest at the start (washing stage) and progressively decreases until equilibrium is reached (saturation). The same physical principle applies whether you are extracting sugar from sugar beets, oil from soybeans, copper from ores, or caffeine from coffee grounds.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

- **Diffusivity of solute in liquid ($D_{AB}$):** typically in the order of $\mathbf{10^{-9}\ m^2/s}$ to $\mathbf{10^{-10}\ m^2/s}$.
- **Equilibrium solubility ($C^*$ or $C_S$):** the maximum concentration of solute in the solvent at the operating temperature; expressed in $\mathbf{kg/m^3}$ or $\mathbf{kmol/m^3}$.
- **Mass transfer coefficient ($k_L$):** measured in $\mathbf{m/s}$.
- **Sherwood number ($Sh$):** dimensionless number characterizing convective mass transfer: $Sh = \frac{k_L \cdot d_p}{D_{AB}}$.
- **Schmidt number ($Sc$):** ratio of momentum to mass diffusivity: $Sc = \frac{\mu}{\rho D_{AB}}$.
- **Reynolds number ($Re$):** characterizes flow regime around particles: $Re = \frac{d_p \, v \, \rho}{\mu}$.
- **Solid-to-liquid ratio ($R$):** dimensionless, $R = \frac{\text{mass of inert solid}}{\text{mass of solvent}}$.

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners frequently test the **units** and **dimensionless groupings**. Memorizing the units of $D_{AB}$, $k_L$, and the meaning of $Sh$, $Sc$, and $Re$ is a guaranteed 2–3 mark pickup in Part A.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Stages of Leaching (Walker–Weiss–Lucas Framework)

The rate of leaching is not constant — it varies dramatically with time. The classical treatment by **Walker, Weiss, and Lucas (1923)** identified three distinct stages during the contact of a solvent with a porous solid:

**Stage I — Initial Penetration (Washing Stage)**
- The solvent first wets the solid surface and dissolves the **surface-adhered solute**.
- Mass transfer is **very rapid**; controlled by **convective mass transfer** in the external liquid film.
- The rate is essentially constant and high.
- Duration is short (typically a few seconds to a minute depending on agitation).
- Driving force: $(C^* - C_b) \approx C^*$ (since $C_b \approx 0$ at start).

**Stage II — Intermediate Unsteady-State Diffusion (Transition Stage)**
- The surface solute is depleted; solute must now diffuse from the **interior pores** of the solid to the surface.
- Mass transfer is governed by **molecular diffusion through the pore liquid** — described by Fick's Second Law.
- Rate **decays** with time in a predictable manner.
- This is the **rate-controlling** stage in most industrial leaching operations.

**Stage III — Final Slow Diffusion (Exhaustion Stage)**
- Concentration gradient within the particle becomes very small.
- Rate is **very slow**; further leaching becomes uneconomical.
- Process is terminated and solid is separated from the liquid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Stage II is the most important for design calculations. Most KTU numerical problems assume the system is in **Stage II** and use the integrated form of Fick's diffusion equation for a sphere, slab, or cylinder.

### 2.2 Why Rate of Leaching Changes — The Underlying Physical Mechanisms

- **Why does the rate slow down with time?**
  Because the **concentration gradient** within the solid $(C_{in} - C_{out})$ continuously decreases as the solid becomes depleted. Since flux is proportional to this gradient (Fick's Law), the flux must fall.
- **Why is agitation important?**
  Agitation increases the **external mass transfer coefficient** $k_L$ by thinning the hydrodynamic boundary layer around each particle. It also prevents particle settling and ensures uniform solvent distribution.
- **Why does smaller particle size accelerate leaching?**
  Smaller particles offer **larger surface area per unit mass** ($A \propto 1/d_p$) and **shorter internal diffusion paths** ($\ell \propto d_p$). Both effects increase the rate proportionally to $1/d_p^2$ in some correlations.
- **Why does temperature increase the rate?**
  Higher temperature → higher $D_{AB}$ (often by Stokes–Einstein relation), higher solubility $C^*$, and lower solvent viscosity $\mu$ — all of which enhance the rate.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description / Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|----------|----------------------|----------------|
| 1 | $N_A = k_L \, (C^* - C_b)$ | Rate based on overall liquid-side driving force | $\mathrm{kg/(m^2 \cdot s)}$ |
| 2 | $N_A = -D_{AB} \, \dfrac{dC_A}{dx}$ | Fick's First Law (diffusive flux) | $\mathrm{kg/(m^2 \cdot s)}$ |
| 3 | $\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \, \dfrac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial x^2}$ | Fick's Second Law (unsteady-state in solid) | $\mathrm{kg/(m^3 \cdot s)}$ |
| 4 | $\dfrac{M_t}{M_\infty} = 1 - \dfrac{6}{\pi^2} \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \dfrac{1}{n^2} \exp\left(-\dfrac{D \, n^2 \, \pi^2 \, t}{r_p^{\,2}}\right)$ | Fractional leaching from a sphere (infinite series) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\dfrac{M_t}{M_\infty} \approx \dfrac{6}{\sqrt{\pi}} \sqrt{\dfrac{D \, t}{r_p^{\,2}}} - 3 \dfrac{D \, t}{r_p^{\,2}}$ | Short-time approximation for sphere leaching (valid for $\dfrac{M_t}{M_\infty} < 0.4$) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\dfrac{M_t}{M_\infty} = 1 - \dfrac{6}{\pi^2} \exp\left(-\dfrac{\pi^2 D t}{r_p^{\,2}}\right)$ | Long-time approximation for sphere (valid for $\dfrac{M_t}{M_\infty} > 0.6$) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $Sh = 2 + 0.6 \, Re^{1/2} \, Sc^{1/3}$ | Ranz–Marshall correlation (single sphere in agitated liquid) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $k_L = \dfrac{Sh \cdot D_{AB}}{d_p}$ | Definition of mass transfer coefficient from Sherwood number | $\mathrm{m/s}$ |
| 9 | $D_{AB} = \dfrac{k_B T}{6 \pi \, \mu_B \, r_A}$ | Stokes–Einstein equation (estimate $D_{AB}$) | $\mathrm{m^2/s}$ |
| 10 | $\dfrac{1}{K_L} = \dfrac{1}{k_L} + \dfrac{m}{k_S}$ | Two-resistance model (overall = liquid + solid film) | $\mathrm{s/m}$ |
| 11 | $Bi_m = \dfrac{k_L \, d_p}{D_{eff}}$ | Biot number for mass transfer — ratio of internal to external resistance | dimensionless |
| 12 | $\text{Rate} = k_L \, A \, (C^* - C_b)$ | Macroscopic rate using interfacial area $A$ | $\mathrm{kg/s}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key abbreviation to memorize:** $M_t$ = mass of solute leached at time $t$; $M_\infty$ = mass of solute leached at infinite time (i.e., at equilibrium / total extractable amount); $r_p$ = radius of the spherical particle; $D_{eff}$ = effective diffusivity in the porous solid (usually $D_{eff} = \dfrac{\varepsilon \, D_{AB}}{\tau}$ where $\varepsilon$ is porosity and $\tau$ is tortuosity).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Beverage industry:** Extraction of sugar from sugar beets, tea, and coffee.
- **Pharmaceuticals:** Isolation of alkaloids (morphine, quinine) from plant matter.
- **Hydrometallurgy:** Recovery of copper, gold, and uranium from low-grade ores using cyanide/acid leaching.
- **Nuclear waste processing:** Selective leaching of radioactive isotopes from solidified waste matrices.
- **Edible oils:** Solvent extraction of soybean oil using hexane.
- **Environmental remediation:** Washing of contaminated soils.

The **rate equation** allows engineers to design the contact time, agitator power, solvent-to-solid ratio, and number of stages needed in a counter-current extractor (e.g., a Bollman, Hildebrandt, or Rotocel extractor).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Rate Equation for a Spherical Particle (Quasi-Steady-State Diffusion Through Stagnant Film)

We model a single spherical porous particle of radius $r_p$ immersed in a well-agitated solvent. The solvent outside the particle is in **turbulent motion**, but inside the particle pores the liquid is essentially **quasi-stagnant**. Solute must diffuse outward through the liquid-filled pores.

**Setting up the shell balance:**

Consider a thin spherical shell at radius $r$, thickness $dr$. Solute diffuses radially outward by molecular diffusion through the effective pore diffusivity $D_{eff}$.

By Fick's First Law at radius $r$:

$$
N_A \cdot 4\pi r^2 = -D_{eff} \cdot 4\pi r^2 \cdot \frac{dC}{dr}
$$

Cancel the area term $4\pi r^2$ from both sides:

$$
N_A = -D_{eff} \cdot \frac{dC}{dr}
$$

**Quasi-Steady-State Assumption (QSSA):** Since the particle is small and the diffusion path is short, the concentration profile adjusts rapidly compared to the overall depletion of solute. Thus $N_A$ is treated as constant at any instant.

Rearrange the equation:

$$
N_A \, dr = -D_{eff} \, dC
$$

**Apply boundary conditions:**
- At the particle surface ($r = r_p$): $C = C_{s}$ (concentration in solvent at solid surface $\approx C^*$, the saturation value).
- At the particle center ($r = 0$): $\dfrac{dC}{dr} = 0$ (symmetry, no flux at center).

Integrate from $r = r_p$ to $r = 0$:

$$
\int_{r_p}^{0} N_A \, dr = -D_{eff} \int_{C_s}^{C_0} dC
$$

Note: the integral on the left is the rate at the surface, but for a more rigorous form, we integrate from the **bulk solvent** (just outside, where $C = C_b$) to the surface. Let us use the **combined film + pore model** in the full form:

$$
\int_{r_p}^{\infty} N_A \, \frac{dr}{r^2} = -\int_{C_s}^{C_b} \frac{D(r)}{r^2} \cdot \text{(geometry term)} \cdot dC
$$

**Simplified two-resistance form (industry standard):**

The rate is governed by two resistances in series:
1. **External film resistance** (outside the particle): $R_{ext} = \dfrac{1}{k_L A}$
2. **Internal pore diffusion resistance** (inside the particle): $R_{int} = \dfrac{1}{k_S A}$ where $k_S$ is the internal (solid-side) mass transfer coefficient.

The total rate through area $A$ is:

$$
\text{Rate} = \frac{A \cdot (C^* - C_b)}{\dfrac{1}{k_L} + \dfrac{1}{k_S}} = K_L \, A \, (C^* - C_b)
$$

Where the **overall mass transfer coefficient** $K_L$ is given by:

$$
\frac{1}{K_L} = \frac{1}{k_L} + \frac{1}{k_S}
$$

For porous solids with mobile solvents, the solid-side resistance is usually the **limiting (dominant)** one.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Unsteady-State Solution for a Sphere (Fick's Second Law in Spherical Coordinates)

**Governing PDE** (radial diffusion in a sphere):

$$
\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D_{eff} \left[ \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial r^2} + \frac{2}{r} \frac{\partial C}{\partial r} \right]
$$

**Initial and Boundary Conditions:**
- IC: $C(r, 0) = C_i$ (initial uniform concentration inside solid)
- BC1: $C(r_p, t) = C_s$ (surface concentration = saturation, well-mixed solvent)
- BC2: $\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial r}\bigg|_{r=0} = 0$ (symmetry at center)

**Solution via Separation of Variables:**

Define a dimensionless concentration $\theta = \dfrac{C - C_s}{C_i - C_s}$.

The PDE transforms to:

$$
\frac{\partial \theta}{\partial t} = D_{eff} \left( \frac{\partial^2 \theta}{\partial r^2} + \frac{2}{r} \frac{\partial \theta}{\partial r} \right)
$$

With $\theta(r_p, t) = 0$, $\theta(r, 0) = 1$, $\left.\dfrac{\partial \theta}{\partial r}\right|_{r=0} = 0$.

The standard eigenfunction expansion solution is:

$$
\theta(r, t) = \frac{2}{r_p \, r} \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} (-1)^{n+1} \sin\left(\frac{n \pi r}{r_p}\right) \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{D_{eff} \, n^2 \pi^2 t}{r_p^2}\right)
$$

The **average concentration** inside the sphere is:

$$
\bar{\theta}(t) = \frac{1}{M_\infty} \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{12}{n^2 \pi^2} \sin^2(n\pi) \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{D_{eff} \, n^2 \pi^2 t}{r_p^2}\right)
$$

Since $\sin(n\pi) = 0$ for integer $n$, the leading term dominates and the standard result emerges:

$$
\frac{M_t}{M_\infty} = 1 - \frac{6}{\pi^2} \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n^2} \exp\left(-\frac{D_{eff} \, n^2 \pi^2 t}{r_p^2}\right)
$$

This is **Eq. (4)** from the Formula Sheet.

**Short-time limit** (keep only $n=1$ and linearize the exponential): for $\dfrac{D_{eff} t}{r_p^2} < 0.1$:

$$
\frac{M_t}{M_\infty} \approx \frac{6}{\sqrt{\pi}} \sqrt{\frac{D_{eff} t}{r_p^2}} - 3 \frac{D_{eff} t}{r_p^2}
$$

**Long-time limit** (only $n=1$ term retained): for $\dfrac{M_t}{M_\infty} > 0.6$:

$$
\frac{M_t}{M_\infty} \approx 1 - \frac{6}{\pi^2} \exp\left(-\frac{\pi^2 D_{eff} t}{r_p^2}\right)
$$

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A batch leaching experiment is conducted with spherical solid particles of radius $r_p = 2\ \mathrm{mm}$. The effective diffusivity of solute in the pores is $D_{eff} = 1.0 \times 10^{-9}\ \mathrm{m^2/s}$. The solvent is well-agitated and the surface concentration is maintained at $C_s = 0$. The initial uniform concentration in the solid is $C_i = 100\ \mathrm{kg/m^3}$. **Calculate the time required for the solid to be 50% leached and the leaching rate at that instant.**

**Solution — Step 1: Identify the regime**

For $M_t/M_\infty = 0.5$, we are in the **intermediate regime**, so neither short- nor long-time approximation is precise. Use the full series with $n = 1, 2, 3$ (higher terms negligible):

$$
0.5 = 1 - \frac{6}{\pi^2} \left[ \exp(-A) + \frac{1}{4} \exp(-4A) + \frac{1}{9} \exp(-9A) + \cdots \right]
$$

Where $A = \dfrac{\pi^2 D_{eff} t}{r_p^2}$.

Solve iteratively. Guess $A = 0.21$:

$$
\frac{6}{\pi^2}(0.81 + 0.205 \cdot 0.43 + 0.111 \cdot 0.15) \approx 0.609 \cdot (0.81 + 0.088 + 0.017) = 0.609 \cdot 0.915 = 0.557
$$

So $1 - 0.557 = 0.443$ — slightly under. Try $A = 0.18$:

$$
\frac{6}{\pi^2}(0.835 + 0.205 \cdot 0.487 + 0.111 \cdot 0.197) = 0.609 \cdot (0.835 + 0.100 + 0.022) = 0.609 \cdot 0.957 = 0.583
$$

$1 - 0.583 = 0.417$ — closer to 0.5. Try $A = 0.16$:

$$
0.609 \cdot (0.852 + 0.205 \cdot 0.527 + 0.111 \cdot 0.236) = 0.609 \cdot (0.852 + 0.108 + 0.026) = 0.609 \cdot 0.986 = 0.601
$$

$1 - 0.601 = 0.399$. Interpolation: at $A = 0.16$, $\eta = 0.399$; at $A = 0.18$, $\eta = 0.417$. We need $\eta = 0.5$... this is too low. Recheck — at $A = 0.10$:

$$
0.609 \cdot (0.905 + 0.205 \cdot 0.670 + 0.111 \cdot 0.406) = 0.609 \cdot (0.905 + 0.137 + 0.045) = 0.609 \cdot 1.087 = 0.662
$$

$1 - 0.662 = 0.338$. The function $1 - \frac{6}{\pi^2}\sum \frac{1}{n^2}e^{-n^2 A}$ increases monotonically with $A$. So 0.5 corresponds to an $A$ between 0.10 and 0.18. Interpolating linearly: $A \approx 0.10 + (0.18-0.10)\cdot\frac{0.5-0.338}{0.417-0.338} = 0.10 + 0.08 \cdot \frac{0.162}{0.079} \approx 0.10 + 0.164 = 0.264$. Let us recompute at $A = 0.26$:

$$
0.609 \cdot (0.771 + 0.205 \cdot 0.351 + 0.111 \cdot 0.092) = 0.609 \cdot (0.771 + 0.072 + 0.010) = 0.609 \cdot 0.853 = 0.520
$$

$1 - 0.520 = 0.480$. So $A \approx 0.245$ gives 0.5. Take $A \approx 0.24$:

$$
0.609 \cdot (0.787 + 0.205 \cdot 0.382 + 0.111 \cdot 0.114) = 0.609 \cdot (0.787 + 0.078 + 0.013) = 0.609 \cdot 0.878 = 0.535
$$

$1 - 0.535 = 0.465$. Hmm. The value $A = 0.21$ gave 0.557... wait, $1 - 0.557 = 0.443$ — I miscalculated earlier. Let me reconsider: we want the **fraction leached** $= M_t/M_\infty = 0.5$. So $1 - (\text{series sum scaled}) = 0.5$, i.e., series term = 0.5.

Recompute: we want $\frac{6}{\pi^2}\left[e^{-A} + \frac{1}{4}e^{-4A} + \frac{1}{9}e^{-9A} + \cdots\right] = 0.5$

At $A = 0.10$: $0.609 \cdot 1.087 = 0.662$ — too high.
At $A = 0.26$: $0.609 \cdot 0.853 = 0.520$ — too high.
At $A = 0.50$: $0.609 \cdot (0.607 + 0.205 \cdot 0.135 + 0.111 \cdot 0.011) = 0.609 \cdot (0.607 + 0.028 + 0.001) = 0.609 \cdot 0.636 = 0.387$ — too low.

So $A$ lies between 0.26 and 0.50. Interpolate: $A \approx 0.26 + 0.24 \cdot \frac{0.520 - 0.500}{0.520 - 0.387} = 0.26 + 0.24 \cdot 0.150 = 0.296$.

**Step 2: Compute time**

$$
t = \frac{A \cdot r_p^2}{\pi^2 \cdot D_{eff}} = \frac{0.296 \cdot (2 \times 10^{-3})^2}{\pi^2 \cdot 1.0 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{0.296 \cdot 4 \times 10^{-6}}{9.87 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 120\ \mathrm{s}
$$

**Step 3: Compute leaching rate at that time**

$$
\text{Rate} = -\frac{d M}{dt} = \frac{6 D_{eff} M_\infty}{r_p^2} \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} e^{-n^2 A} \approx \frac{6 \cdot 10^{-9} \cdot M_\infty}{(2 \times 10^{-3})^2} \cdot (0.745) = 1.12 \times 10^{-3} \cdot M_\infty\ \mathrm{kg/s}
$$

(assuming $M_\infty$ corresponds to the extractable mass per particle).

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU numericals usually ask for **leaching time for a specified % extraction** OR for **% extraction in a given time**. Use the appropriate asymptotic form (short- or long-time) based on the value of $Dt/r_p^2$.

### 3.4 Comparison of Mass Transfer Resistances

| Condition | Dominant Resistance | Controlling Step | Equation to Use |
|-----------|---------------------|------------------|-----------------|
| $Bi_m = k_L d_p / D_{eff} \ll 1$ | External film | Liquid-phase mass transfer | $N_A = k_L (C^* - C_b)$ |
| $Bi_m \gg 1$ | Internal pore diffusion | Solid-phase diffusion | Fick's law series (Eq. 4) |
| $Bi_m \approx 1$ | Both equally important | Two-resistance model | $1/K_L = 1/k_L + 1/k_S$ |
| Agitated vessel, coarse particles | Internal | Pore diffusion | Eq. 4, 5, or 6 |
| Agitated vessel, fine particles | External | Film diffusion | $k_L$ from Ranz–Marshall |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Three Stages of Leaching — Concentration Profile Evolution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0(["START: Fresh Solid + Pure Solvent"]) --> A1["Stage I: PENETRATION WASHING"]
    A1 --> A2["Duration: a few seconds"]
    A2 --> A3["Rate: VERY HIGH, nearly constant"]
    A3 --> A4["Mechanism: Surface dissolution + convective removal"]
    A4 --> A5["Bulk concentration: Cb rises rapidly"]
    A5 --> A6["Marker: Visual color/turbidity change stops"]
    A6 --> B1["Stage II: TRANSITION UNSTEADY DIFFUSION"]
    B1 --> B2["Duration: minutes to hours"]
    B2 --> B3["Rate: DECAYS with time"]
    B3 --> B4["Mechanism: Molecular diffusion inside pores"]
    B4 --> B5["Governing law: FICK'S SECOND LAW"]
    B5 --> B6["Design equation: M_t/M_inf series solution"]
    B6 --> C1["Stage III: FINAL SLOW DIFFUSION"]
    C1 --> C2["Duration: very long, uneconomical"]
    C2 --> C3["Rate: NEGLIGIBLE"]
    C3 --> C4["Mechanism: Residual internal concentration gradient"]
    C4 --> C5["Action: TERMINATE process, separate spent solid"]
    C5 --> D1(["END: Spent Solid + Extract Liquor"])
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Mass Transfer Resistances in Leaching

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A["SOLID PHASE INTERIOR"]
        S1["Bulk of solid particle C = Ci initial"]
        S2["Concentration gradient inside pores"]
        S3["Solid-liquid interface C = Cs saturation"]
    end
    subgraph B["LIQUID PHASE OUTSIDE"]
        L1["Hydrodynamic boundary film C rising"]
        L2["Bulk solvent well mixed C = Cb"]
    end
    R1["Internal diffusion resistance 1 over kS"] -.-> S2
    R2["External film resistance 1 over kL"] -.-> L1
    S1 -->|"Step 1: Internal diffusion"| S2
    S2 -->|"Step 2: Film diffusion"| S3
    S3 -->|"Step 3: Bulk mixing"| L1
    L1 --> L2
    R1 --> R2
    R2 --> E["Overall resistance 1 over KL dominates total rate"]
```

### 4.3 Process Flow of a Typical Batch Leaching Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Feed Preparation: crushing, grinding, sizing of solid"] --> P2["Mixing Tank: solid + solvent charged"]
    P2 --> P3["Agitation at controlled RPM and temperature"]
    P3 --> P4["Settling and filtration"]
    P4 --> P5["Recovery of extract liquor rich in solute"]
    P4 --> P6["Spent solid washed and discarded"]
    P5 --> P7["Downstream separation crystallization or evaporation"]
    P3 --> M1["Monitor: Samples drawn at intervals"]
    M1 --> M2["Plot: M t over M inf versus t curve"]
    M2 --> M3["Identify: Stage I II III regions"]
    M3 --> M4["Calculate: Diffusion coefficient D eff by fitting"]
```

### 4.4 Effect of Operating Variables on Leaching Rate (Schematic Cause-Effect)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    V1["INCREASE TEMPERATURE"] --> V1A["Higher D AB by Stokes Einstein"] --> V1B["FASTER leaching"]
    V2["DECREASE PARTICLE SIZE"] --> V2A["Larger specific area and shorter pore path"] --> V2B["MUCH FASTER leaching"]
    V3["INCREASE AGITATION RPM"] --> V3A["Thinner film, higher kL"] --> V3B["FASTER leaching initially"]
    V4["INCREASE SOLVENT TO SOLID RATIO"] --> V4A["Lower Cb, larger driving force"] --> V4B["FASTER leaching"]
    V5["INCREASE SOLUTE SOLUBILITY in solvent choice"] --> V5A["Higher C star"] --> V5B["FASTER leaching"]
    V1B --> OUT["RATE OF LEACHING"]
    V2B --> OUT
    V3B --> OUT
    V4B --> OUT
    V5B --> OUT
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Define the term "rate of leaching". What are the three stages of leaching as identified by Walker, Weiss, and Lucas?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Rate of leaching** is defined as the instantaneous mass (or moles) of soluble solute transferred from the solid phase to the liquid phase per unit time per unit interfacial area, expressed mathematically as $N_A = k_L (C^* - C_b)$ where $k_L$ is the mass transfer coefficient, $C^*$ is the saturation concentration of the solute, and $C_b$ is the bulk concentration of solute in the solvent.
>
> The **three stages** identified by Walker, Weiss, and Lucas are:
> 1. **Stage I – Penetration (Washing) stage:** Rapid, convective, surface-controlled dissolution. Solvent wets the solid and dissolves surface-adhered solute very quickly.
> 2. **Stage II – Intermediate unsteady-state diffusion stage:** The surface solute is depleted. Solute diffuses from the interior of the particle through pore liquid. This is the **rate-controlling** stage for design and is governed by **Fick's Second Law of diffusion**.
> 3. **Stage III – Final slow diffusion (exhaustion) stage:** Concentration gradient inside the solid is small; rate is very low and uneconomical. Process is terminated.

**(3 Marks: 1 for definition, 2 for the three stages with brief explanation)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any four factors that affect the rate of leaching and explain how each one influences the rate.**

**Model Answer:**

| Factor | Influence on Rate |
|--------|-------------------|
| 1. **Particle size** | Smaller particles → larger surface area per unit mass and shorter internal diffusion path → rate increases (often as $1/d_p^2$). |
| 2. **Temperature** | Higher temperature → higher $D_{AB}$ (Stokes–Einstein), higher solubility $C^*$, lower viscosity → rate increases. |
| 3. **Agitation speed** | Higher RPM → thinner hydrodynamic boundary film → higher $k_L$ → rate increases (mainly affects Stage I). |
| 4. **Solvent-to-solid ratio** | Higher ratio → lower $C_b$ → larger driving force $(C^* - C_b)$ → rate increases. |
| 5. **Solvent choice / Solute solubility** | Higher $C^*$ → larger driving force → rate increases. |

**(3 Marks: 1 for listing, 2 for proper explanation with cause-effect linkage)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice Pattern

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4, Internal Choice]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **three stages of leaching** based on the **concentration profile inside a solid particle** as a function of time. Discuss the **rate-controlling mechanism** in each stage. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A batch leaching experiment is conducted with **spherical solid particles of radius $r_p = 1.5\ \mathrm{mm}$**. The effective diffusivity of the solute inside the particle pores is $D_{eff} = 5 \times 10^{-10}\ \mathrm{m^2/s}$. The solid is initially at uniform concentration $C_i = 200\ \mathrm{kg/m^3}$ and the surface concentration is instantaneously dropped to zero by an excess of pure solvent. **Determine the time required to leach 60% of the solute from the particle.** Also, **comment on whether the long-time approximation is valid** for this extent of leaching. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

Refer to **Section 4.1** for the Mermaid diagram. The written explanation should cover:

| Stage | Duration | Mechanism | Rate Law |
|-------|----------|-----------|----------|
| I — Penetration (Washing) | Short (s) | Convective dissolution of surface solute | $N_A = k_L (C^* - C_b)$, constant |
| II — Transition Diffusion | Long (min–h) | Unsteady molecular diffusion in pores (Fick's 2nd Law) | Series solution $\dfrac{M_t}{M_\infty}$ |
| III — Final Slow Diffusion | Very long | Residual gradient becomes negligible | Exponentially decaying |

**Sketch:** A graph of $\dfrac{M_t}{M_\infty}$ vs $t$ showing a steep initial rise, a curved transition, and a flat tail.

> **Mark distribution (a):** Sketch + labeling = 2, Stage descriptions = 3, Rate law for each = 2.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:** $r_p = 1.5\ \mathrm{mm} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}\ \mathrm{m}$, $D_{eff} = 5 \times 10^{-10}\ \mathrm{m^2/s}$, $M_t/M_\infty = 0.60$.

**Step 1 — Check applicability of long-time approximation:**

Long-time approximation valid when $M_t/M_\infty > 0.6$. Here $M_t/M_\infty = 0.60$, which is borderline. We will use the **long-time approximation** (Eq. 6 from the Formula Sheet).

**Step 2 — Apply Eq. (6):**

$$
0.60 = 1 - \frac{6}{\pi^2} \exp\left(-\frac{\pi^2 D_{eff} t}{r_p^2}\right)
$$

Rearrange:

$$
\frac{6}{\pi^2} \exp\left(-\frac{\pi^2 D_{eff} t}{r_p^2}\right) = 0.40
$$

$$
\exp\left(-\frac{\pi^2 D_{eff} t}{r_p^2}\right) = \frac{0.40 \times \pi^2}{6} = 0.6579
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the time exponent:**

$$
-\frac{\pi^2 D_{eff} t}{r_p^2} = \ln(0.6579) = -0.4186
$$

$$
t = \frac{0.4186 \cdot r_p^2}{\pi^2 \cdot D_{eff}} = \frac{0.4186 \times (1.5 \times 10^{-3})^2}{\pi^2 \times 5 \times 10^{-10}}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical computation:**

Numerator: $0.4186 \times 2.25 \times 10^{-6} = 9.418 \times 10^{-7}$

Denominator: $9.8696 \times 5 \times 10^{-10} = 4.935 \times 10^{-9}$

$$
t = \frac{9.418 \times 10^{-7}}{4.935 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 191\ \mathrm{s} \approx 3.18\ \mathrm{minutes}
$$

**Step 5 — Validity check:**

Dimensionless time $\tau = \dfrac{D_{eff} t}{r_p^2} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{-10} \times 191}{(1.5 \times 10^{-3})^2} = \dfrac{9.55 \times 10^{-8}}{2.25 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.0424$

For long-time approximation, $\tau > 0.1$ is typically preferred. Since $\tau = 0.0424$, the long-time approximation is **slightly under-valid** here. The result $t \approx 191$ s is a **reasonable estimate**, but the actual time would be slightly longer (use the full series for higher accuracy).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently **forget to convert mm to m** (a unit error costs 1 mark), or they apply the **short-time approximation** to a 60% extraction (where it is invalid). Always check the value of $M_t/M_\infty$ first: $< 0.4$ → short-time, $> 0.6$ → long-time, in between → use the full series.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Starting from **Fick's Law of diffusion**, derive the **rate equation for leaching from a spherical particle** under the **quasi-steady-state assumption**. Clearly state the assumptions and the boundary conditions used. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** In an agitated batch leaching tank, **spherical oil seeds of diameter $2\ \mathrm{mm}$** are treated with a solvent. The properties are: $D_{eff} = 1.2 \times 10^{-9}\ \mathrm{m^2/s}$, solvent viscosity $\mu = 0.9 \times 10^{-3}\ \mathrm{Pa \cdot s}$, density $\rho = 870\ \mathrm{kg/m^3}$, and agitator tip speed $v = 0.5\ \mathrm{m/s}$. **Calculate the external mass transfer coefficient $k_L$ using the Ranz–Marshall correlation**, and hence **comment on whether the leaching is controlled by external film resistance or internal pore diffusion.** Assume $Sc = 500$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — State Fick's First Law:**

$$
N_A = -D_{eff} \frac{dC}{dr}
$$

**Step 2 — Geometric consideration for a sphere:**

The flux through a spherical shell at radius $r$ is constant (QSSA). The total molar rate through the shell is:

$$
\dot{n}_A = N_A \cdot 4\pi r^2 = \text{constant}
$$

So:

$$
N_A = \frac{\dot{n}_A}{4\pi r^2} = -D_{eff} \frac{dC}{dr}
$$

**Step 3 — Separate variables and integrate:**

$$
\dot{n}_A \int_{r_p}^{r_0} \frac{dr}{4\pi r^2} = -D_{eff} \int_{C_s}^{C_0} dC
$$

For a sphere where diffusion ends at the particle center (or more rigorously, from the bulk solvent to the surface), the integration gives the standard result:

$$
\dot{n}_A = \frac{4\pi D_{eff} \, r_p \, (C_s - C_0)}{1 - (r_p/r_0)} \approx 4\pi D_{eff} \, r_p \, (C_s - C_0)
$$

(assuming $r_0 \to \infty$, i.e., the bulk solvent is far away, or using a film-thickness model).

**Step 4 — Convert to flux form:**

$$
N_A = \frac{\dot{n}_A}{4\pi r_p^2} = \frac{D_{eff}}{r_p} (C_s - C_0) = k_S (C_s - C_b)
$$

where the **solid-side mass transfer coefficient** $k_S = D_{eff}/r_p$.

**Assumptions to list:**
1. Particle is spherical and isotropic.
2. Quasi-steady-state: concentration profile is established instantaneously.
3. Effective diffusivity $D_{eff}$ is constant (independent of concentration).
4. Pore structure is uniform.
5. Solvent at the surface is at saturation concentration $C_s$.

**Boundary conditions:**
- At $r = r_p$: $C = C_s$ (surface saturation)
- At $r = 0$: $\dfrac{dC}{dr} = 0$ (symmetry)

> **Mark distribution (a):** Fick's law setup = 2, integration = 3, assumptions and BCs = 2.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:** $d_p = 2 \times 10^{-3}\ \mathrm{m}$, $D_{eff} = 1.2 \times 10^{-9}\ \mathrm{m^2/s}$, $\rho = 870\ \mathrm{kg/m^3}$, $\mu = 0.9 \times 10^{-3}\ \mathrm{Pa \cdot s}$, $v = 0.5\ \mathrm{m/s}$, $Sc = 500$.

**Step 1 — Calculate Reynolds number:**

$$
Re = \frac{d_p \, v \, \rho}{\mu} = \frac{(2 \times 10^{-3})(0.5)(870)}{0.9 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{0.87}{9 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 967
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Sherwood number (Ranz–Marshall):**

$$
Sh = 2 + 0.6 \, Re^{1/2} \, Sc^{1/3} = 2 + 0.6 \cdot (967)^{0.5} \cdot (500)^{1/3}
$$

$$
Sh = 2 + 0.6 \cdot 31.1 \cdot 7.94 = 2 + 0.6 \cdot 246.9 = 2 + 148.1 \approx 150
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate $k_L$:**

$$
k_L = \frac{Sh \cdot D_{eff}}{d_p} = \frac{150 \cdot 1.2 \times 10^{-9}}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 9.0 \times 10^{-5}\ \mathrm{m/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate internal mass transfer coefficient $k_S$:**

$$
k_S = \frac{D_{eff}}{r_p} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{-9}}{1 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.2 \times 10^{-6}\ \mathrm{m/s}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare and conclude:**

External resistance: $1/k_L = 1.11 \times 10^{4}\ \mathrm{s/m}$
Internal resistance: $1/k_S = 8.33 \times 10^{5}\ \mathrm{s/m}$

Since $1/k_S \gg 1/k_L$ (by a factor of **~75**), the **internal pore diffusion is the rate-controlling step**. The leaching rate is governed by the slow diffusion of solute from the interior of the seed to its surface, not by the external film.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students **omit the "2"** in the Ranz–Marshall equation. The constant **"2"** represents the contribution of pure molecular diffusion in the limit of zero flow and **must not be dropped** for accurate results at low Reynolds numbers. Also, students often forget that $d_p$ is diameter (not radius) in the Ranz–Marshall equation, while $r_p$ (radius) is used in the Fickian diffusion model — mixing these up costs 1–2 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Rate of Leaching**

- [x] **Leaching** = solid–liquid extraction; solute moves from solid → liquid by diffusion.
- [x] **Three stages** of Walker–Weiss–Lucas: **Penetration (washing) → Unsteady diffusion → Final slow diffusion**.
- [x] **Stage II is rate-controlling** in most industrial operations.
- [x] **Driving force** = $(C^* - C_b)$, where $C^*$ is the saturation solubility.
- [x] **Rate equation** (overall): $N_A = k_L (C^* - C_b)$; with $k_L$ from Sherwood number.
- [x] **Ranz–Marshall correlation** (KTU favorite): $Sh = 2 + 0.6 \, Re^{1/2} \, Sc^{1/3}$.
- [x] **Fick's 2nd Law for a sphere** gives the master equation:
      $\dfrac{M_t}{M_\infty} = 1 - \dfrac{6}{\pi^2} \sum \dfrac{1}{n^2} \exp\left(-\dfrac{\pi^2 D_{eff} n^2 t}{r_p^2}\right)$.
- [x] **Short-time** (valid $M_t/M_\infty < 0.4$): $\dfrac{M_t}{M_\infty} \approx \dfrac{6}{\sqrt{\pi}} \sqrt{\dfrac{D_{eff} t}{r_p^2}}$.
- [x] **Long-time** (valid $M_t/M_\infty > 0.6$): $\dfrac{M_t}{M_\infty} \approx 1 - \dfrac{6}{\pi^2} \exp\left(-\dfrac{\pi^2 D_{eff} t}{r_p^2}\right)$.
- [x] **Smaller particle size** → faster leaching (rate $\propto 1/r_p^2$).
- [x] **Higher temperature** → faster leaching (Stokes–Einstein: $D_{AB} \propto T/\mu$).
- [x] **Higher agitation** → faster Stage I (better $k_L$); does not help Stage II much.
- [x] **Two-resistance model:** $\dfrac{1}{K_L} = \dfrac{1}{k_L} + \dfrac{1}{k_S}$.
- [x] **Biot number** $Bi_m = k_L d_p / D_{eff}$: $Bi_m \ll 1$ → film control; $Bi_m \gg 1$ → pore control.
- [x] **Units check:** $D_{AB}$ in $\mathrm{m^2/s}$, $k_L$ in $\mathrm{m/s}$, $r_p$ in $\mathrm{m}$ (convert mm!).
- [x] **Effective diffusivity** $D_{eff} = \dfrac{\varepsilon \, D_{AB}}{\tau}$ with $\varepsilon$ = porosity, $\tau$ = tortuosity.

> **Final KTU Mantra for Rate of Leaching:**
> *"Know the three stages, master the Fickian sphere solution, apply the right asymptotic form based on the % extraction, and always check units — especially $mm$ to $m$ conversions and the $d_p$ vs $r_p$ distinction."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factors Affecting Rate of Leaching. Working Principles of Leaching Equipment - Shank's System - Thickeners
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT501 - MASS TRANSFER OPERATIONS II
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Factors Affecting Rate of Leaching. Working Principles of Leaching Equipment - Shank's System - Thickeners

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Leaching — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> **Leaching** is a solid-liquid extraction operation in which a soluble constituent is separated from a solid mixture by dissolving it in a liquid solvent. The process involves the selective transfer of one or more soluble components (the **solute**) from a solid phase into a liquid phase (the **solvent**), producing a **leach liquor (extract)** and a **depleted solid residue (raffinate/spent solid)**.

The operation is governed by the fundamental mass transfer mechanism: **diffusion of the solute through the pores of the solid matrix**, followed by **convective transport into the bulk of the solvent**. Leaching is a key **stage-contact differential** (or **equilibrium-stage**) mass transfer operation used in:
- Extraction of oil from seeds (soybean, sunflower, groundnut)
- Leaching of sugar from sugar beets
- Extraction of metals from ores (gold, copper, uranium — hydrometallurgy)
- Pharmaceutical extraction of active ingredients from plant matrices
- Beverage industry (tea, coffee extraction)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tea Bag" Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Tea Cup Model:**
> Imagine dropping a tea bag (solid containing soluble tea compounds) into hot water (solvent). The brown color (solute) slowly seeps out into the clear water. The rate at which your tea becomes dark and flavorful depends on:
> 1. **How finely crushed** the tea leaves are (particle size)
> 2. **How hot** the water is (temperature)
> 3. **How vigorously** you stir the cup (agitation)
> 4. **How much water** you use relative to tea (solvent-to-solid ratio)
> 5. **How many times** you reuse the same bag (multistage counter-current operation)

This is **exactly** the phenomenon of leaching — the tea bag is a continuous-contact extractor, your cup is a single-stage batch extractor, and an industrial Shank's system is essentially a **chain of such "tea cups" arranged in counter-current flow**, with the spent tea bag being squeezed between cups and the brewing water being recycled.

## 1.3 The Three Engineering Sub-Operations of an Industrial Leaching Plant

Any commercial leaching system — whether Shank's, Hildebrandt, Bollman, Kennedy, or continuous counter-current decantation — involves three core physical operations:

| Sub-Operation | Purpose | Equipment Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Solid-Liquid Contacting** | Brings fresh solvent into contact with solids for mass transfer | Agitated tanks, extractors |
| **Phase Separation** | Separates clear extract (leach liquor) from solid residue | Thickeners, filters, clarifiers |
| **Solvent Recovery** | Recovers solvent from both extract and spent solid for reuse | Evaporators, dryers, strippers |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:**
> A typical KTU question on this module often tests whether students can identify that **thickeners are not extractors** — they are **phase-separation devices**. The leaching happens in the contactor (Shank's tanks, Bollman baskets, etc.); the thickener merely separates the two phases. Confusing these two roles is a common error that costs marks.

## 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics (KTU Convention)

The following dimensionless and dimensional metrics are used throughout this module:

- **N** = Number of ideal stages (Shank's system)
- **R** = Underflow ratio (kg solution retained per kg insoluble solid) → unitless
- **V** = Mass of solution (solvent + dissolved solute) per kg insoluble solid
- **Y_n** = Concentration of solute in overflow from stage *n* (kg solute / kg solvent)
- **X_n** = Concentration of solute in underflow from stage *n* (kg solute / kg solution)
- **x_A, x_B** = Mass fractions of solute in feed solid and spent solid

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Underflow Ratio" Matters in KTU Problems:**
> In Shank's system calculations, the assumption is that each unit of insoluble solid **carries a constant mass of solution** out of each thickener stage — this is the **constant underflow assumption**, denoted by **R**. It is the single most-used parameter in KTU numerical problems on leaching.

---

## 1.5 Working Principle of Shank's System (Pre-Overview)

The **Shank's System** is a **counter-current, multi-stage, continuous leaching system** consisting of a series of **agitated leaching tanks** (called **diffusers** or **extraction tanks**) each connected to an underlying **thickener**. It is one of the most widely used leaching systems in industry — particularly for **sugar beet processing** and **oilseed extraction** (historically).

**Key Feature:** Solids move in **one direction** (say, left → right), while solvent moves in the **opposite direction** (right → left). This counter-current arrangement allows:
- **Fresh solvent** to contact **almost-spent solids** → maximum recovery of residual solute
- **Fresh solids** to contact **enriched solvent** → maximum concentration of extract

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Factors Affecting the Rate of Leaching

The rate of leaching is governed by **mass transfer resistance** across the solid-liquid interface. By analogy with other mass transfer operations, the **rate equation** takes the form:

$$N_A = k_L \cdot a \cdot (C_s^* - C_b)$$

Where:
- $N_A$ = molar flux of solute (kmol/m²·s)
- $k_L$ = liquid-side mass transfer coefficient (m/s)
- $a$ = interfacial area per unit volume (m²/m³)
- $C_s^*$ = concentration of solute at solid surface (kg/m³) — equilibrium value
- $C_b$ = bulk concentration of solute in solvent (kg/m³)

Every factor that affects leaching does so by influencing **one of these three parameters**: $k_L$, $a$, or the driving force $(C_s^* - C_b)$.

### 2.1.1 Particle Size

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mechanism:** Smaller particle size → larger surface area → higher $a$ and shorter diffusion path within pores.

- Finer grinding exposes more solute to the solvent and reduces the distance the solute must travel through the solid's pore network.
- However, **excessively fine particles** can form a **sludge** that blocks solvent flow, reduces permeability, and makes downstream thickening difficult.
- **Optimum particle size** is a trade-off between extraction kinetics and downstream handling.

$$a \propto \frac{1}{d_p}$$

Where $d_p$ = mean particle diameter.

### 2.1.2 Temperature

- Higher temperature → higher solubility of solute in solvent → higher $(C_s^* - C_b)$ → faster leaching.
- Temperature also increases the **diffusion coefficient** $D_{AB}$ via the Stokes-Einstein relation:
$$D_{AB} \propto \frac{T}{\mu}$$
- **Limitation:** Some solutes (e.g., sugars, proteins) are **heat-sensitive** and may degrade. Pharmaceutical extractions often operate near ambient or sub-ambient conditions.

### 2.1.3 Agitation / Mixing Intensity

- Agitation **renews the solvent film** at the solid surface, increasing $k_L$.
- It prevents **local saturation** of the solvent adjacent to the solid.
- Agitation also helps **suspend the particles**, ensuring all particles contact fresh solvent.
- **Excessive agitation** causes mechanical attrition, produces fines, and consumes energy.

### 2.1.4 Solvent-to-Solid Ratio

- Higher solvent ratio → lower bulk concentration $C_b$ → higher driving force $(C_s^* - C_b)$ → faster leaching.
- Also dilutes the extract, requiring **higher downstream evaporation** costs → engineering trade-off.

### 2.1.5 Nature of the Solute and Solid Matrix

- **Solubility** of solute in solvent is the single most important thermodynamic factor.
- **Porosity** of the solid: more porous → easier internal diffusion.
- **Cellular structure**: intact cells (e.g., oilseeds) may require **cell-wall rupture** (cooking, flaking) before leaching.
- **Chemical reaction**: some leaching processes (e.g., metal ores) involve **chemical reaction** with the solvent (acid leaching, cyanidation), adding a reaction-rate term.

### 2.1.6 Diffusion Path & Internal Mass Transfer

Within the solid particle, the solute diffuses through tortuous pores. The effective diffusion coefficient in the solid is:

$$D_{eff} = \frac{\varepsilon \cdot D_{AB}}{\tau}$$

Where:
- $\varepsilon$ = porosity (void fraction) of the solid
- $\tau$ = tortuosity factor (typically 2–6 for natural solids)

### 2.1.7 Viscosity of the Solvent

- Lower viscosity → higher $D_{AB}$ and higher $k_L$ → faster leaching.
- Higher viscosity → slower molecular diffusion → slower leaching.

### 2.1.8 Concentration Driving Force

- The instantaneous driving force **decreases** with time as $C_b$ approaches $C_s^*$.
- **Counter-current operation** maintains a relatively high driving force at every stage, which is why Shank's system is preferred over single-stage batch leaching.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Master these formulas for KTU 2024 ESE — they cover 90% of numerical questions on this module.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N_A = k_L \cdot a \cdot (C_s^* - C_b)$ | Rate of leaching (overall mass transfer) | kmol/m²·s |
| 2 | $\text{Rate} \propto \frac{1}{d_p}$ | Particle size effect | — |
| 3 | $D_{AB} \propto \frac{T}{\mu}$ | Temperature-viscosity-diffusivity relation | m²/s |
| 4 | $D_{eff} = \dfrac{\varepsilon \cdot D_{AB}}{\tau}$ | Effective diffusivity in porous solid | m²/s |
| 5 | $N = \dfrac{\ln\left[\dfrac{x_A - x_N^*}{x_B - x_N^*}\right]}{\ln(V/B)}$ (if $V=B$) | Number of ideal stages — Shank's system (variable underflow) | unitless |
| 6 | $R = \dfrac{\text{kg solution in underflow}}{\text{kg insoluble solid}}$ | Underflow ratio (constant- $R$ assumption) | unitless |
| 7 | $V_{n+1} = V_n = R + \text{solute in underflow}$ | Underflow volume balance at stage $n$ | kg/kg insoluble |
| 8 | $Y_n = \dfrac{X_n \cdot R}{1 - X_n}$ | Relation between overflow & underflow compositions | — |
| 9 | $Y_{n+1} = \dfrac{X_n \cdot R - X_A + Y_n}{R-1}$ (Kremser) | Single-stage operating line | — |
| 10 | $\eta_{extraction} = \dfrac{x_A - x_B}{x_A} \times 100\%$ | Percentage of solute extracted | % |

---

## 2.3 Working Principles of Leaching Equipment

### 2.3.1 Classification of Leaching Equipment

| Class | Equipment | Mode | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Percolation Type** | Shank's system, Bollman extractor, Hildebrandt extractor | Continuous | Solvent percolates through a stationary or moving bed of solids |
| **Agitated Bed Type** | Pachuca tanks, Dorr agitator, agitated leaching tanks | Batch or semi-continuous | Solids kept in suspension by mechanical/agitation |
| **Screw-Type / Continuous** | Hildebrandt, Bonotto, Kennedy extractor | Continuous | Solids transported by screws/conveyors |
| **Counter-Current Decantation (CCD)** | Series of thickeners + mixers | Continuous | Thickener-based multi-stage washing |

### 2.3.2 Shank's System — Detailed Working Principle

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU):**
> The **Shank's system** is a **counter-current multi-stage leaching system** in which solids and solvent flow in opposite directions through a series of **agitated leaching tanks** (diffusers) each paired with a **thickener** for phase separation.

#### Configuration of a Typical Shank's System (4-Stage)

The system consists of a series of **identical units**, each containing:
1. **A leaching tank (diffuser)** — fitted with mechanical agitator, false bottom with screen.
2. **A connecting thickener** — for settling the slurry and producing clear overflow.
3. **Piping & pumps** — for counter-current transfer of solids (forward) and solvent (backward).

#### Sequence of Operations in Each Stage

For each leaching tank in a cycle:

1. **Fill with Solids + Recycle Solvent** from the previous stage.
2. **Agitate** for a fixed contact time (typically 30–60 min) — leaching occurs.
3. **Stop agitation**, allow **brief settling** of coarse solids on the false bottom.
4. **Drain the strong extract** (overflow) from the top — this becomes the **product extract** (in the first stage) or the **feed solvent** for the next stage (in subsequent stages).
5. **Transfer the leached solids** (underflow) to the next tank via screw/pump, along with the adhering solution (underflow).
6. The **weakest solvent (fresh solvent)** enters the **last stage** of the system, contacting the **most-spent solids** — this is the heart of counter-current operation.

#### Counter-Current Flow Logic

$$\text{Solids flow: Stage 1 (feed)} \rightarrow \text{Stage 2} \rightarrow \text{Stage 3} \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow \text{Stage N (spent solid outlet)}$$

$$\text{Solvent flow: Stage N (fresh solvent)} \rightarrow \text{Stage N-1} \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow \text{Stage 1 (concentrated extract outlet)}$$

#### Material Balance Across Stage *n*

$$\text{In:}\quad Y_{n+1}(V) + x_A(V_s) = \text{Out:}\quad Y_n(V) + X_n(R)$$

Where $V$ = mass of solvent entering/leaving per kg of insoluble solid.

#### Stage-to-Stage Equation (Kremser Form for Variable Underflow)

For the Shank's system, the operating line on a **Ponchon-Savarit-type** $X$–$Y$ diagram is:

$$Y_{n+1} = \frac{X_n \cdot R - X_{feed} + Y_n}{R - 1}$$

The **number of ideal stages** is determined graphically by **stepping off** between the operating line and the equilibrium line.

#### Applications of Shank's System

- **Sugar beet diffusion** (classical) — extracts sucrose from cossettes (sliced beets).
- **Oilseed extraction** (older plants).
- **Mineral processing** — copper, uranium ores.
- **Tannin extraction** from bark.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:**
> When asked to "design a Shank's system for given feed & product specifications," the standard approach is:
> 1. Calculate underflow ratio **R** from solid-liquid equilibrium data.
> 2. Compute **mass of solvent V** per kg of insoluble solid.
> 3. Construct the operating line on a Ponchon diagram using the stage-to-stage equation.
> 4. Step off stages between operating line and equilibrium line.

---

### 2.3.3 Thickeners — Detailed Working Principle

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU):**
> A **thickener** is a **large, circular, gravity-settling vessel** used to separate solids from liquids in a slurry by sedimentation, producing a **clarified overflow (clear liquid)** and a **concentrated underflow (sludge)**. It is the **workhorse of solid-liquid separation** in hydrometallurgy, leaching, and wastewater treatment.

#### Structural Components of a Thickener

1. **Cylindrical Tank** — typically 5–200 m diameter, 2–6 m deep. Constructed of steel, concrete, or earth with liner.
2. **Feed Well (Center)** — central cylindrical well where the slurry is introduced gently, allowing flocculation and reducing turbulence.
3. **Feed Pipe (Center Column)** — delivers the feed slurry to the feed well.
4. **Rake Arms (Radial Trusses)** — slowly rotating (0.5–2 RPM) arms fitted with blades (pickets) that gently move settled solids toward the central discharge cone.
5. **Drive Mechanism (Central Shaft)** — rotates the rake arms; includes a torque alarm to detect overload.
6. **Overflow Launder (Peripheral Cylindrical Trough)** — collects clarified liquid at the top rim.
7. **Underflow Discharge (Central Cone)** — concentrated sludge exits at the bottom through a cone.

#### Working Principle — Step-by-Step

1. **Feed Entry:** Slurry enters the **feed well** at the center of the thickener.
2. **Flocculation:** In the feed well, the slurry is gently mixed with **flocculants** (polymers like polyacrylamide) to aggregate fine particles into larger flocs that settle faster.
3. **Sedimentation:** The flocculated slurry spreads radially outward. Solids settle under gravity; the clarified liquid rises to the top.
4. **Thickening Zone:** At the bottom, solids form a thick slurry bed. The **rake arms** slowly move the settled solids toward the central discharge cone.
5. **Clarification Zone:** The upper region contains clarified liquid that overflows into the **peripheral launder** and is collected as the **overflow**.
6. **Discharge:**
   - **Overflow** (top) → clear liquid → sent to next stage, evaporator, or product tank.
   - **Underflow** (bottom) → concentrated slurry (~50–70% solids) → pumped to filtration or next stage.

#### Settling Velocity (Key Design Parameter)

The settling velocity of a particle in a thickener is given by **Stokes' Law** (for laminar settling):

$$v_s = \frac{(\rho_s - \rho_L) \cdot g \cdot d_p^2}{18 \cdot \mu}$$

Where:
- $\rho_s$ = density of solid particle (kg/m³)
- $\rho_L$ = density of liquid (kg/m³)
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration (9.81 m/s²)
- $d_p$ = particle diameter (m)
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity of liquid (Pa·s)

> [!WARNING]
> Stokes' Law applies only when **Re_p < 0.2** (laminar regime). For larger particles, the **Newton's law** or **transition regime** correlations are used. KTU problems typically specify "laminar" or "small particle" to use Stokes' Law.

#### Thickener Capacity (Solid Flux Method — Koyoma / Coe-Clevenger)

The thickener area required is determined by the **solid flux method**:

$$A = \frac{M_s}{G_L}$$

Where:
- $A$ = thickener cross-sectional area (m²)
- $M_s$ = mass rate of solids fed (kg/s)
- $G_L$ = limiting solid flux (kg/m²·s), determined by **Koyoma's** test or from the **Coe-Clevenger** method

#### Role of Thickeners in Shank's System

In Shank's system, **each leaching tank is paired with a thickener** that:
1. **Recovers the leach liquor** (overflow) — sent to the next stage (counter-current) or to the product tank (first stage).
2. **Returns the solids** (underflow) — transferred to the next leaching tank with the associated entrained solution.
3. **Maintains the underflow ratio R** — the amount of solution carried by the solids from one stage to the next.

#### Why Thickeners (and not filters) in Shank's System

- Thickeners operate **continuously** and handle **large slurry volumes** efficiently.
- Filters (e.g., rotary vacuum filters) are used for **final dewatering** after the last thickener, not as intermediate stage separators.
- In a CCD (Counter-Current Decantation) circuit, multiple thickeners in series perform the multi-stage washing.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application of Shank's System + Thickeners |
|---|---|
| **Sugar Industry** | Extraction of sucrose from sugar beet cossettes (diffusers + thickeners) |
| **Vegetable Oil** | Older oil mills used Shank's-type diffusion batteries for oilseed extraction |
| **Hydrometallurgy** | Leaching of copper/uranium/gold ores followed by CCD thickener circuits |
| **Pharmaceuticals** | Extraction of active compounds from herbal matrices |
| **Food & Beverage** | Coffee, tea, chicory extraction |
| **Wastewater Treatment** | Sludge thickening in activated sludge plants |
| **Mineral Processing** | Counter-current washing of leach residues |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Rate of Leaching (Mass Transfer Model)

We model a single solid particle as a **sphere** of radius $R_p$ immersed in an infinite solvent bath. The solute diffuses out of the sphere into the surrounding liquid.

### Step 1: Fick's First Law at the Particle Surface

The flux of solute from the solid surface into the bulk liquid is given by Fick's law applied across a thin liquid film of thickness $\delta$:

$$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC}{dr}\bigg\vert_{r=R_p}$$

### Step 2: Integrate Across the Liquid Film

Assume steady-state, spherical geometry, and dilute solution:

$$N_A \cdot 4\pi R_p^2 = -D_{AB} \cdot 4\pi r^2 \frac{dC}{dr}$$

Rearranging:

$$-N_A \cdot \frac{dr}{r^2} = D_{AB} \cdot dC$$

### Step 3: Integrate from $r = R_p$ (surface) to $r = R_p + \delta$ (bulk)

$$\begin{aligned}
-N_A \int_{R_p}^{R_p+\delta} \frac{dr}{r^2} &= D_{AB} \int_{C_s^*}^{C_b} dC \\
-N_A \left[-\frac{1}{r}\right]_{R_p}^{R_p+\delta} &= D_{AB} (C_b - C_s^*) \\
N_A \left[\frac{1}{R_p} - \frac{1}{R_p+\delta}\right] &= D_{AB} (C_b - C_s^*) \\
N_A \cdot \frac{\delta}{R_p(R_p+\delta)} &= D_{AB} (C_b - C_s^*)
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: For $\delta \ll R_p$, approximate $R_p + \delta \approx R_p$

$$N_A \cdot \frac{\delta}{R_p^2} = D_{AB} (C_b - C_s^*)$$

Rearranging:

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB}}{\delta} (C_s^* - C_b)$$

### Step 5: Define Liquid-Side Mass Transfer Coefficient

By definition, the **liquid-side mass transfer coefficient** is:

$$k_L = \frac{D_{AB}}{\delta}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{N_A = k_L (C_s^* - C_b)}$$

Multiplying by the total interfacial area $A$:

$$\boxed{\text{Rate} = k_L \cdot A \cdot (C_s^* - C_b) = k_L \cdot a \cdot V \cdot (C_s^* - C_b)}$$

This is the **fundamental rate equation for leaching** (and indeed for most liquid-side mass transfer controlled operations including gas-liquid absorption at low concentrations).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Settling Velocity in a Thickener (Stokes' Law)

### Step 1: Force Balance on a Spherical Particle Falling Under Gravity

Three forces act on the particle:
1. **Gravitational force (downward):** $F_g = \frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \rho_s g$
2. **Buoyancy force (upward):** $F_b = \frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \rho_L g$
3. **Drag force (upward, opposing motion):** $F_D = C_D \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} d_p^2 \cdot \frac{\rho_L v_s^2}{2}$

### Step 2: At Terminal Velocity, Net Force = 0

$$\begin{aligned}
F_g - F_b - F_D &= 0 \\
\frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 (\rho_s - \rho_L) g &= C_D \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} d_p^2 \cdot \frac{\rho_L v_s^2}{2} \\
\frac{d_p (\rho_s - \rho_L) g}{3} &= \frac{C_D \rho_L v_s^2}{4}
\end{aligned}$$

Solving for $v_s$:

$$v_s = \sqrt{\frac{4 d_p (\rho_s - \rho_L) g}{3 C_D \rho_L}}$$

### Step 3: For Laminar Flow (Re_p < 0.2), $C_D = 24/Re_p$

Substitute $C_D = 24/Re_p = 24\mu / (\rho_L v_s d_p)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
v_s &= \sqrt{\frac{4 d_p (\rho_s - \rho_L) g}{3 \cdot \frac{24\mu}{\rho_L v_s d_p} \cdot \rho_L}} \\
v_s &= \sqrt{\frac{4 d_p^2 (\rho_s - \rho_L) g}{72 \mu}} \\
v_s^2 &= \frac{d_p^2 (\rho_s - \rho_L) g}{18 \mu}
\end{aligned}$$

Taking square root:

$$\boxed{v_s = \frac{d_p^2 (\rho_s - \rho_L) g}{18 \mu}}$$

This is **Stokes' Law**, valid for Re_p < 0.2.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Stage-to-Stage Equation for Shank's System

### Step 1: Define Variables for Stage *n*

Let $V$ = mass of solvent (kg solvent per kg insoluble solid) entering stage $n$ from the previous solvent stage.
Let $R$ = underflow ratio (kg solution retained per kg insoluble solid in underflow).
Let $x_A$ = mass fraction of solute in feed solid (on solute + insoluble basis).
Let $Y_n$ = concentration in overflow from stage $n$ (kg solute / kg solvent).
Let $X_n$ = concentration in underflow from stage $n$ (kg solute / kg solution in underflow).

### Step 2: Solute Mass Balance Around Stage *n*

**Inputs to stage *n*:**
- Solute in overflow from stage $(n+1)$: $Y_{n+1} \cdot V$ (kg solute per kg insoluble solid)
- Solute in feed solid (entering at the **first** stage only): $x_A$ (kg solute per kg insoluble solid)
- Solute in solution carried by solids from stage $(n-1)$: $X_{n-1} \cdot R$ (or $X_n \cdot R$ for stage > 1 with proper indexing)

**Outputs from stage *n*:**
- Solute in overflow going to stage $(n-1)$: $Y_n \cdot V$
- Solute in underflow going to stage $(n+1)$: $X_n \cdot R$

### Step 3: At Steady State

$$\text{Input} = \text{Output}$$

$$Y_{n+1} \cdot V + x_A + X_{n-1} \cdot R = Y_n \cdot V + X_n \cdot R$$

### Step 4: Define $Y_n$ in terms of $X_n$ (Equilibrium / Constant Underflow Assumption)

For constant underflow ratio $R$, the relationship between overflow and underflow concentrations is:

$$Y_n = \frac{X_n \cdot R}{1 - X_n}$$

### Step 5: Rearrange the Stage-to-Stage Equation

For a **first stage** (where feed solid enters, $x_A$ enters as fresh solute load):

$$Y_2 \cdot V + x_A + X_N \cdot R = Y_1 \cdot V + X_1 \cdot R$$

For subsequent stages (no fresh solid feed), the simplified form is:

$$\boxed{Y_{n+1} = \frac{X_n \cdot R - X_{feed} + Y_n}{R - 1}}$$

where $X_{feed}$ represents the solute input with fresh solid feed.

This equation is the **operating line of the Shank's system** on the $X$–$Y$ diagram, and is used with the equilibrium line to **graphically determine the number of ideal stages**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Factors Affecting Leaching Rate — Quantified Effects

| Factor | Effect on $k_L$ | Effect on $a$ | Effect on $(C_s^* - C_b)$ | Net Effect on Rate | KTU Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Decreasing particle size** | Small change | ↑↑ Strongly increases | No effect (if equilibrium maintained) | ↑↑ Strongly increases | Most powerful practical lever |
| **Increasing temperature** | ↑ Increases (via $\uparrow D_{AB}$) | No effect | ↑ Increases (higher solubility) | ↑↑ Strongly increases | Limited by solute stability |
| **Increasing agitation** | ↑↑ Strongly increases | Marginal (keeps particles suspended) | ↑ Increases (lower $C_b$) | ↑↑ Strongly increases | Power cost trade-off |
| **Increasing solvent-to-solid ratio** | No effect | No effect | ↑ Increases (lower $C_b$) | ↑ Increases | Dilutes extract, higher evaporation cost |
| **Increasing porosity** | ↑ Slightly (less internal resistance) | ↑ Increases (more accessible area) | No effect | ↑ Increases | Critical for ores, seeds |
| **Lowering solvent viscosity** | ↑ Increases (via $\uparrow D_{AB}$) | No effect | No effect | ↑ Increases | Heated solvent typically lower $\mu$ |
| **Use of fresh solvent (counter-current)** | No effect | No effect | ↑↑ Strongly increases (always high) | ↑↑ Strongly increases | This is the Shank's advantage! |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Style Problem Statement:**
> A Shank's system is used to leach oil from ground soybean flakes. The feed solid contains **20% oil by mass** (balance insoluble solid). The final spent solid contains **0.5% oil by mass**. The fresh solvent enters at a rate of **3.5 kg solvent per kg insoluble solid** and the underflow ratio is **R = 1.8 kg solution/kg insoluble solid**. Equilibrium between the underflow and overflow is described by **Y = 1.5X / (1 - 0.5X)**. Determine the **number of ideal stages** using the graphical/Kremser method.

#### Given Data

- $x_A = 0.20$ kg oil/kg insoluble solid
- $x_B = 0.005$ kg oil/kg insoluble solid
- $V = 3.5$ kg solvent/kg insoluble solid
- $R = 1.8$ kg solution/kg insoluble solid
- Equilibrium: $Y = \dfrac{1.5X}{1 - 0.5X}$

#### Step 1: Operating Line Equation (Kremser)

The operating line for stage $n$:

$$Y_{n+1} = \frac{X_n \cdot R - x_A + Y_n}{R - 1}$$

Substituting $R = 1.8$ and $x_A = 0.20$:

$$Y_{n+1} = \frac{1.8 X_n - 0.20 + Y_n}{0.8}$$

#### Step 2: Construct Equilibrium Curve

For various $X$, compute $Y$:

| $X$ | $Y = 1.5X / (1 - 0.5X)$ |
|---|---|
| 0.00 | 0.000 |
| 0.05 | 0.077 |
| 0.10 | 0.158 |
| 0.15 | 0.243 |
| 0.20 | 0.333 |
| 0.25 | 0.429 |
| 0.30 | 0.529 |
| 0.35 | 0.636 |
| 0.40 | 0.750 |

#### Step 3: Construct Operating Line

For the operating line, choose two points:

**Point 1:** At $X_n = X_B = 0.005$, $Y_n = Y_1$ (extract composition, top of operating line):

At the **last stage** (where spent solid leaves), $Y_{n+1} = 0$ (fresh solvent), and $X_n = X_B = 0.005$:

$$0 = \frac{1.8(0.005) - 0.20 + Y_n}{0.8}$$

$$Y_n = 0.20 - 0.009 = 0.191$$

So the operating line passes through the point $(0.005, 0.191)$.

**Point 2:** At $X_n = 0.20$, $Y_n$ to be determined from the operating line slope.

The slope of the operating line is $R/(R-1) = 1.8/0.8 = 2.25$.

#### Step 4: Determine Composition of Final Extract $Y_1$

Overall solute balance (whole system):

$$x_A + Y_{N+1} \cdot V = X_B \cdot R + Y_1 \cdot V$$

Since $Y_{N+1} = 0$ (fresh solvent) and $X_B = 0.005$:

$$0.20 + 0 = 0.005 \times 1.8 + Y_1 \times 3.5$$

$$0.20 - 0.009 = 3.5 Y_1$$

$$Y_1 = \frac{0.191}{3.5} = 0.0546 \text{ kg oil/kg solvent}$$

#### Step 5: Graphical Stepping (Summary)

Plot the operating line from $(0, 0.085)$ to $(0.4, 0.78)$ and the equilibrium curve. Step off stages between operating line and equilibrium line starting from $Y_1 = 0.0546$ at the bottom-left.

- **Stage 1:** $Y_1 = 0.0546$ → $X_1$ from operating line → $Y_2$ from equilibrium
- **Stage 2:** $Y_2$ → $X_2$ → $Y_3$
- ...continue until $X_n \leq X_B = 0.005$.

For this system, **3–4 ideal stages** are typically required (exact count from graph).

#### Result

$$\boxed{N_{\text{ideal}} \approx 4 \text{ stages}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note:** Always round **up** the number of stages in the final answer (use ceiling function). The number of **real stages** would be higher due to stage efficiency ($\eta < 1$).
